# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain how the 8086 generates a 20-bit physical address from a 16-bit logical address. What is the role of segment and offset?

**Model Answer:**
The 8086 uses **segmentation** to generate a 20-bit physical address from a 16-bit logical address. A logical address is expressed in the form `Segment : Offset`, where the **Segment** is a 16-bit value from one of the four segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) and the **Offset** is a 16-bit displacement.
- The Segment is **shifted left by 4 bits** (multiplied by `10H`), appending four `0` bits to produce a 20-bit base address aligned on a 16-byte boundary.
- The 16-bit Offset is then **added** to this base to obtain the final 20-bit Physical Address:
$$\text{PA} = (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$$
Any carry beyond bit 20 is discarded. This mechanism allows the 8086 to address 1 MB of physical memory using 16-bit registers.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** List the four segment registers of 8086 and state the default offset register paired with each.

**Model Answer:**
| Segment Register | Purpose | Default Offset Register(s) |
|---|---|---|
| **CS (Code Segment)** | Points to the area containing the executing program | **IP (Instruction Pointer)** |
| **DS (Data Segment)** | Points to the data area used by the program | BX, SI, DI, [BP+disp] (data access) |
| **SS (Stack Segment)** | Points to the stack area | **SP**, BP |
| **ES (Extra Segment)** | Auxiliary segment, mainly for string instructions | **DI** (destination in string ops) |

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ CHOICE A — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Physical Memory Organization** of the 8086 microprocessor. Discuss the role of `BHE` and `A0` signals in bank selection.

**Model Solution:**

The 8086 microprocessor has a 20-bit address bus, enabling it to address **1 MB of physical memory** ranging from `00000H` to `FFFFFH`. Because the data bus is 16 bits wide, the entire memory is divided into **two independent 512 KB banks**:

1. **Even (Lower) Bank** — Contains data bytes for all *even* addresses. Connected to data lines `D0 – D7`.
2. **Odd (Upper) Bank** — Contains data bytes for all *odd* addresses. Connected to data lines `D8 – D15`.

#### Role of BHE and A0 Signals
| Signal | Pin | Function | Bank Activated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A0** | Pin 5 (AD0) | Selects the Even Bank | Active when A0 = 0 |
| **BHE** | Pin 34 | Selects the Odd Bank | Active when BHE = 0 (low) |

#### Bank Selection Truth Table
| BHE | A0 | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **16-bit word** read/write from/to EVEN address (both banks active) |
| 0 | 1 | **Upper byte** read/write from/to ODD address (Odd bank only) |
| 1 | 0 | **Lower byte** read/write from/to EVEN address (Even bank only) |
| 1 | 1 | **Idle** — no bank selected |

#### Access Timing
- **Even-aligned 16-bit read** (e.g., from `20000H`): One bus cycle of **4 T-states**. Both banks activated simultaneously.
- **Odd-aligned 16-bit read** (e.g., from `20001H`): Requires **two bus cycles (8 T-states)**. The 8086 first reads the byte at `20000H` (even bank), then `20002H` (odd bank), and internally assembles the word.

The multiplexing of address and data on `AD0–AD15` requires an external **74LS373 octal latch** controlled by the `ALE` signal to *de-multiplex* the address from the data.

---

**Question A (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Segmentation** in the 8086. How is the **Physical Address** calculated from a logical address? Illustrate with an example.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Segmentation is the memory management technique in the 8086 that divides the 1 MB physical memory into logical **64 KB segments** (variable size), each starting on a **16-byte (paragraph) boundary**. Each logical address is specified as `Segment : Offset`, and the physical address is derived using a 20-bit binary adder inside the **Address Generation Unit (AGU)**.

#### Physical Address Calculation
The 20-bit physical address is computed as:
$$
\text{Physical Address (PA)} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}
$$

**Steps:**
1. The 16-bit value in the **Segment Register** is shifted left by 4 bits (or multiplied by 16 = `10H`). This produces a 20-bit base address.
2. The 16-bit **Offset** is added to the 20-bit base.
3. Any carry generated from bit 20 is **discarded** (wrap-around modulo 1 MB).

#### Worked Example
**Given Logical Address: `3050H : 2050H`**

**Step 1:** Compute the base:
- `3050H × 10H = 30500H`

**Step 2:** Add the offset:
- `30500H + 02050H = 32550H`

**Result:** The Physical Address is **`32550H`**.

#### Key Properties of Segmentation
- A segment is **64 KB max** but can be smaller.
- Segments can **overlap** since a new segment can begin every 16 bytes.
- The same memory byte can be addressed by **4096 different logical addresses** (`Segment : Offset` pairs).
- Segmentation enables **relocatable code**, **modular programming**, and separation of code/data/stack.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — 14 MARKS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**Question B (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **signal descriptions** of the 8086 microprocessor. Mention the function of the multiplexed address/data bus and the role of `BHE`, `ALE`, `RD`, and `CLK`.

**Model Solution:**

The 8086 is a **40-pin DIP** IC powered by **+5 V DC** with a **5 MHz clock**. Because it must carry 20 address lines and 16 data lines, Intel **multiplexed** several pins to fit within 40 pins. The major signals are:

#### 1. Multiplexed Address/Data Bus — `AD0–AD15` (Pins 39, 2–16)
- These 16 lines carry the **lower 16 bits of the address** during the **T1** state of the bus cycle.
- During **T2, T3, T4, and Tw (wait) states**, the same lines carry **16-bit data**.
- An external **74LS373 latch**, triggered by the `ALE` signal, captures the address during T1 so it remains valid on the address bus while data is being transferred.

#### 2. Multiplexed Address/Status Bus — `A16/S3` to `A19/S6` (Pins 35–38)
- During T1: Carry the upper 4 bits of the 20-bit address.
- During T2–T4: Carry status signals — `S3` and `S4` indicate the **currently active segment register** (00 = ES, 01 = SS, 10 = CS, 11 = DS), `S5` reflects the **IF (Interrupt Flag)**, and `S6 = 0` always.

#### 3. `BHE / S7` (Pin 34)
- **During T1:** `BHE` (Bus High Enable, active low) enables the **upper 8-bit (D8–D15) data bus** and the **odd memory bank**.
- **During T2–T4:** Acts as a status bit `S7` (always 1 in 8086, reserved in 8087 NDP).

#### 4. `ALE` — Address Latch Enable (Pin 25)
- Active high output, indicates that a valid address is present on the `AD0–AD15` and `A16/S3–A19/S6` lines during T1. Used to latch the address externally.

#### 5. `RD` — Read Strobe (Pin 32)
- Active low output; signals that the 8086 is performing a **memory or I/O read** operation. The addressed device must place data on the bus.

#### 6. `CLK` — Clock Input (Pin 19)
- Provides the timing reference for the 8086. Must have a **33% duty cycle** (high for 1/3 period, low for 2/3). The internal clock generator divides the external `CLK` by 3 to produce the internal processor clock (e.g., 15 MHz external → 5 MHz internal for standard 8086).

#### 7. Other Critical Signals
- `M/IO` (Pin 28): Distinguishes **memory** (`1`) vs **I/O** (`0`) access.
- `WR` (Pin 29): Write strobe (active low).
- `READY` (Pin 22): Insert wait states.
- `INTR` (Pin 18): Maskable interrupt request.
- `NMI` (Pin 17): Non-maskable interrupt (type 2).
- `RESET` (Pin 21): CPU reset (high for ≥ 4 clock cycles).
- `TEST` (Pin 23): Used with WAIT instruction for 8087 NDP synchronization.
- `HOLD` (Pin 31) & `HLDA` (Pin 30): DMA handshake.
- `MN/MX` (Pin 33): Selects Minimum (1) or Maximum (0) mode.

---

**Question B (b) [7 Marks]** For the 8086 microprocessor, the logical address is given as `4A2BH : 12B0H`. Calculate the **Physical Address**. Also, determine the memory range accessible within this segment.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Logical Address = `4A2BH : 12B0H`

**Step 1: Compute the Segment Base (20 bits)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment Base} &= 4\text{A2BH} \times 10\text{H} \\
&= 4\text{A2B0H} \quad \text{(append a 0 in hex)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Add the Offset**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment Base} &= 4\text{A2B0H} \\
\text{Offset} &= 12\text{B0H} \\
\text{Physical Address} &= 4\text{A2B0H} + 12\text{B0H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Performing the hex addition:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{4A2B0H} &= 0100\ 1010\ 0010\ 1011\ 0000 \\
+\ \text{012B0H} &= 0000\ 0001\ 0010\ 1011\ 0000 \\
\hline
\text{PA} &= 0100\ 1011\ 0101\ 0110\ 0000 \\
\text{PA} &= \mathbf{4B560H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Physical Address = **`4B560H`**

**Step 3: Memory Range of the Segment**
- **Lower Bound (Offset = 0000H):** `4A2B0H`
- **Upper Bound (Offset = FFFFH):** `4A2B0H + FFFFH = 5A2AFH`

So the **accessible memory range** of segment `4A2BH` is from **`4A2B0H` to `5A2AFH`** (a total of 65,536 = 64 KB bytes).

**Verification:** Computed PA = `4B560H` lies within the range `[4A2B0H, 5A2AFH]`. ✔

**Binary Verification:**
- `4A2BH` = `0100 1010 0010 1011`
- Shift left by 4 → `0100 1010 0010 1011 0000` = `4A2B0H`
- Add `12B0H` → `4A2B0H + 12B0H = 4B560H` ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Carry Discard Mistake:** When `Segment × 10H + Offset` exceeds `FFFFF`, students often write the carry as part of the address. The correct rule: the 8086 has only a 20-bit address, so any carry beyond bit 20 is **discarded (wraparound)**.
> 2. **Confusing ×10H with ×10D:** Many students multiply by decimal 10 instead of hexadecimal 10 (= 16 in decimal). Use **hex multiplication** or **shift left by 4 bits**.
> 3. **Forgetting to append 0H:** Students often forget that `× 10H` is equivalent to appending a `0` digit in hex (e.g., `3050H → 30500H`).
> 4. **Confusing BHE polarity:** `BHE` is **active LOW**. `BHE = 0` (low) enables the odd bank, not `BHE = 1`.
> 5. **Address/Data multiplexing:** Students often draw the `AD0–AD15` bus as a permanent address bus, forgetting the **time-multiplexing** (address in T1, data in T2–T4).
> 6. **ALE timing:** `ALE` goes high only during **T1** to latch the address — students must clearly mark this in timing diagrams.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **1 MB Memory:** 8086 has a 20-bit address bus → `2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes`, addressed `00000H–FFFFF`.
- **Two Memory Banks:** Even bank (D0–D7, A0=0) and Odd bank (D8–D15, BHE=0), each 512 KB.
- **16-bit word read at odd address = 2 bus cycles (8 T-states)**; at even address = 1 bus cycle (4 T-states).
- **Segmentation Formula:** `PA = (Segment × 10H) + Offset`, with **carry discarded** beyond 20 bits.
- **Paragraph = 16 bytes**; segments are paragraph-aligned (lowest 4 bits of base = `0000`).
- **Four Segment Registers:** CS, DS, SS, ES — each paired with a default offset register (IP, BX/SI/DI, SP, DI respectively).
- **Overlap:** A single memory byte can have up to **4096 different logical addresses**.
- **8086 is 40-pin DIP**, **+5 V DC**, **5 MHz clock** (33% duty cycle external, divided by 3 internally).
- **Multiplexed Pins:** `AD0–AD15` (address in T1, data in T2–T4) and `A16/S3–A19/S6` (address in T1, status in T2–T4).
- **BHE/S7:** Active low to enable the odd bank in T1; status `S7` in T2–T4.
- **ALE:** High in T1 only; used to latch the address into external **74LS373**.
- **Control signals (min mode):** `RD`, `WR`, `M/IO`, `ALE`, `BHE`, `DEN`, `DT/R`.
- **Interrupts:** `INTR` (maskable, IF-gated), `NMI` (non-maskable, edge-triggered, type 2), `RESET` (≥ 4 clock cycles high).
- **DMA signals:** `HOLD` and `HLDA` for direct memory access handshaking.
- **MN/MX Pin:** `1` = Minimum mode (single CPU), `0` = Maximum mode (with 8288 bus controller; pins become S0, S1, S2, RQ/GT0, RQ/GT1, LOCK, QS0, QS1).
- **TEST pin:** Used with `WAIT` instruction to synchronize with the 8087 numeric coprocessor.
- **READY pin:** Insertion of wait states (Tw) for slow memory/peripherals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Bus Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** General Bus Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In microprocessor and microcontroller system architecture, a **bus** is defined as a set of common signal lines (wires/traces) that provide a communication pathway for the transfer of data, addresses, and control signals between the Central Processing Unit (CPU), memory, and Input/Output (I/O) peripheral devices. **General Bus Operation** refers to the standardized sequence and timing of signals asserted on these bus lines during memory read, memory write, I/O read, and I/O write transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student is expected to clearly understand the **three-bus architecture** — the Address Bus, Data Bus, and Control Bus — and the standard timing waveforms (T1, T2, T3, T4 states) of the 8086/8088 microprocessor as a foundational model before progressing to advanced controllers.

## 1.2 The Three-Bus Architecture

A typical microprocessor communicates with external devices using three distinct functional buses:

| Bus Type | Direction | Width (in 8086 example) | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | 20 bits (A0–A19) | Carries the physical memory or I/O address of the operand. |
| **Data Bus** | Bidirectional (CPU ↔ Memory/I/O) | 16 bits (D0–D15) | Carries the actual data being read or written. |
| **Control Bus** | Mixed (bidirectional signals) | Multiple lines | Carries timing & command signals (RD, WR, M/IO, ALE, READY). |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a large post office sorting facility.
> - The **Address Bus** is like the *delivery zip code* written on every parcel — it tells the system *where* the parcel is going.
> - The **Data Bus** is the *actual parcel* being moved on the conveyor belt — it is the *content* itself.
> - The **Control Bus** is the *supervisor's whistle and traffic lights* — it gives signals like "start moving," "stop," "load parcel," and "unload parcel."
>
> Without any one of these three components, the system would either deliver parcels to the wrong place, have nothing to deliver, or have complete chaos with no coordination.

## 1.4 Why General Bus Operation Matters

The bus is the **backbone of every digital system**. Every instruction fetch, every data read, and every I/O handshake ultimately reduces to a precisely timed bus operation. The speed, efficiency, and reliability of the entire microprocessor system are governed by how cleanly these bus operations are executed.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric:** For the 8086 microprocessor running at a **5 MHz clock**, one bus cycle (T-state) = **200 ns (nanoseconds)**. A standard 4-T bus cycle therefore consumes **800 ns**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Bus Cycle

A bus cycle is the complete sequence of events required to perform a single memory or I/O access. It is composed of multiple **clock states (T-states)**. For the 8086, a typical bus cycle is **4T** long (T1, T2, T3, T4).

- **T1 State (Address Phase):**
  - The CPU places the address of the memory/I/O location on the **Address Bus**.
  - The **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** signal goes HIGH to instruct the external address latch (e.g., 8282) to capture the address, because the 8086 uses a **multiplexed address/data bus** (AD0–AD15 and A16–A19/S3–S6).
  - The **M/IO** signal is asserted to indicate whether the operation targets memory (HIGH) or I/O (LOW).
  - The **BHE (Bus High Enable)** signal is activated for upper-byte transfers.

- **T2 State (Bus Transition Phase):**
  - The address is removed from the AD lines.
  - The **Data Bus** is now activated. For a *write* cycle, the CPU places data on the bus. For a *read* cycle, the bus goes to high-impedance (tristate) to let memory drive it.
  - Control signals **RD (Read)** or **WR (Write)** are asserted (active LOW).
  - **DEN (Data Enable)** is activated to enable external data transceivers (e.g., 8286).

- **T3 State (Data Phase / Wait Phase):**
  - If READY = 1 (memory is fast enough), data is sampled at the end of T3 for reads, or the write completes.
  - If READY = 0, **Wait States (TW)** are inserted between T3 and T4 until memory signals it is ready.

- **T4 State (Completion Phase):**
  - Data transfer is complete.
  - All bus signals return to their inactive/idle state.
  - The CPU prepares for the next bus cycle.

## 2.2 Read vs. Write Operation Differences

| Feature | Memory/I/O Read | Memory/I/O Write |
|---|---|---|
| Data direction | Memory/I/O → CPU | CPU → Memory/I/O |
| RD signal | Asserted LOW (active) | Held HIGH (inactive) |
| WR signal | Held HIGH (inactive) | Asserted LOW (active) |
| Data bus activity | CPU receives during T3/T4 | CPU drives during T2 |
| DEN signal | Active to receive | Active to send |

## 2.3 Address Latching & Bus Demultiplexing

The 8086 has a **multiplexed bus** (AD0–AD15 carries both address and data in different T-states). A demultiplexing circuit is therefore mandatory:

1. CPU outputs address on AD0–AD15 + A16–A19/S3–S6.
2. ALE goes HIGH → 8282 latch transparently passes the address.
3. ALE goes LOW → 8282 latch holds the address, freeing AD0–AD15 for data use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why demultiplex?** The pin count of the IC package is reduced (cost & space saving), but the external world requires a stable, dedicated address bus to feed memory and decoders.

## 2.4 Wait States — Synchronizing the Asynchronous World

The CPU is extremely fast (200 ns per T-state) compared to many memory chips (often 400–700 ns access time). **Wait states** are idle clock periods inserted by the CPU when the READY pin is held LOW. Each wait state extends the bus cycle by **200 ns**.

- **Number of Wait States** = (Memory Access Time − CPU Cycle Time) ÷ Clock Period
- These are critical in interfacing slow peripherals like EPROM, ADC, and LCD.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Period (T-state) | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds (s) |
| 8086 Clock Frequency | $f_{CLK} = 5 \text{ MHz}$ | standard max for 8086 |
| One T-state duration | $T_{state} = 200 \text{ ns}$ | at 5 MHz |
| Standard Bus Cycle | $T_{bus} = 4 \times T_{state} = 800 \text{ ns}$ | without wait states |
| Bus Cycle with Wait States | $T_{bus} = (4 + n_{W}) \times T_{state}$ | $n_{W}$ = number of wait states |
| Wait State Calculation | $n_{W} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{access} - T_{cycle}}{T_{state}} \right\rceil$ | minimum integer ≥ value |
| Addressable Memory (8086) | $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 = 1 \text{ MB}$ | 20-bit address bus |
| Addressable I/O (8086) | $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$ | 16-bit I/O address |
| Data Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{16 \text{ bits}}{T_{bus}}$ | bits per second |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The General Bus Operation forms the **design grammar** of every embedded system, PC motherboard, and System-on-Chip (SoC). Engineers use these principles for:
- **Memory Interfacing:** Connecting EPROM, SRAM, and DRAM to microprocessors.
- **Peripheral Mapping:** Decoding chip-select logic for I/O devices.
- **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** Taking control of the bus from the CPU for high-speed data transfer.
- **Bus Arbitration:** Allowing multiple masters (CPU, DMA controller, co-processor) to share the bus.
- **Modern Standards:** The bus concept has evolved into PCIe, USB, I2C, SPI, and CAN in modern embedded designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Wait States for Slow Memory Interfacing

> **Problem:** An 8086 system runs at **5 MHz**. It is interfaced with an EPROM whose access time is **450 ns**. Calculate the number of wait states required so that the CPU correctly reads the EPROM data. Also calculate the total bus cycle time.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters

The clock frequency is given as $f_{CLK} = 5 \text{ MHz}$.

The memory access time is $T_{access} = 450 \text{ ns}$.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Duration of One T-state

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{state} &= \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} \\
&= \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&= 200 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

So each clock state (T-state) lasts **200 ns**.

### Step 3 — Determine Standard CPU Bus Cycle Time (Without Waits)

For the 8086, a standard read/write bus cycle consists of **4 T-states**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,std} &= 4 \times T_{state} \\
&= 4 \times 200 \text{ ns} \\
&= 800 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare Memory Access Time vs. CPU Cycle Time

The CPU finishes its standard bus cycle in 800 ns, but the memory needs 450 ns to present valid data. We must check if the standard 4T cycle is enough to cover this access time.

Since the CPU samples the data bus toward the **end of T3 (or end of last wait state before T4)**, the data must be valid *before* that sampling instant. In a standard 4T cycle, the available time for the memory to respond equals the cycle duration of 800 ns.

Because $T_{access} = 450 \text{ ns} < 800 \text{ ns} = T_{cycle,std}$, the memory is *fast enough* and **0 wait states are needed** in this particular case.

### Step 5 — Re-evaluate with a Slower Memory

To make the derivation more illustrative, let us consider a **slower EPROM** with access time $T_{access} = 1050 \text{ ns}$. Such memories were common in 1980s industrial systems.

Now the CPU's standard 800 ns cycle is **insufficient**. We must extend it using wait states.

### Step 6 — Apply the Wait State Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{W} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{access} - T_{cycle,std}}{T_{state}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{1050 \text{ ns} - 800 \text{ ns}}{200 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{250 \text{ ns}}{200 \text{ ns}} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 1.25 \rceil \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So we need **2 wait states** to safely access this slow EPROM.

### Step 7 — Calculate the New Total Bus Cycle Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bus,total} &= (4 + n_{W}) \times T_{state} \\
&= (4 + 2) \times 200 \text{ ns} \\
&= 6 \times 200 \text{ ns} \\
&= 1200 \text{ ns} \\
&= 1.2 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — Verify the Result

The new bus cycle gives the EPROM a full 1200 ns — comfortably longer than the required 1050 ns. The READY line is held LOW for exactly 2 clock cycles (TW states) by external "ready generation" logic, then driven HIGH to let the CPU proceed to T4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every additional wait state costs **200 ns** of throughput. In high-performance systems, engineers minimize wait states by using fast memories (cache, SRAM) and zero-wait-state memory designs.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Bus Signal Behavior Across T-States

| Signal | T1 (Start) | T2 (Mid) | T3 (Active) | TW (Wait) | T4 (End) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AD15–AD0 | Address (valid) | Data (write) / Hi-Z (read) | Data (valid) | Data (held) | Hi-Z |
| A19/S6–A16/S3 | Address (valid) | Status S3–S6 | Status S3–S6 | Status S3–S6 | Status S3–S6 |
| **ALE** | HIGH (latch address) | LOW | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| **BHE** | Active if needed | — | — | — | — |
| **M/IO** | M=HIGH, IO=LOW set | Held | Held | Held | Held |
| **RD** | HIGH | Goes LOW | LOW | LOW | HIGH |
| **WR** | HIGH | HIGH | Goes LOW (for write) | LOW | HIGH |
| **DEN** | LOW | Goes HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | LOW |
| **DT/R** | HIGH (write) / LOW (read) | Held | Held | Held | — |
| **READY** | HIGH (no wait) | Sampled | If LOW → inserts TW | Must go HIGH to exit | Sampled |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Microprocessor Bus System

The following Mermaid diagram represents the *interconnection* logic of the three-bus system in a typical 8086-based microcomputer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["8086 CPU Core"] -->|Address Bus A0-A19| AddLatch["8282 Address Latch x3"]
    CPU -->|Data Bus AD0-AD15| DataBuf["8286 Data Transceiver x2"]
    CPU -->|Status S3-S6| StatusLatch["8282 Status Latch"]
    CPU -->|Control Signals| CtrlLogic["Control Bus Generator"]
    CtrlLogic -->|RD, WR, M/IO, BHE| Memory["Memory EPROM and SRAM"]
    CtrlLogic -->|RD, WR, M/IO| IO["I/O Devices 8255 and 8251"]
    AddLatch -->|Latched A0-A19| Memory
    AddLatch -->|Latched A0-A19| IO
    DataBuf -->|Buffered D0-D15| Memory
    DataBuf -->|Buffered D0-D15| IO
    Memory -->|Chip Select via Decoder| Decoder["3-to-8 Address Decoder 74LS138"]
    IO -->|Chip Select via Decoder| Decoder
    Decoder -->|CS Lines| Memory
    Decoder -->|CS Lines| IO
    ReadyGen["Ready Generation Circuit"] -->|READY signal| CPU
    ClockGen["8284 Clock Generator"] -->|CLK and RESET| CPU
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Timing of a Memory Read Operation

This diagram visualizes the **order of signal assertions** during one full 4T memory read cycle. The 8086's `IOR` and `MEMR` semantics are the underlying control logic.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as Clock Generator
    participant CPU as 8086 CPU
    participant AD as Address/Data Bus
    participant CTRL as Control Signals
    participant MEM as Memory

    Note over CLK,MEM: T1 State Begins
    CLK->>CPU: Rising Edge (T1)
    CPU->>AD: Place Address on AD0-AD15 and A16-A19
    CPU->>CTRL: ALE = HIGH (latch the address)
    CPU->>CTRL: M/IO = HIGH (Memory operation)

    Note over CLK,MEM: T2 State Begins
    CLK->>CPU: Rising Edge (T2)
    CPU->>AD: Remove address, float data bus (for read)
    CPU->>CTRL: RD = LOW (start read)
    CPU->>CTRL: DEN = HIGH (enable transceiver)
    CPU->>CTRL: DT/R = LOW (receive direction)

    Note over CLK,MEM: T3 State Begins
    CLK->>CPU: Rising Edge (T3)
    MEM->>AD: Place data on the bus
    CPU->>CTRL: Sample READY pin
    alt READY = HIGH
        CPU->>CPU: Proceed to T4 (no wait)
    else READY = LOW
        CPU->>CPU: Insert Wait State TW
    end

    Note over CLK,MEM: T4 State Begins
    CLK->>CPU: Rising Edge (T4)
    CPU->>AD: Latch the data from bus
    CPU->>CTRL: RD = HIGH, DEN = LOW
    CPU->>CPU: Bus cycle complete
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Wait State Insertion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPU initiates Read or Write]) --> T1[T1: Place Address on Bus]
    T1 --> ALEHigh{ALE = HIGH?}
    ALEHigh -->|Yes| LatchAddr[External Latch captures Address]
    ALEHigh -->|No| ErrorA[Bus Error - Invalid Timing]
    LatchAddr --> T2[T2: Activate RD or WR signal]
    T2 --> T3[T3: Sample READY pin state]
    T3 --> ReadyCheck{READY = HIGH?}
    ReadyCheck -->|Yes| T4[T4: Latch data and complete cycle]
    ReadyCheck -->|No| InsertTW[Insert Wait State TW = 200 ns]
    InsertTW --> Recheck{READY now HIGH?}
    Recheck -->|No| InsertTW
    Recheck -->|Yes| T4
    T4 --> End([Bus Cycle Complete - Return to Idle])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the three-bus architecture of the 8086 microprocessor with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer:**
The 8086 microprocessor uses a **three-bus architecture** consisting of:
1. **Address Bus (20 bits, A0–A19):** Unidirectional; carries the physical address of memory or I/O location. Allows access to $2^{20} = 1 \text{ MB}$ of memory.
2. **Data Bus (16 bits, D0–D15):** Bidirectional; carries the actual data being read or written between the CPU and memory/I/O.
3. **Control Bus:** A set of control and timing signals like **RD, WR, M/IO, ALE, BHE, READY, DEN, DT/R** that synchronize and govern the data transfer.

The address and data buses are **multiplexed** in the 8086 (AD0–AD15 lines carry both), and external latches (8282) and transceivers (8286) are used to demultiplex them.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a wait state? Why is it needed in a bus cycle?**

**Model Answer:**
A **wait state (TW)** is an extra clock period inserted between T3 and T4 of a bus cycle to extend the duration of the cycle, giving slow memory or I/O devices enough time to respond. The CPU inserts a wait state whenever the **READY** pin is held LOW by external "ready generation" logic. Each wait state adds **one T-state duration** (e.g., 200 ns at 5 MHz) to the bus cycle. It is needed because the CPU operates much faster than many peripheral/memory devices, and without wait states, data corruption or bus errors would occur.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the **8086 memory read bus cycle** in detail. Identify the role of each control signal. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An 8086 system operates at **8 MHz** clock frequency and is interfaced with a memory chip having an access time of **600 ns**. Compute the number of wait states required and the total bus cycle time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Overview:** The 8086 memory read cycle consists of **4 T-states** (T1, T2, T3, T4) with no wait states in a zero-wait-state system.

**Step 2 — T1 (Address Phase):**
- CPU outputs the 20-bit address on AD0–AD15 and A16–A19/S3–S6.
- **ALE** goes HIGH so that the 8282 latch captures the address on its falling edge.
- **M/IO** is set HIGH (memory operation).
- **BHE** is asserted LOW if the upper byte (D8–D15) is being read.
- **DT/R** is set LOW (receive direction — CPU is reading).

**Step 3 — T2 (Bus Transition):**
- The CPU removes the address from the AD lines.
- The AD lines are floated (high-impedance) so memory can drive them.
- **RD** goes LOW (active) to signal memory to place data on the bus.
- **DEN** goes HIGH to enable the 8286 data transceiver for the CPU's receive direction.

**Step 4 — T3 (Data Sampling):**
- Memory places valid data on D0–D15.
- The CPU samples the **READY** pin. If HIGH, the cycle proceeds to T4. If LOW, wait states (TW) are inserted.

**Step 5 — T4 (Completion):**
- The CPU latches the data from D0–D15 into its internal register.
- **RD** returns HIGH, **DEN** returns LOW.
- The bus cycle ends and the CPU begins the next operation.

**Step 6 — Timing Diagram Representation:**

| T-State | AD Bus | ALE | RD | M/IO | DEN | DT/R |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Address A0–A19 | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | LOW |
| T2 | Floating | LOW | ↓LOW | HIGH | ↑HIGH | LOW |
| T3 | Data from Memory | LOW | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | LOW |
| T4 | Latched | LOW | ↑HIGH | HIGH | ↓LOW | LOW |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**

The clock frequency is $f_{CLK} = 8 \text{ MHz}$.

The memory access time is $T_{access} = 600 \text{ ns}$.

**Step 2 — Calculate T-state Duration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{state} &= \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \\
&= 0.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Bus Cycle Time (4T, no waits):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,std} &= 4 \times T_{state} = 4 \times 125 \text{ ns} = 500 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare and Calculate Wait States:**

Since $T_{access} = 600 \text{ ns} > 500 \text{ ns} = T_{cycle,std}$, wait states are required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{W} &= \left\lceil \frac{T_{access} - T_{cycle,std}}{T_{state}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{600 - 500}{125} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 0.8 \rceil = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **1 wait state** is required.

**Step 5 — Total Bus Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{bus,total} &= (4 + 1) \times 125 \text{ ns} = 5 \times 125 \text{ ns} \\
&= 625 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total bus cycle time is **625 ns**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**a)** Explain the **need for address demultiplexing** in the 8086 system. Describe the role of the **8282 latch** and **8286 transceiver** with a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Differentiate between **memory read/write** and **I/O read/write** bus cycles. List the various machine cycles supported by 8086. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — The Multiplexing Problem:**
The 8086 uses a 40-pin DIP package but needs to handle 20 address bits, 16 data bits, and many control signals. To fit within the pin limit, Intel multiplexed the lower 16 address bits with the 16 data bits, creating the **AD0–AD15** bus. The upper 4 bits (A16–A19) are multiplexed with status signals S3–S6. During T1, these pins carry the address; during T2–T4, they carry data (or status).

**Step 2 — The Need for Demultiplexing:**
Memory and I/O devices require a *stable* address throughout the entire bus cycle. The 8086 holds the address on AD lines only during T1. Therefore, an **external latch** is required to *freeze* the address after T1, freeing the AD lines for data transfer.

**Step 3 — Role of 8282 Address Latch:**
- The 8282 is an **8-bit transparent latch**.
- It is enabled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal from the 8086.
- When ALE = HIGH, the latch output follows the input (transparent mode).
- When ALE goes LOW, the latch holds (freezes) the last address.
- Three 8282 ICs are needed to demultiplex all 20 address bits.

**Step 4 — Role of 8286 Data Transceiver:**
- The 8286 is an **8-bit bidirectional bus transceiver**.
- It is controlled by **DEN (Data Enable)** and **DT/R (Data Transmit/Receive)**.
- **DT/R = HIGH:** CPU transmits data (write cycle).
- **DT/R = LOW:** CPU receives data (read cycle).
- **DEN = HIGH:** Transceiver is enabled (active).
- Two 8286 ICs are needed to handle the full 16-bit data bus.

**Step 5 — Block Diagram:**

```
                  +--------+        +--------+
   AD0-AD7   ---->| 8282   |---->  A0-A7   (to memory/decoder)
   AD8-AD15  ---->| (x3)   |---->  A8-A15  (to memory/decoder)
   A16-A19   ---->|        |---->  A16-A19 (to memory/decoder)
                  +--------+
                  ALE=↑ (Latch)
                  
                  +--------+        +--------+
   D0-D7     <--->| 8286   |<--->  D0-D7   (to memory)
   D8-D15    <--->| (x2)   |<--->  D8-D15  (to memory)
                  +--------+
                  DEN=↑, DT/R controls direction
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Memory vs. I/O Bus Cycle Comparison:**

| Feature | Memory Cycle | I/O Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| M/IO signal | HIGH (1) | LOW (0) |
| Address width | 20 bits (1 MB space) | 16 bits (64 KB space) |
| Address bus usage | A0–A19 | A0–A15 (lower 16 bits) |
| Typical device | RAM, ROM | 8255, 8251, ADC |
| Speed | Often requires wait states | Usually requires wait states |
| Purpose | Read/write program or data | Read/write peripheral registers |

**Step 2 — Machine Cycles of 8086:**
The 8086 supports the following **fundamental machine cycles** (each requires 4 T-states minimum):
1. **Opcode Fetch** – Read instruction from memory.
2. **Memory Read** – Read data byte/word from memory.
3. **Memory Write** – Write data byte/word to memory.
4. **I/O Read** – Read from I/O port.
5. **I/O Write** – Write to I/O port.
6. **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)** – Response to interrupt request.
7. **Bus Request / Grant (HOLD/HLDA)** – DMA or master transfer.
8. **HALT / Wait** – Idle state cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing ALE and DEN:** Many students write "ALE enables data transfer" — this is WRONG. **ALE** latches the **address**; **DEN** enables the **data** transceiver. Mixing these up will cost 2 marks directly.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention M/IO:** When explaining the difference between memory and I/O cycles, the **M/IO signal level is the single key distinguishing factor**. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Not writing units in numerical answers:** If your answer is "625" without "ns", you will lose 1 mark. Always write the unit.
> 4. **Skipping the ceiling function in wait state calculation:** Writing $\frac{250}{200} = 1.25$ and concluding 1.25 wait states is mathematically invalid. The result must be the **ceiling** (2 in this case) because wait states are discrete clock periods.
> 5. **Missing the role of 8284 clock generator:** Many students forget that the 8284 chip provides not just the clock but also the **READY synchronization** and **RESET** signals — these are high-yield 2-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bus Definition:** A set of shared communication lines for address, data, and control signals.
- **Three Buses:** Address (unidirectional, 20-bit), Data (bidirectional, 16-bit), Control (mixed signals).
- **8086 Multiplexing:** Lower 16 address bits are multiplexed with data on AD0–AD15; upper 4 bits multiplexed with status on A16/S3–A19/S6.
- **Bus Cycle States:** T1 (Address) → T2 (Transition) → T3 (Data) → T4 (Completion), with optional TW (Wait) inserted between T3 and T4.
- **ALE:** Address Latch Enable — HIGH in T1 to demultiplex address.
- **DEN & DT/R:** Control the 8286 data transceiver (direction and enable).
- **RD/WR:** Active LOW read/write signals asserted in T2.
- **M/IO:** HIGH = Memory cycle, LOW = I/O cycle.
- **READY Pin:** When LOW, wait states are inserted.
- **Wait State Formula:** $n_{W} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{access} - T_{cycle,std}}{T_{state}} \right\rceil$.
- **Clock Period:** $T_{state} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ — at 5 MHz, $T_{state} = 200$ ns.
- **Total Bus Time:** $T_{bus} = (4 + n_{W}) \times T_{state}$.
- **External Support Chips:** 8282 (address latch), 8286 (data transceiver), 8284 (clock generator + ready sync).
- **Memory Space:** 1 MB ($2^{20}$); **I/O Space:** 64 KB ($2^{16}$).
- **8 Standard Machine Cycles:** Opcode fetch, memory R/W, I/O R/W, INTA, HOLD/HLDA, HALT/Wait.
- **Zero-Wait-State Memory:** Memory that completes access within one standard CPU cycle (e.g., fast SRAM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Capability - Assembler Directives - Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Capability - Assembler Directives - Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Addressing Capability

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
Addressing capability of a microprocessor is defined as the maximum range of distinct memory locations that the processor can directly reference using its address bus. It is mathematically determined by the width (number of bits) of the address bus of the processor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Formula:**
> $$\text{Addressing Capability} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}$$
> where **n** = number of address lines (bits) in the address bus.

**Addressing Capability of Popular KTU-Relevant Processors:**

| Processor | Address Bus Width | Addressing Capability | Memory Space |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 8085 | 16-bit | $2^{16}$ = **65,536** | **64 KB** |
| 8086 | 20-bit | $2^{20}$ = **1,048,576** | **1 MB** |
| 80286 | 24-bit | $2^{24}$ = **16,777,216** | **16 MB** |
| 80386 / 80486 | 32-bit | $2^{32}$ | **4 GB** |
| Pentium (P5) | 32-bit | $2^{32}$ | **4 GB** |
| Core (x64 mode) | 40-bit (PAE) | $2^{40}$ | **1 TB** |

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the address bus as the **number of house numbers on a street**. If a postman can dial only 16 buttons on his pad, he can only deliver mail to **65,536 houses (64 KB)**. If you give him 20 buttons, he can reach over **1 million houses (1 MB)**. The address bus is essentially the postal network of the processor; its width directly decides how big the "neighborhood" (memory) the CPU can talk to is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data bus width ≠ Address bus width.** The 8086 has a **16-bit data bus** but a **20-bit address bus**, which is precisely why it became historically significant — Intel introduced segmentation to bridge this gap.

---

### 1.2 Assembler Directives (Pseudo-Ops)

**Formal Definition:**
Assembler directives (also called **pseudo-operations** or **pseudo-ops**) are instructions to the **assembler/translator** that guide the assembly process. They do **NOT** generate any machine code (no opcode is produced). They are removed by the assembler after performing their function during the translation phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Distinction:** A genuine instruction (e.g., `MOV AX, BX`) is translated to **machine code**, while a directive (e.g., `DB 25H`) is **not** — it is consumed only by the assembler to allocate memory or define program structure.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a director instructing a film crew. "Lights! Camera! Action!" is a directive — the actors do not appear on screen performing "lights-camera-action." Similarly, directives tell the assembler *"create a variable here,"* *"start a code block here,"* *"equate this name to a value,"* but no actual microprocessor instruction is generated for them.

---

### 1.3 Addressing Modes

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
An addressing mode specifies the **rule or method** by which the operand (data) of an instruction is located. It answers the question: *"From where does the CPU fetch the actual data to perform this operation?"*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Suppose your mother tells you: *"Get my red box."* This is ambiguous — where is the red box? She must add a **mode**:

- *"Get the red box **on the table**"* → **Direct mode** (fixed address).
- *"Get the red box **in your hand**"* → **Register mode** (CPU register).
- *"Get the red box **from the place I showed you yesterday**"* → **Indirect mode** (pointer holds address).
- *"Get **five rupees** worth of toffees"* → **Immediate mode** (data is embedded in the instruction itself).

The 8086 supports **17 different addressing modes** grouped into **8 categories** according to the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Address Generation in 8086 (Segmentation)

The 8086 has a **20-bit physical address bus**, but its internal registers are only **16 bits**. Intel solved this using the **segmentation** scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-Most-Important Formula:**
> $$\text{Physical Address (PA)} = (\text{Segment Base} \times 16) + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. The 16-bit **Segment Register** (CS, DS, SS, ES) holds the starting address of a memory segment.
2. It is **left-shifted by 4 bits** (i.e., multiplied by 16) to form a 20-bit **Segment Base Address**.
3. The 16-bit **Offset / Effective Address (EA)** is added to this base.
4. The resulting 20-bit value is placed on the **address bus** to access physical memory.

> [!NOTE]
> The default segment-offset pairings are: `CS:IP` (code), `DS:EA` (data), `SS:SP` (stack), `ES:DI` (string destination).

---

### 2.2 Detailed Classification of Assembler Directives

Directives are categorized based on their function:

**(a) Data Definition / Data Allocation Directives**

| Directive | Full Form | Size Allocated | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `DB` | Define Byte | 1 byte (8-bit) | `DB 25H` |
| `DW` | Define Word | 2 bytes (16-bit) | `DW 1234H` |
| `DD` | Define Doubleword | 4 bytes (32-bit) | `DD 12345678H` |
| `DQ` | Define Quadword | 8 bytes (64-bit) | `DQ ...` |
| `DT` | Define Tenbytes | 10 bytes (BCD) | `DT 12.3456` |

**(b) Segment Definition Directives**
- `SEGMENT` / `ENDS` — defines a logical segment.
- `ASSUME` — tells the assembler which segment register to use for each segment.
- `GROUP` — combines multiple segments into one 64 KB group.

**(c) Procedure Definition Directives**
- `PROC` / `ENDP` — defines a near or far procedure/subroutine.
- Example: `MYPROC PROC NEAR ... MYPROC ENDP`

**(d) Equate Directive**
- `EQU` — assigns a constant value or expression to a symbol.
- Example: `COUNT EQU 100` → substitutes `100` wherever `COUNT` appears.

**(e) Address Control / Pointer Operators**
- `OFFSET` — returns the offset of a label/variable.
- `PTR` — overrides the default size/type of an operand.
- `LABEL` / `THIS` — assigns a name with specified type to current location.
- Example: `PTR BYTE PTR [BX]` forces byte access.

**(f) Data Repetition Directive**
- `DUP` — duplicates data.
- Example: `DB 10 DUP(0)` → reserves 10 bytes initialized to 0.

**(g) External Linkage Directives**
- `PUBLIC` — declares symbols accessible to other modules.
- `EXTRN` — declares symbols defined in other modules.

**(h) Program Termination**
- `END` — marks the end of the assembly source file; entry point is specified here.
- Example: `END START`

---

### 2.3 The 8 Categories of 8086 Addressing Modes (KTU 2024)

1. **Register Addressing Mode**
2. **Immediate Addressing Mode**
3. **Direct Addressing Mode**
4. **Register Indirect Addressing Mode**
5. **Based Relative Addressing Mode**
6. **Indexed Relative Addressing Mode**
7. **Based Indexed Addressing Mode**
8. **Based Indexed with Displacement Mode**

*(Plus implicit modes like String, Port, Relative, and Implied.)*

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Addressing Capability | $2^{n}$ | n = address bus width (bits) |
| Physical Address | $\text{PA} = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{EA}$ | 20-bit result |
| Segment Base | $\text{Seg} \times 16$ (or $\text{Seg} \ll 4$) | Left-shift by 4 bits |
| Effective Address (BX+SI+DISP) | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI} + \text{DISP}$ | 16-bit value |
| Effective Address (BP+DI+DISP) | $\text{EA} = \text{BP} + \text{DI} + \text{DISP}$ | 16-bit value |
| Word Memory | 2 consecutive bytes | Little-endian: LSB first |
| Stack Growth | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2$ (PUSH) | Downward in memory |
| Max Segment Size | $2^{16} = \text{65,536 bytes}$ | 64 KB per segment |
| Max Memory (8086) | $2^{20} = \text{1,048,576 bytes}$ | 1 MB total |

> [!NOTE]
> The `$\vert$` (absolute value bars) are intentionally rendered using LaTeX commands to avoid breaking the markdown table. KTU valuation scripts often use `$\vert$` for modulus / segment separators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address Calculation in 8086

**Problem Statement:** Given `DS = 4000H` and an offset `EA = 1234H`, calculate the 20-bit physical address.

**Step 1 — Identify the segment base.**
The Data Segment register holds `DS = 4000H`. This is a 16-bit value.

**Step 2 — Left-shift the segment base by 4 bits (multiply by 16).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment Base} &= \text{DS} \times 16 \\
&= 4000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} \\
&= 40000\text{H} \;\; (\text{in hexadecimal})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiplying a hexadecimal number by 16H is equivalent to appending a zero in hex, or shifting left by 4 bits in binary.

**Step 3 — Add the Effective Address (Offset) to the segment base.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= \text{Segment Base} + \text{Offset} \\
&= 40000\text{H} + 01234\text{H} \\
&= 41234\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Standard 20-bit addition; result is the absolute address in the 1 MB memory space.

**Step 4 — Verify the range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum address} &= 00000\text{H} \\
\text{Maximum address} &= \text{FFFFFH} \\
\text{Range} &= 2^{20} = 1,\!048,\!576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The physical address `41234H` is valid and lies within the 1 MB memory space.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Generating Effective Address for Each Mode

The 8086 computes the **EA** (Effective Address) differently based on the addressing mode. The formula varies:

| Mode | EA Formula | Default Segment |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Register Indirect (`[BX]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BX}$ | DS |
| Register Indirect (`[BP]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BP}$ | SS |
| Based Relative (`[BX + disp]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{disp}$ | DS |
| Indexed Relative (`[SI + disp]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{SI} + \text{disp}$ | DS |
| Indexed Relative (`[DI + disp]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{DI} + \text{disp}$ | DS |
| Based Indexed (`[BX + SI]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI}$ | DS |
| Based Indexed (`[BP + DI]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BP} + \text{DI}$ | SS |
| Based Indexed + Disp (`[BX+SI+disp]`) | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI} + \text{disp}$ | DS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** "**B**ased uses **B**X (default DS), **B**ased-Special uses **B**P (default SS)." The **'B' family** uses BX by default, except when BP appears, which forces SS as the default.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Converting an Instruction into Physical Address

**Problem:** Find the physical address of the data accessed by:
`MOV AX, [BX + SI + 0200H]`
Given: `DS = 2000H`, `BX = 0300H`, `SI = 0400H`.

**Step 1 — Compute Effective Address (EA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{BX} + \text{SI} + \text{disp} \\
&= 0300\text{H} + 0400\text{H} + 0200\text{H} \\
&= 0900\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Physical Address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= \text{DS} \times 16 + \text{EA} \\
&= 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 0900\text{H} \\
&= 20000\text{H} + 0900\text{H} \\
&= 20900\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer:** The data is read from physical memory location **`20900H`** (and the next byte `20901H` for the 16-bit word). This is the canonical KTU worked example.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Physical Address Generation Pipeline

