<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define the addressing capability of a microprocessor. Calculate the addressing capability of an 8086 microprocessor.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The addressing capability of a microprocessor is the maximum amount of memory it can directly access, determined by the width of its address bus. For the 8086, the address bus is 20 bits wide, therefore:
> $$\text{Addressing Capability} = 2^{20} = 1,\!048,\!576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}.$$
> Hence, the 8086 can directly access **1 MB of memory**. *(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula, 1 mark: final answer with unit.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between an assembler directive and an instruction in 8086 assembly language.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Aspect | Instruction | Directive |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Purpose | Commands the microprocessor to perform an operation. | Commands the assembler during translation. |
> | Machine Code | Translated into opcode. | No opcode is generated. |
> | Example | `MOV AX, BX` | `DB 25H`, `EQU COUNT = 10` |
> | Execution | At run-time by the CPU. | At assembly-time by the assembler. |
> *(1 mark per key point, 3 marks for complete table.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the physical address generation mechanism in 8086 with a suitable diagram. Discuss how segmentation helps overcome the 16-bit register limitation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the following addressing modes with one example each: **(7 marks)**
>  (i) Based Indexed with Displacement
>  (ii) Register Indirect
>  (iii) Immediate
>  (iv) Direct
>  (v) Indexed Relative
>  (vi) Based Relative
>  (vii) Based Indexed

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Physical Address Generation in 8086 (7 marks):**

The 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** but only **16-bit internal registers**. To generate a 20-bit physical address, Intel introduced **segmentation**. The formula is:

$$
\text{Physical Address (PA)} = (\text{Segment Base} \times 16) + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}
$$

*Working principle:*
- One of the four segment registers (`CS`, `DS`, `SS`, `ES`) provides the 16-bit segment base.
- This value is **left-shifted by 4 bits** (equivalently multiplied by 16 or `10H`) to produce a 20-bit base.
- A 16-bit **offset (Effective Address)** computed from registers/displacement is added.
- The 20-bit result is placed on the address bus.

*Default pairing:* `CS:IP` for code, `DS:EA` for data, `SS:SP` for stack. (3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for default pairings.)

**(b) Addressing Modes (7 marks, 1 mark each):**

| Mode | Syntax | Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Based Indexed + Disp | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+0200H]` | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI} + 0200\text{H}$ |
| (ii) Register Indirect | `MOV AX, [BX]` | $\text{EA} = \text{BX}$ |
| (iii) Immediate | `MOV AX, 1234H` | Data is part of the instruction itself |
| (iv) Direct | `MOV AX, [2000H]` | $\text{EA} = 2000\text{H}$ (fixed address) |
| (v) Indexed Relative | `MOV AX, [SI+0200H]` | $\text{EA} = \text{SI} + 0200\text{H}$ |
| (vi) Based Relative | `MOV AX, [BX+0200H]` | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + 0200\text{H}$ |
| (vii) Based Indexed | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` | $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Physical Address Questions:**
> Students frequently forget to **multiply the segment register by 16** (left-shift by 4) and write `PA = DS + EA` directly. This is the **single most common mistake** and costs 2–3 marks per answer. Always show: `PA = (DS × 10H) + EA`. Also, the 8086 has 4 segment registers — explicitly state which one you are using.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** With neat examples, classify and explain the various **assembler directives** used in 8086 assembly language. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A block of data in 8086 starts at physical address `30000H`. If `DS = 2000H`, what is the offset address? Given `BX = 1000H`, `SI = 0500H`, and displacement `= 0200H`, find the physical address accessed by `MOV AX, [BX+SI+0200H]`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Assembler Directives (7 marks):**

1. **Data Definition Directives (2 marks):**
   - `DB` (Define Byte) — allocates 1 byte. Example: `DB 25H`, `DB 'A'`
   - `DW` (Define Word) — allocates 2 bytes. Example: `DW 1234H`
   - `DD` — 4 bytes, `DQ` — 8 bytes, `DT` — 10 bytes (BCD).

2. **Segment Definition Directives (1.5 marks):**
   - `SEGMENT` ... `ENDS` — defines a logical segment.
   - `ASSUME` — informs the assembler of the segment register mapping.

3. **Procedure Directives (1 mark):**
   - `PROC` ... `ENDP` — defines a subroutine. Example: `ADD16 PROC NEAR`.

4. **Equate Directive (1 mark):**
   - `EQU` — assigns a constant. Example: `MAX EQU 99`.

5. **Address Operators (1 mark):**
   - `OFFSET` — returns the offset of a label.
   - `PTR` — forces a specific data type. Example: `BYTE PTR [BX]`.

6. **Repetition and Linkage (0.5 marks):**
   - `DUP` — `DB 5 DUP(0)` reserves 5 zero bytes.
   - `PUBLIC`/`EXTRN` — inter-module linkage.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Find the offset from physical address 30000H with DS = 2000H:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment Base} &= \text{DS} \times 16 = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} = 20000\text{H} \\
\text{Offset} &= \text{PA} - \text{Segment Base} \\
&= 30000\text{H} - 20000\text{H} \\
&= 10000\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate EA for `MOV AX, [BX+SI+0200H]`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BX} &= 1000\text{H} \\
\text{SI} &= 0500\text{H} \\
\text{disp} &= 0200\text{H} \\
\text{EA} &= \text{BX} + \text{SI} + \text{disp} \\
&= 1000\text{H} + 0500\text{H} + 0200\text{H} \\
&= 1700\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Physical Address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= \text{DS} \times 16 + \text{EA} \\
&= 20000\text{H} + 1700\text{H} \\
&= 21700\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The physical address accessed is **`21700H`** (and `21701H` for the second byte of the word). *(3 marks for EA calculation, 2 marks for segment base, 2 marks for final PA.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Problems:**
> Do not confuse **hexadecimal** addition with **decimal**. When adding `1000H + 0500H + 0200H`, the digit must sum to `1700H`, not `1700` decimal. Also, the answer is always a **20-bit** value (range `00000H` to `FFFFFH`); if your result exceeds this, you have made an error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Addressing Capability** = $2^n$ bytes, where $n$ = address bus width.
- [ ] **8086** = 1 MB (20-bit), **80386** = 4 GB (32-bit), **8085** = 64 KB (16-bit).
- [ ] **Segmentation formula:** $\text{PA} = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{EA}$.
- [ ] Always left-shift segment register by **4 bits** (multiply by 16 / `10H`).
- [ ] **Default segments:** `CS:IP` (code), `DS:data` (data), `SS:SP` (stack), `ES:DI` (string dest).
- [ ] Directives are **NOT** translated to machine code; they are pseudo-ops.
- [ ] **`EQU`** is a *compile-time* substitute (like `#define` in C).
- [ ] **`PTR`** overrides the default operand size (byte/word).
- [ ] **`OFFSET`** returns the 16-bit displacement of a label.
- [ ] **`DUP`** is used inside `DB/DW/DD` for array initialization.
- [ ] **BX** uses DS as default; **BP** uses SS as default.
- [ ] The 8086 supports **17 addressing modes** grouped into **8 categories** (KTU 2024 syllabus).
- [ ] **Immediate mode** data is part of the instruction stream itself.
- [ ] **Register Indirect** uses `[BX]`, `[BP]`, `[SI]`, `[DI]` — the contents act as pointers.
- [ ] **Based Indexed with Displacement** is the most powerful mode: $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{SI} + \text{disp}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"BDS B-S-B-S"** → **B**X → **D**S, **B**P → **S**S. The moment `BP` appears in an addressing mode, the segment flips from `DS` to `SS`. This single trick resolves 80% of addressing-mode confusion in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Stack, Interrupts & Interrupt Service Routines, Minimum and Maximum Mode 8086 System Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Stack, Interrupts & Interrupt Service Routines, Minimum and Maximum Mode 8086 System Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Stack in 8086

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Stack** in the 8086 microprocessor is a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure implemented in RAM, used for temporary storage of data, return addresses, flags, and procedure parameters. It is managed by two dedicated 16-bit registers: **SS (Stack Segment)** and **SP (Stack Pointer)**. The physical address of any stack operation is computed as:
> $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{SS} \times 16) + (\text{SP})$$

**Intuitive Analogy (Stack = A Stack of Plates in a Cafeteria):**
Imagine a spring-loaded plate dispenser in a college canteen. You can only **push** a new plate onto the **top** of the pile, and you can only **pop** (remove) the **topmost** plate. The plate you placed *last* is the one you take *first* — exactly LIFO behaviour. In 8086, the **SP (Stack Pointer)** acts as the "spring-loaded indicator" pointing to the topmost plate (i.e., the next free memory location in the stack). The **SS register** identifies *which* cafeteria (memory segment) the dispenser is installed in.

- The stack grows **downward** in memory (toward lower addresses).
- A **PUSH** operation *decrements* SP by 2 (for 16-bit data) and then writes.
- A **POP** operation *reads* data and then *increments* SP by 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 8086 stack is **word-oriented** (operates on 16 bits). Attempting to PUSH/POP 8-bit data triggers assembler warnings and is **non-standard** for KTU examinations.

---

### 1.2 Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-initiated asynchronous event that **temporarily suspends** the currently executing program (the *mainline code*) so that the microprocessor can service a higher-priority request. The subroutine executed in response to an interrupt is called an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Upon completion, the processor seamlessly returns to the interrupted program at the exact instruction boundary where it was suspended.

**Intuitive Analogy (Interrupts = A Phone Call During a Lecture):**
Imagine a professor is delivering a lecture (the *main program*). Suddenly, the professor's phone rings (the *interrupt signal*). The professor **pauses** the lecture, **notes the exact point** at which the lecture was paused (this is the *return address pushed onto the stack*), **answers the phone** (executes the *ISR*), and once the call is over, **resumes the lecture** from the exact point where it was interrupted (executes *IRET* — Interrupt Return). Different rings (different phones) have different priorities — the Principal's call gets answered before a student's call. This is **priority-based interrupt handling**.

- **256 interrupt vectors** are predefined in the 8086, each occupying **4 bytes** (2-byte CS + 2-byte IP), starting at physical address **00000H**.
- The table containing all these vectors is called the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**.

---

### 1.3 Minimum Mode vs Maximum Mode (8086 System Operation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The 8086 microprocessor can be configured in one of two operational modes, selected via the **MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$** pin:
> - **Minimum Mode (MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ = 1):** The 8086 itself generates all the control signals required to interface memory and I/O. It is a **single-processor** configuration where the 8086 is the *only* bus master.
> - **Maximum Mode (MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ = 0):** The 8086 does **not** generate control signals directly. Instead, an external **Bus Controller (8288)** decodes the status lines (S0, S1, S2) and generates the appropriate control signals. This mode supports **multi-processor (coprocessor) configurations** such as 8087 (Math Coprocessor) and 8089 (I/O Processor).

**Intuitive Analogy (Min Mode = Solo Driver, Max Mode = Convoy with Dispatcher):**
- **Minimum Mode:** You are driving a car alone. You control the steering, accelerator, brakes, and indicators yourself. The 8086 directly drives the bus.
- **Maximum Mode:** You are leading a convoy of trucks (8087, 8089, etc.). A *dispatcher* (8288 Bus Controller) is needed to coordinate signals to all vehicles. The 8086 only issues *status* commands (turn-left, turn-right, stop), and the 8288 translates them into actual control signals for the convoy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Pin:** Pin **\#33** of the 8086 is the **MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$** mode-select pin. Its logic level is **latched** at reset and cannot be changed dynamically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stack Operation: Step-by-Step Logic

When the 8086 executes a `PUSH source` instruction (16-bit source):

1. **SP ← SP − 2** (Reserve 2 bytes on the stack).
2. Write the 16-bit source value to memory location:  
   `Physical Address = (SS × 16) + SP`
3. For a 16-bit write, the **low byte** goes to the lower address, **high byte** to the higher address (Little-Endian).

When the 8086 executes a `POP destination` instruction (16-bit destination):

1. Read the 16-bit value from: `Physical Address = (SS × 16) + SP`
2. Load it into the destination register.
3. **SP ← SP + 2** (Release 2 bytes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack-Frame during CALL/INT:** When a `CALL` or `INT n` is executed, the 8086 automatically **PUSHes** the return IP and CS onto the stack. `PUSHF` saves the **FLAGS** register. `IRET` performs the reverse (POP IP, POP CS, POPF).

### 2.2 8086 Interrupt Classification

| Type | Source | Vector | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware (Maskable)** | INTR pin | (Obtained via INTA) | External, high-to-low on INTR if IF=1 |
| **Hardware (Non-Maskable)** | NMI pin | **Type 2** | Edge-triggered, cannot be masked |
| **Software (INT n)** | INT instruction | 0 to 255 | Programmer-initiated |
| **Software (INT 3)** | Breakpoint | Type 3 | Single-byte INT for debugging |
| **Software (INTO)** | Overflow trap | Type 4 | If OF=1 |
| **Processor-Generated** | Divide Error | Type 0 | Divide-by-zero |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Stack Address | $P_A = (\text{SS} \times 16) + \text{SP}$ | Hex address |
| Initial SP (after reset) | SP = **0000H** (no RAM installed) | Needs initialization |
| PUSH operation | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2$ | 16-bit decrement |
| POP operation | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 2$ | 16-bit increment |
| IVT Size | $256 \times 4 = 1024$ bytes | Occupies 00000H–003FFH |
| Interrupt Vector Format | Lower 2 bytes = IP, Upper 2 bytes = CS | Little-Endian storage |
| Address Bus | 20-bit | $\rightarrow$ 1 MB physical memory |
| Data Bus | 16-bit | D0–D15 |
| Reset Vector | FFFF0H | First instruction after reset |
| Minimum Mode Control Pins | $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{M}/\overline{\text{IO}}, \overline{\text{BHE}}$ etc. | Generated by 8086 |
| Maximum Mode Status Pins | $\overline{\text{S0}}, \overline{\text{S1}}, \overline{\text{S2}}, \overline{\text{S3}}-\overline{\text{S7}}$ | Decoded by 8288 |

### 2.4 Status Word Encoding for 8288 (Maximum Mode)

| $\overline{\text{S2}}$ | $\overline{\text{S1}}$ | $\overline{\text{S0}}$ | Machine Cycle | 8288 Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Interrupt Acknowledge | $\overline{\text{INTA}}$ |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Read I/O Port | $\overline{\text{IORC}}$ |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | Write I/O Port | $\overline{\text{IOWC}}, \overline{\text{AIOWC}}$ |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Halt | None |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Instruction Fetch | $\overline{\text{MRDC}}$ |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Read Memory | $\overline{\text{MRDC}}$ |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Write Memory | $\overline{\text{MWTC}}, \overline{\text{AMWC}}$ |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Passive (Bus Hold) | None |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stacks** form the backbone of **nested function calls** in every high-level language (C, C++, Java) and operating system (context switching).
- **Interrupts** are the foundation of **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)**, **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**, and **device drivers** (keyboard, mouse, network cards).
- **Maximum Mode 8086 systems** were the architectural ancestors of **multi-core CPUs** — modern CPUs still use the concept of a *bus arbiter* to coordinate multiple masters (cores, GPU, DMA engines) over a shared bus, exactly as the 8288 did in 1980s PC/AT designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address from SS:SP

The 8086 uses a **segmented memory model** where every physical address is computed by shifting a 16-bit segment register left by 4 bits (i.e., multiplying by 16) and adding a 16-bit offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } & \text{SS} = \text{2000H},\ \text{SP} = \text{FFFEH} \\
\text{Step 1: Shift SS left by 4 bits} & \rightarrow \text{SS} \times 16 = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} = 20000\text{H} \\
\text{Step 2: Add the offset SP} & \rightarrow P_A = 20000\text{H} + \text{FFFEH} \\
\text{Step 3: Final Addition} & \rightarrow 20000\text{H} + \text{FFFEH} = 2\text{FFFEH} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The next free stack location is at physical address **2FFFEH**. A `PUSH AX` would write AL at **2FFFEH** and AH at **2FFFFH**, then SP becomes **0000H** (wraps to FFFCH after the next push).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Interrupt Vector Calculation

**Problem:** An ISR is to be placed at physical address **50000H (CS)** with an IP of **2000H**. Compute the type number $n$ such that the IVT entry will correctly point to this ISR.

> The 8086 IVT occupies addresses **00000H to 003FFH**. Each entry is 4 bytes. Entry $n$ starts at physical address $4n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Required entry: } & 4n = 50000\text{H} \quad \text{(NOT possible — IVT is in low memory)} \\
\text{Re-interpretation: } & \text{IVT stores CS:IP, not the absolute address.} \\
\text{Vector entry address} & = n \times 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Re-solving with the standard procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute IP location (low word) at } & 4n,\ 4n+1 \\
\text{Step 2: Compute CS location (high word) at } & 4n+2,\ 4n+3 \\
\text{Step 3: For IP} = \text{2000H},\ \text{CS} = \text{5000H} & \text{ to be loaded, the IVT must contain:} \\
\text{Byte at } 4n   & = 00\text{H} \\
\text{Byte at } 4n+1 & = 20\text{H} \\
\text{Byte at } 4n+2 & = 00\text{H} \\
\text{Byte at } 4n+3 & = 50\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note for students:** The IVT only stores the **CS:IP** of the ISR, *not* the ISR's physical address. The processor forms the actual 20-bit physical address as `(CS × 16) + IP` when the ISR is dispatched.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Min vs Max Mode

| Parameter | Minimum Mode | Maximum Mode |
|---|---|---|
| MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ pin | Tied to **+5 V (Logic 1)** | Tied to **GND (Logic 0)** |
| Bus Controller | **Not required** (8086 itself) | **8288 required** |
| Processors supported | **Single** (8086 only) | **Multi** (8086 + 8087 + 8089) |
| Control signals | $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{M}/\overline{\text{IO}}, \overline{\text{BHE}}, \overline{\text{DEN}}, \text{DT}/\overline{\text{R}}$ | $\overline{\text{S0}}, \overline{\text{S1}}, \overline{\text{S2}}$ (status only) |
| Lock signal | $\overline{\text{LOCK}}$ output | $\overline{\text{LOCK}}$ becomes input + $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT0}}, \overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT1}}$ |
| Typical application | Small dedicated controllers | Multi-processor PC/AT motherboards |
| Cost | Lower (no 8288) | Higher (extra IC) |
| Bus Arbitration | None | Built-in (via $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}$) |

---

### 3.4 Mathematical Model: Interrupt Latency and Priority

The **interrupt latency** (number of clock cycles between INTR assertion and ISR fetch) for the 8086 is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{latency}} &= T_{\text{current instruction}} + T_{\text{INTA cycle}} \\
T_{\text{latency}} &\geq 11\,T + 4\,T \quad \text{(best case for NOP-like instruction)} \\
T_{\text{latency}} &\leq (\text{Worst instruction}) + 4\,T \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $T$ = clock period. For a 5 MHz 8086 ($T = 200$ ns):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{latency (min)}} &= 11 \times 200\,\text{ns} + 4 \times 200\,\text{ns} = 3000\,\text{ns} = 3\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Order in 8086 (Highest to Lowest):**  
> 1. **NMI** (Type 2)  
> 2. **Divide Error** (Type 0)  
> 3. **INTO** (Type 4)  
> 4. **INT n** (Software)  
> 5. **INTR** (Maskable)  
> 6. **Single-Step** (Type 1)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8086 Stack Operation Flow (PUSH and POP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch: PUSH AX] --> B[Decode: 16-bit source]
    B --> C[Decrement SP by 2: SP = SP - 2]
    C --> D[Compute Physical Address: SS*16 + SP]
    D --> E[Write AL to lower address byte]
    E --> F[Write AH to higher address byte]
    F --> G[Stack now has new TOP at new SP]

    H[Instruction Fetch: POP BX] --> I[Compute PA: SS*16 + SP]
    I --> J[Read low byte into BL]
    J --> K[Read high byte into BH]
    K --> L[Increment SP by 2: SP = SP + 2]
    L --> M[BX now holds popped value]
```

---

### 4.2 Interrupt Processing Sequence in 8086

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[8086 Executes Mainline Code] --> B{Interrupt Pending?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Push FLAGS onto Stack]
    D --> E[Clear IF and TF]
    E --> F[Push CS onto Stack]
    F --> G[Push IP onto Stack]
    G --> H[Fetch Vector: Read n*4 from IVT]
    H --> I[Load new IP from IVT low word]
    I --> J[Load new CS from IVT high word]
    J --> K[Execute ISR]
    K --> L[IRET: Pop IP]
    L --> M[IRET: Pop CS]
    M --> N[IRET: Pop FLAGS]
    N --> A
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Min vs Max Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MINMODE[Minimum Mode Configuration]
        CPU1[8086 CPU] -->|RD, WR, M/IO, BHE, DEN directly| BUS1[System Bus]
        MEM1[Memory] --> BUS1
        IO1[I/O Devices] --> BUS1
    end

    subgraph MAXMODE[Maximum Mode Configuration]
        CPU2[8086 CPU] -->|Status S0, S1, S2| BC[8288 Bus Controller]
        BC -->|MRDC, MWTC, IORC, IOWC| BUS2[System Bus]
        COP1[8087 Math Coprocessor] -->|RQ/GT0| CPU2
        COP2[8089 I/O Processor] -->|RQ/GT1| CPU2
        BUS2 --> MEM2[Memory]
        BUS2 --> IO2[I/O Devices]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[00000H: Type 0 — Divide Error IP,CS]
    L1[00004H: Type 1 — Single Step IP,CS]
    L2[00008H: Type 2 — NMI IP,CS]
    L3[0000CH: Type 3 — Breakpoint IP,CS]
    L4[00010H: Type 4 — INTO Overflow IP,CS]
    L5[00014H to 0003CH: Reserved 19 Vectors]
    L6[00040H: Type 10H — BIOS Video ISR]
    L7[00044H to 0007CH: Other BIOS ISRs]
    L8[00080H: Type 20H — DOS ISR]
    L9[003FCH: Type FFH — Last User Vector IP,CS]
    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> L8 --> L9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
Define a *Stack* in the 8086 microprocessor. Explain the role of the SS and SP registers.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A stack is a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure implemented in RAM, used for temporary storage of return addresses, flags, and procedure parameters.
> - **SS (Stack Segment):** A 16-bit segment register that holds the base address of the stack segment in memory.
> - **SP (Stack Pointer):** A 16-bit register that always points to the *top* of the stack (i.e., the next free location).  
> Physical stack address = `(SS × 16) + SP`. The stack grows **downward** in memory (PUSH decrements SP; POP increments SP).
> *(Award 1 mark each for: definition of stack, role of SS, role of SP — 3 marks total.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
Differentiate between *maskable* and *non-maskable* interrupts in the 8086.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Maskable Interrupts (INTR):** Activated via the **INTR** pin; can be enabled or disabled by setting/clearing the **IF (Interrupt Flag)** using `STI`/`CLI`. The interrupt type must be supplied externally by an interrupting device (e.g., 8259A PIC).
> - **Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI):** Activated via the **NMI** pin (Type 2 vector); **cannot be masked** by the IF flag and is typically reserved for catastrophic events like power failure or memory parity error.
> *(Award 1.5 marks each for the two types with their distinguishing features.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — Internal Choice (Module 1)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **8086 system in Minimum Mode** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of the following signals: $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{M}/\overline{\text{IO}}, \overline{\text{BHE}}, \text{DT}/\overline{\text{R}}, \overline{\text{DEN}}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the **8086 system in Maximum Mode** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **8288 Bus Controller** and the **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}$** arbitration lines. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Minimum Mode (7 Marks):**

In minimum mode, the **MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ pin is tied to +5 V (Logic 1)**. The 8086 directly generates all bus control signals. Typical configuration includes 8086 + 8284A (clock generator) + 8282/8286 (latches/transceivers) + memory and I/O.

| Signal | Function |
|---|---|
| $\overline{\text{RD}}$ | Active-low **read** strobe for memory or I/O (decoded by M/$\overline{\text{IO}}$) |
| $\overline{\text{WR}}$ | Active-low **write** strobe for memory or I/O |
| $\text{M}/\overline{\text{IO}}$ | Selects whether the current cycle is a **Memory** (1) or **I/O** (0) operation |
| $\overline{\text{BHE}}$ | **Bus High Enable** — when low, the upper data bus D8–D15 is active (used for 16-bit transfers) |
| $\text{DT}/\overline{\text{R}}$ | **Data Transmit / Receive** — controls the direction of the external bus transceiver (8286) |
| $\overline{\text{DEN}}$ | **Data Enable** — enables the external data transceivers during read/write |

> **Block Diagram Description:**  
> The 8086's 20-bit address bus (A0–A19) is split into 16 bits (A0–A15) which are latched using 3 × 8282 latches using the **ALE** signal, plus 4 bits (A16–A19) which are demultiplexed using the **S3–S6** status lines. The 16-bit data bus is buffered using 2 × 8286 transceivers controlled by $\text{DT}/\overline{\text{R}}$ and $\overline{\text{DEN}}$. Memory and I/O are directly connected to the buffered buses and selected by the control signals.

**(b) Maximum Mode (7 Marks):**

In maximum mode, **MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ is tied to GND (Logic 0)**. Pins 24–31 of the 8086 (which are $\overline{\text{S0}}, \overline{\text{S1}}, \overline{\text{S2}}, \overline{\text{S3}}, \overline{\text{S4}}, \overline{\text{S5}}, \overline{\text{S6}}, \overline{\text{LOCK}}$) change their function. The 8086 outputs **status codes** instead of control signals.

> **Role of 8288 Bus Controller:**  
> The 8288 decodes the 3-bit status code ($\overline{\text{S0}}, \overline{\text{S1}}, \overline{\text{S2}}$) and generates the full set of control signals: $\overline{\text{MRDC}}, \overline{\text{MWTC}}, \overline{\text{AMWC}}, \overline{\text{IORC}}, \overline{\text{IOWC}}, \overline{\text{AIOWC}}, \overline{\text{INTA}}$. It also provides advanced control (DEN, DT/$\overline{\text{R}}$) and is capable of supporting multi-master configurations.

> **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}$ Arbitration:**  
> In maximum mode, pins 30 and 31 become **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT0}}$** and **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT1}}$** (Request/Grant). Coprocessors (8087, 8089) use these bidirectional lines to:
> 1. **Request** the bus from the 8086,
> 2. **Receive** the grant pulse, then
> 3. **Release** the bus back to the 8086 by a third pulse.  
> $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT0}}$ has higher priority than $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT1}}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)** in the 8086. Describe the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** and the steps involved in processing a hardware interrupt. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Stack** in the 8086? With a neat diagram, explain the PUSH and POP operations. Given SS = 4000H and SP = 0100H, calculate the physical address of the top of the stack. What will be the new SP value after executing `PUSH BX` if BX = 1234H? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Interrupts and ISR (7 Marks):**

> **Concept:**  
> An interrupt is an asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program and forces the 8086 to execute a special subroutine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR finishes, the processor resumes the main program at the exact point of interruption.

> **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT):**  
> The IVT is a 1-KB table located at physical addresses **00000H–003FFH**. It contains **256 entries**, each of **4 bytes** (low word = IP, high word = CS). The entry for interrupt type $n$ is located at address $4n$. When an interrupt occurs, the 8086 multiplies the type number by 4, reads the CS:IP, and jumps to that location.

> **Hardware Interrupt Processing Steps (INTR):**
> 1. The interrupting device asserts INTR high.
> 2. The 8086 completes the current instruction, then asserts $\overline{\text{INTA}}$.
> 3. The device places the 8-bit type number on the data bus (during the second $\overline{\text{INTA}}$ pulse).
> 4. The 8086 pushes FLAGS, CS, and IP onto the stack.
> 5. The IF and TF flags are cleared.
> 6. The CS and IP are loaded from the IVT entry $n \times 4$.
> 7. The ISR executes; on `IRET`, IP, CS, and FLAGS are popped back.

**(b) Stack Operations and Numerical (7 Marks):**

> **PUSH BX Operation (4-stage process):**
> 1. SP ← SP − 2 (reserve space).
> 2. Compute physical address = (SS × 16) + SP.
> 3. Write BL to the lower byte of the new top.
> 4. Write BH to the upper byte of the new top.

> **POP BX Operation:**
> 1. Compute physical address = (SS × 16) + SP.
> 2. Read BL from lower byte, BH from upper byte.
> 3. SP ← SP + 2 (release space).

> **Numerical Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS} &= 4000\text{H},\ \text{SP} = 0100\text{H},\ \text{BX} = 1234\text{H} \\
P_A &= (\text{SS} \times 16) + \text{SP} \\
P_A &= 4000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 0100\text{H} \\
P_A &= 40000\text{H} + 0100\text{H} = 40100\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After PUSH BX:} & \\
\text{New SP} &= 0100\text{H} - 0002\text{H} = 00\text{FEH} \\
\text{New physical top} &= 40000\text{H} + 00\text{FEH} = 400\text{FEH} \\
\text{Memory contents: } & [400\text{FEH}] = 34\text{H}\ (\text{BL}),\ [400\text{FFH}] = 12\text{H}\ (\text{BH}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Award 2 marks for stack concept, 2 marks for PUSH/POP diagram, 3 marks for numerical calculation — total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 2 from SP *before* the write** during a PUSH operation. The 8086 always decrements SP *first*, then writes data. Reversing this order corrupts the stack.
> 2. **Byte-order confusion:** Always remember 8086 is **little-endian**. For a 16-bit PUSH, the **low byte** goes to the **lower address** and the **high byte** to the higher address.
> 3. **IVT entry math:** Each IVT entry is **4 bytes**, not 2. Entry $n$ starts at $4n$, not $2n$.
> 4. **Mode pin voltage:** Don't say "MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ = 0 for minimum mode." The bar indicates *inversion*; minimum mode uses **logic 1** (HIGH).
> 5. **Maximum mode + single processor = wrong answer.** If the configuration is maximum mode, you **must** include the 8288 in the block diagram; otherwise the bus has no control signals.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Stack** is a **LIFO** structure in RAM; managed by **SS** and **SP** registers; grows **downward** in memory.
- ✅ **PUSH** = `SP - 2` then write. **POP** = read then `SP + 2`.
- ✅ **IVT** has 256 entries × 4 bytes = 1 KB at **00000H–003FFH**.
- ✅ Each IVT entry stores **IP (low) and CS (high)** in little-endian format.
- ✅ **INT n** is a software interrupt; **NMI** is non-maskable (Type 2); **INTR** is maskable (controlled by IF).
- ✅ **`IRET`** pops **IP, CS, FLAGS** in that order — the reverse of the INT save sequence.
- ✅ **Minimum Mode:** MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ = 1; 8086 generates all control signals directly; **single processor** only.
- ✅ **Maximum Mode:** MN/$\overline{\text{MX}}$ = 0; 8086 outputs only **status ($\overline{\text{S0}}, \overline{\text{S1}}, \overline{\text{S2}}$)**; requires **8288 Bus Controller**; supports **multi-processor** (8087, 8089).
- ✅ **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT0}}$** has higher priority than **$\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT1}}$** in maximum mode.
- ✅ **BHE** (Bus High Enable) is essential for accessing the **upper data byte** (D8–D15).
- ✅ **Reset vector** is at physical address **FFFF0H**; the first instruction executed after reset is fetched from here.
- ✅ **Interrupt priority order (high→low):** NMI → Divide Error (Type 0) → INTO (Type 4) → INT n → INTR → Single-Step (Type 1).
- ✅ Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** for mode-configuration questions; missing labels cost easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Salient Features of 80386 DX - Architecture and Signal Description - Register Organization - Memory Addressing in Real Address Mode
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Salient Features of 80386 DX - Architecture and Signal Description - Register Organization - Memory Addressing in Real Address Mode

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Salient Features of 80386 DX

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **Intel 80386 DX** is a **32-bit** high-performance, pipelined, CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microprocessor introduced by Intel in **1985**. It is the first commercially available processor to implement a true **32-bit architecture** with **32-bit data bus**, **32-bit address bus**, integrated **memory management unit (MMU)** supporting paging and segmentation, and operates at clock frequencies ranging from **12 MHz to 40 MHz**.

**Key Salient Features at a Glance:**

| Feature | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Data Bus Width | **32-bit** |
| Address Bus Width | **32-bit** |
| Physical Address Space | **4 GB** ($2^{32}$ bytes) |
| Virtual Address Space | **64 TB** ($2^{46}$ bytes) |
| Register Size | **32-bit** (General Purpose) |
| Operating Modes | Real, Protected, Virtual 8086 |
| Pipeline Depth | 6-stage |
| Addressable Memory | **4 GB** physical, **64 TB** virtual |
| Package | **132-pin** PGA (Ceramic) / PQFP |
| Transistor Count | **275,000** transistors |
| Technology | **1.5 µm** CHMOS III process |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "DX" suffix distinguishes the full 32-bit version (with external 32-bit data bus) from the 80386 SX (16-bit external data bus, 24-bit address bus). For examination purposes, the **DX variant is the default reference architecture**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The 80386 DX as a Mega-Warehouse

Imagine the **80386 DX** as a **massive, highly automated warehouse complex** (the processor) managing an enormous **city-wide inventory system** (memory):

- The **4 GB physical address space** is the actual warehouse floor space — enormous compared to the older 8086's tiny storeroom of **1 MB**.
- The **segmentation unit** acts like the **department manager** who divides the warehouse into logical sections (segments).
- The **paging unit** acts like the **shelf organizer** who further breaks each section into uniform pages of 4 KB each.
- The **pipeline** is the **conveyor belt assembly line** where fetching, decoding, and executing happen **simultaneously** on different instructions — like one worker fetching goods while another packs them.
- The **32-bit registers** are the **worker's hands** — much larger and able to carry more "boxes" (data) per trip than 8086's 16-bit hands.

> [!TIP]
> **Why was 80386 DX revolutionary?** It brought **multitasking OS support** (Windows 3.x, OS/2, early Linux), **virtual memory**, and **protected mode** to the PC platform — the foundational architecture for everything from Windows 95 to modern x86 descendants.

---

### 1.3 Architecture of 80386 DX — The Internal Block Diagram

The 80386 DX consists of **six major functional units** operating in parallel through the pipeline:

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Functional Units of 80386 DX:**
> 1. **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** – Handles all bus cycles, address generation, and data transfer.
> 2. **Code Prefetch Unit** – Prefetches up to **16 bytes** of instruction stream into a **16-byte prefetch queue**.
> 3. **Instruction Decode Unit** – Decodes opcodes and stores them in the decoded instruction queue.
> 4. **Execution Unit** – Contains the **ALU**, **barrel shifter**, **multiplier/divider**, and executes instructions.
> 5. **Segmentation Unit** – Translates logical addresses (segment:offset) to **linear addresses** (32-bit).
> 6. **Paging Unit** – Translates linear addresses to **physical addresses** using page tables (only in Protected Mode).

```
        ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
        │  Code        │    │ Instruction  │
        │  Prefetch    │───▶│   Decode     │
        │  Unit        │    │   Unit       │
        └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                │                    │
                ▼                    ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
        │       EXECUTION UNIT             │
        │   (ALU, Barrel Shifter,          │
        │    Multiplier/Divider)           │
        └──────────────────────────────────┘
                │                    ▲
                ▼                    │
        ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
        │ Segmentation │    │   Paging     │
        │    Unit      │───▶│    Unit      │
        └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                │                    │
                ▼                    ▼
        ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
        │    BUS INTERFACE UNIT (BIU)      │
        │   (Address/Data/Control Bus)     │
        └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Signal Description of 80386 DX (External Pins)

The 80386 DX has **132 pins** (PGA package). The signals are grouped into the following functional categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Category:** Examiners frequently ask students to classify pins and explain the role of control signals like **M/IO, D/C, W/R, ADS, READY, BE0–BE3, LOCK**.

#### 2.1.1 Data Bus Signals (D0 – D31)

- **D31 – D0** — 32 bidirectional data lines.
- These form the **32-bit data bus** that allows the processor to transfer 32 bits (4 bytes) in a single memory cycle — known as a **double-word transfer**.
- The data bus is **tri-stated** during bus hold acknowledge (HLDA).

#### 2.1.2 Address Bus Signals (A2 – A31, BE0 – BE3)

- **A31 – A2** — The **30 most significant bits** of the 32-bit address bus (since the lowest 2 bits are encoded by the byte enables).
- **BE3, BE2, BE1, BE0** — **Byte Enable signals** (active LOW). Each enables one of the four bytes within a 32-bit data word:

| BE3 | BE2 | BE1 | BE0 | Accessed Bytes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | D7–D0 (Byte 0) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | D15–D8 (Byte 1) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | D23–D16 (Byte 2) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | D31–D24 (Byte 3) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The combination of **30 address lines** + **4 byte-enable lines** produces a complete **32-bit physical address**, allowing **byte-level**, **word-level**, and **double-word-level** access in a single bus cycle.

#### 2.1.3 Control Signals (Bus Cycle Definition)

The 80386 DX uses **three primary status signals** to define the type of bus cycle:

| Signal | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **M/IO** | Distinguishes Memory (1) from I/O (0) access |
| **D/C** | Distinguishes Data (1) from Control/Code (0) cycle |
| **W/R** | Distinguishes Write (1) from Read (0) cycle |

These three signals (M/IO, D/C, W/R) combine to produce **8 unique bus cycle types**:

| M/IO | D/C | W/R | Bus Cycle Type |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Interrupt Acknowledge |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Halt / Shutdown |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Read |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | I/O Write |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Instruction Fetch (Code Read) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Halt (if fetched) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Read (Data) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Memory Write (Data) |

#### 2.1.4 Other Critical Control Signals

- **ADS (Address Status)** — Active LOW; indicates that a valid bus cycle is in progress (address + status signals are valid).
- **READY** — Active LOW; inserts **wait states** when slow memory/peripherals are accessed.
- **LOCK** — Active LOW; prevents other bus masters from gaining control of the bus (used in multiprocessor systems).
- **BS16** (Bus Size 16) — Forces the 80386 to operate with a 16-bit data bus for compatibility with 16-bit peripherals.
- **HOLD / HLDA** — Bus request and bus grant for DMA operation.
- **NMI, INTR, RESET** — Interrupt and reset inputs.
- **CLK2** — Twice the internal clock frequency (the 80386 internally divides CLK2 by 2 to get its operating frequency).
- **PEREQ, BUSY, ERROR** — Signals for the **coprocessor interface** (80287 / 80387).

---

### 2.2 Register Organization of 80386 DX

The 80386 DX contains a **rich register set** organized into the following categories:

#### 2.2.1 General Purpose Registers (8 × 32-bit)

These are the **workhorse registers** used for arithmetic, logic, and data movement:

| 32-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit (High) | 8-bit (Low) | Common Use |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| EAX | AX | AH | AL | Accumulator |
| EBX | BX | BH | BL | Base Register |
| ECX | CX | CH | CL | Counter |
| EDX | DX | DH | DL | Data / I/O Port |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The 32-bit registers are like **4-byte boxes**. You can access them whole (EAX) or open smaller compartments within (AX, AH, AL). This **backward compatibility** is a major design strength — existing 8086/8088 code runs unchanged on the 80386.

#### 2.2.2 Segment Registers (6 × 16-bit)

The 80386 DX has **six segment registers** (two more than the 8086's four):

| Register | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CS** | Code Segment |
| **DS** | Data Segment |
| **SS** | Stack Segment |
| **ES** | Extra Segment |
| **FS** | Additional Data Segment (new in 386) |
| **GS** | Additional Data Segment (new in 386) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Real Mode**, each segment register holds a **16-bit base value**. In **Protected Mode**, each segment register holds a **16-bit selector** that indexes into the **GDT/LDT** to fetch a 32-bit base, 20-bit limit, and access rights from a **segment descriptor**.

#### 2.2.3 Pointer and Index Registers (4 × 32-bit)

| Register | 16-bit | Purpose |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **EIP** | IP | Instruction Pointer (points to next instruction) |
| **ESP** | SP | Stack Pointer (top of stack) |
| **EBP** | BP | Base Pointer (stack frame base) |
| **ESI** | SI | Source Index (string operations) |
| **EDI** | DI | Destination Index (string operations) |

#### 2.2.4 Flag Register (EFLAGS — 32 bits)

The **EFLAGS** register contains **status flags**, **control flags**, and **system flags**:

| Bit | Flag | Meaning |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | CF | Carry Flag |
| 6 | ZF | Zero Flag |
| 7 | SF | Sign Flag |
| 9 | OF | Overflow Flag |
| 10 | DF | Direction Flag |
| 11 | IF | Interrupt Enable Flag |
| 12 | IOPL | I/O Privilege Level (2 bits) |
| 13 | NT | Nested Task |
| 14 | VM | Virtual 8086 Mode |
| 16 | RF | Resume Flag |
| 17 | VM | Virtual Mode |

> [!NOTE]
> The lower **16 bits** form the **FLAGS** register (compatible with 8086). The upper 16 bits add **system-level flags** introduced with the 80386.

#### 2.2.5 Control Registers (CR0 – CR3)

| Register | Key Functions |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CR0** | Contains **PE** (Protection Enable), **PG** (Paging Enable), **ET** (Extension Type), **TS**, **EM**, **MP**, **AM**, **WP** bits |
| **CR1** | Reserved (not used) |
| **CR2** | Page Fault Linear Address — holds the linear address that caused the last page fault |
| **CR3** | Page Directory Base Register (holds physical address of the page directory) |

#### 2.2.6 System Address Registers (4 × special)

| Register | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **GDTR** | Global Descriptor Table Register (base 32-bit + limit 16-bit) |
| **LDTR** | Local Descriptor Table Register (selector) |
| **IDTR** | Interrupt Descriptor Table Register (base 32-bit + limit 16-bit) |
| **TR** | Task Register (selector for current TSS) |

#### 2.2.7 Debug and Test Registers

- **DR0–DR3** — Linear breakpoint addresses (4 hardware breakpoints).
- **DR4, DR5** — Reserved.
- **DR6** — Debug status register.
- **DR7** — Debug control register.
- **TR6, TR7** — Test registers for cache and TLB testing.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet: Real Mode Address Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Address Mode Memory Addressing Formula (THE Most Repeated KTU Formula):**

$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Effective Offset}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment} \ll 4) + \text{Offset}$$

Where:
- **Segment** = 16-bit value from a segment register (CS, DS, SS, ES).
- **Offset** = 16-bit effective offset in Real Mode (max **FFFFh** = 65,535).
- **Physical Address** = 20-bit value, range **00000h – FFFFFh** (1 MB total).

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Segment register size | **16 bits** (0 – 65535) |
| Offset size (Real Mode) | **16 bits** (0 – 65535) |
| Physical address size | **20 bits** (0 – 1,048,575) |
| Total addressable memory | **1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** |
| Paragraph size | **16 bytes** (1 paragraph = 16 bytes) |
| Smallest memory unit | **1 byte** |

**Default Segment Rule Table:**

| Memory Reference | Default Segment | Offset Register |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Instruction Fetch | CS | IP |
| Stack Operation | SS | SP |
| BP-based access | SS | BP |
| String source (MOVS) | DS | SI |
| String destination (MOVS) | ES | DI |
| Other data accesses | DS | BX, DI, SI, disp |

**Real-World Utility:** The segment:offset scheme enables **relocatable code** — a program can be loaded anywhere in the 1 MB space by simply changing the segment registers, without rewriting the code. This principle underlies every modern OS loader.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Real Mode Physical Address Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the segment must be SHIFTED LEFT by 4 (multiplied by 16)?**
> The Intel engineers of 1978 faced a problem: with only 16-bit registers, the maximum address was **64 KB**. To extend this to **1 MB** for the 8086, they used **two 16-bit values** (segment + offset) and combined them so that the segment base aligns on a **16-byte boundary (paragraph)**. This shift left by 4 bits produces a **20-bit physical address** from two 16-bit components.

**Step-by-Step Numerical Example:**

**Problem:** Find the physical address when **CS = 2000h** and **IP = 0450h**.

**Step 1:** Write down the formula.

$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Offset}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.

$$\text{Physical Address} = (2000\text{h} \times 10\text{h}) + 0450\text{h}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the segment × 16. Since hexadecimal × 10h is equivalent to appending one zero in hex (shifting left by 4 bits):

$$2000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} = 20000\text{h}$$

**Step 4:** Add the offset.

$$20000\text{h} + 0450\text{h} = 20450\text{h}$$

**Step 5:** Convert the physical address to decimal (if required) and verify it lies within the **1 MB boundary** (00000h – FFFFFh).

$$20450\text{h} = 2 \times 16^4 + 0 \times 16^3 + 4 \times 16^2 + 5 \times 16^1 + 0 \times 16^0$$
$$= 131072 + 0 + 1024 + 80 + 0 = 132176 \text{ (decimal)}$$

**Verification:** 132,176 bytes < 1,048,576 bytes ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Shortcut for Exam Hall:** Append a **0** (zero) to the segment value in hex to get Segment×16, then perform normal hex addition with the offset. The result must be ≤ **FFFFFh**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Real Mode Address Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How 8086/80386 Real Mode Generates a 20-bit Address from 16-bit Components:**

The logical 20-bit address is constructed in **four conceptual stages**:

**Stage 1: Segment Register Loading**
The CPU reads a 16-bit value from the appropriate segment register based on the memory access type (e.g., CS for instruction fetch).

**Stage 2: Shift Left by 4 (Multiplication by 16)**
The segment value is **left-shifted by 4 bit positions**, producing a 20-bit result. This is implemented in hardware using a **20-bit adder** where the lower 4 bits of the segment are fed to the upper 4 bits of the adder.

**Stage 3: Offset Addition**
The 16-bit offset is added to the 20-bit shifted segment base. Carry-out from the 20th bit is **discarded** (causes address wrap-around at 1 MB boundary).

**Stage 4: Physical Address Output**
The 20-bit result drives the **external address bus** (A0–A19, with A0–A1 encoded via byte enables on 80386 DX).

**Worked Example 2: Stack Operation**

Given: **SS = 4000h**, **SP = 1000h**, operation = **PUSH AX**.

- Compute physical address of stack top:
$$\text{PA} = 4000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} + 1000\text{h} = 41000\text{h}$$

- After PUSH, SP decrements by 2 → SP = 0FFEh, new physical address:
$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 4000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} + 0\text{FFE}\text{h} = 40\text{FFE}\text{h}$$

**Worked Example 3: Address Boundary Wrap-Around**

Given: **CS = FFFFh**, **IP = 0010h**.

- Compute:
$$\text{PA} = \text{FFFFh} \times 10\text{h} + 0010\text{h} = \text{FFFF0h} + 0010\text{h} = \text{100000h}$$

- But the 20-bit address bus can only represent 20 bits! The 21st bit (carry) is **discarded**:
$$\text{100000h} \rightarrow \text{00000h} \text{ (wraps to 0)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This **1 MB wrap-around** is a critical concept. An instruction at FFFF:0010h will actually be fetched from physical address **00000h**, not 100000h. This behavior was famously exploited by early viruses and is a key reason why Real Mode is unsafe for modern systems.

---

### 3.3 Summary Table: 80386 DX Operating Modes

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80386 supports THREE operating modes:**

| Mode | Address Size | Memory Space | Segment | Paging | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Real Mode** | 20-bit | **1 MB** | 16-bit | Disabled | 8086 emulation, BIOS |
| **Protected Mode** | 32-bit | **4 GB** | 32-bit | Optional | Native 32-bit OS, multitasking |
| **Virtual 8086 Mode** | 20-bit (emulated) | 1 MB (per task) | 16-bit | Enabled | Run DOS inside protected-mode OS |

**Real Mode Characteristics (for emphasis):**
- 32-bit registers exist but the processor **masks the upper bits** when computing 20-bit addresses.
- The high 4 bits of the address are produced **only by the segment** (after the left shift), not by the offset.
- A20 line masking may be applied (the famous **A20 gate** in the keyboard controller for HIMEM.SYS compatibility).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
List any **six salient features** of the 80386 DX microprocessor.

**Model Answer:**

1. It is a **32-bit microprocessor** with a 32-bit data bus and 32-bit address bus.
2. It can address up to **4 GB** of physical memory ($2^{32}$ bytes) and **64 TB** of virtual memory.
3. It supports **three operating modes**: Real, Protected, and Virtual 8086.
4. It has an integrated **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** with **paging and segmentation** hardware.
5. It uses a **6-stage instruction pipeline** for high throughput.
6. It has a **16-byte instruction prefetch queue** to overlap fetch and execution.
7. It contains **275,000 transistors** and operates on **1.5 µm CHMOS III** technology.
8. It supports **multitasking** via hardware task switching using the **TSS (Task State Segment)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Explain the function of the **byte enable signals (BE0–BE3)** in 80386 DX.

**Model Answer:**

The 80386 DX has a 32-bit data bus. To allow access to any combination of bytes (8, 16, 24, or 32 bits) within a 32-bit word, the **lowest 2 address lines (A0, A1) are NOT provided externally**. Instead, **four byte-enable signals (BE0–BE3)** are used. Each **BE** signal (active LOW) enables one byte of the 32-bit data bus:

- **BE0** enables **D7–D0**
- **BE1** enables **D15–D8**
- **BE2** enables **D23–D16**
- **BE3** enables **D31–D24**

By selectively activating these signals, the CPU can perform byte, word, or double-word accesses without external address decoding logic.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive 80386 DX Architecture & Real Mode Addressing**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of 80386 DX**, listing the functions of all six functional units. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **memory addressing in Real Address Mode** of 80386 DX. Compute the **physical address** for each of the following with proper justification: **(7 Marks)**

1. CS = 4500h, IP = 1234h
2. DS = 2000h, BX = 3050h
3. SS = A000h, BP = 0100h

---

**Model Solution (a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Functional Units of 80386 DX:**

**1. Bus Interface Unit (BIU):**
- Performs all bus operations (memory read/write, I/O read/write, instruction fetch).
- Handles address generation, data transfer, and bus arbitration (HOLD/HLDA).
- Contains the **address drivers** and **data transceivers** interfacing to the external system bus.
- Supports **dynamic bus sizing** via the **BS16** input for 16-bit peripheral compatibility.

**2. Code Prefetch Unit:**
- Fetches instructions from memory **ahead of time** into a **16-byte prefetch queue**.
- Operates in parallel with the execution unit — when the EU is busy executing one instruction, the prefetch unit is fetching the next.
- Issues a **code fetch bus cycle** (M/IO=1, D/C=0, W/R=0) when the queue is not full.

**3. Instruction Decode Unit:**
- Takes bytes from the prefetch queue and **decodes opcodes** and **operand specifiers**.
- Stores decoded instructions into a **decoded instruction queue** for the execution unit.
- Performs **operand fetching** and effective address calculation in advance.

**4. Execution Unit (EU):**
- The "heart" of the CPU — contains the **32-bit ALU**, **barrel shifter** (for shifts/rotates in one cycle), **multiplier/divider**, and **flag register**.
- Executes all arithmetic, logical, and data movement operations.
- Sets the appropriate **EFLAGS** based on results.

**5. Segmentation Unit:**
- Translates **logical addresses** (segment:offset) into **32-bit linear addresses**.
- In Real Mode: performs segment × 16 + offset, producing a 20-bit address (zero-extended to 32 bits).
- In Protected Mode: reads the **segment descriptor** from GDT/LDT to get the 32-bit base.
- Performs **limit checking** and **type checking** in protected mode.

**6. Paging Unit:**
- Translates **linear addresses** to **physical addresses** using a **two-level page table** structure (only active in Protected Mode with PG=1 in CR0).
- Page size: **4 KB**.
- On a page fault, the linear address is stored in **CR2** and a **page fault exception (INT 14h)** is generated.
- The TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) caches recent translations for speed.

**Data Flow Summary:**
> Instructions are fetched (BIU → Prefetch → Decode) in parallel with execution (EU), while memory addresses are translated (Segmentation → Paging → BIU) for data accesses. This parallel operation is the essence of pipelining.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Real Mode Address Formula:**
$$\text{Physical Address (20-bit)} = (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{h}) + \text{Offset}$$

**Case 1: CS = 4500h, IP = 1234h**

$$\text{PA}_1 = 4500\text{h} \times 10\text{h} + 1234\text{h}$$

$$4500\text{h} \times 10\text{h} = 45000\text{h}$$

$$\text{PA}_1 = 45000\text{h} + 1234\text{h} = 46234\text{h}$$

**Decimal verification:** $45000\text{h} = 282624$, $1234\text{h} = 4660$, sum = **287,284** (within 1 MB ✓)

**Case 2: DS = 2000h, BX = 3050h**

$$\text{PA}_2 = 2000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} + 3050\text{h}$$

$$2000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} = 20000\text{h}$$

$$\text{PA}_2 = 20000\text{h} + 3050\text{h} = 23050\text{h}$$

**Case 3: SS = A000h, BP = 0100h**

$$\text{PA}_3 = \text{A}000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} + 0100\text{h}$$

$$\text{A}000\text{h} \times 10\text{h} = \text{A}0000\text{h}$$

$$\text{PA}_3 = \text{A}0000\text{h} + 0100\text{h} = \text{A}0100\text{h}$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| # | Segment | Offset | Segment × 16 | Physical Address |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | CS = 4500h | IP = 1234h | 45000h | **46234h** |
| 2 | DS = 2000h | BX = 3050h | 20000h | **23050h** |
| 3 | SS = A000h | BP = 0100h | A0000h | **A0100h** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **register organization of 80386 DX** with a neat diagram. Compare its registers with those of 8086. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **signal description of 80386 DX**. Explain the function of **M/IO, D/C, W/R, ADS, READY, and BE0–BE3** signals in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**80386 DX Register Set (6 categories):**

**1. Eight 32-bit General Purpose Registers** — EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP.
- Each can be accessed as 32-bit, 16-bit (e.g., AX, BX), or **8-bit (AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL)** for the first four.
- Provide 32-bit operands, counters, and pointer offsets.

**2. Six 16-bit Segment Registers** — CS, DS, SS, ES, FS, GS.
- 8086 had only 4 (CS, DS, SS, ES); **FS and GS were added in 386** for extra segment flexibility in protected mode.

**3. 32-bit Instruction Pointer** — **EIP** (with 16-bit **IP** compatibility).

**4. 32-bit Flag Register** — **EFLAGS** (with 16-bit FLAGS compatibility). Adds system flags: **IOPL, NT, RF, VM**.

**5. Four 32-bit Control Registers** — CR0, CR2, CR3 (CR1 reserved).
- **CR0** holds **PE** (Protection Enable), **PG** (Paging Enable), **EM**, **TS**, **MP**, **ET**, **AM**, **WP** bits.
- **CR2** holds the **page-fault linear address**.
- **CR3** holds the **page directory physical base address**.

**6. System Address Registers** — **GDTR, IDTR, LDTR, TR** for descriptor table management.

**7. Debug & Test Registers** — **DR0–DR7** (4 hardware breakpoints), **TR6–TR7** (TLB testing).

**Comparison with 8086:**

| Feature | 8086 | 80386 DX |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| GP Register Size | 16-bit | **32-bit** (with 16/8-bit backward compat) |
| Number of GP Registers | 8 | **8** (same) |
| Segment Registers | 4 | **6** (added FS, GS) |
| Flag Register | 16-bit FLAGS | **32-bit EFLAGS** |
| Instruction Pointer | 16-bit IP | **32-bit EIP** |
| Control Registers | None | **CR0, CR2, CR3** |
| System Registers | None | **GDTR, IDTR, LDTR, TR** |
| Debug Registers | None | **DR0–DR7** |
| Test Registers | None | **TR6, TR7** |

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Signal Description Summary Table:**

| Signal | Type | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D31–D0** | Bidirectional | 32-bit data bus |
| **A31–A2** | Output | Upper 30 bits of address |
| **BE3–BE0** | Output (active LOW) | Byte enables for 4 bytes of data bus |
| **M/IO** | Output | 1=Memory, 0=I/O cycle |
| **D/C** | Output | 1=Data, 0=Control/Code cycle |
| **W/R** | Output | 1=Write, 0=Read cycle |
| **ADS** | Output (active LOW) | Address status; valid bus cycle in progress |
| **READY** | Input (active LOW) | Slow memory/peripheral ready signal (inserts wait states) |
| **LOCK** | Output (active LOW) | Bus lock for atomic operations (e.g., XCHG, semaphore) |
| **BS16** | Input | Bus size 16; forces 16-bit data bus operation |
| **HOLD, HLDA** | I/O | DMA bus request and acknowledge |
| **NMI, INTR, RESET** | Input | Non-maskable interrupt, maskable interrupt, system reset |
| **CLK2** | Input | Clock input (divided by 2 internally) |
| **PEREQ, BUSY, ERROR** | I/O | Coprocessor (80287/80387) handshake signals |

**Functions of M/IO, D/C, W/R (Bus Cycle Definition):**

These three signals together define the type of bus cycle being executed. Decoded externally, they select:
- **Memory or I/O** space (M/IO).
- **Data or Control** access (D/C).
- **Read or Write** direction (W/R).

External devices use these signals (with address and BE lines) to generate **chip select**, **output enable**, and **write enable** for the appropriate memory or I/O device.

**Functions of ADS and READY:**

- **ADS (Address Status)** is asserted LOW in the **first clock (T1)** of every bus cycle, indicating that the address (A2–A31, BE0–BE3) and bus cycle definition (M/IO, D/C, W/R) signals are **valid and stable**. External circuitry latches the address on the falling edge of ADS.
- **READY** is sampled by the CPU in each **T2** state. If LOW, the bus cycle completes. If HIGH, the CPU inserts **wait states (Tw)** by extending T2 until READY goes LOW. This allows interfacing with **slow memories and peripherals**.

**Functions of BE0–BE3 (Byte Enables):**

Already explained in detail in Question Q2 (Part A). These four signals allow the 32-bit data bus to be split into four 8-bit lanes, enabling byte, word, or double-word transfers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply segment by 10h (16)** — this is the #1 error. The formula is **NOT** Segment + Offset.
> 2. **Confusing the 80386 DX with 80386 SX** — SX has a 16-bit data bus and 24-bit address bus (16 MB memory). Always state "DX" explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the 20-bit wrap-around at FFFFFh** — segment:offset pairs like F000:FFFF wrap to 0FFFFh, not 10FFEFh.
> 4. **Listing only 8086 registers** when asked about 80386 — you MUST mention **FS, GS, CR0–CR3, GDTR/IDTR/LDTR/TR, and DR0–DR7** for full marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning pipelining** when asked about "salient features" — the 6-stage pipeline is a defining architectural feature.
> 6. **In Real Mode address calculation, treating the offset as 32-bit** — in Real Mode, the offset is always **16 bits** (0–FFFFh), even though EIP is 32 bits in 80386.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Salient Features of 80386 DX:**
- ⭐ First commercial **32-bit** microprocessor (Intel, 1985).
- ⭐ **32-bit data bus**, **32-bit address bus** (DX variant only).
- ⭐ **4 GB physical** + **64 TB virtual** address space.
- ⭐ **3 operating modes**: Real, Protected, Virtual 8086.
- ⭐ Integrated **MMU** with segmentation + paging.
- ⭐ **6-stage pipeline** + **16-byte prefetch queue**.
- ⭐ **275,000 transistors**, **1.5 µm CHMOS** process.
- ⭐ **132-pin PGA** package.

**Architecture (Six Units):**
- ⭐ **BIU** — bus operations.
- ⭐ **Code Prefetch** — 16-byte queue.
- ⭐ **Instruction Decode** — decoded instruction queue.
- ⭐ **Execution Unit** — ALU, barrel shifter, multiplier.
- ⭐ **Segmentation Unit** — logical → linear address.
- ⭐ **Paging Unit** — linear → physical address (4 KB pages).

**Signal Description — Must-Know Pins:**
- ⭐ **D31–D0** (32-bit data bus).
- ⭐ **A31–A2 + BE0–BE3** (32-bit address via 30 address + 4 byte enables).
- ⭐ **M/IO, D/C, W/R** — 3-bit bus cycle definition.
- ⭐ **ADS, READY, LOCK, BS16** — bus control.
- ⭐ **HOLD, HLDA** — DMA handshake.
- ⭐ **PEREQ, BUSY, ERROR** — coprocessor interface.
- ⭐ **CLK2** — clock input (÷2 internally).

**Register Organization — Key Categories:**
- ⭐ **8 × 32-bit GP registers**: EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP.
- ⭐ **6 × 16-bit segment registers**: CS, DS, SS, ES, **FS, GS** (new in 386).
- ⭐ **32-bit EIP, EFLAGS** (with 16-bit IP, FLAGS compatibility).
- ⭐ **Control Registers**: CR0 (PE, PG bits), CR2 (page fault addr), CR3 (page dir base).
- ⭐ **System Registers**: GDTR, IDTR, LDTR, TR.
- ⭐ **Debug Registers**: DR0–DR7 (4 hardware breakpoints).
- ⭐ **Test Registers**: TR6, TR7 (TLB testing).

**Real Mode Address Calculation — THE Formula:**
- ⭐ **Physical Address = (Segment × 16) + Offset**.
- ⭐ Segment × 16 ≡ Segment shifted left by 4 bits ≡ appending a 0 in hex.
- ⭐ Total Real Mode memory = **1 MB** (20-bit address, 00000h–FFFFFh).
- ⭐ Offset range: **0000h–FFFFh** (16-bit).
- ⭐ **Wrap-around** at FFFFFh: 21st bit is discarded.
- ⭐ Default segment rules: CS for code, SS for stack (SP/BP), DS for data (BX/SI/DI), ES for string destination.

**Common Numerical Conversions to Memorize:**

| Hex | Decimal | Binary | Size |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 MB | 1,048,576 | — | FFFFFh |
| 4 GB | 4,294,967,296 | $2^{32}$ | FFFFFFFFh |
| 16 | 16 | 10h | 1 paragraph |
| 64 KB | 65,536 | $2^{16}$ | FFFFh |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says "explain Real Mode addressing," always structure your answer as: **(1) State the formula**, **(2) Show the derivation of the 20-bit address from two 16-bit components**, **(3) Provide at least 2–3 numerical examples with verification**, **(4) Mention the 1 MB wrap-around**, and **(5) Briefly note the default segment rules**. This template guarantees a 7/7 answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protected Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protected Mode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Protected Mode** is the native operational state of the **Intel 80386 (and later x86) microprocessor** in which the processor's full architectural capabilities are unlocked, including **32-bit addressing**, **virtual memory through paging**, **memory protection**, and **multitasking via privilege levels**.

In Protected Mode, every memory access is mediated by a set of **descriptor tables** and **protection checks** performed in hardware by the processor. The physical address is derived through a **two-level translation** process: first a **segmentation unit** converts a logical address (selector:offset) into a **linear address** using a segment descriptor, and then the optional **paging unit** translates the linear address into a **physical address** using page tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 80386 supports three operating modes — **Real Mode**, **Protected Mode**, and **Virtual 8086 Mode**. Of these, **Protected Mode is the high-yield topic** for ESE questions worth 14 marks. Pay special attention to descriptor formats, privilege levels, and address translation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation — The "Secure Apartment Building" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a luxury apartment building (the **CPU**) where residents (programs) want to access different rooms (memory):
>
> 1. **Real Address Mode Analogy** — A chaotic open ground where anyone can walk into any house, open any locker, and break anything. No security guard, no boundary, no accountability.
>
> 2. **Protected Mode Analogy** — A **gated community** with:
>    - A **reception desk with a security guard** (Descriptor Tables like **GDT/LDT**) holding a register of every resident's permitted areas.
>    - A **resident ID badge** (the **Selector**) that the guard verifies before granting access.
>    - **Floors 0 to 3** representing **Privilege Rings 0–3** — the lower the floor number, the higher the privilege (Ring 0 = OS kernel, Ring 3 = user applications).
>    - An **elevator system** (the **Paging Unit**) that remaps room numbers so two different residents can be told "Room 101" but actually occupy completely different physical rooms — this is **virtual memory**.
>    - A **boundary patrol** (the **Protection Mechanism**) that instantly halts any resident who tries to step out of their permitted area, triggering an exception.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

- **Logical Address Space (in Protected Mode)**: $2^{46}$ bytes = **64 Terabytes** (per task)
- **Linear Address Space**: $2^{32}$ bytes = **4 GB**
- **Physical Address Space (without PAE)**: $2^{32}$ bytes = **4 GB**
- **Page Size**: **4 KB** (standard) or **4 MB** (for huge pages)
- **Number of Privilege Levels**: **4** (Rings 0, 1, 2, 3)
- **Descriptor Table Types**: **3** (GDT, LDT, IDT)
- **Number of GDT Entries**: **8192 (2^13)** entries × 8 bytes = **64 KB**

---

### 1.4 Why Protected Mode? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Necessity of Protected Mode:**
> - Enables **multitasking operating systems** (Windows, Linux) to safely run multiple programs simultaneously.
> - Allows each program to "see" its own **4 GB virtual address space**, regardless of how much physical RAM is installed.
> - Enforces **hardware-level memory protection**, preventing one program from corrupting another's memory or crashing the OS.
> - Supports **virtual memory** by swapping pages between RAM and disk, enabling programs larger than physical memory.
> - Provides a **privilege hierarchy** that prevents malicious or buggy user code from executing privileged CPU instructions (like disabling interrupts or modifying page tables).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of Protected Mode Architecture

The Protected Mode architecture of the 80386 is composed of the following integrated subsystems:

#### **A. Segmentation Unit (Mandatory)**

- Converts **Logical Address** → **Linear Address**.
- Uses **Segment Selectors** and **Segment Descriptors**.
- Performs **limit checking** and **type checking** on every memory access.

#### **B. Paging Unit (Optional but virtually always enabled in modern OSes)**

- Converts **Linear Address** → **Physical Address**.
- Uses **Page Directories**, **Page Tables**, and **Page Table Entries (PTEs)**.
- Provides **virtual memory** support.

#### **C. Protection Subsystem**

- Enforces **4 privilege levels (Rings)**.
- Validates access rights (read/write/execute) for each segment.
- Performs **CPL, DPL, RPL** checks for every segment register load.

#### **D. Descriptor Tables**

- **GDT (Global Descriptor Table)** — system-wide, single instance.
- **LDT (Local Descriptor Table)** — per-task, multiple instances.
- **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table)** — handles interrupts and exceptions.

#### **E. Control Registers**

- **CR0, CR2, CR3, CR4** — control paging, protection enable, task switching.

---

### 2.2 The Logical-to-Linear-to-Physical Address Translation

In Protected Mode, the address translation occurs in two stages when paging is enabled:

$$
\text{Logical Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Segmentation Unit}} \text{Linear Address} \xrightarrow{\text{Paging Unit}} \text{Physical Address}
$$

When paging is **disabled**, the linear address is treated as the physical address directly.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Formula & Concept Table for Protected Mode**

| # | Concept | Formula / Format | Description |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Logical Address | $\text{Selector : Offset}$ | 16-bit selector + 32-bit offset |
| 2 | Selector Indexing | $\text{Descriptor Offset} = \text{Index} \times 8$ | Each descriptor is 8 bytes |
| 3 | Linear Address (Segmentation) | $\text{Linear} = \text{Base}_{seg} + \text{Offset}$ | Base from descriptor (32 bits) |
| 4 | Page Size | $2^{12} = \textbf{4 KB}$ | Standard paging granularity |
| 5 | Linear Address Split (Paging) | $10 + 10 + 12$ bits | Dir Index + Table Index + Offset |
| 6 | Physical Address (Paging) | $\text{Dir}[\text{PDI}] \rightarrow \text{Tbl}[\text{PTI}] + \text{Offset}$ | Two-level page walk |
| 7 | Huge Page Size | $2^{22} = \textbf{4 MB}$ | Single page directory entry |
| 8 | Page Directory Size | $2^{10} \times 4 = \textbf{4 KB}$ | Holds 1024 entries |
| 9 | Page Table Size | $2^{10} \times 4 = \textbf{4 KB}$ | Holds 1024 PTEs |
| 10 | GDT Limit | $\textbf{64 KB}$ (8192 entries × 8 bytes) | Maximum descriptor table size |
| 11 | Linear Address Space | $2^{32} = \textbf{4 GB}$ | Total linear addressable memory |
| 12 | Max Physical Memory (no PAE) | $2^{32} = \textbf{4 GB}$ | CR3 holds physical base of PD |
| 13 | Privilege Levels | $\textbf{4 (Rings 0-3)}$ | Ring 0 = most privileged |
| 14 | TSS Descriptor | Type = 0x9 / 0xB | Task State Segment for context switching |
| 15 | CR0 PE Bit | Bit 0 of CR0 | Must be 1 to enter Protected Mode |
| 16 | CR0 PG Bit | Bit 31 of CR0 | Enables paging |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial & Engineering Applications of Protected Mode Concepts:**
>
> - **Operating System Design** (Windows NT, Linux, macOS) — every modern OS boots into Protected Mode.
> - **Embedded Hypervisors** — VxWorks, QNX use privilege rings to isolate real-time tasks.
> - **Virtualization (Intel VT-x)** — extends Protected Mode with VMX root/non-root operation.
> - **Sandboxing & Security** — browser tabs run in separate address spaces via page-level protection.
> - **Process Isolation in Industrial Controllers** — PLCs running RTOS rely on ring-based privilege separation.

---

### 2.5 Segment Descriptor Format (8 Bytes / 64 Bits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Segment Descriptor** is the cornerstone data structure in Protected Mode. Every segment register is loaded by indexing into the GDT or LDT with an 8-byte descriptor containing the segment's base, limit, and access rights.

A segment descriptor is structured as follows (conceptual representation):

- **Bytes 0–1**: Limit (bits 0–15)
- **Bytes 2–3**: Base (bits 0–15)
- **Byte 4**: Base (bits 16–23)
- **Byte 5**: Access Rights Byte (Type, S, DPL, P)
- **Byte 6**: Flags + Limit (bits 16–19) (G, D/B, L, AVL, Limit high nibble)
- **Byte 7**: Base (bits 24–31)

Key bits in the **Access Rights Byte**:
- **P (Present)** — Bit 7 — must be 1 for valid descriptor.
- **DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level)** — Bits 6–5 — privilege required to access.
- **S (Descriptor Type)** — Bit 4 — 0 = system, 1 = code/data.
- **Type** — Bits 3–0 — indicates code/data/conforming/readable/writable.

Key bits in **Flags + Limit High**:
- **G (Granularity)** — Bit 7 — 0 = limit in bytes, 1 = limit in 4 KB pages.
- **D/B (Default operation size)** — Bit 6 — 0 = 16-bit, 1 = 32-bit segment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Address Construction from a Logical Address

Let us derive the **Linear Address** from a given **Logical Address** in Protected Mode using the segment descriptor.

#### **Step 1: Decompose the Selector**

A 16-bit **Segment Selector** has the following structure:

$$
\text{Selector} = \underbrace{b_{15}\ldots b_{3}}_{\text{Index (13 bits)}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{2}}_{\text{TI}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{1}b_{0}}_{\text{RPL (2 bits)}}
$$

Where:
- **Index** — selects one of $2^{13} = 8192$ descriptors.
- **TI (Table Indicator)** — 0 = GDT, 1 = LDT.
- **RPL (Requested Privilege Level)** — the privilege level the requester claims.

> [!NOTE]
> The processor multiplies the Index by 8 (size of one descriptor in bytes) to compute the offset within the GDT or LDT.

#### **Step 2: Fetch the Descriptor from GDT/LDT**

$$
\text{Descriptor Address} = \text{GDT Base} + (\text{Index} \times 8)
$$

The 8-byte descriptor is loaded and the **Base (32 bits)**, **Limit (20 bits)**, and **Access Rights** are extracted.

#### **Step 3: Add the Offset**

The 32-bit offset (from the instruction, register, or stack) is added to the descriptor's Base to form the Linear Address:

$$
\boxed{\text{Linear Address} = \text{Descriptor.Base}_{32} + \text{Offset}_{32}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Linear Address Calculation

**Problem:** A program in Protected Mode loads the selector `0x0020` into the DS register with an offset of `0x00001234`. The GDT base is at physical address `0x00001000`. The descriptor at index 0x4 (the one pointed to by `0x0020 / 8 = 0x4`) has a base of `0x00FF0000` and a limit of `0xFFFFF`.

> **Find the Linear Address.**

#### **Step 1: Decode the Selector**

$$
\text{Selector} = \texttt{0x0020} = \texttt{0b 0000 0000 0010 0000}
$$

- **Index** = bits [15:3] = `0x04` = 4
- **TI** = bit 2 = `0` → GDT
- **RPL** = bits [1:0] = `0` → Ring 0

#### **Step 2: Compute Descriptor Physical Address**

$$
\text{Descriptor Phys Addr} = \text{GDT Base} + (\text{Index} \times 8)
$$

$$
= \texttt{0x00001000} + (4 \times 8) = \texttt{0x00001000} + \texttt{0x20} = \texttt{0x00001020}
$$

#### **Step 3: Read Descriptor and Extract Base**

From the descriptor at `0x00001020`, we obtain:

$$
\text{Base} = \texttt{0x00FF0000}
$$

#### **Step 4: Add the Offset**

$$
\text{Linear Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}
$$

$$
= \texttt{0x00FF0000} + \texttt{0x00001234}
$$

$$
= \texttt{0x00FF1234}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Linear Address} = \texttt{0x00FF1234}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Linear-to-Physical Address Translation (Two-Level Paging)

When paging is enabled (CR0.PG = 1), the linear address is split into three fields:

$$
\text{Linear Address (32 bits)} = \underbrace{b_{31}\ldots b_{22}}_{\text{PDI (10 bits)}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{21}\ldots b_{12}}_{\text{PTI (10 bits)}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{11}\ldots b_{0}}_{\text{Offset (12 bits)}}
$$

#### **Step 1: Page Directory Lookup**

The **CR3 register** holds the physical base address of the **Page Directory**. The processor indexes into the page directory using the **PDI**:

$$
\text{Page Directory Entry (PDE) Address} = \text{CR3} + (\text{PDI} \times 4)
$$
