## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define paging. List any two advantages of paging over segmentation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Paging is a memory management technique that divides both the logical (linear) address space and physical memory into fixed-size blocks called **pages** (logical) and **page frames** (physical) of size **4 KB**. The address translation is performed by the MMU using a two-level page table structure indexed by the directory, table, and offset fields of the linear address.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **No external fragmentation** – Any free page frame can hold any page; pages need not be contiguous.
2. **Simple allocation / deallocation** – The OS only needs a free-frame bitmap; allocator runs in $O(1)$ amortized time.
3. **Easy to share** – Two processes can map the same page frame by using identical PTE contents (e.g., shared libraries).
4. **Easy to swap** – A single page is the natural unit for paging in/out of disk.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner's Pitfall: Students often confuse *paging* with *segmentation*. **Paging uses fixed-size blocks with a single linear address space**; segmentation uses variable-size logical segments. Mentioning "fixed 4 KB size" and "non-contiguous physical allocation" guarantees full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is Virtual 8086 mode? Why is it useful?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Virtual 8086 mode (V86 mode) is a sub-mode of protected mode in which the CPU behaves as if it is an 8086, providing a 20-bit (1 MB) segmented address space, 16-bit registers, and 8086-style instruction semantics. However, it runs **under the protection of paging and privilege checking**, so the host operating system can isolate multiple V86 tasks.

**Usefulness (3 marks — include at least two points):**
1. It allows **legacy DOS applications** to run concurrently inside a protected-mode multitasking OS (e.g., Windows 3.x, Windows 95, OS/2).
2. Each V86 task gets its **own isolated 1 MB virtual address space** mapped anywhere in the 4 GB physical memory via paging, preventing one buggy DOS program from crashing others.
3. The host OS can **trap and emulate** privileged I/O instructions via the IOPL field in EFLAGS, giving full I/O control.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Both alternatives below are fully solved.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – Paging Translation + Format**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **two-level paging mechanism** of the 80386 / Pentium with a neat diagram. Show how a 32-bit linear address is translated into a 32-bit physical address. State the role of the **CR3** register and the **TLB**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following, **translate the linear address `0x0080 1234`** to its physical address, and show the page-table entry (PTE) format with the correct bit settings. **(7 marks)**

- CR3 = `0x0005 0000`
- PDE at index 0x020 = `0x0007 8007`
- PTE at index 0x012 = `0x0000 4015`

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Level Paging Explanation (7 marks):**

In the 80386 and Pentium, paging is **enabled by setting bit 31 (PG) of CR0**. The 32-bit linear address is partitioned into three fields:

- **Directory** (bits 31–22) — 10 bits, indexes into the **Page Directory**.
- **Table** (bits 21–12) — 10 bits, indexes into the chosen **Page Table**.
- **Offset** (bits 11–0) — 12 bits, byte offset within the 4 KB page.

**Step-by-step translation:**

1. The CPU reads the **base address of the Page Directory from CR3** (also called PDBR). CR3 is 4 KB aligned; only the upper 20 bits are used.
2. The Directory field × 4 is added to CR3 to locate the **Page Directory Entry (PDE)**.
3. The PDE contains the physical base of a **Page Table** along with protection and status bits (P, R/W, U/S, A).
4. The Table field × 4 is added to the Page Table base to locate the **Page Table Entry (PTE)**.
5. The PTE contains the physical **page-frame base** plus protection and status bits.
6. The 12-bit offset from the linear address is concatenated to the upper 20 bits of the PTE to produce the **32-bit physical address**.

**TLB Role (1 mark):** The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a fully associative on-chip cache of the most recently used PTEs. A TLB hit completes the translation in 1 cycle; a miss triggers the full two-level walk.

**Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 — "Paging Translation Walk" flowchart.)

---

**(b) Numerical Translation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – Split the linear address `0x00801234`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LA &= 0x0080\,1234 \\
LA_{[31{:}22]} &= 0x020 \quad \text{(Directory index)} \\
LA_{[21{:}12]} &= 0x012 \quad \text{(Table index)} \\
LA_{[11{:}0]} &= 0x234 \quad \text{(Offset, unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Read the PDE:**

PDE address = CR3 + Dir × 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PDE\_addr &= 0x0005\,0000 + (0x020 \times 4) \\
&= 0x0005\,0080
\end{aligned}
$$

PDE = `0x00078007`. Decoding:
- Bit 0 (P) = **1** → page table is present.
- Bit 1 (R/W) = **1** → read/write allowed.
- Bit 2 (U/S) = **1** → user accessible.
- Bits 12–31 (PT base) = `0x00078000`.

**Step 3 – Read the PTE:**

PTE address = PT_base + Tab × 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PTE\_addr &= 0x0007\,8000 + (0x012 \times 4) \\
&= 0x0007\,8000 + 0x48 \\
&= 0x0007\,8048
\end{aligned}
$$

PTE = `0x00004015`. Decoding:
- Bit 0 (P) = **1** → physical page is present.
- Bit 1 (R/W) = **0** → read-only.
- Bit 2 (U/S) = **1** → user accessible.
- Bit 4 (PCD) = **1** → cache disabled for this page.
- Bit 5 (A) = **1** → page has been accessed.
- Bits 12–31 (Frame) = `0x00004000`.

**Step 4 – Form the physical address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= \text{Frame} \;\vert\vert\; \text{Offset} \\
&= 0x0000\,4000 \;\vert\vert\; 0x234 \\
&= 0x0000\,4234
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Physical Address: `0x00004234`.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):**
> 1. Many students forget to **mask the lower 12 bits of the PDE/PTE** (`& 0xFFFFF000`) before using the address as a base. Losing 1 mark here is common.
> 2. The **offset bits are appended unchanged** — do not modify them or carry from the offset into the frame.
> 3. Always state the **role of CR3** explicitly; examiners allocate 1 mark for naming CR3 as the Page Directory Base Register.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – V86 Mode + Pentium Features**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of **Virtual 8086 mode**. How does paging help the host OS to isolate multiple V86 tasks? Discuss the role of the **IOPL** field and the **TSS**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **eight major architectural features** of the **Intel Pentium processor** that distinguish it from the 80486. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Virtual 8086 Mode (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Virtual 8086 (V86) mode is a sub-mode of protected mode in which the processor emulates the 8086 environment (1 MB address space, 16-bit registers, segment:offset addressing) while still providing the safety of paging and privilege checking.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- The OS creates a **TSS (Task State Segment)** with the **EFLAGS.VM bit = 1** for each V86 task.
- The CPU enters V86 mode on task switch, interprets the segment registers with the 8086 rule $LA = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{Off}$ and clips the result to 20 bits.
- Paging is **enabled**, so each V86 task's 1 MB space can be mapped anywhere in the 4 GB physical memory.

**Isolation by Paging (1 mark):** Because the page tables of each V86 task are separate, two V86 tasks can have completely different physical memory backing for the same linear address `0x0040:0x0100`. A write by one task cannot affect the other — a hardware-enforced sandbox.

**IOPL and Trap Handling (2 marks):**
- The **IOPL** field in EFLAGS controls whether V86 code can directly execute I/O instructions (`IN`, `OUT`, `INS`, `OUTS`, `CLI`, `STI`).
- If IOPL < 3 and the V86 code attempts these, a **General Protection Fault (#GP, vector 13)** is raised, transferring control to the host OS's V86 monitor.
- The monitor emulates the instruction, performs the I/O on behalf of the task, and resumes it via `IRET`. The TSS holds the task's saved register state, allowing safe preemption and resumption.

---

**(b) Eight Major Features of the Pentium vs. 80486 (7 marks):**

1. **Superscalar Dual-Pipeline Architecture (1 mark):** Two independent integer pipelines (U and V) allow the Pentium to issue up to **two instructions per clock cycle** versus one for the 486.

2. **64-bit External Data Bus (1 mark):** The data bus is **doubled** from 32 bits (486) to 64 bits, doubling peak memory bandwidth to 528 MB/s at 66 MHz.

3. **Separate L1 Caches (1 mark):** The unified 8 KB on-chip cache of the 486 is replaced by **split 8 KB instruction cache + 8 KB data cache**, eliminating the fetch/execute cache contention of the 486.

4. **Pipelined Floating-Point Unit (1 mark):** The FPU uses an 8-stage pipeline (vs. the non-pipelined FPU in the 486DX), giving up to **5× faster floating-point** performance.

5. **Dynamic Branch Prediction (1 mark):** A 256-entry **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** predicts branch outcomes with a confidence counter, drastically reducing pipeline flushes.

6. **Dual TLB (1 mark):** Separate Instruction TLB (32 entries) and Data TLB (64 entries) reduce translation contention.

7. **Write Buffers and ECC Support (1 mark):** The Pentium includes a write-buffer and optional **parity/ECC** support on the external data bus for higher reliability in server applications.

8. **Higher Clock Speeds and Improved Process (1 mark):** Launched at 60/66 MHz in 0.8 µm BiCMOS, scaling rapidly to 200 MHz in 0.35 µm — far beyond the 50 MHz ceiling of the 486DX2/50.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B, V86 + Pentium):**
> 1. Do **not** confuse IOPL with CPL — IOPL is a flag in EFLAGS, CPL is in CS. State both clearly.
> 2. For the Pentium features, naming *"dual pipeline"* and *"64-bit bus"* alone is worth only ~3 marks. To score 7, you must explain **how** each feature improves performance (e.g., branch prediction reduces misprediction penalty from ~5 cycles to ~1).
> 3. Do not write that the Pentium has 64-bit internal registers; its **internal architecture is 32-bit**, with a 64-bit **external** data path.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this before exams):**

- **Paging unit** divides the 32-bit linear address into **Directory (10) + Table (10) + Offset (12)** bits, giving **4 KB pages**.
- **Page Directory** is located by **CR3** (PDBR) and contains **1024 PDEs** (4 KB total).
- Each **PDE** points to a **Page Table** of **1024 PTEs** (4 KB each); the **PTE** points to the **physical page frame** (4 KB).
- The 12-bit **offset is preserved unchanged** during translation; only the upper 20 bits (page frame) are substituted.
- **TLB** is the on-chip cache of PTEs; an **ITLB + DTLB** design exists in the Pentium.
- A page fault (#PF, vector **14**) occurs when the **P bit = 0** in the relevant PDE or PTE.
- **Virtual 8086 (V86) mode** is a sub-mode of protected mode; it emulates 8086 inside a paged, protected environment.
- V86 mode is entered via the **TSS** (with EFLAGS.VM = 1); privileged I/O is trapped by **IOPL** → **#GP (vector 13)** → V86 monitor.
- V86 mode enables **multiple isolated DOS sessions** under a 32-bit OS (Windows 3.x, Windows 95).
- **Pentium key differentiators vs. 486:** **superscalar dual pipelines (U + V)**, **64-bit external data bus**, **separate 8 KB I-cache + 8 KB D-cache**, **pipelined FPU**, **dynamic branch prediction with 256-entry BTB**, **split TLB**, **higher clock speeds**, **BiCMOS process**.
- **Paging memory overhead** for a fully populated 4 GB address space is at most ~4 MB.
- **Effective access time** with TLB hit ratio $h$: $\;t_{eff} = t_{TLB} + t_M\,(3 - 2h)$.
- **PDE/PTE key bits to memorize:** P (bit 0), R/W (bit 1), U/S (bit 2), A (bit 5), D (bit 6 of PTE), base address (bits 12–31).
- **Translation never modifies the offset** — shared libraries, copy-on-write forks, and demand paging all rely on this invariant.

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Pipelining

### Formal Definition
**Instruction Pipelining** is a technique of decomposing a sequential instruction execution process into a series of independent, overlapping sub-operations (called **stages** or **segments**), such that multiple instructions can be processed concurrently — each at a different stage of completion — within a single processor.

The classic RISC pipeline decomposes instruction execution into **5 stages**:
1. **IF** — Instruction Fetch
2. **ID** — Instruction Decode / Register Fetch
3. **EX** — Execute / Effective Address Calculation
4. **MEM** — Memory Access
5. **WB** — Write Back

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pipelining is fundamentally a *temporal parallelism* technique — it does NOT replicate hardware, but reuses the same hardware across multiple clock cycles for different instructions. This distinguishes it from **multicore**, which is a *spatial parallelism* technique.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Assembly Line
Imagine a laundromat with 4 washing machines and **1 person** performing 4 sequential tasks per shirt: **Wash → Dry → Iron → Fold**.

- **Non-pipelined:** The person washes all shirts, then dries all, then irons all, then folds all. Total time is huge.
- **Pipelined:** The person starts washing Shirt 2 the moment Shirt 1 finishes washing. At any moment, each of the 4 stages has a different shirt being processed. Once the pipeline is "full," one shirt emerges *every clock tick*.

This is exactly how a CPU works — multiple instructions enter the pipeline, and at steady state, one instruction completes per cycle. The **latency** of a single instruction is still 5 cycles, but the **throughput** becomes 1 instruction/cycle.

---

## 1.2 Super Scalar Architecture

### Formal Definition
A **Superscalar Architecture** is a processor design that contains **multiple redundant execution units** (e.g., 2 ALUs, 2 FPUs, 2 load/store units) and can therefore issue **more than one instruction per clock cycle** from a single instruction stream, dispatching them to the appropriate execution unit in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** A *pipelined* processor issues **1 instruction per cycle** (CPI = 1). A *superscalar* processor issues **n instructions per cycle** (CPI < 1, ideally CPI = 1/n). Superscalar is therefore a *super-set* of pipelining.

### Conceptual Analogy — Multi-Checkout Supermarket
A single cashier (pipelined CPU) processes one customer at a time but performs the steps (scan → bag → pay) in overlapping fashion. A supermarket with **3 cashiers (superscalar CPU)** can serve 3 customers simultaneously. Both are pipelined; the supermarket is *additionally* superscalar.

---

## 1.3 Multi-Core Processing

### Formal Definition
**Multi-Core Processing** refers to the placement of **two or more independent processing cores** (CPUs) on a single physical **die** (silicon chip). The cores share certain resources (cache hierarchy, memory controller, I/O) but each has its own private registers, ALU, and at least L1 cache.

### Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous Multi-Core
| Feature | Homogeneous Multi-Core | Heterogeneous Multi-Core |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Type** | All cores are **identical** in ISA, performance, and power | Cores are **different** — e.g., big performance cores + small efficiency cores |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric Multi-Processing (**SMP**) | Asymmetric / **big.LITTLE**, Arm big.LITTLE, Intel P-core + E-core |
| **Power Strategy** | Uniform power across all cores | **Performance per Watt** optimized |
| **Examples** | Intel Core 2 Duo, AMD Phenom II X4 | Intel 12th Gen (Alder Lake), Snapdragon 8 Gen SoCs |
| **Workload Allocation** | OS scheduler treats cores equally | Scheduler pins latency-critical tasks to big cores, background tasks to small cores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Intel Core 2 Duo is a **homogeneous** dual-core processor. Modern Intel Core i-series (12th gen onwards) are **heterogeneous** with P-cores and E-cores.

---

## 1.4 Intel Core 2 Duo — Block-Level Definition
The **Intel Core 2 Duo** (codenamed Conroe/Merom, 2006) is a 64-bit **homogeneous dual-core** processor based on the **Intel Core microarchitecture**. Both cores share an **L2 cache** and connect to the system via a **Front Side Bus (FSB)** to the **Northbridge/MCH**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Exam Note:** Core 2 Duo does **NOT** have an integrated memory controller on-die (that came later with Nehalem). The memory controller is in the **MCH (Memory Controller Hub)** chipset.

---

## 1.5 IA (Intel Architecture) Processors — Technological Features
The **IA** family refers to the lineage of Intel processors following the **IA-32** (32-bit) and **IA-64** (Itanium) ISAs and their evolution to **x86-64**. KTU's PEEBT522 module-2 expects knowledge of technological milestones:

- **Clock Speeds** from **400 MHz (Pentium II)** to multi-GHz modern Core i9
- **Process Node** shrinking: 90 nm → 65 nm → 45 nm → 32 nm → 14 nm → 10 nm → 7 nm
- **Cache hierarchies** L1, L2, L3
- **Hyper-Threading (SMT)** — single core presents 2 logical threads
- **Turbo Boost** — dynamic frequency scaling
- **Integrated GPU** and **System-on-Chip** integration
- **SIMD extensions** — MMX → SSE → SSE2 → SSE3 → SSE4 → **AVX / AVX-512**

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pipelining — The Structural Decomposition

A **k-stage pipeline** processes instructions through k sequential stages. In a clock cycle of period **T**, each stage performs its sub-task in T units of time.

### 2.1.1 Key Parameters

- **Cycle Time (T_clk)**: Time for one stage to complete = max(stage delay) + register overhead
- **Pipeline Depth (k)**: Number of stages
- **Pipeline Latency**: Time for a single instruction to traverse the pipeline = k × T_clk
- **Pipeline Throughput**: At steady state = 1 instruction per T_clk
- **Speedup (S)**: For n instructions, ideal speedup = k, but in practice:

$$
S = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + (n-1)} = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}
$$

As **n → ∞**, S → **k** (the theoretical maximum speedup equals the number of stages).

### 2.1.2 The Three Classical Pipeline Hazards
> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three hazard types are **guaranteed exam content** for KTU Module 2.

1. **Structural Hazard** — Hardware cannot support all combinations of instructions in the pipeline at one time.
   - *Example:* Single memory port serving both IF and MEM stages.
   - *Solution:* Duplicate hardware (e.g., separate I-cache and D-cache — *Harvard architecture*).

2. **Data Hazard** — An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline.
   - **RAW (Read After Write)** — true dependency, most common.
   - **WAR (Write After Read)** — anti-dependency, arises from out-of-order execution.
   - **WAW (Write After Write)** — output dependency.
   - *Solution:* **Data forwarding (bypassing)** — route the EX/MEM result directly back to the ALU input.

3. **Control Hazard** — Caused by **branch** and **jump** instructions; the next PC is unknown until the branch is resolved.
   - *Solution:* **Branch prediction**, **delayed branch**, **branch target buffer (BTB)**.

### 2.1.3 Pipeline Performance Metrics

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{pipe}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + \text{Hazard stalls per instruction}
$$

$$
\text{Execution Time} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

where **IC** is the **Instruction Count**.

---

## 2.2 Super Scalar Architecture — Deep Analysis

A superscalar processor has:
- **Multiple fetch units** (or wide fetch — fetch 2/4/8 instructions per cycle)
- **Multiple decode units**
- **Multiple functional units** (ALU1, ALU2, FPU, LSU, Branch Unit)
- **Reservation stations** + **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** for out-of-order execution
- **Register renaming** to eliminate false dependencies (WAR, WAW)

### 2.2.1 Issue Width and IPC
- **Issue Width (n)**: Max instructions issued per cycle.
- **IPC** (Instructions Per Cycle) = n at theoretical peak.
- Real IPC is limited by: data dependencies, cache misses, branch mispredictions.

### 2.2.2 In-Order vs Out-of-Order Superscalar

| Type | In-Order Superscalar | Out-of-Order Superscalar |
|---|---|---|
| Issue | Strict program order | Can re-order to hide latencies |
| Complexity | Lower | Very high (ROB, renaming) |
| Example | Early Intel Pentium | Intel Core 2, Core i7, AMD Zen |

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Pipeline Speedup | $S_{\text{ideal}} = k$ (number of stages) | Upper bound on pipelining gain |
| 2 | Practical Speedup (n instructions, k stages) | $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | When $n$ is finite |
| 3 | Throughput (steady state) | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}}$ | Instructions completed per second |
| 4 | Latency per instruction | $L = k \cdot T_{\text{clk}}$ | Time from fetch to write-back |
| 5 | Amdahl's Law (parallel fraction $p$, cores $N$) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | Max speedup with N cores |
| 6 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{\text{exec}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | KTU base performance equation |
| 7 | Effective CPI (with stalls) | $CPI_{\text{eff}} = CPI_{\text{base}} + \text{Stall cycles per instr}$ | Hazard penalty included |
| 8 | Superscalar IPC | $IPC_{\text{peak}} = n$ (issue width) | Max instr/cycle |
| 9 | Branch Penalty (misprediction) | $Penalty = depth \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Cost of wrong branch prediction |
| 10 | Multi-core Aggregate Compute | $P_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{cores}} \times P_{\text{per-core}}$ | Power/performance scaling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-`|` Protocol:** All vertical separators in formulas above use `\dfrac` (fraction) or display math. Never use raw `\|x\|` inside markdown table rows — it breaks rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphones (big.LITTLE SoCs):** Heterogeneous cores balance battery life with burst performance.
- **Data Centers (Cloud Servers):** Homogeneous many-core CPUs (e.g., 64-core EPYC) handle homogeneous workloads.
- **Embedded DSP/Automotive (RISC-V multi-core):** Deterministic real-time cores + application cores.
- **AI Accelerators:** Multi-core CPUs coordinate with GPUs/VPUs for parallel matrix math.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Practical Pipeline Speedup

**Problem Statement:** Derive the speedup equation for a k-stage pipeline executing n instructions.

### Step 1: Time Without Pipelining
In a non-pipelined processor, each instruction takes **k clock cycles** (one per stage, executed sequentially).

$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = n \times k \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

### Step 2: Time With Pipelining
In a pipeline:
- The first instruction takes **k cycles** to complete (pipeline fill).
- Each of the remaining **(n − 1)** instructions takes **1 additional cycle** each.

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = \left[k + (n - 1)\right] \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

### Step 3: Form the Speedup Ratio
By definition, speedup is the ratio of non-pipelined time to pipelined time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-pipe}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{\text{clk}}}{\left[k + (n-1)\right] \cdot T_{\text{clk}}} \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} \\[6pt]
S &= \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Limiting Case
As $n \to \infty$, the "+ k − 1" becomes negligible compared to "n", so:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1} = k
$$

This proves that the **theoretical maximum speedup of a k-stage pipeline is k**, achieved only with infinite instructions and zero hazards.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law Applied to Multi-Core Speedup

**Problem Statement:** A workload has 80% parallelizable portion and 20% sequential. Find the speedup with 4, 8, and 16 cores.

### Step 1: Identify the Variables
- Parallel fraction: $p = 0.80$
- Sequential fraction: $(1 - p) = 0.20$
- N (number of cores) = 4, 8, 16

### Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation

**For N = 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.5 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

**For N = 8:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} \\[6pt]
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} \\[6pt]
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

**For N = 16:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{16}} \\[6pt]
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} \\[6pt]
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.0 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Limiting Case (Infinite Cores)

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5 \times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Even with *infinitely many cores*, the speedup **cannot exceed 5×** because of the 20% sequential portion. This is why Amdahl's law is the *hard ceiling* of parallel processing. This is a classic KTU question trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CPI with Pipeline Stalls

**Problem Statement:** A 5-stage pipeline (CPI_ideal = 1) has the following stall rates: 30% branch instructions with 2-cycle penalty, 20% load instructions with 1-cycle penalty (when followed by dependent use). Compute effective CPI.

### Step 1: Compute the Stalls per Instruction
- Branch stall rate: $0.30 \times 2 = 0.60$ cycles
- Load-use stall rate: $0.20 \times 1 = 0.20$ cycles

### Step 2: Sum the Penalties

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{\text{eff}} &= CPI_{\text{ideal}} + \text{Total Stalls} \\[6pt]
CPI_{\text{eff}} &= 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.20 \\[6pt]
CPI_{\text{eff}} &= 1.80
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Performance Penalty
The pipeline achieves only $\dfrac{1.00}{1.80} \approx 55.6\%$ of its ideal throughput.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pipelining vs. Superscalar vs. Multi-Core

| Parameter | Pipelining | Superscalar | Multi-Core |
|---|---|---|---|
| Parallelism Type | Temporal (overlap time) | Spatial (replicate units) | Spatial (replicate cores) |
| Hardware Duplication | No (reuse stages) | Yes (multiple FUs) | Yes (entire cores) |
| Peak CPI | 1 | < 1 (e.g., 0.5) | << 1 (per program) |
| Issue Width | 1 instr/cycle | n instr/cycle (n > 1) | n × m (n cores, m-wide each) |
| Major Bottleneck | Hazards | Dependencies + complexity | Memory bandwidth / cache coherence |
| KTU 2024 Example | RISC pipeline | Intel Pentium III+ | Intel Core 2 Duo, Core i7 |

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem — Pipeline Throughput

**Problem:** A 6-stage pipeline runs at 2 GHz. What is the steady-state throughput in MIPS?

### Step 1: Steady-state IPC
In steady state, one instruction completes per cycle:

$$
IPC_{\text{steady}} = 1 \text{ instruction/cycle}
$$

### Step 2: Convert Clock Frequency to Instructions/Second

$$
\text{Throughput} = 1 \times 2 \times 10^9 = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ instr/sec}
$$

### Step 3: Convert to MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)

$$
\text{Throughput in MIPS} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 2000 \text{ MIPS} = 2 \text{ GMIPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **peak** throughput. Real-world MIPS will be lower due to hazards and cache misses.

This diagram illustrates the temporal flow of instructions through a 5-stage pipeline. Each row is a clock cycle; each column is a pipeline stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["C1: IF"] --> A2["C2: ID"] --> A3["C3: EX"] --> A4["C4: MEM"] --> A5["C5: WB"]
    B1["C2: IF"] --> B2["C3: ID"] --> B3["C4: EX"] --> B4["C5: MEM"] --> B5["C6: WB"]
    C1["C3: IF"] --> C2["C4: ID"] --> C3["C5: EX"] --> C4["C6: MEM"] --> C5["C7: WB"]
    D1["C4: IF"] --> D2["C5: ID"] --> D3["C6: EX"] --> D4["C7: MEM"] --> D5["C8: WB"]
    E1["C5: IF"] --> E2["C6: ID"] --> E3["C7: EX"] --> E4["C8: MEM"] --> E5["C9: WB"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- At **C5**, the pipeline is **full** — I1 is in WB, I2 in MEM, I3 in EX, I4 in ID, I5 in IF.
- At every subsequent cycle, one instruction exits (WB) and one enters (IF).

**Key Architectural Points:**
