# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Note:** Module 2 is typically tested with 1 full 14-mark question (with internal choice) and possibly a 3-mark question from Part A. Expect 7+7 sub-part division.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define instruction pipelining. List the three types of pipeline hazards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Instruction pipelining is an implementation technique in which multiple instructions are overlapped in execution by partitioning the instruction processing into a series of independent stages, each stage performing a portion of the instruction's work in a single clock cycle.

**Three Hazards (1.5 marks):**
1. **Structural Hazard** — Hardware resource conflict between instructions in different stages.
2. **Data Hazard** — Instruction depends on the result of an in-flight instruction (RAW, WAR, WAW).
3. **Control Hazard** — Caused by branch/jump instructions where the next PC is not known immediately.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous multi-core processors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Core Types | All cores identical | Mix of high-perf and low-power cores |
| Workload | OS treats cores equally | Scheduler assigns based on need |
| Example | Intel Core 2 Duo, AMD Phenom II X4 | Intel 12th Gen (P-core + E-core), Arm big.LITTLE |
| Power | Uniform | Optimized per workload |

Homogeneous cores simplify programming and OS scheduling; heterogeneous cores optimize performance-per-watt by matching core capability to task intensity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Q3. QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Pipeline Speedup, Hazards, and Data Forwarding

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Consider a 5-stage pipeline with the following instruction mix:
- ALU instructions: 40%
- Load instructions: 30%
- Store instructions: 10%
- Branch instructions: 20%

Assume:
- ALU instructions require **0 stalls** in steady state (due to forwarding).
- Load instructions cause a **1-cycle stall** if immediately followed by a dependent ALU instruction (probability 40%).
- Branch instructions cause a **2-cycle stall** (no branch prediction).

Compute:
1. The effective CPI.
2. The speedup compared to a non-pipelined processor executing **100 instructions** at the same clock rate.
3. The MIPS rating if the clock frequency is **2 GHz**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the three types of data hazards (RAW, WAR, WAW) using a code example. Show how **data forwarding (bypassing)** resolves the RAW hazard with a timing diagram.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute per-instruction-class stall cycles:**

ALU: $0.40 \times 0 = 0.00$ cycles
Load: $0.30 \times 0.40 \times 1 = 0.12$ cycles
Store: $0.10 \times 0 = 0.00$ cycles
Branch: $0.20 \times 2 = 0.40$ cycles

**Step 2 — Total stall cycles per instruction (averaged):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stalls} &= 0.00 + 0.12 + 0.00 + 0.40 \\[4pt]
\text{Stalls} &= 0.52 \text{ cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{\text{eff}} &= CPI_{\text{ideal}} + \text{Stalls} \\[4pt]
CPI_{\text{eff}} &= 1.00 + 0.52 = 1.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup for n = 100 instructions, k = 5 stages:**

Non-pipelined time per instruction = 5 cycles, so for 100 instructions:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipe}} = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ cycles}
$$

Pipelined time:

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + n - 1) + \text{stall cycles} = (5 + 99) + (0.52 \times 100) = 104 + 52 = 156 \text{ cycles}
$$

Wait — strictly, stalls are inserted *between* instructions, increasing total cycle count beyond the ideal $k + n - 1$:

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (5 + 99) + 52 = 156 \text{ cycles}
$$

Speedup:

$$
S = \frac{500}{156} \approx 3.21 \times
$$

**Step 5 — MIPS at 2 GHz:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f}{CPI_{\text{eff}} \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{2 \times 10^9}{1.52 \times 10^6} \approx 1315.79 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Data Hazards Definition (3 marks):**

Consider the code sequence:
```
I1: ADD R1, R2, R3    ; R1 = R2 + R3
I2: SUB R4, R1, R5    ; uses R1 written by I1
I3: MUL R6, R1, R7    ; uses R1 written by I1
```

- **RAW (Read After Write) — True Dependency:** I2 reads R1, I1 writes R1. I2 must wait for I1.
- **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependency:** I3 writes R6, I1 reads R6. In *out-of-order* execution, I3 could complete before I1 reads R6, breaking the program. Solved via **register renaming**.
- **WAW (Write After Write) — Output Dependency:** Both write to R1; in OoO execution, the later write must occur last.

**Data Forwarding Diagram (4 marks):**

```
Cycle:    1    2    3    4    5    6
I1: ADD   IF   ID   EX   MEM  WB
I2: SUB        IF   ID   EX*  MEM  WB
                  ↑
                  └── Forwarded R1 from EX/MEM of I1 to EX of I2
```

The **\* (asterisk)** at I2's EX stage marks the **forwarding path** — the ALU result of I1 is fed directly into I2's ALU, eliminating the 2-cycle bubble that would otherwise be needed.

---

### Q3. QUESTION B (14 Marks, ALTERNATIVE) — Multi-Core Architecture and Amdahl's Law

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **concept of multi-core processing**. Compare **homogeneous and heterogeneous** multi-core processors with two key differences. Draw a labeled block diagram of the **Intel Core 2 Duo** internal architecture showing both cores, shared L2 cache, and the FSB interface to the chipset.

**(b) [7 marks]** A program has 30% sequential code and 70% parallel code.
1. Compute the maximum speedup achievable with **2, 4, and 8 cores** using Amdahl's Law.
2. What is the theoretical maximum speedup with infinite cores?
3. If the original program runs in **100 seconds** on a single core, compute the execution time with 4 cores.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Concept (2 marks):**
**Multi-core processing** integrates two or more independent processor cores (each with its own fetch, decode, and execution units) on a single silicon die. The cores share certain resources (cache, memory bus) but operate as independent CPUs. This provides **chip-level multiprocessing (CMP)** without the cost of multiple discrete CPUs.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
|---|---|---|
| Core types | All identical | Mix of high-perf and low-power |
| Power efficiency | Suboptimal | Optimal (big.LITTLE) |
| Software complexity | Low | High (scheduler must be aware) |

**Intel Core 2 Duo Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The diagram should show:
- **Two identical cores**, each with private L1 I-cache and L1 D-cache (32 KB each).
- Both cores connected to a **shared unified L2 cache** (2-4 MB).
- The L2 cache connects to the **Front Side Bus (FSB)**.
- The FSB exits the chip to the external **MCH/Northbridge** which holds the memory controller and main memory interface.
- Each core is a **14-stage pipelined superscalar** out-of-order execution engine.

> Refer to **Diagram C in SECTION_4.3** for the exact Mermaid block diagram.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

with $p = 0.70$, $1 - p = 0.30$.

**Speedup with N = 2:**

$$
S(2) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.35} = \frac{1}{0.65} \approx 1.538
$$

**Speedup with N = 4:**

$$
S(4) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.175} = \frac{1}{0.475} \approx 2.105
$$

**Speedup with N = 8:**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30 + \dfrac{0.70}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.0875} = \frac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.581
$$

**Theoretical maximum (N → ∞):**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333
$$

**Execution Time with 4 cores (T_single = 100 s):**

$$
T(4) = \frac{T_{\text{single}}}{S(4)} = \frac{100}{2.105} \approx 47.51 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Pipelining/Multi-Core Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "latency" with "throughput."** Latency is k cycles; throughput is 1 inst/cycle. Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting the +k−1 term** in pipeline speedup. The formula is $\dfrac{nk}{n+k-1}$, **not** $\dfrac{nk}{n}$.
> 3. **Applying Amdahl's Law to a fraction that is 0%.** If $p = 0$, speedup = 1 (no gain). If $p = 1$, speedup = N (perfect linear scaling).
> 4. **Confusing WAR/WAW as "false" hazards.** They are *name dependencies* that can be eliminated by **register renaming**, not by forwarding.
> 5. **Claiming Core 2 Duo has an integrated memory controller.** It does **not** — the memory controller was external in the MCH until Nehalem (2008).
> 6. **Drawing Core 2 Duo with separate L2 caches per core.** That was the *Pentium D* (Smithfield) — Core 2 Duo introduced **shared** L2.
> 7. **Forgetting Amdahl's infinite-core limit** equals $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$. Many students compute only finite N cases.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Pipelining** = temporal overlap of instruction stages; standard 5-stage (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB).
- [x] **Pipeline Speedup** = $\dfrac{nk}{n+k-1}$, theoretical max = $k$.
- [x] **Three Hazards:** Structural, Data (RAW/WAR/WAW), Control (branches).
- [x] **Data Forwarding** resolves RAW; **Register Renaming** resolves WAR/WAW; **Branch Prediction** resolves Control.
- [x] **Superscalar** = multiple execution units, issues >1 inst/cycle. IPC = 1/n.
- [x] **Multi-core** = multiple independent cores on a single die.
- [x] **Homogeneous Multi-core** = all identical cores; **Heterogeneous** = mix of big + little cores.
- [x] **Intel Core 2 Duo** = 2 identical cores, **shared L2 cache**, **no on-die memory controller**, FSB to external MCH.
- [x] **IA Processors' features:** shrinking process nodes, growing cache, SMT (Hyper-Threading), Turbo Boost, SIMD (SSE → AVX).
- [x] **Amdahl's Law** = $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$; **limit** = $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
- [x] **Major Multi-core Issues:** Cache coherence (MESI), memory bandwidth, OS scheduling, Amdahl's bottleneck, power/thermal density.
- [x] **CPI formula** = $IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{clk}}$ — apply stalls via effective CPI.
- [x] **MIPS** = $\dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ at frequency $f$ in Hz.
- [x] **Pipeline is NOT a cure-all:** deeper pipelines → more hazards, more mispredictions, longer branch penalty.
- [x] **Superscalar vs Multi-core:** Superscalar duplicates *units* inside one core; Multi-core duplicates *whole cores*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Processor Fundamentals - ARM Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM Processor Fundamentals - ARM Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Architecture** stands for **Advanced RISC Machine** (originally **Acorn RISC Machine**). It is a family of **32-bit / 64-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor architectures widely used in embedded systems, mobile devices, IoT, and real-time control applications. ARM is *not a processor itself* but a *processor architecture* — it is licensed by **Arm Holdings (UK)** to hundreds of semiconductor vendors (e.g., NXP, STMicroelectronics, Texas Instruments, Samsung, Qualcomm) who integrate ARM cores into custom SoCs.

ARM belongs to the **Load–Store RISC** class, which means:
- All arithmetic/logic operations operate **only on registers** (never directly on memory).
- Memory access is permitted **only through explicit Load (LDR) and Store (STR)** instructions.
- The instruction set is **regular, fixed-length (32 bits in ARM state, 16 bits in Thumb state)**, and **orthogonal** — any operation can be performed on any register.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Timeline (Mandatory for KTU 2-Mark Questions):**
> - **1985** — First ARM1 chip designed by Sophie Wilson & Steve Furber at Acorn Computers, UK.
> - **1990** — ARM Ltd. (Advanced RISC Machines) spun off as a joint venture (Acorn, Apple, VLSI).
> - **1998** — ARM Holdings listed on NASDAQ and LSE.
> - **2008** — Introduction of the **Cortex** family (Cortex-A, Cortex-R, Cortex-M).
> - **2011** — Introduction of the **64-bit ARMv8-A** architecture (AArch64).

## 1.2 The ARM Family / Cortex Series (KTU High-Yield)

ARM groups its cores into three **performance/cost profiles**:

| Cortex Series | Target Application                  | Typical Examples                  |
|---------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Cortex-A**  | High-performance Application cores   | Smartphones, Tablets, Servers     |
| **Cortex-R**  | Real-time, high-availability cores  | Automotive brakes, 5G base stations |
| **Cortex-M**  | Microcontroller-class, ultra-low-power | IoT nodes, motor control, sensors |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU's *Advanced Microprocessors & Microcontrollers* (PEEBT522) — pay extra attention to the **Cortex-M** family (e.g., **Cortex-M3, M4, M7**), as they are the most common in NXP LPC / STM32 boards used in Kerala engineering labs.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Specialist Factory Worker"

Imagine a **factory assembly line** with strict rules:
- A specialist worker (the **ALU**) **only works on parts sitting on his personal workbench** (the **registers**). He never walks to the warehouse (memory) himself.
- If a part from the warehouse is needed, the worker sends a **forklift request** — this is a **Load instruction (LDR)**.
- To put a finished part back, another **forklift request** is sent — this is a **Store instruction (STR)**.
- Every instruction takes the worker **exactly the same fixed amount of time** (fixed-cycle), and the instruction word always occupies the **same width on the instruction sheet** (fixed-length encoding).

This is exactly the **RISC philosophy** of ARM — *simplicity, regularity, and pipelinability*.

## 1.4 Key Architectural Properties (Bullet Form)

- **Load/Store architecture** — ALU operates on registers only.
- **Fixed 32-bit instruction encoding** in ARM state; **16-bit fixed encoding** in Thumb state.
- **37 × 32-bit registers** in the ARM core (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers), banked by mode.
- **Orthogonal register set** — most instructions can use any of the 16 visible registers.
- **Conditional execution** of almost every instruction (unique to ARM) — eliminates most short branches.
- **Multiple processor modes** — User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System.
- **Single-cycle execution** for most simple instructions.
- **Built-in hardware multiplier** (32 × 8 → 40-bit, or 32 × 32 → 64-bit in newer cores).

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU misconception: "ARM = Reduced Functionality". **RISC** means *Reduced Instruction Set Computer* — fewer, simpler, regular instructions — **not** reduced functionality. Modern ARM Cortex-M7 outperforms many CISC DSPs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RISC vs CISC — The Theoretical Foundation

| Parameter                | RISC (ARM)                                 | CISC (x86, 8051)                    |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Instruction width        | **Fixed** (32-bit)                         | **Variable** (1–15 bytes)           |
| Memory access            | Only via **Load/Store**                    | Any instruction can access memory   |
| Addressing modes         | Few (typically 3–5)                        | Many (12+)                          |
| Instruction count        | Small (≈ 50–100 core instructions)         | Large (≈ 500–1500)                  |
| Decoder complexity       | Simple, **hardwired**                      | Complex, microcoded                 |
| Pipeline                 | Deep, **efficient**                        | Hard to pipeline                    |
| Register file            | Large (16/31 visible)                      | Small (8 typical)                   |
| Execution time per instr | Mostly **1 cycle**                         | **Variable** (1–hundreds of cycles) |
| Compiler role            | **Heavy** (scheduling done by compiler)    | Light                               |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does ARM use RISC?** Simpler hardware → higher clock frequency, easier pipelining → better instructions-per-cycle (IPC) → higher performance at lower power. ARM's low power-per-MIPS is the reason it dominates mobile/embedded.

## 2.2 ARM Register Set — The Heart of the Architecture

In **User mode**, the programmer sees **16 general-purpose 32-bit registers**:
- **R0–R12** → General purpose, free for arithmetic and addressing.
- **R13** → **SP** (Stack Pointer) — banked in privileged modes.
- **R14** → **LR** (Link Register) — holds the return address after a subroutine call (BL/BLX).
- **R15** → **PC** (Program Counter) — read/write in ARM state, giving PC-relative addressing.

There is **one status register per mode**:
- **CPSR** → Current Program Status Register (exists in *all* modes).
- **SPSR** → Saved Program Status Register (saves CPSR on exception, exists *only* in privileged modes except User/System).

### 2.2.1 CPSR / SPSR Bit Layout (KTU 2-Mark Favorite)

| Bit(s) | Field            | Meaning                                                            |
|--------|------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 31     | **N (Negative)** | Set if ALU result is negative (MSB = 1).                          |
| 30     | **Z (Zero)**     | Set if ALU result is zero.                                         |
| 29     | **C (Carry)**    | Set if unsigned overflow/borrow produced.                          |
| 28     | **V (oVerflow)** | Set if signed overflow produced.                                   |
| 27–25  | **IT[1:0], J**   | Thumb-2 IT state, Jazelle state.                                   |
| 24     | **J**            | 1 = Jazelle bytecode execution.                                    |
| 23–20  | Reserved         | (RAZ/SBZP on most cores)                                          |
| 19–16  | **GE[3:0]**      | Greater-than-or-Equal flags (for SIMD/DSP).                        |
| 15–10  | **IT[7:2]**      | Upper bits of If-Then state.                                       |
| 9–8    | Reserved         |                                                                    |
| 7      | **I (IRQ disable)** | 1 = IRQ interrupts disabled.                                   |
| 6      | **F (FIQ disable)** | 1 = FIQ interrupts disabled.                                   |
| 5     | **T (Thumb)**    | 1 = Thumb state; 0 = ARM state.                                    |
| 4–0   | **M[4:0] (Mode)** | Processor mode (see below).                                       |

### 2.2.2 Processor Modes (M[4:0])

| Mode            | Code | Privilege | Use                                                |
|-----------------|------|-----------|----------------------------------------------------|
| **User**        | `10000` (0x10) | Unprivileged | Normal program execution, OS apps              |
| **FIQ**         | `10001` (0x11) | Privileged | Fast Interrupt, banked R8–R14, R13_fiq, R14_fiq    |
| **IRQ**         | `10010` (0x12) | Privileged | Normal Interrupt, banked R13_irq, R14_irq          |
| **Supervisor**  | `10011` (0x13) | Privileged | OS / SWI / Reset entry                             |
| **Abort**       | `10111` (0x17) | Privileged | Memory access failure                              |
| **Undefined**   | `11011` (0x1B) | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap                         |
| **System**      | `11111` (0x1F) | Privileged | OS tasks using User registers, no SPSR            |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love the trick: "How many modes are there in ARM?" — Answer: **7** (sometimes written 7+1 if counting System separately from User, but the canonical answer is **7 modes** with 6 privileged + 1 unprivileged).

### 2.2.3 Banked Registers

When switching to a privileged mode, **R13 (SP)** and **R14 (LR)** are automatically swapped for a *physical* bank unique to that mode. This means:
- No need to save User's SP/LR on every interrupt — hardware does it.
- **FIQ additionally banks R8–R14**, allowing very fast context save (no push to memory needed for the first few registers).

## 2.3 ARM Data Types

| Type            | Size         | ARM naming convention |
|-----------------|--------------|-----------------------|
| Byte            | 8 bits       | **Byte**              |
| Halfword        | 16 bits      | **Halfword**          |
| Word            | 32 bits      | **Word**              |
| Doubleword      | 64 bits      | **Doubleword** (v5TEJ and above) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **ARM core registers are 32 bits wide**, and **all data processing operations are performed on 32-bit operands**. 8-bit and 16-bit quantities must be zero- or sign-extended to 32 bits *before* entering the ALU — this is automatic on Load.

## 2.4 ARM Memory Organization

- ARM uses a **single, flat 32-bit address space** (4 GB) in ARMv7-A/R, expanded to **48-bit / 52-bit** in ARMv8.
- The architecture is **byte-addressable** (lowest addressable unit is a byte), but it is **word-aligned** for instructions: instructions must be aligned to a **4-byte boundary** in ARM state and **2-byte boundary** in Thumb state.
- Endianness is **bi-endian** — configurable per system (little-endian is the modern default; ARM historically supported both BE-32 and LE).

## 2.5 ARM Instruction Set Overview (KTU 4-Mark Typical)

Six broad classes:

1. **Data Processing** — `ADD, SUB, AND, ORR, EOR, MOV, CMP, TST, MUL, MLA` (operate on registers only).
2. **Load / Store** — `LDR, STR, LDRB, STRB, LDRH, STRH, LDM, STM` (memory ↔ register).
3. **Branch** — `B, BL, BX, BLX` (with optional condition code on B/BL).
4. **Multiply** — `MUL, MLA, MULL, MLAL, UMULL, UMLAL, SMLAL` (long versions 64-bit).
5. **Exception / Status** — `MRS, MSR, CPS, SRS, RFE` (read/write CPSR/SPSR).
6. **Coprocessor / DSP / SIMD** — `CDP, LDC, STC, MCR, MRC`, plus in newer cores **Saturated math, MAC, SIMD pack/unpack**.

> [!NOTE]
> Every instruction (except a few v5/v6 ones) can be conditionally executed by appending a 4-bit **condition code suffix**:
> `EQ, NE, CS/HS, CC/LO, MI, PL, VS, VC, HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, AL, NV`.
> This single feature eliminates **~50 % of branches** in compiled code, dramatically shrinking pipelines and reducing branch-misprediction cost.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | $N$ = #instructions, $CPI$ = cycles/instruction, $T_{clk}$ = clock period |
| 2 | **CPI for a mix** | $CPI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times F_i \right)$, where $F_i = \dfrac{N_i}{N}$ | Weighted average over instruction classes |
| 3 | **MIPS Rating** | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Millions of Instructions Per Second |
| 4 | **Pipeline Throughput (ideal)** | $TP = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{1} = f_{clk}$ inst/s | One instruction completes per cycle |
| 5 | **Pipeline Speed-up (k stages)** | $S_k = \dfrac{k \times T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}}$ — ideal $S_k = k$ | $k$ = number of pipeline stages |
| 6 | **Pipeline Penalty on mispredict** | $Penalty_{branch} = misprediction\_rate \times flush\_cycles$ | ARM has conditional exec. → penalty ≈ 0 for many branches |
| 7 | **Pipeline Throughput (real)** | $TP_{real} = \dfrac{1}{CPI_{avg}} \times f_{clk}$ | CPI includes stalls, hazards, flushes |
| 8 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $s$ = speedup of enhancement |
| 9 | **Power (dynamic)** | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor, $C$ = load cap |
| 10 | **Memory Read Time** | $T_{mem} = T_{access} + \dfrac{\text{Block}}{BW}$ | Hierarchical cache makes $T_{mem}^{eff} \ll T_{mem}^{DRAM}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value** in your KTU answer script — e.g., $\vert CPI - 1 \vert$, never the bare pipe $\vert CPI - 1 \vert$ inside table rows. The bare pipe will break the markdown table parser.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs:** Apple A-series, Snapdragon, Exynos — all use **ARM Cortex-A** customized cores (e.g., Apple's "Avalanche", Qualcomm's "Kryo").
- **Microcontrollers:** **STM32 (Cortex-M)** and **LPC (NXP)** are the standard KTU lab boards.
- **Automotive:** Cortex-R5/R8 in ECU braking (Bosch, Continental), Cortex-A53 in ADAS.
- **Servers/Cloud:** AWS Graviton (ARM Neoverse N1) — 60 % better perf/Watt for cloud workloads.
- **IoT:** Cortex-M0+ consumes as little as **9 µA/MHz**, making it ideal for battery-powered sensor nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipeline Speed-up with Stalls

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):** An ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch–Decode–Execute). Each stage takes **1 clock cycle**. If a program of **N = 1000 instructions** has **40 branch instructions** with a **mis-prediction penalty of 2 cycles** each (the pipeline must be flushed), and the **ideal pipeline latency is 3 cycles for the first instruction**, compute:
- (a) The **total cycles** for the program.
- (b) The **effective CPI**.
- (c) The **pipeline speed-up** vs. a non-pipelined implementation where each instruction takes 3 cycles.

### Step 1 — Compute Ideal (no-stall) cycles for the pipelined version

In a *k*-stage pipeline, the first instruction takes *k* cycles, and each subsequent instruction takes 1 cycle (ideal). So:

$$C_{ideal} = k + (N - 1) \times 1$$

Substituting $k = 3$ and $N = 1000$:

$$C_{ideal} = 3 + (1000 - 1) \times 1$$

$$C_{ideal} = 3 + 999 = 1002 \; \text{cycles}$$

### Step 2 — Add the branch misprediction penalties

Each mis-predicted branch flushes 2 extra cycles. There are 40 such branches:

$$C_{stall} = 40 \times 2 = 80 \; \text{cycles}$$

### Step 3 — Total cycles for the pipelined implementation

$$C_{pipe} = C_{ideal} + C_{stall}$$

$$C_{pipe} = 1002 + 80 = 1082 \; \text{cycles}$$

### Step 4 — Effective CPI

$$CPI_{eff} = \dfrac{C_{pipe}}{N}$$

$$CPI_{eff} = \dfrac{1082}{1000} = 1.082 \; \text{cycles/instruction}$$

### Step 5 — Non-pipelined total cycles

Each instruction takes 3 cycles in the non-pipelined version:

$$C_{non-pipe} = N \times 3 = 1000 \times 3 = 3000 \; \text{cycles}$$

### Step 6 — Pipeline Speed-up

$$S_{pipe} = \dfrac{C_{non-pipe}}{C_{pipe}}$$

$$S_{pipe} = \dfrac{3000}{1082} = 2.773$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 3-stage pipeline *theoretically* gives 3× speed-up, but branch mis-predictions bring it down to **2.77×** — a realistic figure for ARM7TDMI.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law for an ARM Speed-up (KTU 7-Mark)

**Problem:** A new ARM Cortex-M4 design team proposes adding a **hardware floating-point unit (FPU)** that runs floating-point instructions **5× faster**, but only **20 %** of program execution time uses FP instructions. What is the **overall speed-up**?

### Step 1 — Identify parameters

Let $f$ = fraction of execution time that is enhanced by FPU = **0.20**, and $s$ = speed-up of that enhanced portion = **5**.

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law

$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$$

Substituting:

$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.20) + \dfrac{0.20}{5}}$$

$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.80 + 0.04}$$

$$S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{0.84}$$

$$S_{overall} = 1.1905$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Even an *infinite* FPU speed-up ($s \to \infty$) would only yield a maximum speed-up of $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.20} = 1.25 \times$. **This is why architects focus on the common-case first.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Reduction by Voltage Scaling

**Problem:** A Cortex-M0 running at $f_1 = 50$ MHz, $V_{dd1} = 1.8$ V consumes $P_1 = 12$ mW (dynamic only). If we scale to $f_2 = 25$ MHz, $V_{dd2} = 1.2$ V, what is the new power $P_2$?

### Step 1 — Recall the dynamic power formula

$$P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$$

Assume $\alpha$ and $C$ are constant. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{V_{dd2}^{2} \cdot f_2}{V_{dd1}^{2} \cdot f_1}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$\dfrac{P_2}{12 \, \text{mW}} = \dfrac{(1.2)^2 \times 25}{(1.8)^2 \times 50}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{12} = \dfrac{1.44 \times 25}{3.24 \times 50}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{12} = \dfrac{36}{162} = 0.2222$$

$$P_2 = 12 \times 0.2222 = 2.667 \; \text{mW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A **33 %** frequency drop and **33 %** voltage drop give nearly **78 %** power savings — this is the **square-law benefit** that makes ARM the king of low-power designs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ARM Pipeline Stages Across Families

| Pipeline Stage → / Core ↓ | Fetch | Decode | Read Reg | Shift | ALU | Sat / MAC | Memory | Write Reg |
|---------------------------|:------:|:------:|:--------:|:-----:|:---:|:---------:|:------:|:---------:|
| **ARM7TDMI** (3-stage)    | ✔      | ✔      | ⊕        | ⊕     | ✔   | —         | ✔      | ⊕         |
| **ARM9** (5-stage)        | ✔      | ✔      | ✔        | ✔     | ✔   | —         | ✔      | ✔         |
| **ARM11** (8-stage)       | ✔      | ✔      | ✔        | ✔     | ✔   | —         | ✔      | ✔ (split) |
| **Cortex-A8** (13-stage, dual-issue) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| **Cortex-M3** (3-stage)   | ✔      | ✔      | —        | —     | ✔   | —         | ✔      | ✔ (merged with F) |

> [!TIP]
> Note: **Cortex-M3** uses only a **3-stage pipeline** to keep interrupt latency **deterministic and < 12 cycles** — critical for hard real-time control (BLDC motors, robotics).

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Summary (Cortex-M Family)

| Parameter                | Cortex-M0       | Cortex-M3        | Cortex-M4           | Cortex-M7              |
|--------------------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Architecture             | ARMv6-M         | ARMv7-M          | ARMv7E-M            | ARMv7E-M               |
| Pipeline Stages          | 3               | 3                | 3                   | 6 (dual-issue)         |
| DMIPS/MHz                | **0.9**         | **1.25**         | **1.25**            | **2.14**               |
| CoreMark/MHz             | ~2.33           | ~3.34            | ~3.42               | ~5.01                  |
| FPU                      | No              | No               | Single-precision    | Double-precision       |
| DSP Extensions           | No              | No               | Yes                 | Yes (MAC + SIMD)       |
| Typical use              | Coin-cell IoT   | Automotive ECU   | Motor control / DSP | Industrial / audio     |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Core Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[External 32-bit Address Bus]
    P1[External 32-bit Data Bus]
    P2[Coprocessor Interface]
    P3[AMBA Bus Bridge]

    P4[Instruction Decoder and Condition Logic]
    P5[Control Unit FSM]
    P6[Register Bank 37 x 32 bit Banked by Mode]
    P7[Barrel Shifter]
    P8[ALU 32 bit with MAC]
    P9[Multiplier 32x32 to 64 bit]
    P10[Address Generator and Incrementer]
    P11[Load Store Unit LSU]
    P12[Write Buffer 3 words]
    P13[CPSR SPSR Banked per Mode]
    P14[Exception and Vector Table Logic]

    P0 --> P10
    P1 --> P11
    P11 --> P6
    P10 --> P6
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P5 --> P7
    P5 --> P8
    P5 --> P9
    P6 --> P7
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P11
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P1
    P11 --> P6
    P9 --> P6
    P5 --> P13
    P14 --> P5
    P5 --> P2
    P11 --> P3
```

## 4.2 ARM 3-Stage Pipeline Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycle1[Cycle 1]
        I1F[Instr 1: F]
    end
    subgraph Cycle2[Cycle 2]
        I1D[Instr 1: D]
        I2F[Instr 2: F]
    end
    subgraph Cycle3[Cycle 3]
        I1E[Instr 1: E]
        I2D[Instr 2: D]
        I3F[Instr 3: F]
    end
    subgraph Cycle4[Cycle 4]
        I2E[Instr 2: E]
        I3D[Instr 3: D]
        I4F[Instr 4: F]
    end
    subgraph Cycle5[Cycle 5]
        I3E[Instr 3: E]
        I4D[Instr 4: D]
        I5F[Instr 5: F]
    end
    Cycle1 --> Cycle2 --> Cycle3 --> Cycle4 --> Cycle5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **F** = Fetch, **D** = Decode (includes operand read + barrel shifter setup), **E** = Execute (includes ALU, memory access, write-back). Notice the staggered "wave" — at Cycle 5, three instructions are simultaneously in different stages. **Throughput = 1 instruction / cycle**.

## 4.3 CPSR Condition Code Flag Generation (Conditional Execution Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ALU Operation ADD SUB AND ORR]
    A1{Result MSB is 1}
    A2{Result equals 0}
    A3{Unsigned overflow borrow}
    A4{Signed overflow}
    A5N[Set N flag bit 31]
    A5Z[Set Z flag bit 30]
    A5C[Set C flag bit 29]
    A5V[Set V flag bit 28]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A0 --> A4
    A1 -->|Yes| A5N
    A2 -->|Yes| A5Z
    A3 -->|Yes| A5C
    A4 -->|Yes| A5V
```

## 4.4 Exception Entry & Mode-Switch Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Exception or Interrupt Triggered]
    B1[Switch to Privileged Mode FIQ IRQ SVC ABT UND]
    B2[Save CPSR into SPSR of new mode]
    B3[Set LR mode with return address plus 4 or 8]
    B4[Set CPSR I bit or F bit to mask further IRQ FIQ]
    B5[Set PC to Vector Address 0x00 to 0x1C]
    B6[Execute Exception Handler]
    B7[Restore CPSR from SPSR via SUBS and MOVS]
    B8[Return to User by PC = LR minus offset]

    B0 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vector Table (KTU must-know):**
> - 0x00 → Reset (Supervisor mode)
> - 0x04 → Undefined Instruction
> - 0x08 → Software Interrupt (SWI / SVC)
> - 0x0C → Prefetch Abort
> - 0x10 → Data Abort
> - 0x14 → Reserved
> - 0x18 → IRQ
> - 0x1C → FIQ
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A has **5 questions of 3 marks each** drawn from the module, of which ~1–2 will test ARM fundamentals. The model answers below are written in the **exact 3-paragraph KTU-expected length** (Definition → Feature → Example).

### **Q1.** *Explain the salient features of the ARM processor architecture. Mention the difference between ARM and Thumb instruction sets.* [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The ARM architecture is a **32-bit RISC Load–Store** architecture characterized by: (i) a large uniform register file of **37 × 32-bit registers** (16 visible in User mode), (ii) **fixed-length 32-bit instructions** in ARM state that simplify pipelining, (iii) **Load/Store memory model** where ALU operations act only on registers, and (iv) **conditional execution** of nearly every instruction via a 4-bit condition code suffix, which eliminates most short branches. The architecture supports seven processor modes (one unprivileged + six privileged) and bi-endian memory access.

The **ARM state** uses 32-bit fixed instructions offering full functionality (data processing, multiply, load/store multiple, coprocessor). The **Thumb state** uses 16-bit compressed instructions that sacrifice some features (no conditional execution on most, no full barrel shifter access) to achieve **~30 % higher code density** — critical for memory-constrained embedded systems. Modern **Thumb-2** (ARMv7) bridges both worlds, allowing mixed 16/32-bit instructions.

---

### **Q2.** *List the seven processor modes of ARM and explain the role of CPSR and SPSR.* [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**
ARM supports **seven processor modes** encoded in CPSR bits M[4:0]: **User (0b10000)** — normal unprivileged execution; **FIQ (0b10001)** — fast interrupt, banks R8–R14; **IRQ (0b10010)** — normal interrupt; **Supervisor (0b10011)** — entered on Reset and SWI/SVC; **Abort (0b10111)** — memory access failure; **Undefined (0b11011)** — undefined instruction trap; and **System (0b11111)** — privileged mode using User's registers, used by OS tasks.

The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a 32-bit register present in *all* modes, containing the **condition flags (N, Z, C, V)**, the **interrupt mask bits (I, F)**, the **Thumb state bit (T)**, and the **mode field (M[4:0])**. The **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** exists only in the **six privileged exception modes** and is used to *save a copy of CPSR* automatically when an exception is taken; on exception return, SPSR is copied back into CPSR, restoring the pre-exception state.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B has **full-14-mark questions** with internal choice. Below are two independent alternatives; both can appear in the same paper.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) Describe the ARM processor architecture in detail. Explain its register organization and CPSR layout with a neat diagram. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture Classification:** ARM is a **32-bit RISC, Load-Store, Von-Neumann** architecture (with Harvard cache). The key philosophy is "**simpler instructions executed faster**" — achieved by regular instruction encoding, register-only ALU, and deep pipelining.

**2. Register Organization:** The core contains **37 physical 32-bit registers**:
- **R0 – R12** — 13 general-purpose registers visible in User mode.
- **R13 (SP)** — Stack Pointer, **banked per mode** (SP_usr, SP_svc, SP_irq, SP_fiq, SP_abt, SP_und, SP_mon).
- **R14 (LR)** — Link Register, **banked per mode**, holds the subroutine return address set by `BL`.
- **R15 (PC)** — Program Counter, points to current instruction + 8 in ARM state (2 stages ahead), 4 in Thumb.
- **R13_fiq – R14_fiq** plus **R8_fiq – R12_fiq** — *extra* banked registers for FIQ to allow rapid context save.
- **CPSR** — one global status register; **SPSR** — one per privileged mode (6 SPSRs total).

**3. CPSR Layout:**

| Bit | 31 | 30 | 29 | 28 | 27–8 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4–0 |
|-----|----|----|----|----|------|---|---|---|-----|
| Field | **N** | **Z** | **C** | **V** | Reserved / IT / J / GE | **I** | **F** | **T** | **M[4:0]** |

- **N (bit 31)** = Negative flag — set when ALU result is negative.
- **Z (bit 30)** = Zero flag — set when ALU result is zero.
- **C (bit 29)** = Carry / unsigned overflow / extend flag.
- **V (bit 28)** = oVerflow / signed overflow flag.
- **I (bit 7)** = IRQ disable — set to mask IRQ.
- **F (bit 6)** = FIQ disable — set to mask FIQ.
- **T (bit 5)** = Thumb state — 1 means processor interprets instructions as 16-bit Thumb.
- **M[4:0] (bits 4–0)** = Processor mode (see §5.1 Q2).

**4. Key Architectural Highlights:**
- All instructions are **conditionally executed** (4-bit `cond` field in every instruction).
- **Barrel shifter** can shift/rotate the second operand of any data-processing instruction in a single cycle.
- **Single-cycle ALU** for most ops; **multi-cycle MUL** (1–4 cycles).
- **Bi-endian** — supports both little- and big-endian memory layouts (LE is modern default).

---

#### (b) Explain the RISC design philosophy. Compare RISC and CISC with examples. How is ARM an improvement over classical RISC? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. RISC Philosophy — Five Principles:**
1. **Single-cycle execution** — most instructions complete in 1 cycle.
2. **Fixed instruction length** — simplifies fetch/decode and pipelining.
3. **Load/Store architecture** — memory access only via `LDR/STR`.
4. **Large register file** — keeps operands on-chip, reducing memory traffic.
5. **Hardwired control** — no microcode; decoder is combinatorial logic.

**2. RISC vs CISC Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RISC (ARM, MIPS) | CISC (x86, 8051) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Examples | ARM7, ARM9, Cortex-M3, MIPS R3000 | Intel x86, Motorola 68k, 8051 |
| Instruction length | Fixed 32 bits (Thumb: 16) | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| Memory access | Only via LDR/STR | Any instruction |
| Addressing modes | Few (3–5) | Many (12+) |
| Pipeline | Deep, efficient | Hard to pipeline |
| CPI | Mostly 1 | Variable (1–100) |
| Code size | Larger (mitigated by Thumb) | Smaller |
| Compiler complexity | High | Low |
| Power efficiency | High | Low |

**3. How ARM Improves Classical RISC:**
- **Conditional execution** of *every* instruction (including data-processing and load/store in v5+), eliminating most short branches → higher IPC.
- **Barrel shifter** in-line with ALU — second operand can be shifted/rotated *for free* during the ALU cycle, enabling single-instruction address calculations.
- **Thumb-2 / ThumbEE** — gives RISC the **code density** advantage of CISC while retaining RISC's 1-CPI core.
- **Banked registers** for fast exception context switching — a major RISC weakness (no implicit state) is fixed in hardware.
- **NEON / SIMD** and **DSP extensions** add specialized RISC engines for multimedia, solving CISC's multimedia advantage.
- **Low-power design** — 32-bit RISC cores at sub-100 µA/MHz active current.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the ARM 3-stage pipeline. Discuss pipeline hazards and how ARM mitigates them. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Three Pipeline Stages:**
- **Stage 1 — FETCH (F):** Instruction is read from memory at address in PC. The PC is incremented by 4 (ARM) or 2 (Thumb).
- **Stage 2 — DECODE (D):** Instruction is decoded; source registers are read from the register bank; for data-processing instructions, the **barrel shifter** pre-shifts operand 2 (all in parallel — *operand pre-fetch*).
- **Stage 3 — EXECUTE (E):** The ALU performs the operation; for Load/Store, memory access occurs; results are **written back** to the destination register in the *first half* of the next cycle (a write-forwarding trick that lets a following instruction read the result in its decode stage with only a **1-cycle interlock**).

**2. Timing Diagram (simplified):**

| Cycle →  | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inst 1   | F | D | E |   |   |   |
| Inst 2   |   | F | D | E |   |   |
| Inst 3   |   |   | F | D | E |   |

After the initial 3-cycle latency, **one instruction completes per cycle** → CPI = 1.

**3. Pipeline Hazards and ARM's Mitigations:**

| Hazard Type | Cause | ARM Mitigation |
|-------------|-------|----------------|
| **Data (Read-After-Write, RAW)** | Producing instruction hasn't written back when consumer reads register | **Internal forwarding** from ALU output / write-back to register file read in the next cycle; only **1-cycle interlock** required |
| **Data (Write-After-Read, WAR)** | Consumer writes before producer reads | Eliminated by **strict in-order** issue and read-before-write in the same stage |
| **Data (Write-After-Write, WAW)** | Out-of-order write of same destination | Eliminated by **in-order completion** of pipeline |
| **Control (Branch)** | PC changes mid-pipeline | (i) **Conditional execution** removes most short branches; (ii) Branch prediction (ARM9+); (iii) **Branch-and-link exchange** delay slot filled by hardware on BX/BLX |
| **Structural** | Two stages need same resource | **Write buffer** decouples LSU; separate buses for instruction fetch vs. data on Harvard cache |

**4. Pipeline Flush on Exception:** When an IRQ/FIQ/Reset occurs, the pipeline is **flushed** and PC is forced to the **vector address** (0x18 for IRQ, 0x1C for FIQ). This costs a few cycles but is **bounded and deterministic** — important for real-time.

---

#### (b) An ARM-based embedded system runs at $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz. A program has $N = 1{,}200{,}000$ instructions with the following mix: [7 Marks]

| Instruction Class | Count $N_i$ | $CPI_i$ |
|-------------------|------------|---------|
| Data processing   | 600,000    | 1       |
| Load/Store        | 360,000    | 2       |
| Branch            | 120,000    | 3       |
| Multiply          | 120,000    | 4       |

**Compute (i) Average CPI, (ii) CPU execution time, (iii) MIPS rating, (iv) speed-up if Multiply CPI is reduced to 2.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Average CPI:**

$$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (N_i \times CPI_i)}{N}$$

$$\sum (N_i \times CPI_i) = 600{,}000 \times 1 + 360{,}000 \times 2 + 120{,}000 \times 3 + 120{,}000 \times 4$$

$$= 600{,}000 + 720{,}000 + 360{,}000 + 480{,}000 = 2{,}160{,}000$$

$$CPI_{avg} = \dfrac{2{,}160{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} = 1.8$$

**Step 2 — CPU Execution Time:**

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI_{avg}}{f_{clk}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000 \times 1.8}{100 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{2.16 \times 10^{6}}{10^{8}} = 21.6 \; \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — MIPS Rating:**

$$MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{6}}{1.8 \times 10^{6}} = 55.56 \; \text{MIPS}$$

**Step 4 — Speed-up with improved Multiply:**

New sum:

$$\sum (N_i \times CPI_i)_{new} = 600{,}000 \times 1 + 360{,}000 \times 2 + 120{,}000 \times 3 + 120{,}000 \times 2$$

$$= 600{,}000 + 720{,}000 + 360{,}000 + 240{,}000 = 1{,}920{,}000$$

$$CPI_{new} = \dfrac{1{,}920{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} = 1.6$$

$$T_{new} = \dfrac{1{,}200{,}000 \times 1.6}{10^{8}} = 19.2 \; \text{ms}$$

$$\text{Speed-up} = \dfrac{T_{CPU}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{21.6}{19.2} = 1.125 \times$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** — convert $f$ to Hz (not MHz) and MIPS to a per-second denominator. KTU markers deduct **½ mark** for mixed units.
> 2. **Total instruction count** must be the *denominator* for average CPI — never the number of instruction *types*.
> 3. MIPS is **instruction-set dependent** — a "higher MIPS" does *not* always mean faster! It is meaningless when comparing ARM to x86 because instructions per program differ.
> 4. The branch CPI of 3 already accounts for a *predicted-taken, 1-cycle flush* — state this assumption explicitly in the answer.
> 5. Many students forget to convert the **clock period** properly: $f = 100$ MHz means $T_{clk} = 10$ ns, and $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ gives the same $21.6$ ms. Showing **both** methods earns a +1 evaluation mark.
> 6. For the speed-up, always quote the **execution-time ratio**, not the CPI ratio — they are mathematically equal here *only* because $N$ and $f_{clk}$ are unchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ARM** = **Advanced RISC Machine**, 32-bit Load-Store architecture, licensed by Arm Holdings.
- [x] **Full form of CPSR** = Current Program Status Register; **SPSR** = Saved Program Status Register.
- [x] **7 processor modes** — User (unprivileged) + FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS (privileged).
- [x] **37 physical registers** — 31 general-purpose (16 visible in User) + 6 status (1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs; System & User share CPSR).
- [x] **R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC**; PC reads at `current + 8` (ARM) or `current + 4` (Thumb) due to pipeline.
- [x] **CPSR layout** — N(31), Z(30), C(29), V(28), I(7), F(6), T(5), M[4:0](4–0).
- [x] **ARM state** = 32-bit instructions; **Thumb state** = 16-bit compressed; **Thumb-2** = mixed 16/32-bit.
- [x] **Bi-endian** — supports both little and big endian; little is the modern default.
- [x] **3-stage pipeline** = F → D → E; **CPI = 1** in the ideal case; flush on exception.
- [x] **Condition codes** (EQ, NE, CS, CC, MI, PL, VS, VC, HI, LS, GE, LT, GT, LE, AL) apply to almost all instructions.
- [x] **Vector table** at 0x00–0x1C: Reset, UND, SWI, PABT, DABT, Reserved, IRQ, FIQ.
- [x] **Cortex series** — **A** (Applications), **R** (Real-time), **M** (Microcontroller).
- [x] **CPU time formula** = $N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ ; **MIPS** = $f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^6)$.
- [x] **Amdahl's law** = $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/s]$; $S_{max} = 1/(1-f)$ when $s \to \infty$.
- [x] **Power** scales as $V_{dd}^{2} \times f$ → halving voltage reduces dynamic power 4× (key to ARM's low power).
- [x] **Difference between ARM7 and Cortex-M3** — ARM7 = Von-Neumann, 3-stage; Cortex-M3 = Harvard, 3-stage, deterministic interrupt latency, Thumb-2 only.
- [x] **Barrel shifter** can perform shift/rotate in the *same cycle* as the ALU.
- [x] **Load/Store multiple** (`LDM/STM`) can move 16 registers to/from memory in a single instruction.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for ARM modes (M[4:0] codes):** *"**U**ncle **F**red **I**s **S**moking **A** **U**nique **S**moke"* → **U**ser, **F**IQ, **I**RQ, **S**VC, **A**bort, **U**ndef, **S**ystem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model (Basic Level Only) - Memory Format
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model (Basic Level Only) - Memory Format

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model?

The **ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model** is the conceptual representation of the processor's resources (registers, memory addressing, data formats, operating modes) that are directly visible and accessible to the programmer or compiler when writing assembly/C code. Unlike the hardware architecture (gates, buses, pipelines), the programmer's model is the **logical interface** used to understand how the CPU stores data, addresses instructions, and switches between privileged states.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The ARM7TDMI programmer's model defines the set of registers visible to the programmer at any instant, the privileged operating modes of the processor, the CPSR/SPSR status registers, and the supported memory data formats (Big-Endian and Little-Endian). It uses a **32-bit linear address space** of **4 GB** (2³² bytes), which is **byte-addressable**.

The "TDMI" suffix indicates the enhanced feature set of the ARM7 core:
- **T** → **Thumb** instruction set (16-bit compressed instructions)
- **D** → **Debug** extension (on-chip debug hardware)
- **M** → **Multiplier** (enhanced 32-bit hardware multiplier)
- **I** → **EmbeddedICE** (in-circuit emulator logic)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a sophisticated office workspace. The **CPU core** is the *chief executive*, who is highly efficient but has very limited "active desk space" (registers) to keep papers (data) in immediate reach. The **file cabinets** in the basement represent the **memory (4 GB space)**, where every document has a unique file number (address).

The **programmer's model** is the rulebook that tells the executive:

1. **How many active desk drawers** he has (31 general-purpose registers + 6 status registers).
2. **Which drawer becomes accessible** when he switches hats — e.g., when acting as the *HR Head* (Supervisor mode) vs. *Security Officer* (IRQ mode) — the drawers rearrange to give him role-specific tools.
3. **How files are stored** in the basement — whether they are filed *left-to-right* (Little-Endian) or *right-to-left* (Big-Endian).

So the programmer's model is essentially the **"user manual of the desk and filing system"** for the CPU executive.

## 1.3 Core Components of the Model (High-Level Listing)

The ARM7TDMI programmer's model (basic level) consists of the following four pillars:

| # | Component | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **General-Purpose Registers (R0–R15)** | Fast on-chip storage for operands and addresses |
| 2 | **Program Status Registers (CPSR & SPSR)** | Track condition flags, interrupt disables, and current mode |
| 3 | **Processor Modes (7 modes)** | Define privilege levels and exception handling contexts |
| 4 | **Memory Format (Endianness + Word Alignment)** | Rules defining how 32-bit/16-bit/8-bit data is stored in 4 GB byte-addressable memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Memorize:**
> - **Total addressable memory = 2³² bytes = 4 GB**
> - **Total registers in physical design = 37** (31 general-purpose 32-bit + 6 status)
> - **Visible registers at any instant ≤ 18** (R0–R15 + CPSR, plus banked registers of the active mode)
> - **Number of processor modes = 7**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 37 Registers of ARM7TDMI — A Layered View

The ARM7TDMI does **not** show all 37 registers to the programmer at once. The 37 registers are arranged into *banks*, and depending on the **current processor mode**, only one specific subset is *visible* (mapped onto the physical register file).

### 2.1.1 Unbanked Registers (Always Visible)

These are available in **every** processor mode, including User mode:

- **R0 – R12** → Thirteen **general-purpose registers** used for data manipulation, address calculation, and scratch operations.
- **R13** → The **Stack Pointer (SP)** — points to the top of the current stack.
- **R14** → The **Link Register (LR)** — stores the return address when a subroutine is called (e.g., after `BL` instruction).
- **R15** → The **Program Counter (PC)** — holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched (PC = current instruction address + 8 in ARM state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Banked vs Unbanked:**
> In the user-visible register set, **R0–R7 are truly unbanked** (same physical register in all modes). **R8–R14 are banked** in FIQ mode (R8_fiq – R14_fiq) for fast interrupt handling, but unbanked in other modes.

### 2.1.2 Banked Registers (Mode-Specific)

Banked registers are *physically separate* registers that share the same *name* (e.g., R13) but are distinct storage locations. They become visible only when the processor enters the corresponding mode.

| Mode | Banked Registers |
|------|------------------|
| FIQ | R8_fiq, R9_fiq, R10_fiq, R11_fiq, R12_fiq, R13_fiq, R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq |
| IRQ | R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq |
| Supervisor (SVC) | R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc |
| Abort (ABT) | R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt |
| Undefined (UND) | R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und |

> System mode and User mode **share the same unbanked User registers** and do **not** have an SPSR.

## 2.2 The Seven Processor Modes

The ARM7TDMI supports **7 operating modes**, divided into *privileged* and *user* categories.

| # | Mode | Abbreviation | Privilege | Purpose |
|---|------|--------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **User** | usr | Non-privileged | Normal application execution |
| 2 | **Fast Interrupt Request** | fiq | Privileged | High-priority data transfer / interrupt |
| 3 | **Interrupt Request** | irq | Privileged | General-purpose interrupt handling |
| 4 | **Supervisor** | svc | Privileged | OS kernel / software interrupt (SWI) entry |
| 5 | **Abort** | abt | Privileged | Memory access violation handling |
| 6 | **Undefined** | und | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap |
| 7 | **System** | sys | Privileged | Operating system tasks with User register set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode Switching Trigger:**
> - **User ↔ System** → Cannot be entered by exception; entered only by writing to the CPSR (privileged code).
> - **FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND** → Entered *automatically* by the core on receiving the corresponding exception (interrupt, SWI, data abort, etc.).

## 2.3 CPSR and SPSR — The Status Registers

### 2.3.1 CPSR (Current Program Status Register)

A **32-bit** register that is *always* accessible in privileged modes. It contains:
- **Condition flags** (bits 31–28): N, Z, C, V
- **Interrupt disable bits** (bits 7, 6): I, F
- **Thumb state bit** (bit 5): T
- **Mode bits** (bits 4–0): M[4:0]

```
Bit:   31 30 29 28  27 26 25 24-8  7   6   5   4  3  2  1  0
       N  Z  C  V   --reserved--   I   F   T   M4 M3 M2 M1 M0
```

| Flag | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **N** | Negative / sign bit (set if MSB of result is 1) |
| **Z** | Zero (set if result is 0) |
| **C** | Carry (set on unsigned overflow / borrow) |
| **V** | oVerflow (set on signed overflow) |
| **I** | IRQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **F** | FIQ disable (1 = masked) |
| **T** | Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) |
| **M[4:0]** | Mode encoding (e.g., 10011 = Supervisor) |

### 2.3.2 SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)

Each privileged mode (except System/User) has its own **SPSR**, which is used to **save the CPSR value automatically** when the mode is entered on an exception. The SPSR is later used to **restore** the CPSR when returning from the exception handler.

## 2.4 Memory Format — The Heart of This Module

### 2.4.1 Byte-Addressable Memory

The ARM7TDMI treats memory as a **linear, byte-addressable** array of 2³² bytes (4 GB). Every byte has a unique 32-bit address, ranging from **0x00000000** to **0xFFFFFFFF**.

### 2.4.2 Word, Half-Word, and Byte Alignment Rules

Although the bus is 32 bits wide, the ARM7TDMI supports three data sizes, each with strict alignment requirements:

| Data Type | Size | Address Alignment |
|-----------|------|-------------------|
| **Byte** | 8 bits | Any address (A, A+1, A+2, …) |
| **Half-word** | 16 bits | Must be aligned to 2 bytes (A = 2n) |
| **Word** | 32 bits | Must be aligned to 4 bytes (A = 4n) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Misaligned Access:** ARM7TDMI will generate a **Data Abort exception** if a word/half-word is accessed at a non-aligned address (unless the specific implementation has been configured otherwise). This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall.

### 2.4.3 Endianness — Two Memory Formats

The ARM7TDMI supports **both Big-Endian and Little-Endian** byte ordering, selectable by a hardware pin (BIGEND or via system control coprocessor configuration).

- **Little-Endian (Default in most ARM systems):**
  - The **least significant byte (LSB)** of a word is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
  - Address `A` → bits `[7:0]`, `A+1` → bits `[15:8]`, `A+2` → bits `[23:16]`, `A+3` → bits `[31:24]`.

- **Big-Endian:**
  - The **most significant byte (MSB)** of a word is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
  - Address `A` → bits `[31:24]`, `A+1` → bits `[23:16]`, `A+2` → bits `[15:8]`, `A+3` → bits `[7:0]`.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Boundary Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| Total addressable memory | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ | Bytes (4 GB) |
| Number of general-purpose registers | 31 (logical) / up to 18 (visible) | 32-bit each |
| Number of status registers | 1 CPSR + 5 SPSR (in 5 priv. modes) | 32-bit each |
| Total physical register file | 37 | Includes banked copies |
| Number of processor modes | **7** | User, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, System |
| Byte size | 8 bits | Addressable unit |
| Half-word size | 16 bits | Aligned to $\vert A \mod 2 \vert = 0$ |
| Word size | 32 bits | Aligned to $\vert A \mod 4 \vert = 0$ |
| PC offset in ARM state | $PC = \text{current instr. addr} + 8$ | Due to 3-stage pipeline |
| Addressable range | $0x00000000 \text{ to } 0xFFFFFFFF$ | Unsigned 32-bit |
| Endianness options | Big-Endian (BE-8/BE-32) or Little-Endian (LE) | Configurable |
| Condition flags | $\vert N, Z, C, V \vert = 4$ | Set by ALU on data ops |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the programmer's model is **non-negotiable** for:

1. **RTOS Porting** — Writing context-switch code that saves R0–R15, CPSR, and banked registers of the *outgoing* task.
2. **Device Driver Development** — Setting up stacks (SP) in privileged modes (SVC for SVC stack, IRQ for IRQ stack).
3. **Firmware Bootloaders** — Initializing the CPSR (clearing I, F; setting SVC mode) at reset.
4. **Cross-Platform Communication** — Endianness matching between ARM (often LE) and network protocols (Big-Endian, network byte order) — requiring `htonl()` / `ntohl()` byte-swap routines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Storing a 32-bit Word in Both Endian Formats

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU-style derivation):**
> Store the 32-bit value `0x12345678` at base address `0x40000000` in both Big-Endian and Little-Endian memory formats. Identify which byte is at `0x40000000`, `0x40000001`, `0x40000002`, and `0x40000003`.

### Step 1 — Decompose the 32-bit value into 4 bytes

The 32-bit word consists of 4 bytes, which we extract as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0 (MSB)} &= 0x12 \\
\text{Byte 1} &= 0x34 \\
\text{Byte 2} &= 0x68 \\
\text{Byte 3 (LSB)} &= 0x78
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The MSB occupies the upper 8 bits (`bits[31:24]`), and the LSB occupies the lower 8 bits (`bits[7:0]`). We slice the word into 4 sequential 8-bit groups from MSB to LSB.

### Step 2 — Little-Endian Placement

In Little-Endian mode, the **LSB is stored at the lowest address**. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address } 0x40000000 &\rightarrow 0x78 \quad (\text{LSB, bits}[7:0]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000001 &\rightarrow 0x56 \quad (\text{bits}[15:8]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000002 &\rightarrow 0x34 \quad (\text{bits}[23:16]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000003 &\rightarrow 0x12 \quad (\text{MSB, bits}[31:24])
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The byte order is *reversed* relative to the natural MSB→LSB reading. Index `i` of the address receives byte `i` of the LSB-first decomposition.

### Step 3 — Big-Endian Placement

In Big-Endian mode, the **MSB is stored at the lowest address**. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address } 0x40000000 &\rightarrow 0x12 \quad (\text{MSB, bits}[31:24]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000001 &\rightarrow 0x34 \quad (\text{bits}[23:16]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000002 &\rightarrow 0x56 \quad (\text{bits}[15:8]) \\
\text{Address } 0x40000003 &\rightarrow 0x78 \quad (\text{LSB, bits}[7:0])
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The byte sequence matches the natural MSB-to-LSB reading. This is the "human-readable" order and is the network byte order standard.

### Step 4 — Memory Map Visualization

| Address | Big-Endian Byte | Little-Endian Byte |
|---------|-----------------|--------------------|
| 0x40000000 | 0x12 | 0x78 |
| 0x40000001 | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| 0x40000002 | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| 0x40000003 | 0x78 | 0x12 |

### Step 5 — Address Calculation for the nth Byte

A general formula for the byte stored at base address `A` in either mode:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BE: } \quad \text{Memory}(A + n) &= \text{Word}[31 - 8n \,:\, 24 - 8n], \quad n \in \{0,1,2,3\} \\
\text{LE: } \quad \text{Memory}(A + n) &= \text{Word}[8n + 7 \,:\, 8n], \quad n \in \{0,1,2,3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Verifying a Load Operation

**Problem:** A load instruction `LDR R0, [R1]` is executed where `R1 = 0x40000000`, and the memory holds the bytes `0x12 0x34 0x56 0x78` starting at `0x40000000`. Determine the final value of R0 in both endian formats.

### Step 1 — Verify Alignment

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address } 0x40000000 \mod 4 &= 0 \quad \text{(Word-aligned, OK for LDR)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Little-Endian Read

In LE, the byte at the lowest address is the LSB of the loaded word:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &= (0x78 \ll 0) \, \vert \, (0x56 \ll 8) \, \vert \, (0x34 \ll 16) \, \vert \, (0x12 \ll 24) \\
R0 &= 0x78 + 0x5600 + 0x340000 + 0x12000000 \\
R0 &= 0x12345678
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Big-Endian Read

In BE, the byte at the lowest address is the MSB of the loaded word:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &= (0x12 \ll 24) \, \vert \, (0x34 \ll 16) \, \vert \, (0x56 \ll 8) \, \vert \, (0x78 \ll 0) \\
R0 &= 0x12000000 + 0x340000 + 0x5600 + 0x78 \\
R0 &= 0x12345678
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic (Important Insight):** When the *same* memory content is read in either endian format, the loaded register value is **identical** — the difference is *where* each byte was stored, not what the word *means*. The endianness is a *property of the store/load pair*, not the data itself.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Address Range Calculation

**Problem:** Calculate the *last valid byte address* in the 32-bit ARM7TDMI memory map.

### Step 1 — Establish the Formula

The 32-bit address space spans:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address Range} &= [0, \, 2^{32} - 1] \\
&= [0x00000000, \, 0xFFFFFFFF]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Number of Distinct Byte Locations

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bytes}} &= 0xFFFFFFFF - 0x00000000 + 1 \\
&= 2^{32} \\
&= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 4 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in Scaled Units

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of 32-bit words} &= \frac{4 \text{ GB}}{4 \text{ Bytes/word}} = 2^{30} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ words} \\
\text{Number of 16-bit half-words} &= \frac{4 \text{ GB}}{2 \text{ Bytes/halfword}} = 2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \text{ half-words}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Register Visibility Analysis

**Problem:** How many *general-purpose* 32-bit registers (R0–R15) are *physically distinct* in the ARM7TDMI register file? Show your derivation.

### Step 1 — Count the Unbanked Registers

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 \text{ through } R7 &: 8 \text{ registers (truly unbanked, common to all modes)} \\
R15 \text{ (PC)} &: 1 \text{ register (unbanked)} \\
\text{Unbanked subtotal} &= 8 + 1 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Count the Banked Variants of R8–R14

$$
\begin{aligned}
R8 \text{ through } R12 &: 5 \text{ User-mode registers} + 5 \text{ FIQ-mode registers} = 10 \text{ physical} \\
R13 \text{ (SP)} &: 1 \text{ User + 5 mode-specific (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND)} = 6 \text{ physical} \\
R14 \text{ (LR)} &: 1 \text{ User + 5 mode-specific (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND)} = 6 \text{ physical} \\
\text{Banked subtotal} &= 10 + 6 + 6 = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Add the Status Registers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR} &: 1 \\
\text{SPSR (in 5 privileged exception modes)} &: 5 \\
\text{Status register subtotal} &= 1 + 5 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Physical Register File

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 9 + 22 + 6 = 37 \text{ registers}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the KTU syllabus value of **37 registers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** This derivation proves *why* the ARM7TDMI is advertised as a 37-register architecture while the programmer only ever sees a maximum of 18 in a single mode. The "missing" 19 registers are the banked copies that activate when the mode changes.
