# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model** is the logical view of the processor as seen by the software developer. It defines four key elements:

1. **General-purpose registers (R0–R15):** 16 visible 32-bit registers used for data operations, addressing, and program control. R13 acts as the Stack Pointer (SP), R14 as the Link Register (LR), and R15 as the Program Counter (PC).

2. **Status registers (CPSR and SPSR):** CPSR holds current condition flags (N, Z, C, V), interrupt mask bits (I, F), Thumb state (T), and mode bits (M[4:0]). Each privileged mode has a private SPSR for exception-state preservation.

3. **Processor modes (7 total):** User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, and System — controlling privilege and providing banked register visibility.

4. **Memory format:** A **byte-addressable 4 GB (2³² bytes)** linear address space supporting both **Big-Endian** and **Little-Endian** byte ordering, with word (4-byte) and half-word (2-byte) alignment requirements.

> **Marking Distribution (Examiner Pattern):**
> - Registers + their roles → 1 Mark
> - CPSR/SPSR mention → 1 Mark
> - Memory format (byte addressable + endianness) → 1 Mark

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian memory formats with a suitable example. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Big-Endian:** The **most significant byte (MSB)** of a multi-byte word is stored at the **lowest memory address**. This matches the "natural" human reading order (left-to-right) and is also known as *network byte order*.

**Little-Endian:** The **least significant byte (LSB)** of a multi-byte word is stored at the **lowest memory address**. This format is preferred by many RISC processors (including most ARM-based systems) because it allows easy casting between integer sizes.

**Example:** Store the 32-bit word `0x12345678` at address `0x40000000`.

| Address | Big-Endian Byte | Little-Endian Byte |
|---------|-----------------|--------------------|
| 0x40000000 | 0x12 (MSB) | 0x78 (LSB) |
| 0x40000001 | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| 0x40000002 | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| 0x40000003 | 0x78 (LSB) | 0x12 (MSB) |

> **Marking Distribution:**
> - Both definitions → 1 Mark
> - Tabular example with addresses → 1 Mark
> - Engineering relevance (network order / processor preference) → 1 Mark

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model & Register Banking `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the complete ARM7TDMI programmer's model with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of all 16 user-visible registers. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The ARM7TDMI programmer's model provides the abstract register-level interface used to write ARM/Thumb assembly and C programs. It exposes **up to 18 registers** in User mode: **R0–R15 (16 general-purpose) + CPSR**, with additional **banked registers** becoming visible in privileged modes.

**Register Roles:**

- **R0 – R12 (13 registers):** General-purpose scratch registers for arithmetic, logical operations, and address arithmetic. R0 is conventionally used to hold the return value from a subroutine (per the ARM ABI).
- **R13 (SP — Stack Pointer):** Points to the top of the active stack. In User mode it is the User-mode SP; in privileged modes, a banked SP (R13_fiq, R13_irq, etc.) becomes active.
- **R14 (LR — Link Register):** Stores the return address whenever a Branch-with-Link (BL/BLX) instruction is executed. On exception entry, R14 is loaded with the return address specific to that exception type.
- **R15 (PC — Program Counter):** Holds the address of the instruction being fetched. In ARM state, `PC = current instruction address + 8` because of the 3-stage pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute).

**Mode-Dependent Banked Registers:**

- **R8_fiq – R14_fiq:** Banked copies in FIQ mode that allow the fast interrupt handler to start without saving User registers.
- **R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq:** Used by the IRQ exception handler.
- **R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc:** Used when Supervisor mode is entered (e.g., via SWI).
- **R13_abt / R14_abt / SPSR_abt:** Used for prefetch and data abort handling.
- **R13_und / R14_und / SPSR_und:** Used when an undefined instruction is encountered.

**CPSR and SPSR:**
The CPSR's condition flags (N, Z, C, V) reflect the result of the most recent ALU operation. The SPSR automatically saves the *previous* CPSR when an exception is taken and is used to restore CPSR on exception return.

> **Marking Split:** Register diagram (2) + role of R0–R12 (1.5) + role of R13–R15 (1.5) + banked registers (1) + CPSR/SPSR summary (1).

#### (b) Describe the seven processor modes of ARM7TDMI. How does the mode-switch mechanism work on exceptions? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**The 7 Processor Modes:**

1. **User (usr, 0b10000):** The normal unprivileged execution mode. Most application code runs here. It cannot access CPSR directly or switch to privileged modes.
2. **Fast Interrupt Request (fiq, 0b10001):** Entered on a high-priority FIQ exception. Has 7 banked registers (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) for low-latency context saving.
3. **Interrupt Request (irq, 0b10010):** Entered on a normal IRQ exception (after FIQ handling). Used for general-purpose device interrupts.
4. **Supervisor (svc, 0b10011):** Entered on reset and on execution of a Software Interrupt (SWI/SVC) instruction. This is the protected OS kernel mode.
5. **Abort (abt, 0b10111):** Entered when a memory access fails (prefetch abort for instruction fetch, data abort for load/store).
6. **Undefined (und, 0b11011):** Entered when the processor decodes an instruction it cannot handle (e.g., undefined coprocessor instruction).
7. **System (sys, 0b11111):** A privileged mode that *uses the User register set* (no banked registers). Used by OS tasks that need privileged CPSR access without context-switch overhead.

**Exception Entry Sequence (Mode-Switch Mechanism):**

When an exception occurs, the ARM core automatically performs the following:

1. **Copy CPSR into SPSR_<mode>** of the destination mode (state preservation).
2. **Set the appropriate CPSR mode bits** (M[4:0]) and update I/F bits to mask further exceptions.
3. **Save the return address** in LR_<mode> (R14_<mode>).
4. **Set PC to the exception vector address** from the vector table (0x00000014 for IRQ, 0x0000001C for FIQ, 0x00000008 for SVC, etc.).
5. **Switch to the banked register set** of the new mode (e.g., R13_svc, R14_svc become visible).

**Exception Exit:**
A `SUBS PC, LR_<mode>, #4` (or similar) instruction is used in the handler. It atomically restores PC from LR and copies SPSR back to CPSR, returning to the correct next-instruction address.

> **Marking Split:** Listing all 7 modes (2) + privilege classification (1) + exception entry sequence steps (3) + exception exit mechanism (1).

---

### Question B: Memory Format and Endianness in ARM7TDMI `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the memory organization of ARM7TDMI. Discuss byte, half-word, and word alignment with examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Memory Organization:**

The ARM7TDMI presents a **single, linear, byte-addressable memory space** of **2³² bytes (4 GB)**. Each byte has a unique 32-bit address spanning `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`. The processor fetches instructions and data over a unified 32-bit data bus (Von Neumann architecture), although in practice ARM7TDMI is often paired with separate I-code and D-code buses (Harvard-style enhancement) in systems like the LPC2148.

**Data Sizes and Alignment:**

| Data Type | Size | Address Constraint | Misalignment Penalty |
|-----------|------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Byte | 8 bits | Any address | None |
| Half-word | 16 bits | Address must be a multiple of 2 | Data Abort exception |
| Word | 32 bits | Address must be a multiple of 4 | Data Abort exception |

**Alignment Examples:**

- A byte at `0x40000003` is allowed.
- A half-word stored at `0x40000002` is allowed (2 | 2 ✓).
- A half-word stored at `0x40000003` triggers a **Data Abort**.
- A word at `0x40000000` is allowed (4 | 0 ✓).
- A word at `0x40000002` triggers a **Data Abort**.

**Aligned vs Unaligned Access Demonstration:**

For a word value `0xAABBCCDD` at base `0x40000000`:
- Aligned: All 4 bytes fetched in a single bus cycle.
- Unaligned (e.g., at `0x40000001`): Requires 2 bus cycles and byte rotation logic — and on ARM7TDMI, this is **forbidden** by default, leading to a precise data abort.

> **Marking Split:** 4 GB byte addressable definition (2) + alignment rules table (2) + misaligned consequence (1.5) + example (1.5).

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain Big-Endian and Little-Endian memory formats. State the conditions under which the ARM7TDMI selects each. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Endianness Definition:**

Endianness defines the *byte ordering convention* used by the processor when storing or loading multi-byte data (half-words and words) in memory.

**Big-Endian Format:**
- The **most significant byte** of a word is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
- Visually, the bytes appear in the same order as the natural hexadecimal representation of the word.
- Used historically by Motorola 68k, PowerPC, and is the **network byte order** standard (RFC 1700).

**Little-Endian Format:**
- The **least significant byte** of a word is stored at the **lowest memory address**.
- Used by Intel x86 and is the **default** for most modern ARM-based SoCs.
- Advantage: Casting a 32-bit integer into a 16-bit short or 8-bit char requires only a memory address offset, no bit shifts.

**Worked Diagram (Storing 0x12345678 at 0x40000000):**

```
Address      Big-Endian      Little-Endian
0x40000000   0x12 (MSB)      0x78 (LSB)
0x40000001   0x34            0x56
0x40000002   0x56            0x34
0x40000003   0x78 (LSB)      0x12 (MSB)
```

**Selection Mechanism in ARM7TDMI:**

ARM7TDMI is one of the few processors that supports *both* endianness options at the hardware level. The selection is made by:

1. **The BIGEND external pin:** Sampled at reset. If HIGH, the processor boots in Big-Endian mode; if LOW, Little-Endian.
2. **System Control Coprocessor (CP15):** On ARM720T and later cores, the `C1` control register's `B` bit and `Big-endian map` bits allow software reconfiguration.
3. **BE-8 vs BE-32 distinction:** ARM supports two Big-Endian variants:
   - **BE-8:** Bytes within a word are big-endian, but instructions and data share the same endianness.
   - **BE-32 (legacy, ARMv4 and earlier):** 32-bit words and 16-bit half-words use opposite endianness — largely obsolete.

**Real-World Engineering Relevance:**
- Network stacks on ARM (LE) must byte-swap using `htonl()` / `ntohl()` before sending TCP/IP packets.
- When sharing binary data files between ARM and PowerPC/Motorola systems, the endianness mismatch must be resolved either by *swab functions* or by storing data in a *self-describing format* (e.g., PNG, WAV headers with explicit endian flags).

> **Marking Split:** Both definitions with diagram (3) + worked example table (2) + selection mechanism (pin + CP15) (1.5) + engineering relevance (0.5).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Endianness of the data, not the address:** Students often write "Big-Endian means the address starts with the biggest value" — this is **wrong**. Endianness describes *byte storage order*, not the address range.
> 2. **R15 is not just a counter:** Many students treat R15 (PC) as a normal register. In ARM state, reading R15 returns `current + 8` due to pipelining, and writing R15 is equivalent to a branch. This subtlety is a frequent 1–2 mark loss in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **CPSR vs SPSR confusion:** The CPSR is *always* present, but the SPSR is *only* present in the 5 privileged exception modes (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). System and User modes do **not** have an SPSR.
> 4. **Mode-bit encoding:** The 5-bit mode field in CPSR has specific bit patterns (e.g., Supervisor = 0b10011 = 19, not 3). Writing the wrong mode bits leads to "undefined behavior" trap.
> 5. **Alignment for STR/LDR:** The exam loves testing whether `LDR R0, [R1]` will abort if R1 is `0x40000001` (yes — Data Abort). Make this a reflex.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ARM7TDMI Programmer's Model (Memory Format)**

### Registers & Banking
- ⭐ ARM7TDMI has **37 physical 32-bit registers**: 31 general-purpose + 1 CPSR + 5 SPSRs.
- ⭐ **Maximum 18 registers visible** to the programmer at any instant (in any non-FIQ mode).
- ⭐ **R0–R7 are truly unbanked** (identical physical register in all modes).
- ⭐ **R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC** in all modes.
- ⭐ **In ARM state, PC = current instruction address + 8** (pipeline).
- ⭐ **FIQ mode has 7 extra banked registers** (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) for fast context save.

### Processor Modes
- ⭐ **7 modes total**: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor (SVC), Abort, Undefined, System.
- ⭐ **User and System share the same register set** (no banked SP/LR/SPSR).
- ⭐ **SPSR exists in 5 modes only**: FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND.
- ⭐ On exception, CPSR → SPSR_<mode>, PC → vector address, mode bits updated.

### CPSR Layout
- ⭐ Bits **31–28**: Condition flags (N, Z, C, V).
- ⭐ Bit **7**: I (IRQ mask); Bit **6**: F (FIQ mask).
- ⭐ Bit **5**: T (Thumb state).
- ⭐ Bits **4–0**: Mode encoding (M[4:0]).

### Memory Format
- ⭐ **4 GB (2³² bytes) byte-addressable** linear address space.
- ⭐ Address range: `0x00000000` to `0xFFFFFFFF`.
- ⭐ **Word = 4 bytes, must be 4-byte aligned.**
- ⭐ **Half-word = 2 bytes, must be 2-byte aligned.**
- ⭐ **Misaligned accesses cause Data Abort.**
- ⭐ **Big-Endian**: MSB at lowest address.
- ⭐ **Little-Endian**: LSB at lowest address.
- ⭐ Endianness selected by **BIGEND pin** (or CP15 C1 register in newer cores).
- ⭐ Little-Endian is the **default** for most ARM SoCs (LPC2148, STM32, etc.).
- ⭐ Network byte order is **Big-Endian** — byte-swap required on LE systems.

### Engineering Use-Cases to Remember
- ⭐ RTOS context switch saves R0–R15 + CPSR + banked registers.
- ⭐ Bootloader initializes CPSR to SVC mode, disables I and F.
- ⭐ Network code uses htonl/ntohl for endianness portability.
- ⭐ Von Neumann with unified 32-bit data bus (despite Harvard-style code/data split in some implementations).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Applications. ARM Based SoC - General Features - Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture - System Level Interconnection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Embedded Applications. ARM Based SoC - General Features - Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture - System Level Interconnection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Embedded Systems — Formal KTU Definition

An **Embedded System** is a dedicated computing subsystem that is designed to perform a specific, predefined set of functions under real-time, resource-constrained operating conditions. Unlike a general-purpose computer (PC, laptop, server), an embedded system is tightly coupled to the physical hardware it controls and is *not* user-programmable in the conventional sense.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, an embedded system is characterized by the trinity:
- **Application-specific functionality** (purpose-built, not general)
- **Real-time deterministic response** (bounded latency)
- **Tight HW/SW integration** (firmware and silicon co-designed)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Checkpoint:** If a device has a processor but its primary value is *not* computing (e.g., washing machine, smartwatch, ECU in a car), it is an **embedded system**, not a computer.

### 1.2 System-on-Chip (SoC) — The Modern Embedded Paradigm

A **System-on-Chip (SoC)** is a single integrated circuit (IC) that integrates *all* functional components of a complete electronic system onto a single silicon die (substrate). These components typically include:

- One or more **processor cores** (CPU, optionally DSP/GPU/NPU)
- **On-chip memory** (SRAM, ROM, cache, sometimes DRAM controller)
- **Peripheral controllers** (UART, SPI, I²C, USB, Ethernet, CAN, GPIO)
- **External memory interfaces** (DDR, Flash, eMMC controllers)
- **Analog/RF blocks** (PLL, ADC, DAC, RF transceiver)
- **Interconnect fabric** (bus matrix, NoC — Network-on-Chip)
- **Power management unit (PMU)** and clock tree

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Watch:** An SoC is *not* a microcontroller (MCU). A microcontroller is one *type* of component that can be embedded inside an SoC. The SoC concept is broader — it can include CPUs, GPUs, DSPs, and dedicated accelerators all on one die.

### 1.3 ARM-Based SoC — The Industry Dominant Architecture

An **ARM-based SoC** is any SoC whose central processing core(s) are based on the **ARM architecture** (originally Acorn RISC Machine, now Advanced RISC Machines by Arm Holdings). Note that Arm Holdings licenses IP cores; it does not manufacture chips itself — companies like Qualcomm, Samsung, Apple, MediaTek, NXP, STMicroelectronics, and Texas Instruments build SoCs around ARM cores.

#### Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Smart City" Metaphor

Imagine an SoC as a **miniaturized smart city on a single piece of land**:

- **The CPU Core** is the **City Hall** — issues commands, makes decisions, coordinates activity.
- **Cache Memory (L1/L2/L3)** is the **City Hall's internal office** — fastest, closest, smallest.
- **Main Memory (DRAM)** is the **Suburban Warehouse District** — larger but further away, slower access.
- **Buses (AXI/AHB/APB)** are the **Highways and Roads** that connect districts.
- **Peripherals (UART, I²C, GPIO)** are the **Border Checkpoints and Service Stations** that interface with the outside world.
- **Power Management Unit** is the **Power Grid** of the city.
- **Clock Generator / PLL** is the **Metronome** — keeps the entire city synchronized to one heartbeat.

> [!TIP]
> When a student reads the phrase "ARM Cortex-A53 quad-core with Mali-G52 GPU and 12nm process," translate it as: *"A small city with 4 city halls (CPU), 1 graphics bureau (GPU), built using a refined 12nm construction technique."*

### 1.4 Why ARM Dominates Embedded SoCs

| Property | ARM Value | Engineering Benefit |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| ISA Type | **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computing) | Simpler decoder → smaller die, lower power |
| Licensing Model | **IP licensing** (Arm does not fab) | Hundreds of vendors → massive ecosystem |
| Power Efficiency | **mW range** (Cortex-M) to **W range** (Cortex-A) | Battery-friendly mobile/IoT devices |
| Scalability | Cortex-M → Cortex-R → Cortex-A | One architecture spans MCUs to smartphones |
| Toolchain Maturity | GCC, Keil, IAR, ARM DS, LLVM | Industry-standard, university-standard |
| Market Share | > **95%** of smartphone SoCs | Universally relevant for KTU placements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RISC for SoCs?** In embedded systems, power, cost, and predictability outweigh raw peak performance. RISC's fixed-length instructions simplify pipelining, enabling **deterministic instruction timing** — a critical requirement for real-time embedded applications.

### 1.5 Module-3 Topic Roadmap (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

This topic bridges **embedded application design** with **silicon-level integration**. The KTU 2024 module-3 syllabus lists four sub-topics we will cover:

1. **Embedded Applications** — what embedded systems do, and what their SoC requirements are.
2. **ARM Based SoC — General Features** — distinguishing traits of ARM-based SoCs.
3. **Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture** — the internal blocks and how they fit together.
4. **System Level Interconnection** — the on-chip buses (AMBA family) that tie everything together.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Embedded Applications — Categorical Taxonomy

Embedded systems are not monolithic. The KTU syllabus expects you to recognize application domains because *each domain dictates SoC choice*. Below is a layered taxonomy used by industry and academia.

#### 2.1.1 By Performance Class (Klaus Elk 2010 framework, used in KTU references)

| Class | CPU Power | Example SoC/Cores | Application Examples |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **High-performance** | > 1 GHz, MPU-class | ARM Cortex-A9/A15/A53/A72, multi-core | Smartphones, tablets, infotainment |
| **Mid-range** | 100 MHz – 1 GHz, MCU-class | ARM Cortex-R4/R5/R7, Cortex-M7 | Automotive ECUs, HDDs, printers |
| **Low-power / IoT** | < 100 MHz, MCU-class | ARM Cortex-M0/M0+/M3/M4 | Wearables, sensor nodes, smart meters |
| **Ultra-low-power** | kHz range, sub-1V | ARM Cortex-M0+, MSP430-class | Energy harvesting, pacemakers, RFID |

#### 2.1.2 By Application Domain

- **Consumer electronics** — Smart TV SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-A73 in TVs), smart speakers, set-top boxes.
- **Automotive** — ADAS, engine control, infotainment; ARM Cortex-R52 (functional safety) is now the gold standard.
- **Industrial automation** — PLCs, motor control; ARM Cortex-M4 with FPU.
- **Medical** — Pacemakers, infusion pumps, MRI controllers; ultra-low-power Cortex-M0+ typical.
- **Telecommunications** — Base stations, routers, 5G modems; multi-core Cortex-A with DSP co-processors.
- **IoT/Edge AI** — Microcontroller + NPU (e.g., Cortex-M55 + Ethos-U55 NPU for TinyML).

> [!NOTE]
> **Application drives SoC selection**: A wrong choice is catastrophic. Putting a Cortex-A53 in a battery-powered soil-moisture sensor wastes power; putting a Cortex-M0+ in a 4K video decoder creates unacceptable latency. KTU exam questions often ask you to *justify* the choice.

### 2.2 General Features of ARM-Based SoC

ARM-based SoCs exhibit a set of consistent architectural features that distinguish them from general-purpose CPUs and from older microcontroller designs.

#### 2.2.1 Load/Store RISC Architecture

In ARM, **data-processing instructions operate only on registers**, not on memory directly. Memory is accessed only via explicit `LDR` (load) and `STR` (store) instructions.

**Why it matters in SoC design**:
- Decouples CPU pipeline from memory latency → easier to integrate multiple clock domains.
- Simplifies bus arbitration — the interconnect sees *predictable* memory access patterns.
- Allows energy-efficient designs: only wake memory when LDR/STR is executed.

#### 2.2.2 Fixed-Length Instructions

Most ARM instructions (in ARM mode) are 32 bits; Thumb/Thumb-2 are 16/16-32. Fixed width simplifies **fetch + decode** logic → smaller silicon area → lower cost per die.

#### 2.2.3 Large Register File (Visible to Compiler)

ARM has **16 user-visible 32-bit registers** (R0–R15, where R13=SP, R14=LR, R15=PC). This is generous compared to 8-register ISAs (e.g., 8051) and supports efficient function calls and compiler optimization.

#### 2.2.4 Conditional Execution (Almost) Everywhere

Nearly every ARM instruction can be executed conditionally based on the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** flags (N, Z, C, V). This eliminates many branches → smaller code footprint, no pipeline flush on short conditions.

#### 2.2.5 Low-Power Operating Modes

ARM provides multiple power-saving modes for SoC use:
- **Normal mode** — full operation.
- **Idle mode** — CPU clock gated, peripherals still running.
- **Sleep / Standby** — most clocks off, RTC running.
- **Deep Sleep / Power-down** — core voltage off, wakeup via interrupt.

#### 2.2.6 Configurable Cache Coherency

In multi-core ARM SoCs, the **Coherent Interconnect (CCI-400, CCI-500, CCI-700)** ensures L1/L2 caches of different cores see consistent data. This is critical for symmetric multiprocessing (SMP).

#### 2.2.7 Integrated Interrupt Controller

ARM SoCs typically use the **Generic Interrupt Controller (GIC)** — GICv1, GICv2, GICv3, GICv4. GIC supports:
- **SGI** (Software Generated Interrupts) — inter-core communication.
- **PPI** (Private Peripheral Interrupts) — per-core.
- **SPI** (Shared Peripheral Interrupts) — system-wide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GIC is the brain of interrupt handling in modern ARM SoCs.** A KTU 14-mark question may ask you to compare GICv2 vs GICv3 — know that GICv3 adds: 1) message-based interrupts via LPI, 2) Affinity routing, 3) better support for >8 cores.

### 2.3 Basic System-on-Chip (SoC) Architecture — Block-by-Block

A generic ARM-based SoC can be decomposed into **five functional layers**. We will use this layered model throughout the module.

#### Layer 1: Application Subsystem

- **Application CPU cores** — Cortex-A series (e.g., quad-core Cortex-A53 in Snapdragon 410).
- **Application cache** — typically 32 KB L1 D-cache + 32 KB I-cache per core, 512 KB – 4 MB L2 shared.
- **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** — required for running Linux/Android; Cortex-M has MPU instead.
- **NEON SIMD engine** — 128-bit SIMD for media codecs.
- **GPU** (optional but common) — Mali, Adreno, PowerVR for graphics.

#### Layer 2: Real-Time Subsystem (Optional, common in automotive/industrial SoCs)

- **Cortex-R cores** — hard real-time, deterministic latency.
- **Tightly Coupled Memory (TCM)** — single-cycle access, no cache misses.
- **Lock-step dual cores** — for functional safety (ASIL-D in ISO 26262).

#### Layer 3: Microcontroller Subsystem

- **Cortex-M core** — sensor hub, always-on domain.
- **Wakeup logic** — runs when Cortex-A is in deep sleep.

#### Layer 4: System Interconnect

- **AMBA AXI** for high-speed masters (CPU, GPU, DMA).
- **AMBA AHB** for medium-speed blocks.
- **AMBA APB** for low-speed peripherals (UART, I²C, timers).
- **Bus matrix / NoC** as the crossbar switch.

#### Layer 5: Peripheral & I/O Subsystem

- High-speed: USB 3.0, PCIe, SATA, Ethernet (1G/10G), HDMI, MIPI.
- Mid-speed: SD/MMC, CAN, SPI, I²C, UART.
- Low-speed: GPIO, ADC, PWM, RTC, watchdog, timers.

### 2.4 System Level Interconnection — The AMBA Bus Family

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is Arm Holdings' open-standard on-chip interconnect specification. It defines how IP blocks (CPU, memory, peripherals) communicate within an SoC. AMBA is the *de facto* industry standard — used in billions of chips.

#### 2.4.1 AMBA Generations and Variants

| AMBA Spec | Year | Key Traits | Typical Use |
|-----------|------|------------|-------------|
| **ASB** | 1990s | System bus, older | Obsolete |
| **APB** (v1, v2) | 1990s | Simple, low-power, no bursts | Peripheral access (UART, timers) |
| **AHB** (v1, v2) | 1990s–2000s | Pipelined, single-edge clock | Mid-speed (CPU ↔ SRAM) |
| **AHB-Lite** | 2000s | Single master variant of AHB | Sub-systems (microcontroller + peripherals) |
| **AXI3** | 2003 | Multiple outstanding, bursts | High-speed (CPU ↔ DDR) |
| **AXI4** | 2010 | Widens burst to 4 KB, QoS | Modern SoCs |
| **AXI4-Lite** | 2010 | Address-mapped register access | Control/status registers |
| **AXI4-Stream** | 2010 | Unidirectional data flow | DSP, video, DMA |
| **ACE / ACE-Lite** | 2011 | Cache coherency extensions | Multi-core coherency |
| **CHI** (Coherent Hub Interface) | 2013 | Network-on-Chip based | Large multi-core SoCs (>8 cores) |
| **AMBA 5 CHI** | 2017+ | 256+ cores support | Data-center / server class |

#### 2.4.2 AMBA AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface) — The Modern Backbone

AXI is the most-tested AXI family in KTU exams. Five independent channels:
- **AW** — Write Address channel
- **W** — Write Data channel
- **B** — Write Response channel
- **AR** — Read Address channel
- **R** — Read Data channel

**Why 5 channels?** Decouples address and data phases → multiple outstanding transactions → higher throughput.

> [!TIP]
> **AXI Key Exam Points:**
> - Up to **256 outstanding transactions** per master.
> - Burst length up to **4 KB** (16 beats × 64-bit = 512 bits/beat max; commonly 4 × 64 = 256 bits).
> - Supports **out-of-order completion**.
> - QoS signals for traffic prioritization.
> - Independent read and write, no shared address/data bus.

#### 2.4.3 AMBA AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)

AHB is older than AXI but still used. Features:
- **Single shared address/data bus** (multiplexed).
- Burst support: 4, 8, 16 beats (INCR, WRAP).
- Single-cycle bus handover.
- Split and retry transactions.
- Used in MCU-class sub-systems.

#### 2.4.4 AMBA APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)

APB is the simplest. It is **not pipelined**, has **no bursts**, and is used for low-bandwidth peripherals.

- Two-cycle transfer: **SETUP → ENABLE**.
- One master (the AHB-to-APB bridge).
- Up to 32-bit data.
- Low power (no clock required for non-active peripherals in APB v3+).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** "Bridge between AHB and APB." The AHB-to-APB bridge handles the protocol translation. Only one peripheral is selected at a time (via `PSELx`). Data transfer happens in ENABLE phase.

#### 2.4.5 Bridge-Based Interconnection Topology

A typical ARM SoC uses **bridges** to connect busses of different speeds:

```
Cortex-A / GPU  <--AXI4-->  AXI Interconnect  <--AXI4-->  DDR Controller
                                      |
                                      +--AXI4--->  AXI-to-AHB Bridge ---> AHB Bus ---> SRAM, DMA
                                                                              |
                                                                              +--AHB-to-APB Bridge ---> APB Bus ---> UART, I²C, Timers, GPIO
```

This is the **classic 3-tier AMBA hierarchy** that you must draw in exams.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the *only* engineering math you need to know for this topic. They are *not* arbitrary — they govern real chip-design trade-offs. Memorize the units, not just the symbols.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Bus Bandwidth** (Peak) | $BW_{peak} = W \cdot f_{bus}$ | bits/s | $W$ = bus width (bits), $f_{bus}$ = bus clock (Hz) |
| 2 | **Bus Throughput** (Effective) | $BW_{eff} = BW_{peak} \cdot \eta$ | bits/s | $\eta$ = protocol efficiency (0 to 1) |
| 3 | **Bytes per second** | $BW_{bytes} = \dfrac{W \cdot f_{bus}}{8}$ | Bytes/s | 1 Byte = 8 bits |
| 4 | **AXI Transaction Time** | $T_{txn} = T_{addr} + T_{data}$ | seconds | Address + Data phases |
| 5 | **Burst Transfer Time** | $T_{burst} = T_{overhead} + \dfrac{N \cdot W}{f_{bus}}$ | seconds | $N$ = number of beats |
| 6 | **AMBA APB Transfer Time** | $T_{APB} = 2 \cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | 2 cycles (SETUP + ENABLE) |
| 7 | **AMBA AHB Transfer Time** (single) | $T_{AHB} = T_{addr} + T_{data}$ | seconds | Pipelined: back-to-back possible |
| 8 | **Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | seconds | |
| 9 | **Dynamic Power** (CMOS) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = capacitance |
| 10 | **Static Power** (Leakage) | $P_{stat} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{dd}$ | Watts | Dominates in 28 nm and below |
| 11 | **Memory Latency** | $L_{mem} = N_{cycles} \cdot T_{clk}$ | seconds | |
| 12 | **Address Decoding** | $A_{decoded} = A_{chip} \cdot A_{offset}$ | lines | Chip select when bits match |
| 13 | **Interrupt Latency** | $L_{IRQ} = T_{recog} + T_{vec}$ | seconds | Recognition + vector fetch |
| 14 | **MIPS Rating** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \cdot 10^{6}}$ | millions of instr/s | $CPI$ = cycles per instruction |
| 15 | **CoreMark Score** (industry metric) | $\text{CoreMark} = \dfrac{\text{iterations}}{T_{run}}$ | – | Higher is better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE for tables:** All vertical bars `$\vert$` and `$\mid$` in formula cells are typeset using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. **Never write `\|` or raw `|` inside table rows.**

### 2.6 Engineering Trade-off Map — Real-World Utility

| Design Decision | Impact on SoC | Real Example |
|----------------|---------------|--------------|
| Increase bus width (32 → 64) | Doubles peak bandwidth | Cortex-A53 64-bit vs Cortex-M4 32-bit |
| Increase clock (200 → 800 MHz) | 4× bandwidth, but 4× dynamic power | DDR3L-800 vs DDR3L-1600 |
| Use AXI instead of AHB | 2-3× throughput, but more area | Modern AP vs MCU-class sub-system |
| Add cache | Reduces average memory latency by 5-20× | 4 MB L3 in Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 |
| Add coherency fabric | Enables SMP, but ~20% area overhead | CCI-500 in Snapdragon 835 |
| Use APB for peripheral | Saves power, lower latency budget | Every UART in every SoC |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Note for Examiner Valuation:** All mathematical transitions below are explicit. There are *no* "obviously, we can show that" shortcuts. Every step contains a textual conversion logic row. KTU examiners award partial marks for *each correct intermediate step*, so this is also a guide for how to write in your exam copy.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Bus Bandwidth of an AMBA AXI Channel

**Problem statement:** An ARM Cortex-A53 SoC has a 64-bit AXI4 interconnect running at 533 MHz. Calculate the peak read bandwidth, the peak write bandwidth, and the total peak bidirectional bandwidth in MB/s (Mebibytes per second, where 1 MiB = $2^{20}$ Bytes).

**Given data:**
- Bus width $W = 64$ bits.
- Bus clock $f_{bus} = 533$ MHz $= 533 \times 10^{6}$ Hz.
- 1 Byte = 8 bits.
- 1 MiB = $2^{20}$ Bytes $= 1\,048\,576$ Bytes.

#### Step 1: Identify the applicable formula

From the KTU Formula Sheet (row #1):

$$BW_{peak} = W \cdot f_{bus}$$

*Logic:* Bandwidth is the product of "how wide the pipe is" ($W$ in bits) and "how many pipes pass per second" ($f_{bus}$ in Hz). The result is bits per second.

#### Step 2: Calculate peak read bandwidth in bits/s

$$BW_{read,bits} = 64 \times 533 \times 10^{6}$$

$$BW_{read,bits} = 64 \times 533 \times 10^{6} = 34\,112 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$$

*Logic:* Multiply 64 (the bus width) by 533 million (the clock). The unit is bits per second because we have not yet divided by 8.

#### Step 3: Convert bits/s to Bytes/s

$$BW_{read,bytes} = \dfrac{64 \times 533 \times 10^{6}}{8}$$

$$BW_{read,bytes} = 8 \times 533 \times 10^{6} = 4\,264 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s}$$

*Logic:* Divide by 8 to convert bits to bytes.

#### Step 4: Convert Bytes/s to MiB/s

$$BW_{read,MiB} = \dfrac{4\,264 \times 10^{6}}{2^{20}} = \dfrac{4\,264 \times 10^{6}}{1\,048\,576}$$

$$BW_{read,MiB} \approx 4.066 \text{ MiB/s}$$

*Logic:* 1 MiB = $2^{20}$ Bytes = 1,048,576 Bytes. Divide Bytes/s by this to get MiB/s.

#### Step 5: Note that read and write channels are independent in AXI

AXI4 has **separate** read and write data channels. Therefore, the peak *bidirectional* bandwidth is the sum of peak read and peak write (assuming symmetric traffic):

$$BW_{total,bits} = BW_{read,bits} + BW_{write,bits} = 2 \times 34\,112 \times 10^{6} = 68\,224 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}$$

#### Step 6: Convert bidirectional to MiB/s

$$BW_{total,MiB} = \dfrac{68\,224 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 2^{20}} = \dfrac{8\,528 \times 10^{6}}{1\,048\,576}$$

$$BW_{total,MiB} \approx 8.13 \text{ MiB/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** $BW_{read} \approx 4.066$ MiB/s; $BW_{total,bidi} \approx 8.13$ MiB/s. Modern DDR4 controllers actually deliver 12.8 – 25.6 GB/s, but those use wider buses (128-bit) and higher clocks (1600 MHz DDR), and use multiple AXI ports. Our calculation only considered *one* AXI channel.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — APB Transfer Time and Latency Budget

**Problem statement:** The APB bus in an ARM Cortex-M0+ sub-system runs at 24 MHz. A peripheral register read takes one APB transfer. How much time does a single register read take? How many register reads can be done in 1 ms?

**Given data:**
- APB clock $f_{APB} = 24$ MHz.
- 1 APB transfer = 2 clock cycles (SETUP + ENABLE).

#### Step 1: Find clock period

$$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{APB}} = \dfrac{1}{24 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{clk} \approx 41.667 \text{ ns}$$

*Logic:* The period is the reciprocal of frequency.

#### Step 2: Apply the APB transfer time formula (row #6 of Formula Sheet)

$$T_{APB} = 2 \cdot T_{clk}$$

$$T_{APB} = 2 \times 41.667 \text{ ns} = 83.333 \text{ ns}$$

*Logic:* APB is a two-phase protocol. The master asserts the address and control signals in SETUP, the peripheral latches in ENABLE. Both phases are 1 clock cycle each.

#### Step 3: Calculate number of register reads in 1 ms

$$N_{reads} = \dfrac{T_{budget}}{T_{APB}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{83.333 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$N_{reads} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{83.333 \times 10^{-9}} = 12\,000 \text{ reads/ms}$$

*Logic:* Divide the available time budget by the time per read.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why APB is fine for UART (≤115,200 baud) but *insufficient* for a 1080p video stream (~3 GB/s). AXI is required for high-bandwidth masters.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — AMBA AHB Burst Transfer Time

**Problem statement:** An AHB master performs a 4-beat INCR4 burst transfer. The bus is 32 bits wide at 100 MHz. The address phase takes 1 cycle, and each data beat takes 1 cycle. There is no wait state. Calculate the total transfer time and the effective bandwidth.

**Given data:**
- Burst length $N = 4$ beats.
- Bus width $W = 32$ bits $= 4$ Bytes.
- Clock frequency $f_{AHB} = 100$ MHz.

#### Step 1: Determine total transfer time

The transfer time is the sum of the address phase and the data phase (row #7 of Formula Sheet):

$$T_{AHB} = T_{addr} + N \cdot T_{data}$$

$$T_{AHB} = 1 \cdot T_{clk} + 4 \cdot T_{clk} = 5 \cdot T_{clk}$$

#### Step 2: Compute $T_{clk}$

$$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 10 \text{ ns}$$

#### Step 3: Compute $T_{AHB}$

$$T_{AHB} = 5 \times 10 \text{ ns} = 50 \text{ ns}$$

#### Step 4: Compute total bytes transferred

$$\text{Bytes}_{total} = N \cdot \dfrac{W}{8} = 4 \times \dfrac{32}{8} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ Bytes}$$

#### Step 5: Compute effective bandwidth

$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{Bytes}_{total}}{T_{AHB}} = \dfrac{16 \text{ Bytes}}{50 \text{ ns}}$$

$$BW_{eff} = \dfrac{16}{50 \times 10^{-9}} = 320 \times 10^{6} \text{ Bytes/s} = 320 \text{ MB/s}$$

*Logic:* Effective bandwidth accounts for the address phase overhead. If we ignored address phase and assumed a pure streaming scenario, peak would be $4 \times 32/8 \div 10\text{ns} = 1.6 \text{ GB/s}$. The 1 address cycle reduces throughput by 20% here.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Power of an AXI Bus Link

**Problem statement:** An AXI bus operates at 800 MHz, with 64-bit width, 1.0 V supply, load capacitance $C = 5$ pF, and switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate the dynamic power consumption in mW.

**Given data:**
- $V_{dd} = 1.0$ V.
- $f = 800 \times 10^{6}$ Hz.
- $C = 5 \times 10^{-12}$ F.
- $\alpha = 0.2$.

#### Step 1: Apply the dynamic power formula (row #9 of Formula Sheet)

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$$

#### Step 2: Substitute values

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.0)^{2} \times 800 \times 10^{6}$$

#### Step 3: Compute step by step

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.0 \times 800 \times 10^{6}$$

$$= 0.2 \times 5 \times 800 \times 10^{-12+6}$$

$$= 0.2 \times 5 \times 800 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 0.2 \times 4000 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 800 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$$

#### Step 4: Convert to mW

$$P_{dyn} = 0.8 \text{ mW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** The $V_{dd}^{2}$ term shows why SoC voltages have dropped from 3.3 V (1990s) to 0.7–0.9 V (modern 7nm). Halving voltage quarters the dynamic power — this is the single biggest power-saving lever in modern SoCs.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Address Decoding for Memory-Mapped Peripheral

**Problem statement:** An ARM SoC has a 4 GB address space ($2^{32}$). A peripheral block needs 64 KB. The designer wants to place it at base address `0x4000_0000`. Calculate the chip-select logic and determine the highest address in the block.

**Given data:**
- Address space: 32-bit.
- Block size: 64 KB $= 2^{16}$ Bytes.
- Base address: `0x4000_0000`.

#### Step 1: Express base in binary

$$0x4000\_0000 = 0100\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2$$

#### Step 2: Identify the upper address bits

Since the block is 64 KB, the lower 16 bits (`A[15:0]`) are *offset* within the block. The upper 16 bits (`A[31:16]`) must equal `0x4000` to select this block.

#### Step 3: Compute highest address

$$\text{End} = \text{Base} + \text{Size} - 1$$

$$\text{End} = 0x4000\_0000 + 0x0001\_0000 - 1 = 0x4000\_FFFF$$

#### Step 4: Construct chip-select logic

$$\overline{CS} = \overline{(A_{31} \cdot \overline{A_{30}} \cdot \overline{A_{29}} \cdot \ldots \cdot \overline{A_{16}})}$$

In words: assert chip select when bit 31 = 1, bits 30-16 = 0. Lower 16 bits are don't-cares for the chip select.

*Logic:* The high address bits serve as a "page" selector. The lower bits select registers within the peripheral.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AXI vs AHB vs APB

This is a high-yield exam table. The KTU 2024 syllabus often asks for a 7-mark comparison.

| Parameter | AXI4 | AHB | APB |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| Bus width (typical) | 64 / 128 bits | 32 / 64 bits | 8 / 16 / 32 bits |
| Pipelined | Yes (multi-channel) | Yes (address + data) | No (2-phase) |
| Burst support | Up to 16 beats (4 KB) | INCR4/8/16, WRAP4/8/16 | None |
| Outstanding transactions | Up to 256 | Limited (1) | 1 (no pipelining) |
| Out-of-order completion | Yes | No | No |
| Independent read/write | Yes (5 channels) | No (shared) | No |
| Multiple masters | Yes (via interconnect) | Yes (with arbiter) | No (single master) |
| Address phase | Separate AR/AW | 1 cycle | 1 cycle (SETUP) |
| Data phase | Separate R/W + B | Pipelined, 1 cycle | 1 cycle (ENABLE) |
| QoS signaling | Yes (AWQOS, ARQOS) | No | No |
| Power (relative) | High | Medium | Low |
| Typical use | CPU↔DDR, GPU, DMA | SRAM, MCU sub-system | UART, I²C, GPIO, Timers |
| Designed by | ARM Ltd. | ARM Ltd. | ARM Ltd. |
| First introduced | 2003 (AXI3), 2010 (AXI4) | 1990s (AMBA 2.0) | 1990s (AMBA 1.0) |

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Write Transaction Flow on an AMBA AXI4 Bus

This is a **timing-flow diagram** showing the *5 independent channels* of AXI during a single write transaction. The key insight: address and data can be pipelined, so multiple writes are in flight simultaneously.

```mermaid

    participant S as AXI SLAVE
    Note over M,S: AXI4 WRITE TRANSACTION 5 INDEPENDENT CHANNELS

    Note over M,S: ADDRESS PHASE
    M->>S: W CHANNEL: WVALID WREADY WDATA WSTRB WLAST

    S->>M: B CHANNEL: BVALID BREADY BRESP
    Note over M,S: WRITE RESPONSE OKAY OR EXOKAY
    Note over M,S: SEPARATE READ TRANSACTION 3 CHANNELS
    M->>S: AR CHANNEL: ARVALID ARREADY ARADDR ARBURST ARSIZE
    S->>M: R CHANNEL: RVALID RREADY RDATA RLAST
    Note over M,S: READ CAN HAPPEN PARALLEL TO WRITE
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — APB Write Transfer State Machine

This is the classic 3-state machine (`IDLE → SETUP → ENABLE`) for APB transactions, taken directly from the AMBA APB specification (ARM IHI 0024).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> SETUP : PSEL asserted and transfer starts
    SETUP --> ENABLE : on rising edge of PCLK after SETUP
    ENABLE --> SETUP : if more than one transfer needed
    ENABLE --> IDLE : on rising edge of PCLK if no more transfers
    SETUP --> IDLE : PSEL deasserted
    note right of IDLE : Default state, no transfer active
    note right of SETUP : Address and control signals driven
    note right of ENABLE : Slave samples data on ENABLE phase
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A = 3 marks each (short answer), Part B = 14 marks each (full structured answer with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B). Each sub-part is 7 marks. Model answers are exhaustive — written exactly as a KTU topper's answer script would be.

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### Question A.1 [3 Marks] — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEBT522]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Microcontroller (MCU) and a System-on-Chip (SoC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 80 words):**

A **Microcontroller (MCU)** is a single IC containing a CPU, small RAM/ROM, and basic peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO) on one chip. It is designed for control-oriented, low-power applications. An **SoC**, in contrast, integrates an entire system — multiple CPUs, GPU, DSP, large memory controllers, high-speed I/O (USB, PCIe, Ethernet) — on a single die. An MCU is essentially a *subset* of what an SoC contains. SoCs target high-performance, multi-function devices like smartphones, while MCUs target embedded control tasks in appliances and sensors.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Scoring:** Full 3 marks awarded only if you mention *both* "MCU is a subset" *and* "SoC integrates multiple complex IPs (CPU+GPU+DSP)". A 1-line definition gets only 1 mark.

#### Question A.2 [3 Marks] — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEBT522]`

**Q: List any three general features of ARM-based SoCs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 70 words):**

1. **RISC Load/Store Architecture** — Data processing operates only on registers; memory is accessed only via explicit load/store instructions, simplifying pipelining and reducing power.

2. **Large Register File** — 16 user-visible 32-bit registers (R0–R15) support efficient function calls and compiler optimization.

3. **Multiple Power-Saving Modes** — Normal, Idle, Sleep, and Deep Sleep modes enable fine-grained power management critical for battery-operated embedded devices.

> [!NOTE]
> Acceptable alternatives: Fixed-length 32-bit instructions, conditional execution on most instructions, integrated GIC, low die area due to RISC, etc.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **either** Q-A **or** Q-B.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEEBT522 - Modified]`

**Q-A: With a neat block diagram, explain the basic architecture of an ARM-based System-on-Chip (SoC). List the major functional blocks and explain the role of the AMBA interconnect.**

##### Q-A (a) [7 Marks] — Draw and explain the basic SoC architecture with major functional blocks

**Model Answer:**

The basic architecture of an ARM-based SoC consists of the following major functional blocks, organized into a layered hierarchy:

**1. Application Processing Subsystem (APS):**
- Contains one or more **ARM Cortex-A** CPU cores (e.g., quad-core Cortex-A53).
- Each core has private **L1 cache** (split I-cache and D-cache, typically 32 KB each).
- A shared **L2 cache** (256 KB – 4 MB) and sometimes L3 (up to 8 MB).
- **MMU** for virtual memory (required for Linux/Android).
- Optional **NEON SIMD** engine for media acceleration.
- Optional **GPU** (Mali, Adreno) for graphics.

**Role:** Runs the high-level operating system (Linux, Android, RTOS) and user applications.

**2. Real-Time / Safety Subsystem (RTS):**
- Contains **ARM Cortex-R** cores (e.g., Cortex-R5 dual-core in lock-step for ASIL-D).
- Uses **Tightly Coupled Memory (TCM)** for deterministic, single-cycle access.
- Used in automotive ECUs, industrial motor control.

**3. Microcontroller Subsystem (MCS):**
- **ARM Cortex-M** core (e.g., Cortex-M0+, M4, M7).
- Functions as a **sensor hub** or **always-on domain**.
- Operates while APS is in deep sleep.

**4. Memory Subsystem:**
- **Boot ROM** (for first-stage bootloader).
- **On-chip SRAM** (32 KB – 1 MB) for low-latency scratchpad.
- **External memory controllers**: DDR, LPDDR, Flash, eMMC.
- **Cache Coherent Interconnect (CCI)** for multi-core coherency.

**5. Peripheral & I/O Subsystem:**
- **High-speed I/O**: USB 3.x, PCIe, SATA, Ethernet, HDMI, MIPI.
- **Mid-speed I/O**: SD/MMC, CAN, SPI, I²C, UART.
- **Low-speed I/O**: GPIO, ADC, PWM, RTC, watchdog, timers.

**6. Power, Clock, and Reset Infrastructure:**
- **PMU (Power Management Unit)** — voltage scaling, power-gating.
- **Clock tree** with **PLL** and dividers for different clock domains.
- **Reset controller** for cold-start and warm-reset sequences.

**Block Diagram (as would be drawn in exam):**

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                       ARM SoC DIE                        |
|                                                          |
|  +----------+    +----------+    +----------+            |
|  | Cortex-A |    | Cortex-R |    | Cortex-M |            |
|  | (APS)    |    | (RTS)    |    | (MCS)    |            |
|  +----+-----+    +-----+----+    +-----+----+            |
|       |   \         |     \          |                  |
|  +----v-+ +v---+  +-v---+  v---+   +-v---+               |
|  | L1   | |MMU |  | TCM |  |FPU |   |PMU  |               |
|  +------+ +----+  +-----+  +----+   +-----+               |
|       \         AMBA AXI/AHB/APB Interconnect            |
|                       |                                  |
|  +-------v-----------v-------------v--------+             |
|  |       Memory & I/O Subsystem            |             |
|  |  - DDR Ctrl  - Flash Ctrl  - USB       |             |
|  |  - SRAM      - ROM         - Ethernet  |             |
|  |  - SD/MMC    - CAN         - SPI/I2C   |             |
|  +-----------------------------------------+             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

##### Q-A (b) [7 Marks] — Explain the role of AMBA interconnect in SoC

**Model Answer:**

The **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** is Arm's open on-chip interconnect specification. It serves as the **central nervous system** of the SoC, connecting all IPs together.

**Roles of AMBA:**

1. **Standardization:** AMBA provides a common protocol so that IP blocks from different vendors (e.g., a third-party USB controller + Arm's CPU) interoperate without custom glue logic.

2. **Performance Tiers:** AMBA defines a hierarchy of bus types, each tuned for a different performance-power point:
   - **AXI4** — high-performance, pipelined, burst, up to 256 outstanding, used by Cortex-A, GPU, DDR.
   - **AHB** — medium performance, single shared bus, used by MCU sub-system, SRAM.
   - **APB** — low-power, non-pipelined, used by simple peripherals (UART, timers).

3. **Bus Bridging:** AMBA supports **bridges** (AXI→AHB, AHB→APB) that translate protocols when crossing performance tiers, allowing high-speed and low-speed IPs to coexist on the same die.

4. **Quality of Service (QoS):** AXI4 carries QoS signals (AWQOS, ARQOS) that allow traffic prioritization — critical for real-time video where late frames cause visible glitches.

5. **Cache Coherency Extensions:** ACE and CHI extensions of AMBA provide hardware coherency across multiple CPU clusters.

6. **Address Map and Decode:** The AMBA interconnect contains the **address decoder** that maps physical addresses to specific peripherals. A 32-bit address space is partitioned: e.g., 0x0000_0000–0x000F_FFFF for boot ROM, 0x4000_0000–0x4FFF_FFFF for peripherals, 0x6000_0000–0x9FFF_FFFF for DRAM.

7. **Arbitration:** When multiple masters (CPU + GPU + DMA) request the bus simultaneously, the AMBA interconnect performs **arbitration** using a configurable policy (fixed priority, round-robin, weighted).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "AMBA is a bus" — this loses 1 mark. AMBA is a **specification** that *defines* multiple bus types (AXI, AHB, APB). Be precise with terminology.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEEBT522 - Modified]`

**Q-B: Explain the AMBA bus family in detail. Compare AXI, AHB, and APB with respect to their features, performance, and typical use cases.**

##### Q-B (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the AMBA bus family (AXI, AHB, APB)

**Model Answer:**

The **AMBA bus family** is a set of on-chip bus protocols standardized by Arm Holdings. Each protocol targets a different performance/power point in the SoC.

**1. AMBA AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface):**
- Introduced in AMBA 3.0 (2003); current version is AXI4 (2010).
- **Five independent channels**: AW (write address), W (write data), B (write response), AR (read address), R (read data).
- **Burst-based** with up to 16 beats per burst (4 KB max).
- **Up to 256 outstanding transactions** per master.
- **Out-of-order completion** supported.
- **Address/data phase decoupled** — high throughput.
- **QoS signals** for traffic prioritization.
- Used by: CPU cores, GPU, DDR controller, high-speed DMA.

**2. AMBA AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):**
- Introduced in AMBA 2.0 (1990s).
- **Single shared address + data bus** (multiplexed).
- Burst support: INCR4/8/16, WRAP4/8/16.
- **Pipelined** — address phase can overlap with data phase.
- **Split & retry** for handling slow slaves.
- Used by: MCU sub-system, on-chip SRAM, mid-speed DMA.

**3. AMBA APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):**
- Introduced in AMBA 1.0 (1990s).
- **Two-phase protocol**: SETUP (1 cycle) + ENABLE (1 cycle).
- **Not pipelined**, no bursts.
- **Single master** (the AHB-to-APB bridge).
- **Low power** — clock can be gated when not active.
- **Simple interface** — minimal decoder logic.
- Used by: UART, I²C, SPI, GPIO, timers, watchdog, RTC.

**Bridge-Based Hierarchy:**

In a real SoC, these three busses are connected via **bridges**:

- **AXI ↔ AHB bridge** — protocol translation + clock domain crossing.
- **AHB ↔ APB bridge** — single-master APB access from AHB.

This forms a **3-tier hierarchy** (see SECTION 4.2 for diagram) that balances performance, area, and power.

**ACE/CHI extensions:**
- **ACE (AXI Coherency Extensions)** — adds Snoop channels for hardware cache coherency.
- **CHI (Coherent Hub Interface)** — for >8-core SoCs, uses packet-based network on chip.

##### Q-B (b) [7 Marks] — Compare AXI, AHB, and APB

**Model Answer (Use this comparison table + brief prose):**

| Parameter | AXI4 | AHB | APB |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| Bus width | 64/128 bit | 32/64 bit | 8/16/32 bit |
| Channels | 5 independent | 1 shared (multiplexed) | 1 shared (2-phase) |
| Pipelining | Yes, multi-channel | Yes (1-deep) | No |
| Burst support | Up to 16 beats | INCR/WRAP4/8/16 | None |
| Outstanding txns | Up to 256 | 1 | 1 |
| Out-of-order | Yes | No | No |
| Multiple masters | Yes | Yes (with arbiter) | No (single master) |
| QoS | Yes | No | No |
| Power | High | Medium | Very Low |
| Area | Largest | Medium | Smallest |
| Typical use | CPU, GPU, DDR | MCU sub-system, SRAM | UART, GPIO, timers |

**Synthesis:**
- **AXI4** is the workhorse of modern SoCs — chosen wherever bandwidth and concurrency matter (multi-core CPU clusters, GPU, video encoder, DDR access).
- **AHB** is a legacy bus still used inside microcontroller-class sub-systems. It is cheaper and simpler than AXI but cannot match AXI's throughput.
- **APB** is the universal choice for slow, register-based peripherals. Its simplicity translates to tiny silicon area and negligible power, which is why almost every SoC contains an APB segment for legacy peripherals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Drawing APB as having a clock-free ENABLE phase — losing 1 mark. APB's ENABLE phase *is* clocked (1 PCLK cycle). The data transfer happens on the rising edge of PCLK in ENABLE.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **WHERE STUDENTS COMMONLY LOSE MARKS (cumulative impact: 4–6 marks lost on a 14-mark question):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "bus" with "protocol"** — "AXI is a bus" is a 1-mark deduction. Correct: "AXI is a bus *protocol* defined by AMBA; the AXI interconnect is the *implementation*."
> 2. **Forgetting the 5 channels of AXI** — students often say "AXI has 2 channels (read, write)" — but there are 5 (AW, W, B, AR, R). 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Confusing APB's 2-phase with clock gating** — APB still uses a clock, just 1 cycle per phase. 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Not drawing bridges in the block diagram** — a 3-tier AMBA diagram without AXI-to-AHB and AHB-to-APB bridges loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Writing "AMBA is by Intel" or "AXI is a 32-bit bus"** — both wrong; AXI4 is 64/128 bit, and AMBA is by Arm. 1 mark each.
> 6. **No example SoC** — citing a real SoC (e.g., "Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 uses Cortex-A510/A710/A715 cores connected via CHI fabric") elevates a 7-mark answer to 9–10 marks in examiner perception.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final rapid-revision checklist (this is what you read 10 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Embedded System** = dedicated, application-specific, real-time, HW/SW integrated computing subsystem.
- [ ] **SoC** = entire electronic system integrated onto one die (CPU + memory + peripherals + interconnect).
- [ ] **ARM** = RISC ISA, licensed as IP; 16 user-visible 32-bit registers; load/store architecture.
- [ ] **AMBA** = Arm's on-chip interconnect standard; 3 main bus protocols: AXI, AHB, APB.
- [ ] **AXI4** = 5 channels (AW, W, B, AR, R), pipelined, burst, out-of-order, 256 outstanding — used by high-speed masters.
- [ ] **AHB** = shared multiplexed bus, bursts INCR4/8/16, pipelined, used by mid-speed blocks.
- [ ] **APB** = 2-phase (SETUP + ENABLE), no bursts, single master, low power — used by slow peripherals.
- [ ] **3-tier AMBA hierarchy** = AXI → AHB → APB, connected via bridges.
- [ ] **GIC** (Generic Interrupt Controller) = SGI, PPI, SPI; used for IRQ management in modern ARM SoCs.
- [ ] **Bus bandwidth formula**: $BW = W \cdot f$ (bits/s); divide by 8 for Bytes/s; divide by $2^{20}$ for MiB/s.
- [ ] **APB transfer time** = 2 clock cycles.
- [ ] **Dynamic power** $\propto \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ — voltage-squared is the dominant lever.
- [ ] **Real ARM SoC examples to cite**: Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos, Apple A-series, NXP i.MX8, STM32MP1.
- [ ] **Cortex family mnemonic**: **A**pplication (Cortex-A, MMU, Linux), **R**eal-time (Cortex-R, TCM, no MMU, automotive), **M**icrocontroller (Cortex-M, MPU only, bare-metal).
- [ ] **Why ARM dominates**: RISC = small die, low power, IP licensing = ecosystem, scalability from M0 to A72.
- [ ] **Application drives SoC choice**: IoT → Cortex-M0+/M4; smartphone → Cortex-A53/72/710; automotive ECU → Cortex-R5; data center → Neoverse.

> [!TIP]
> **Topper's Strategy:** If asked for a 14-mark answer and the topic is "AMBA," always draw the **3-tier hierarchy diagram first** *before* writing prose. A diagram earns you 2-3 marks even if the prose is incomplete, because it demonstrates structural understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
