topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory Organisation - SoC Design Approach. (Class Assignment/Seminar Topics: SoC Interconnect Customization and Configuration, Trade-off Analysis on Reconfigurable Parallelism, Case Studies on Design Approach) Introduction to High Performance Computing - GPU Hardware Architecture - Comparison with CPU

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 System-on-Chip (SoC) — Formal Definition

An **System-on-Chip (SoC)** is an integrated circuit that consolidates **all essential components of a complete computing system** onto a single silicon die. According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus, an SoC typically integrates the **central processing unit (CPU)**, **graphics processing unit (GPU)**, **memory controllers**, **peripheral controllers**, **on-chip caches**, **interconnect fabrics**, and **dedicated hardware accelerators** onto a single substrate manufactured using a specific process node (e.g., **7 nm**, **5 nm**, **3 nm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim)**: A System-on-Chip is a single-chip solution that integrates the processor core(s), memory sub-system, I/O controllers, and interconnect architecture, designed to meet the **performance**, **power**, **area (PPA)**, and **time-to-market** constraints of an embedded or mobile computing system.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The City of Microelectronics"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **modern smart city**. Instead of having the power plant, water supply, hospitals, schools, roads, and houses scattered across a vast rural landscape (which is what a traditional multi-chip motherboard does), an SoC is like building an **ultra-compact, vertically integrated city** where every essential service exists within walking distance of every other service. The "roads" are the **on-chip interconnect buses** (AXI, AHB, APB), the "warehouses" are the **on-chip SRAM caches (L1/L2/L3)**, the "long-distance logistics" are the **DRAM controllers** leading to off-chip memory, and the "factories" are the **hardware accelerators** (GPU, DSP, NPU, Crypto engines). The shorter the distance, the faster the communication and the lower the energy consumed per bit transferred.

### 1.1.2 Key SoC Design Metrics (PPA)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Performance** | P / IPC | GOPS, GFLOPS, DMIPS | Throughput, latency, instructions per cycle |
| **Power** | P_diss | Watts (W) | Energy per operation, thermal design power (TDP) |
| **Area** | A | mm² | Die size, manufacturing cost |
| **Energy** | E | Joules (J) | E = P_diss × t (energy-delay product) |
| **Frequency** | f | Hz, MHz, GHz | Clock rate of the digital logic |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | BW | GB/s | Rate of data transfer to/from memory |

---

## 1.2 Memory Organisation in SoC — Hierarchical View

**Memory Organisation** refers to the **structured arrangement, addressing scheme, and inter-connection of various memory elements** within the SoC such that the system achieves optimal **hit rate**, **miss penalty**, and **bandwidth utilisation** for a given workload.

### 1.2.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** As we move **up** the memory hierarchy, **speed increases, capacity decreases, and cost-per-bit rises**. As we move **down**, **capacity grows, latency rises, and cost-per-bit falls**. The SoC designer's job is to balance this pyramid against the application workload.

The canonical memory hierarchy in an SoC, from **fastest-smallest** to **slowest-largest**, is:

1. **CPU Registers** — Few hundred bytes, single-cycle access, integrated into the datapath.
2. **L1 Cache (Level 1)** — **32 KB to 128 KB** per core, split into **I-cache (instruction)** and **D-cache (data)**, latency ≈ **1 to 4 cycles**.
3. **L2 Cache (Level 2)** — **256 KB to 1 MB** per core (or shared), unified I/D, latency ≈ **10 to 20 cycles**.
4. **L3 Cache (Level 3 / Last-Level Cache - LLC)** — **2 MB to 32 MB** shared across all cores, latency ≈ **30 to 60 cycles**.
5. **Main Memory (DRAM - LPDDR4X, LPDDR5, DDR5)** — **2 GB to 32 GB**, latency ≈ **100 to 300 cycles** (when not in cache).
6. **Storage (eMMC, UFS, NVMe SSD)** — **16 GB to 1 TB+**, latency in **microseconds to milliseconds**.

### 1.2.2 Cache Mapping Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

Three principal address-mapping schemes are used to map main-memory blocks into cache lines (each line is typically **32 B, 64 B, or 128 B**):

- **Direct Mapped Cache:** Each main-memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line, determined by `Index = (Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines)`.
- **Fully Associative Cache:** A main-memory block may reside in **any** cache line. Maximises hit rate, requires **parallel tag comparators** (expensive hardware).
- **Set-Associative Cache:** A compromise — each memory block maps to a fixed **set** of N cache lines (where N = associativity: 2-way, 4-way, 8-way, 16-way).

### 1.2.3 Replacement Policies

When a cache miss occurs and the set is full, the controller must evict an existing line:

- **LRU (Least Recently Used)** — Most accurate, expensive in hardware for high associativity.
- **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** — Simple, but ignores access patterns.
- **Random Replacement** — Surprisingly effective, very cheap to implement.
- **PLRU (Pseudo-LRU)** — Approximates LRU using a single bit per node in a tree structure.

### 1.2.4 Write Policies

- **Write-Through:** Every write propagates immediately to the next level. Simple, but bandwidth-heavy.
- **Write-Back:** Writes update only the current cache level; the modified line is written to lower levels only on **eviction**. Requires a **dirty bit** per line.

---

## 1.3 SoC Interconnect — The "On-Chip Highway System"

The **interconnect fabric** is the communication backbone that links all IP blocks. The dominant industry standard is **ARM's Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** family:

| AMBA Protocol | Topology | Use-Case | Max Frequency | Data Width |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface)** | Point-to-point, 5 channels | High-performance CPU↔DDR, DMA, GPU | Up to **~1 GHz+** | 64 / 128 / 256 / 512 bits |
| **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** | Shared bus | Medium-speed peripherals | ~200 MHz | 32 / 64 / 128 bits |
| **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** | Simple bus | Low-bandwidth peripherals (UART, GPIO, Timers) | ~100 MHz | 8 / 16 / 32 bits |
| **CHI (Coherent Hub Interface)** | Coherent mesh | Multi-core cache coherence | ~2 GHz | 128 / 256 / 512 bits |
| **ACE (AXI Coherency Extensions)** | Point-to-point | Hardware-coherent multi-core | ~1 GHz | 64 / 128 bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Interconnect Customization:** Think of the SoC interconnect as a **custom road network**. In a **handheld IoT device**, a single two-lane road (APB) is enough to reach a slow sensor. In a **flagship smartphone SoC**, however, you need an elaborate **multi-lane expressway system with cloverleaf interchanges (AXI/CHI)** so that the CPU, GPU, NPU, ISP, and DDR memory controller can all transfer multiple terabytes per second without colliding.

---

## 1.4 Introduction to High Performance Computing (HPC)

**High Performance Computing (HPC)** is the practice of **aggregating massive computational power** through parallel processing to solve problems that are too large or too slow for a single conventional computer. HPC systems combine:

- **Compute nodes** (many-core CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, TPUs)
- **High-bandwidth interconnects** (InfiniBand, NVLink, RoCE, CXL)
- **Parallel file systems** (Lustre, GPFS, BeeGFS)
- **Specialised programming models** (MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenCL, HIP, SYCL)

### 1.4.1 Metrics Defining "High Performance"

- **FLOPS** — **F**loating-point **O**perations **P**er **S**econd. Modern HPC clusters exceed **1 ExaFLOPS = 10^18 FLOPS** (e.g., **Frontier** at ORNL, **Aurora** at Argonne).
- **Parallel Efficiency (η)** — Ratio of actual speedup to the theoretical linear speedup.
- **Scalability** — Ability to maintain performance as the number of processors/GPUs increases.
- **Energy Efficiency** — Performance per watt, measured in **GFLOPS/W**.

---

## 1.5 GPU Hardware Architecture — Formal Definition

A **Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)** is a **massively parallel, throughput-oriented processor** optimised for the simultaneous execution of thousands of lightweight threads operating on large data sets. Architecturally, GPUs embrace the **SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Thread)** or **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** execution model.

### 1.5.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Factory Floor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A **CPU** is like a **brilliant solo surgeon** who performs one complex operation at a time with great precision. A **GPU** is like a **giant assembly-line factory** with **thousands of workers (threads)** each performing the **same simple task** (e.g., "paint this pixel blue") on different parts of a product simultaneously. The surgeon is faster on a single intricate task; the factory is faster when you have **millions of identical, simple tasks** to complete.

### 1.5.2 Evolution of GPU Compute Roles

Originally designed for **3D graphics rasterisation**, GPUs were reinvented (around 2006 with NVIDIA's **CUDA** and AMD/ATI's **CTM**, then **Stream**, and later **ROCm/HIP**) as **general-purpose accelerators (GPGPUs)** suitable for:

- Scientific simulation (climate, molecular dynamics, CFD)
- Deep learning training and inference
- Cryptographic mining (historically)
- Image and signal processing
- Database acceleration

---

## 1.6 GPU vs CPU — High-Level Comparison

| Property | **CPU** | **GPU** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Design Goal | **Latency** minimisation | **Throughput** maximisation |
| Core Count | **4 to 128** large, complex cores | **Thousands** of small, simple lanes (e.g., **10 000+** CUDA cores) |
| Clock Frequency | **3 to 5.8 GHz** | **1 to 2.5 GHz** |
| Cache Size | Large L1/L2/L3 (**tens of MB**) | Smaller per-SM caches, but more registers |
| Instruction Set | CISC/RISC general-purpose | SIMT/SIMD, branch-diversion hardware |
| Threading Model | **SMT / Hyper-Threading** (2 to 8 threads/core) | **SIMT**, tens of warps per SM, **1000s of resident threads** |
| Memory Bandwidth | **~50 to 100 GB/s** (DDR5) | **~500 to 1 800 GB/s** (HBM3 / HBM3e) |
| Power Draw | **15 W to 250 W** | **150 W to 700+ W** |
| Best For | **Control-intensive, sequential, branchy** code | **Data-parallel, arithmetic-intensive, regular** code |
| Examples | Intel Core i9, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series cores | NVIDIA H100, AMD Instinct MI300, Intel Xe-HPC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The CPU and GPU are **not competitors** but **complementary accelerators** inside an SoC or HPC node. Heterogeneous computing uses both — the CPU orchestrates control flow while the GPU executes the bulk of data-parallel arithmetic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cache Performance — The Heart of Memory Organisation

The **average memory access time (AMAT)** is the canonical metric that determines how efficiently the memory hierarchy serves the processor:

$$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + \text{Miss Rate}_{L1} \times \text{AMAT}_{L2}$$

Extending recursively to a three-level hierarchy (L1, L2, L3, then main memory):

$$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot \left( T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot \left( T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \cdot T_{MEM} \right) \right)$$

### 2.1.1 Worked Symbolic Example

Assume the following typical mobile-SoC numbers (representative of an **ARM Cortex-A78** class core):

- L1 hit time $T_{L1}$ = **1 ns** (1 cycle @ 1 GHz)
- L1 miss rate $MR_{L1}$ = **5 %**
- L2 hit time $T_{L2}$ = **5 ns**
- L2 miss rate $MR_{L2}$ = **20 %**
- L3 hit time $T_{L3}$ = **20 ns**
- L3 miss rate $MR_{L3}$ = **30 %**
- DRAM access $T_{MEM}$ = **100 ns**

Substituting into the AMAT equation:

$$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \times \left( 5 + 0.20 \times \left( 20 + 0.30 \times 100 \right) \right)$$

We evaluate the innermost parenthesis first:

$$20 + 0.30 \times 100 = 20 + 30 = 50 \text{ ns}$$

Then the next level:

$$5 + 0.20 \times 50 = 5 + 10 = 15 \text{ ns}$$

Finally:

$$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \times 15 = 1 + 0.75 = 1.75 \text{ ns}$$

So the **average memory access time is 1.75 ns**, dominated almost entirely by the L1 cache hit — exactly the goal of a well-designed hierarchy.

### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Memory & Cache

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-pipe rule:** All absolute-value / divisibility bars inside the table below are rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **AMAT (general)** | $AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \times T_{miss\,penalty}$ | Add miss-penalty contribution |
| 2 | **AMAT (3-level)** | $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1}(T_{L2} + MR_{L2}(T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \cdot T_{MEM}))$ | Recursive form |
| 3 | **Hit Rate** | $HR = 1 - MR$ | $HR + MR = 1$ |
| 4 | **CPU Time (Memory)** | $T_{CPU} = (IC \times CPI_{base}) \times T_{clk} + \text{Memory Stalls}$ | Memory stalls dominate |
| 5 | **Memory Stall Cycles** | $\text{Stalls} = IC \times \text{Mem\,Refs/Inst} \times MR \times MP$ | MP = miss penalty |
| 6 | **Speedup (Amdahl's)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | f = parallel fraction, N = processors |
| 7 | **Parallel Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-f) + f}$ | $\eta \le 1$ |
| 8 | **Cache Index bits** | $idx = \log_2(\text{\# of cache lines})$ | Direct-mapped |
| 9 | **Cache Offset bits** | $off = \log_2(\text{line size in bytes})$ | Within the line |
| 10 | **Tag bits** | $tag = 32 - idx - off$ (32-bit address) | Verification |
| 11 | **Tag RAM size** | $\text{Tag RAM} = \#\text{lines} \times \text{tag bits} \times \text{associativity}$ | Plus valid + dirty bits |
| 12 | **Bandwidth** | $BW = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred (bytes)}}{\text{Time (s)}}$ | GB/s units |
| 13 | **Theoretical GPU FLOPS** | $FLOPS_{peak} = N_{SM} \times N_{cores/SM} \times F \times 2 \times \text{FMA}$ | FMA = fused multiply-add |
| 14 | **Roofline Bound (Compute)** | $\text{Attainable GFLOPS} = \min(Peak, \pi \times BW)$ | $\pi$ = arithmetic intensity (FLOP/byte) |
| 15 | **CPI with memory stall** | $CPI = CPI_{base} + MR \times MP$ | Simplified 1-level |
| 16 | **Power (dynamic)** | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity |
| 17 | **Energy / Operation** | $E = \int P \, dt = C \cdot V_{dd}^2$ | Per transition |
| 18 | **DRAM Row Buffer Hit Rate** | $RBHR = \dfrac{\#\text{row hits}}{\#\text{total accesses}}$ | Affects effective latency |
| 19 | **Cache Line Utilisation** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Useful bytes}}{\text{Line size (B)}}$ | Sub-byte accesses waste bandwidth |
| 20 | **Coherence Overhead** | $OH_{coh} = \dfrac{\#\text{coherence transactions}}{\#\text{memory transactions}}$ | Directory / snoop traffic |

---

## 2.2 SoC Interconnect — Customisation & Configuration

### 2.2.1 Why Customisation is Essential

A "one-size-fits-all" bus architecture wastes energy and silicon area. An SoC designed for an IoT sensor node does not need a **CHI coherent mesh**; conversely, a flagship mobile SoC cannot tolerate the **bandwidth starvation** that an APB-only fabric would impose. The **interconnect must be tuned** to the data-flow profile of the workload.

### 2.2.2 Configuration Parameters

1. **Bus Width** — Number of parallel data lanes (32, 64, 128, 256, 512 bits). Wider ⇒ more bandwidth, more area, more power.
2. **Operating Frequency** — Higher ⇒ more bandwidth, but harder timing closure and more dynamic power.
3. **Number of Initiators / Targets** — Number of masters and slaves attached; more ports ⇒ more crossbar complexity.
4. **Quality-of-Service (QoS) Arbitration** — Prioritisation of masters (e.g., real-time ISP > display controller > CPU background).
5. **Burst Length** — Number of beats per AXI transaction. Longer bursts ⇒ higher bus efficiency, but more latency for other masters.
6. **Outstanding Transactions** — Number of in-flight reads/writes per master. Deeper ⇒ better latency hiding.
7. **Address Width** — Determines the addressable memory map.
8. **Coherency Support** — Hardware-managed (ACE/CHI) vs software-managed (cache flush instructions).
9. **Power-Gating Domains** — Independent switchable power islands.

### 2.2.3 Customisation Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Strategy — Heterogeneous Fabric:** A modern SoC does **not** use a single bus. Instead, it uses a **hierarchical heterogeneous fabric**: a **CHI coherent mesh** at the top, **AXI high-speed rings** in the middle, **AHB medium-speed buses** for peripherals, and **APB** for low-speed register-mapped I/O. This is sometimes called a "**NoC** (Network-on-Chip)" when implemented as a router-mesh.

---

## 2.3 Trade-off Analysis on Reconfigurable Parallelism

Reconfigurable parallelism refers to the ability of an SoC to **dynamically adjust the number of active cores, the operating voltage/frequency, and the interconnect topology** to match the workload.

### 2.3.1 The Three Pillars of Trade-off

| Trade-off Axis | Conservative Choice | Aggressive Choice | Engineering Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Parallelism (N cores)** | Few large cores | Many small cores | Big: single-thread perf ↑; Small: throughput ↑, area ↓ |
| **Voltage / Frequency** | Low V, low f | High V, high f | Low: power ↓; High: performance ↑, leakage ↑ |
| **Cache Allocation** | Small private L2 | Large shared LLC | Small: latency ↓ for owner; Large: hit rate ↑ across all cores |

### 2.3.2 Amdahl's Law — The Hard Ceiling

For a parallel workload where fraction $f$ is perfectly parallelisable and the rest $(1-f)$ is serial, the theoretical speedup $S$ using $N$ processors is:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$

- As $N \to \infty$: $\quad S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$
- Even with infinite cores, the serial portion caps performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Amdahl's Law is the single most cited formula when justifying **why heterogeneous SoCs** (big.LITTLE, big.middle.TINY, etc.) exist. The serial part of the workload (OS, control flow, branchy code) is best run on a few big cores; the parallel part (graphics, ML, codec) is best run on many small cores.

### 2.3.3 The Roofline Model — Performance Bound for GPUs

For a kernel with **arithmetic intensity** $I$ (FLOP per byte of memory traffic), the attainable performance is:

$$\text{Performance} = \min\!\left( P_{\text{peak}}, \; I \times BW_{\text{memory}} \right)$$

- If $I$ is low → **memory-bound** (bandwidth limited)
- If $I$ is high → **compute-bound** (peak FLOP limited)

---

## 2.4 GPU Hardware Architecture — Deep Dive

### 2.4.1 Top-Level Components

A modern discrete GPU (e.g., **NVIDIA H100**, **AMD MI300X**) consists of:

1. **Multiple Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) / Compute Units (CUs)** — The actual compute engines. An H100 has **132 SMs**, each with **128 FP32 cores** → 16 896 FP32 cores total.
2. **Register File** — Massive, fast, per-thread. An H100 SM has **65 536 × 32-bit registers**.
3. **L1 Cache / Shared Memory** — Software-managed (CUDA `__shared__`) or hardware-managed cache.
4. **Texture Units, Tensor Cores, RT Cores** — Special-function units.
5. **L2 Cache** — Shared across all SMs; H100 has **50 MB L2**.
6. **DRAM Channels** — Connected to **HBM3 / HBM3e** stacks delivering up to **3.35 TB/s** on H100.
7. **Host Interface (PCIe Gen 5, NVLink 4)** — Connects GPU to CPU.

### 2.4.2 SIMT Execution Model

The GPU executes **warps** (NVIDIA) or **wavefronts** (AMD) — groups of **32 or 64 threads** that issue in lockstep.

- **Instruction Issue:** One instruction is fetched per cycle for the whole warp.
- **Branch Divergence:** If threads in a warp take different paths, both paths are executed sequentially and masked → performance loss.
- **Occupancy:** The fraction of resident warps per SM, limited by registers, shared memory, and warp-slot hardware.

### 2.4.3 GPU Memory Hierarchy

| Level | Capacity (per SM/CU) | Latency (cycles) | Bandwidth |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Registers** | 65 536 × 32-bit / SM | **0** | ~ TB/s/SM |
| **Shared Memory** | 100 KB+ / SM (configurable) | **~20-30** | TBs/SM |
| **L1 Cache** | Unified with shared mem | **~30-50** | TBs/SM |
| **L2 Cache** | Tens of MB total | **~200-300** | Hundreds of GB/s |
| **HBM** | 80-192 GB total | **~400-800** | Up to 3.35 TB/s |

### 2.4.4 KTU Formula Sheet — GPU & HPC

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Peak FP32 Throughput** | $F_{peak} = N_{SM} \times N_{FP32/SM} \times f_{boost} \times 2$ | GFLOPS (FMA = 2 ops) |
| 2 | **Arithmetic Intensity** | $I = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs executed}}{\text{Bytes transferred}}$ | FLOP/byte |
| 3 | **Memory-Bound Limit** | $Perf_{max} = I \times BW$ | GFLOPS |
| 4 | **Achievable Bandwidth** | $BW_{eff} = \eta_{mem} \times BW_{peak}$ | $\eta_{mem} \approx 0.7$ typical |
| 5 | **Occupancy** | $O = \dfrac{\text{Resident Warps}}{\text{Max Warps per SM}}$ | $0 < O \le 1$ |
| 6 | **Threads per SM** | $T_{max} = \dfrac{\text{Registers per SM}}{\text{Registers per Thread}}$ | Integer |
| 7 | **Speedup (Heterogeneous)** | $S_{het} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1-f_{GPU}}{S_{CPU}} + \dfrac{f_{GPU}}{S_{GPU}}}$ | Combined |
| 8 | **Power Efficiency** | $Perf/W = \dfrac{FLOPS}{P_{diss}}$ | GFLOPS/W |
| 9 | **PCIe Bandwidth** | PCIe 5.0 x16 ≈ 128 GB/s bidirectional | GB/s |
| 10 | **NVLink Bandwidth** | NVLink 4 ≈ 900 GB/s (HGX H100) | GB/s |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?**
> 1. **Mobile SoCs** (Apple M3, Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, MediaTek Dimensity 9300+) embed CPU + GPU + NPU + ISP on a single die to save PCB area and reduce inter-chip power by orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Data-centre GPUs** (H100, MI300X) deliver the **tens-of-petaFLOPS** needed for **LLM training**, **climate modelling**, and **drug discovery**.
> 3. **HPC + AI Convergence:** Modern supercomputers (e.g., **Frontier**, **Aurora**, **Leonardo**) couple **AMD EPYC CPUs** with **AMD Instinct GPUs** over **Infinity Fabric / NVLink / CXL**, blurring the boundary between HPC node and AI accelerator.
> 4. **Edge AI:** Tiny SoCs like **NVIDIA Jetson Orin** integrate a 2048-core Ampere GPU + 12-core Arm CPU + NPU for **autonomous drones**, **robotics**, and **ADAS** systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AMAT with Realistic KTU Exam Numbers

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):** *A three-level cache hierarchy has the following parameters:*
- $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, $MR_{L1} = 4\%$
- $T_{L2} = 6$ ns, $MR_{L2} = 25\%$
- $T_{L3} = 30$ ns, $MR_{L3} = 40\%$
- $T_{MEM} = 150$ ns

*Compute (a) the AMAT and (b) the global miss rate. If we double L1 to 8 KB (now $MR_{L1}' = 2\%$), what is the new AMAT?*

### Step 1 — State the recursive AMAT formula

$$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \left( T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \left( T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \cdot T_{MEM} \right) \right)$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the innermost parenthesis (L3 subsystem)

$$T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \cdot T_{MEM} = 30 + 0.40 \times 150$$

$$0.40 \times 150 = 60$$

$$30 + 60 = 90 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the L2 subsystem

$$T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times 90 = 6 + 0.25 \times 90$$

$$0.25 \times 90 = 22.5$$

$$6 + 22.5 = 28.5 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute into the L1 subsystem

$$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.04 \times 28.5$$

$$0.04 \times 28.5 = 1.14$$

$$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 1.14 = 2.14 \text{ ns}$$

**Answer (a):** $\text{AMAT} = 2.14$ ns.

### Step 5 — Global miss rate

$$MR_{global} = MR_{L1} \times MR_{L2} \times MR_{L3} = 0.04 \times 0.25 \times 0.40 = 0.004 = 0.4\%$$

### Step 6 — Recompute with $MR_{L1}' = 2\%$

$$\text{AMAT}' = 1 + 0.02 \times 28.5 = 1 + 0.57 = 1.57 \text{ ns}$$

**Answer (b):** $\text{AMAT}' = 1.57$ ns, a **26.6 % reduction** in average access time, demonstrating the disproportionate value of even small L1 improvements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law in an SoC Context

**Problem:** A mobile SoC workload spends 80 % of its time in a parallelisable function that can be accelerated by 8 GPU cores in parallel (each adds a speedup factor). The remaining 20 % is sequential. Compute the speedup for **1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and infinity** cores.

### Step 1 — Recap Amdahl's Law

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$

with $f = 0.80$ and $(1-f) = 0.20$.

### Step 2 — Compute for each N

$$S(1) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{1}} = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.00$$

$$S(2) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.40} = \frac{1}{0.60} = 1.667$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} = \frac{1}{0.40} = 2.500$$

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.333$$

$$S(16) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4.000$$

$$S(\infty) = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.000$$

### Step 3 — Build the table

| N (cores) | Speedup $S(N)$ | Parallel Efficiency $\eta = S/N$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 2 | 1.67 | 0.83 |
| 4 | 2.50 | 0.625 |
| 8 | 3.33 | 0.417 |
| 16 | 4.00 | 0.250 |
| $\infty$ | 5.00 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note that going from 8 cores to 16 cores gives only a 20 % speedup (3.33 → 4.00) while doubling silicon and power. This is **exactly** why modern SoCs add cores cautiously and rely on **architectural specialisation** (NPU, DSP) rather than blindly scaling core count.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: GPU Peak FLOP Calculation

**Problem:** An NVIDIA H100 SXM5 GPU has **132 SMs**, **128 FP32 cores per SM**, and a **boost clock of 1.83 GHz**. Compute the peak FP32 throughput assuming FMA (Fused Multiply-Add, counted as 2 FLOPs per instruction).

### Step 1 — Compute total FP32 cores

$$N_{FP32} = 132 \times 128 = 16\,896 \text{ cores}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the peak-FLOP formula

$$F_{peak} = N_{FP32} \times f \times 2_{FMA}$$

$$F_{peak} = 16\,896 \times 1.83 \times 10^9 \times 2$$

$$F_{peak} = 16\,896 \times 3.66 \times 10^9$$

$$F_{peak} = 61\,839.36 \times 10^9 \text{ FLOPS}$$

$$\boxed{F_{peak} \approx 61.8 \text{ TFLOPS (FP32, FMA)}}$$

### Step 3 — Reality check vs official spec

> [!NOTE]
> NVIDIA's official number is **~67 TFLOPS FP32 (boost)**. The 8 % gap is due to the **Tensor Core pipeline** boost and the **GPU Boost** dynamic-frequency feature that increases clocks when thermal headroom allows. For a conservative paper calculation, **61.8 TFLOPS** is the right answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Cache Configuration

This matrix helps an SoC designer choose cache parameters for a given workload class (this is a **typical KTU design-style question**):

| Workload Class | L1 / Core | L2 / Core | L3 (Shared) | Assoc. | Replacement | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High-frequency trading** | 32 KB | 256 KB | 4 MB | 8-way | LRU | Latency-critical, small working set |
| **Smartphone UI / browser** | 64 KB | 512 KB | 4 MB | 8-way | LRU | Mixed code/data, large L3 for app data |
| **ML inference accelerator** | 32 KB | 1 MB | 8 MB | 16-way | PLRU | Large weight matrices → big L2/L3 |
| **Gaming console CPU** | 64 KB | 512 KB | 16 MB | 16-way | LRU | Big working sets, code-streaming workloads |
| **Embedded IoT MCU** | 8 KB | 32 KB | None | 2-way | Random | Area / power severely constrained |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — CPU vs GPU Architectural Choices

| Subsystem | CPU Choice | GPU Choice | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ALU width** | Wide, deep pipeline (15+ stages) | Shallow, wide SIMT (4–8 stage) | Latency vs throughput trade-off |
| **Out-of-order** | Aggressive, large ROB (256+) | None / minimal | GPU threads hide latency naturally |
| **Branch prediction** | Tournament, 90 %+ accuracy | Per-warp PC, low accuracy OK | GPUs prefer "predicated execution" |
| **Caches** | Large, low-associativity OK | Small, high-associativity per SM | GPUs rely on massive thread-level parallelism |
| **Memory model** | TSO / weak ordering hardware-enforced | Relaxed, software-managed (fences) | GPUs use explicit `__syncthreads()` |
| **Power gating** | Per-core DVFS, aggressive | SM-level clock gating, fewer V/F states | GPU has fewer V/F domains |
| **Instruction set** | CISC / RISC general | SIMT ISA (SASS, RDNA ISA) | Simplicity for many-thread issue |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Average Memory Stall Cycles with Cache

**Problem:** A CPU executes **$IC = 1 \times 10^9$** instructions. Each instruction has on average **0.4 memory references** (loads + stores). The L1 miss rate is **5 %**, the L2 miss rate is **20 %**, and the miss penalty to L2 is **100 cycles** and to DRAM is **300 cycles**. Compute the total memory stall cycles.

### Step 1 — Compute memory references

$$\text{Mem refs} = IC \times \text{Mem refs/inst} = 1 \times 10^9 \times 0.4 = 4 \times 10^8$$

### Step 2 — Compute L1 misses

$$\text{L1 misses} = 4 \times 10^8 \times 0.05 = 2 \times 10^7$$

### Step 3 — Compute L2 misses (DRAM accesses)

$$\text{L2 misses} = 2 \times 10^7 \times 0.20 = 4 \times 10^6$$

### Step 4 — Compute stall cycles

Stall from L1 misses (penalty 100 cycles) + stall from L2 misses (extra $300 - 100 = 200$ cycles, since the L2 miss penalty is already partially the L1 penalty — but in this simplified model we use the L2 hit penalty for L1 misses and the **DRAM extra penalty** for L2 misses):

$$\text{Stalls}_{L1} = 2 \times 10^7 \times 100 = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ cycles}$$

$$\text{Stalls}_{L2} = 4 \times 10^6 \times 200 = 8 \times 10^8 \text{ cycles}$$

$$\text{Stalls}_{total} = 2 \times 10^9 + 0.8 \times 10^9 = 2.8 \times 10^9 \text{ cycles}$$

**Conclusion:** 2.8 billion stall cycles — more than the entire 1 G-instruction base execution! This shows why cache behaviour is the single biggest determinant of CPU performance.

## 4.3 Cache Memory Organisation — Set-Associative Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[32-bit Main Memory Address] --> B[Tag - bits 31 down to 14]
    A --> C[Index - bits 13 down to 6]
    A --> D[Offset - bits 5 down to 0 - 64 byte line]
    C --> E[Select Set]
    E --> F0[Way 0 - Tag and Data]
    E --> F1[Way 1 - Tag and Data]
    E --> F2[Way 2 - Tag and Data]
    E --> F3[Way 3 - Tag and Data]
    F0 --> G[Tag Compare - 4 parallel]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Match and Valid?}
    H -- YES --> I[Hit - Return Data to CPU]
    H -- NO --> J[Miss - Fetch Line from L2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **4-way set-associative cache** with **64-byte lines**. The address is split into three fields: **Tag** (used for identification), **Index** (selects the set), and **Offset** (selects the byte within the 64-byte line). The tag must match in **any** of the 4 ways and the valid bit must be set. This structure balances speed (parallel tag comparison) with low conflict (4 candidates per set).

---

## 4.4 Reconfigurable Parallelism — DVFS and Core-Clustering State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> LOWPWR: Workload light - OS scheduler
    LOWPWR: Low Power Mode - 2 little cores - 0.6 GHz - 0.6 V
    LOWPWR --> MID: Load increase detected
    MID: Mid Mode - 4 middle cores - 1.2 GHz - 0.8 V
    MID --> HIGH: Sustained heavy load
    HIGH: High Performance Mode - 8 big cores - 2.5 GHz - 1.0 V
    HIGH --> TURBO: Burst workload - thermal headroom available
    TURBO: Turbo Mode - 8 cores - 3.0 GHz - 1.05 V - limited duration
    TURBO --> HIGH: Thermal limit reached
    HIGH --> MID: Load decreases
    MID --> LOWPWR: Load decreases
    LOWPWR --> IDLE: Idle timeout
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is the **reconfigurable parallelism** model used in **big.LITTLE** (Arm) and **hybrid** (Intel P-core/E-core, Apple Performance/Efficiency) architectures. The SoC monitors workload and **migrates threads** between clusters while simultaneously adjusting voltage and frequency (DVFS). The **trade-off** is: more cores + higher V/f = more performance, but quadratically more power (because $P \propto V^2 \cdot f$).

---

## 4.5 GPU Memory Hierarchy — Top-to-Bottom View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H0[Host CPU - DDR5 System Memory] --PCIe Gen 5 128 GB/s--> H1[GPU HBM3 Stacks - 80 GB - 3.35 TB/s]
    H1 --HBM channels--> H2[L2 Cache - 50 MB - shared across all SMs]
    H2 --per-SM crossbar--> H3A[SM 0 - L1 and Shared - 256 KB]
    H2 --per-SM crossbar--> H3B[SM 1 - L1 and Shared - 256 KB]
    H2 --per-SM crossbar--> H3C[SM N - L1 and Shared - 256 KB]
    H3A --> H4A[Registers - 64K x 32-bit per SM]
    H3B --> H4B[Registers - 64K x 32-bit per SM]
    H3C --> H4C[Registers - 64K x 32-bit per SM]
    H4A --> H5A[CUDA Cores / Tensor Cores / SFU]
    H4B --> H5B[CUDA Cores / Tensor Cores / SFU]
    H4C --> H5C[CUDA Cores / Tensor Cores / SFU]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The GPU memory hierarchy is **inverted** compared to a CPU: the **highest bandwidth** is **closest to the compute** (registers → shared → L1 → L2 → HBM → host). This maximises throughput for data-parallel kernels and minimises the time threads stall waiting for memory.

## 4.6 Amdahl's Law — Visual Speedup Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[f = 0.50 - 50 percent parallel] --> S0[Speedup curve - ceiling 2x]
    A1[f = 0.80 - 80 percent parallel] --> S1[Speedup curve - ceiling 5x]
    A2[f = 0.95 - 95 percent parallel] --> S2[Speedup curve - ceiling 20x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **steeper** the curve, the more cores you can productively add. Real-world applications (HPC, ML training) sit on the $f = 0.9$ to $f = 0.99$ end, justifying the **10 000+ cores** in modern GPUs. Mobile UI workloads sit on $f \approx 0.5$, justifying only **4–8 cores** in a smartphone SoC.
