# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse "hit time" with "miss penalty".** Hit time is the time to deliver data on a *cache hit*. Miss penalty is the *additional* time to fetch from the *next lower level* on a miss. The AMAT formula uses **hit time + miss rate × miss penalty**.
> 2. **Address partition is the most-skipped step.** Students forget to compute the **offset, index, and tag bits** separately and lose 2–3 marks instantly. Always: offset = $\log_2(\text{line size})$, index = $\log_2(\#\text{lines})$, tag = address bits − index − offset.
> 3. **AMDAHL'S LAW: "$N$" is the number of parallel workers, NOT the total problem size.** Many students write $S = 1/(1-f) + f/N$ — *wrong operator placement* — and lose a full step.
> 4. **GPU vs CPU trade-off questions** expect you to name **at least three** differences (latency vs throughput, core count, cache size). Writing only one is a half-answer.
> 5. **SoC interconnect** questions demand the **full names** of the protocols (AXI, AHB, APB, CHI, ACE) and at least one **technical justification** for using each.
> 6. **Write policies:** "Write-back with write-allocate" is the **default modern** policy. If you say "write-through" without justification, the examiner will mark you down.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the term **AMAT (Average Memory Access Time)** and state its formula for a **two-level** cache hierarchy. Why is reducing the L1 miss rate more impactful than reducing the L2 miss rate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

AMAT is the **average time** the processor spends per memory access, averaged over hits and misses across the memory hierarchy. For a two-level cache:

$$\text{AMAT} = T_{L1} + MR_{L1} \times T_{L2}$$

where $T_{L1}$ is the L1 hit time and $T_{L2}$ is the L2 access time on a miss.

**Why L1 miss rate matters more:** Because L1 misses gate *every* subsequent level. A 1 % reduction in $MR_{L1}$ removes $T_{L2} \approx 5$ ns of stall per access, while a 1 % reduction in $MR_{L2}$ removes only $0.01 \times MR_{L1} \times T_{MEM} \approx 0.05$ ns. **L1 improvements dominate the AMAT equation.**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **big.LITTLE** heterogeneous architecture used in modern mobile SoCs. Mention any **two trade-offs**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**big.LITTLE** combines two types of CPU cores on the same die:
- **"big" cores** — High-performance, out-of-order, high-frequency (e.g., Cortex-X4 @ 3.2 GHz) — handle latency-critical serial code.
- **"LITTLE" cores** — Energy-efficient, in-order, low-frequency (e.g., Cortex-A520 @ 1.5 GHz) — handle background and parallel code.

The OS scheduler migrates threads to the appropriate cluster based on load and thermal conditions.

**Two trade-offs:**
1. **Software complexity** — The OS scheduler must profile load and decide migration; bugs cause thermal throttling.
2. **Cache coherency cost** — Maintaining coherence between big and LITTLE clusters consumes interconnect bandwidth and power.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **three-level cache hierarchy** of a modern SoC with a neat block diagram. Compute the **AMAT** for the following data and comment on the result.

- $T_{L1} = 1$ ns, $MR_{L1} = 5\%$
- $T_{L2} = 7$ ns, $MR_{L2} = 30\%$
- $T_{L3} = 25$ ns, $MR_{L3} = 50\%$
- $T_{MEM} = 120$ ns

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[CPU Core] --> L1C[L1 Cache - 32 to 64 KB per core - 1 ns]
    L1C --miss 5 percent--> L2C[L2 Cache - 256 KB to 1 MB - 7 ns]
    L2C --miss 30 percent--> L3C[L3 Cache - 4 to 16 MB shared - 25 ns]
    L3C --miss 50 percent--> DRAMC[DRAM Controller]
    DRAMC --> DRAM[Off-chip LPDDR - 120 ns]
```

**AMAT Calculation:**

Step 1: Innermost

$$T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \times T_{MEM} = 25 + 0.50 \times 120 = 25 + 60 = 85 \text{ ns}$$

Step 2: L2 subsystem

$$T_{L2} + MR_{L2} \times 85 = 7 + 0.30 \times 85 = 7 + 25.5 = 32.5 \text{ ns}$$

Step 3: L1 subsystem

$$\text{AMAT} = 1 + 0.05 \times 32.5 = 1 + 1.625 = 2.625 \text{ ns}$$

**Comment:** The AMAT (2.625 ns) is **2.6× the L1 hit time** (1 ns) — a modest penalty. This shows a well-designed hierarchy where **95 % of accesses are served by L1**, and only 0.75 % of accesses (0.05 × 0.30 × 0.50) ever reach DRAM.

**Global miss rate:** $0.05 \times 0.30 \times 0.50 = 0.0075 = 0.75\%$.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the **SoC interconnect customisation** problem. With a neat diagram, explain the **AMBA** family of buses. Justify the choice of bus protocol for **three distinct on-chip blocks**: a **GPU**, a **UART**, and a **DDR memory controller**.

**Model Solution:**

**SoC Interconnect Customisation — The Problem:**

A monolithic SoC has many IP blocks with vastly different bandwidth, latency, and power requirements. A "one bus fits all" approach is **suboptimal**: a high-bandwidth fabric wastes power for slow peripherals; a low-bandwidth fabric starves the GPU. Hence, the SoC designer **customises** the interconnect by:
1. Selecting the **bus protocol** (AXI, AHB, APB, CHI).
2. Tuning **data width**, **clock frequency**, and **QoS arbitration**.
3. Adding **bridges** to translate between protocols.
4. Using **NoC (Network-on-Chip)** routers for large designs.

**AMBA Family Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CHI[CHI / ACE - Coherent Hub - CPU clusters and LLC]
    AXI[AXI - High Speed - DMA, GPU, NPU, ISP, DDR Ctrl]
    AHB[AHB - Medium Speed - USB, Ethernet, SD card]
    APB[APB - Low Speed - UART, GPIO, Timers, RTC]
    CHI <--> AXI
    AXI <--> AHB
    AHB <--> APB
```

**Justification of Bus Choices:**

| IP Block | Bus | Justification |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **GPU** | **AXI (128-bit, high freq)** | Requires **100s of GB/s** of bandwidth to fetch textures, write framebuffers. AXI supports **burst transfers**, **out-of-order completion**, and **64/128/256/512-bit widths**. |
| **UART** | **APB (32-bit, low freq)** | UART serial streams at **115.2 kbps to 12 Mbps** — bandwidth is trivial. APB's simple non-pipelined protocol saves area and power; the UART only needs register-mapped access. |
| **DDR Memory Controller** | **AXI / CHI (256+ bit)** | The DDR controller must serve **all masters** in the SoC; AXI/CHI's multi-channel, high-width design delivers the multi-GB/s throughput needed to avoid stalling the GPU and CPU. CHI additionally provides **hardware cache coherency** so that CPU L1/L2 modifications are visible to the GPU's read requests. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often answer "use AXI for everything" — this is **wrong**. Using AXI for a UART wastes area (the AXI decoder is hundreds of gates), wastes power (line toggling on a 128-bit bus for a 1-byte write), and complicates the firmware. The whole point of **customisation** is heterogeneous bus choice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **GPU hardware architecture** of a modern discrete GPU. Compare it against a **multi-core CPU** across **at least five** architectural dimensions.

**Model Solution:**

**GPU Architecture Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOST[Host CPU] --> PCIE[PCIe Gen 5 - 128 GB/s]
    PCIE --> HOSTIF[Host Interface and IOMMU]
    HOSTIF --> GPC0[GPC 0 - Graphics Processing Cluster]
    HOSTIF --> GPC1[GPC 1]
    HOSTIF --> GPCN[GPC N]
    GPC0 --> SM00[SM 0 - 128 FP32 cores]
    GPC0 --> SM01[SM 1 - 128 FP32 cores]
    GPC0 --> SM0N[SM N - 128 FP32 cores]
    SM00 --> RF0[Register File - 256 KB]
    SM00 --> SHM0[Shared Memory - 100 KB]
    SM00 --> TC0[Tensor Cores]
    SM00 --> RT0[RT Cores]
    SHM0 --> L20[L2 Cache - 50 MB shared]
    GPC1 --> L20
    GPCN --> L20
    L20 --> HBM[HBM3 Memory - 80 GB - 3.35 TB/s]
```

**Five Architectural Comparisons:**

| Dimension | Multi-core CPU | Modern GPU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Design Goal** | Minimise **latency** per thread | Maximise **throughput** across thousands of threads |
| **2. Core Count / Type** | **4 to 128** large out-of-order cores | **10 000s** of small in-order SIMT lanes |
| **3. Clock Frequency** | **3 to 5.8 GHz** | **1 to 2.5 GHz** |
| **4. Cache Hierarchy** | Large L1/L2/L3, **MBs** | Small per-SM cache (KBs) but huge HBM |
| **5. Branch Handling** | Sophisticated prediction, speculative exec. | Per-warp predicate masks, no speculation |
| **6. Memory Bandwidth** | ~50–100 GB/s (DDR5) | ~500–3350 GB/s (HBM3) |
| **7. Threading Model** | SMT, 2–8 threads/core | SIMT, 1024s of resident warps/SM |
| **8. Power Envelope** | 15–250 W | 150–700+ W |

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A research lab executes a workload that is **95 % parallelisable**. Apply **Amdahl's Law** to compute the speedup for **N = 1, 4, 16, 64, 256, and infinity** processors. Plot (or tabulate) the speedup and **comment** on the design implication for an HPC cluster of **1024 GPU nodes**.

**Model Solution:**

**Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ with $f = 0.95$, $(1-f) = 0.05$.

| N | $S(N)$ Calculation | $S(N)$ | Efficiency $\eta = S/N$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $1/(0.05 + 0.95/1)$ | **1.000** | 1.000 |
| 4 | $1/(0.05 + 0.95/4) = 1/(0.05+0.2375)$ | **3.478** | 0.870 |
| 16 | $1/(0.05 + 0.95/16) = 1/(0.05+0.05938)$ | **9.143** | 0.571 |
| 64 | $1/(0.05 + 0.95/64) = 1/(0.05+0.01484)$ | **15.402** | 0.241 |
| 256 | $1/(0.05 + 0.95/256) = 1/(0.05+0.00371)$ | **18.622** | 0.073 |
| $\infty$ | $1/0.05$ | **20.000** | 0.000 |

**Design Implication for 1024 GPU Nodes:**

For $N = 1024$:

$$S(1024) = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.95/1024} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.000928} = \frac{1}{0.050928} \approx 19.63$$

**Comments:**
1. **Diminishing returns** are extreme. The HPC cluster of 1024 GPUs achieves only a **19.6×** speedup — barely 0.002 % of the theoretical 1024× linear scaling.
2. The **serial 5 %** of the workload (file I/O, inter-node communication, synchronisation, OS scheduling) is the bottleneck.
3. **Implication for the lab:** To justify 1024 GPUs, the lab must **reduce the serial fraction** (e.g., overlap I/O with compute, use asynchronous collectives like NCCL, eliminate synchronisation barriers).
4. **Energy efficiency:** A 1024-node cluster running this workload has **efficiency $\eta = 0.0019$** — meaning 99.8 % of the compute capability is wasted. Better to use **64–128 nodes** (still ~15× speedup, with $\eta \approx 0.24$) and save 80 % of the power.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memory Organisation & SoC / GPU Architecture**

### Memory Hierarchy & Cache
- Hierarchy order: **Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → DRAM → Storage**. Smaller/faster as you go up.
- **AMAT formula** (3-level): $AMAT = T_{L1} + MR_{L1}(T_{L2} + MR_{L2}(T_{L3} + MR_{L3} \cdot T_{MEM}))$.
- Address partition: **Tag / Index / Offset** with $offset = \log_2(\text{line size})$, $index = \log_2(\#\text{lines})$.
- Mapping types: **Direct, Fully Associative, Set-Associative** — trade-off: speed vs conflict rate vs hardware cost.
- Write policies: **Write-Through (simple, slow)** vs **Write-Back (fast, needs dirty bit)**. Modern: **write-back with write-allocate**.
- Replacement: **LRU** (best, expensive), **PLRU** (approximation), **FIFO**, **Random**.

### SoC Design Approach
- **SoC** integrates CPU + GPU + memory controller + peripherals + interconnect on a single die.
- **PPA trade-off** — Performance, Power, Area — the central design triangle.
- **AMBA family** — AXI (high-speed), AHB (medium), APB (low-speed), CHI/ACE (coherent multi-core).
- **Customisation** = choose the right bus, width, and frequency for each IP block; **bridges** translate between protocols.
- **Heterogeneous fabric** (CHI mesh + AXI rings + AHB/APB branches) is the modern SoC norm.
- **Reconfigurable parallelism** = DVFS + core-cluster migration (big.LITTLE); trade-off: performance vs power.

### Amdahl's Law & Parallelism
- $S(N) = 1 / ((1-f) + f/N)$; ceiling $S_{\max} = 1/(1-f)$.
- **Serial fraction dominates** as $N$ grows — diminishing returns above $\sim$10–100 cores for typical workloads.

### GPU Architecture
- **SIMT / SIMD** execution: warps of 32/64 threads, lockstep, masked branch divergence.
- **Peak FP32 FLOPS** = $N_{SM} \times N_{FP32/SM} \times f \times 2$ (FMA).
- **Memory hierarchy:** Registers → Shared → L1 → L2 → **HBM** (up to 3.35 TB/s) → Host DDR.
- **Compute-bound** when arithmetic intensity $I \gg P_{peak}/BW_{mem}$; **memory-bound** otherwise (**Roofline model**).
- **Branch divergence** and **occupancy** are the two biggest performance killers.
- **Tensor Cores** accelerate matrix-multiply-accumulate (FP16/BF16/INT8/FP8) — essential for AI.

### CPU vs GPU — Key Differences
- **CPU** = latency-optimised, few large OoO cores, large caches, control-intensive.
- **GPU** = throughput-optimised, thousands of in-order SIMT lanes, high-bandwidth HBM, data-parallel.
- They are **complementary**, not competitors — use both via **heterogeneous computing**.

### HPC & Industry
- HPC aggregates compute + interconnect + parallel file system to solve large problems.
- Modern HPC: **ExaFLOPS**-class machines (**Frontier**, **Aurora**, **Leonardo**) coupling CPUs and GPUs.
- **GPUs dominate AI** (training and inference of LLMs) because of massive tensor throughput and HBM bandwidth.
- **Mobile SoCs** integrate CPU + GPU + NPU + ISP — e.g., **Apple M3, Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, Dimensity 9300+**.

### Numerical Items to Memorise for the Exam
- AMAT formula (3-level).
- Amdahl's Law (1, 2, 4, 8, $\infty$ cases with $f = 0.8$ and $f = 0.95$).
- GPU peak FLOP computation example.
- Address partition for a given cache size and line size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always draw a **neat labelled block diagram** (worth 2–3 marks by itself), then provide a **formula**, then a **numerical example**, then a **comment / design implication**. This four-part structure is what KTU examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPU Programming Mode - GPU in Radiation Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GPU Programming Mode - GPU in Radiation Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3 – Embedded & Heterogeneous Computing)**
> The topic *GPU Programming Mode – GPU in Radiation Imaging* bridges the architectural understanding of modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) with their deployment as **parallel co-processors** in latency-critical medical and scientific workloads, particularly **Computed Tomography (CT), PET, SPECT, and Cone-Beam X-Ray imaging**.

---

## 1.1 What is a GPU Programming Mode?

A **GPU Programming Mode** is a *software execution paradigm* that exposes the massively-parallel SIMT (Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread) hardware of a GPU to the application programmer. It is the contract that defines:

- How the **host CPU** offloads work to the **device GPU**
- How **thousands of lightweight threads** are organized (grids → blocks → warps)
- How **memory hierarchies** (registers, shared, L1/L2, global, constant, texture) are addressed
- How **synchronization, data movement, and kernel launch** are managed

The dominant programming modes used in scientific and medical imaging are:

| Mode | Vendor / Body | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **CUDA** | NVIDIA | Gold standard for medical imaging pipelines |
| **OpenCL** | Khronos Group | Cross-platform (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, ARM, FPGA) |
| **DirectCompute** | Microsoft | DX11/DX12 shader-based compute |
| **Vulkan Compute** | Khronos Group | Low-overhead modern compute |
| **OpenMP / OpenACC** | Compiler directive-based | Easy GPU offload from C/C++/Fortran |
| **SYCL / oneAPI** | Intel / Khronos | Single-source C++ heterogeneous programming |
| **ROCm/HIP** | AMD | CUDA-like API for AMD GPUs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Ready):**
> *A GPU Programming Mode is an execution model that partitions a computational problem into a hierarchy of fine-grained, data-parallel threads, distributes these threads across the GPU's many-core SIMT engine, and coordinates host–device memory transfer to exploit massive arithmetic throughput (typically **≥ 10 TFLOPS** in modern parts) for highly-parallel workloads such as image reconstruction.*

---

## 1.2 What is GPU in Radiation Imaging?

Radiation imaging modalities (CT, PET, SPECT, digital X-ray, Cone-Beam CT) require the conversion of **raw detector sinograms/projection data** into diagnostically useful cross-sectional images. The computational core is a **reconstruction algorithm** (e.g., Filtered Back-Projection, OS-EM, MLEM, FDK) that is *embarrassingly parallel* — exactly the workload GPUs excel at.

**Key Performance Demands of Radiation Imaging:**

- **Latency budget:** < **1 second** per slice (clinical workflow)
- **Data volume:** A single 512×512×512 volumetric CT ≈ **134 M voxels (≈ 512 MB)**
- **Compute intensity:** FBP for a 1024-projection, 1024-detector row scan ≈ **~10¹² floating-point ops**
- **Determinism:** Hard real-time requirements for interventional imaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A modern clinical CT scanner cannot meet its reconstruction budget on a CPU alone. GPUs deliver **30×–300× speedup** over multi-core CPUs, making **real-time, iterative, model-based reconstruction** clinically viable.

---

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Parallel Workshop" 🏭

Imagine you have to paint **1 million identical tiles** in a complex pattern:

- **CPU approach (the master craftsman):** One highly skilled painter does them one at a time, with extreme care and complex judgement. He is fast per tile, but there are simply too many tiles.
- **GPU approach (the army of apprentices):** You gather **10,000 apprentices**, give each a *simple rule* ("read pixel (x,y), multiply by kernel value, accumulate") and the *same instruction broadcast* to all. Each apprentice does one tiny task simultaneously. The total time collapses by orders of magnitude.

This is exactly what happens in **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**:
- Every pixel in the reconstructed image is computed **independently**
- The same filter kernel and back-projection geometry are applied to each
- A GPU runs *thousands* of such pixel-operations per clock cycle, in **SIMT lockstep**

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of GPU programming is that we trade per-thread sophistication (the apprentice is dumb) for **massive, coordinated parallelism** (the army is huge and synchronised).

---

## 1.4 Why GPUs Changed Radiation Imaging

| Bottleneck (Pre-GPU) | GPU-Enabled Reality |
|---|---|
| Reconstruction took **minutes per volume** | Sub-second reconstruction |
| Only analytical FBP feasible | **Iterative reconstruction (OS-EM, IRIS, MBIR)** now routine |
| Static, low-resolution imaging | **4D dynamic**, gated cardiac, perfusion imaging |
| High radiation dose needed for clean images | **Low-dose imaging** with statistical/Priors denoising |
| Reconstruction locked to dedicated hardware | **Software-defined scanners** on commodity GPUs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟩 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Host–Device Heterogeneous Programming Model

Modern radiation-imaging systems follow the **heterogeneous computing model** (sometimes called the *device/host* model):

```
┌──────────────────────┐         PCIe / NVLink          ┌──────────────────────┐
│   HOST (CPU + DRAM)  │  ◀──────────────────────────▶   │   DEVICE (GPU)       │
│   - C/C++ program    │    Kernel launch + data DMA     │   - SIMT cores       │
│   - I/O, controls    │                                 │   - VRAM (HBM/GDDR)  │
│   - Reconstruction   │                                 │   - Texture units    │
│     orchestrator     │                                 │   - Tensor cores*    │
└──────────────────────┘                                 └──────────────────────┘
```

A typical **GPU kernel launch** for back-projection looks like:

```c
// Conceptual sketch (NOT for execution, NOT a Python algorithm)
dim3 grid(512, 512);   // 1 thread per image pixel
dim3 block(16, 16);    // 16×16 thread block
backprojectKernel<<<grid, block>>>(d_sinogram, d_image, N_proj, N_det);
```

- The **host CPU** allocates device memory, copies sinogram from system RAM → VRAM, launches the kernel, and copies the reconstructed volume back.
- The **device GPU** executes the kernel in SIMT fashion: every thread runs the *same* code on a *different* pixel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *programming mode* is the abstraction that hides this heterogeneous choreography from the algorithm designer. The student must understand the abstraction, not memorise vendor API syntax.

---

## 2.2 The SIMT Execution Hierarchy

The SIMT (Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread) model of a GPU is **strictly hierarchical**:

| Level | Quantity (typical) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Thread** | Tens of thousands | Smallest unit; executes one instance of the kernel |
| **Warp** (NVIDIA) / **Wavefront** (AMD) | **32** threads (NVIDIA) | Lockstep group; 1 instruction issued per cycle |
| **Block** / **CTA** | Up to 1024 threads | Resident on one SM; can use shared memory and `__syncthreads()` |
| **Grid** | Many blocks | Covers the entire problem (e.g., the image) |
| **SM** (Streaming Multiprocessor) | 100+ per GPU | Executes blocks; owns register file + shared mem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch divergence** within a warp is the #1 performance killer. In radiation imaging, we avoid it by structuring loops so neighbouring threads follow the same path (e.g., ray-driven back-projection is divergence-heavy, while pixel-driven back-projection is divergence-friendly).

---

## 2.3 GPU Memory Hierarchy — The Bandwidth Story

GPU performance in radiation imaging is **memory-bound**, not compute-bound. Reconstruction must saturate VRAM bandwidth.

| Memory | Scope | Size (typ.) | Bandwidth (typ.) | Latency (cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Register** | Per thread | 256 KB per SM | ~ TB/s (internal) | 0 |
| **Shared memory** | Per block | 48–228 KB per SM | ~ TB/s | ~20 |
| **L1 / Texture cache** | Per SM | 128 KB | TB/s | ~30 |
| **L2 cache** | Global to GPU | 4–96 MB | 5–10 TB/s | ~200 |
| **Global VRAM (HBM3/GDDR7)** | Whole GPU | 16–80 GB | **0.8 – 1.8 TB/s** | ~400 |
| **Constant cache** | Read-only | 64 KB | TB/s | ~30 |
| **Host DRAM (over PCIe Gen5)** | CPU side | System | **~64 GB/s** | ~1000+ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **throughput** (GB/s) with **latency** (ns). GPUs *hide* latency by switching between thousands of warps, not by reducing it. This is called **latency-hiding by TLP (Thread-Level Parallelism)**.

---

## 2.4 Radiation Imaging Reconstruction — Algorithm Taxonomy

Reconstruction algorithms are the workload that GPUs accelerate. They split into two families:

### A. Analytical Reconstruction (deterministic, fast)
- **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** — 1D filtering of projections + smearing back across the image grid. Parallelises trivially.
- **Feldkamp–Davis–Kress (FDK)** — FBP extended to cone-beam geometry.
- **Ramp filter, Hamming, Shepp–Logan, Cosine, Hann** windowed filters applied in Fourier domain.

### B. Iterative Reconstruction (statistical, low-dose, high quality)
- **MLEM** (Maximum-Likelihood Expectation-Maximisation) — EM-based, no closed form.
- **OS-EM** (Ordered-Subset EM) — accelerates MLEM by sub-grouping projections.
- **MAP / Bayesian** — adds prior (e.g., total variation) for denoising.
- **MBIR / IRIS** — model-based iterative reconstruction (state-of-art in modern CT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Iterative methods are **inherently sequential** between iterations, but **embarrassingly parallel within an iteration**. The GPU programming model exploits the *within-iteration* parallelism while the host CPU manages the *outer iteration* loop and convergence check.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Gold:** Memorise these equations. They appear almost verbatim in Part A numericals and Part B derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S(N) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** — speedup with N parallel processors, P parallel fraction | dimensionless |
| 2 | $S_{\text{Gustafson}}(N) \;=\; N - \alpha(N-1)$ | **Gustafson's Law** (scaled-speedup), $\alpha$ = serial fraction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $e \;=\; \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | Parallel **efficiency** | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $T_{\text{wall}} \;=\; T_{\text{serial}} \cdot \left[(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}\right]$ | Wall-clock time with N workers | seconds |
| 5 | $F \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Tasks}}{\text{Time}}$ | **Throughput** (FLOPS, projections/s) | varies |
| 6 | $T_{\text{float}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Op count}}{F_{\text{peak}}}$ | Theoretical minimum runtime | seconds |
| 7 | $\text{MEM}_\text{needed} \;=\; N_x \cdot N_y \cdot N_z \cdot 4$ bytes | Volume memory (float32), 4 bytes/voxel | bytes |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{mem}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Useful bytes}}{\text{Total bytes transferred}}$ | **Memory efficiency / coalescing ratio** | 0–1 |
| 9 | $R_{\text{recon}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{slices}}}{T_{\text{recon}}}$ | **Reconstruction rate** | slices/s |
| 10 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \;=\; \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} \cdot \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}}$ | Poisson-limited SNR (radiation physics) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_{\text{dose}} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{photons}}}$ | **Dose** inversely proportional to photons | mGy |
| 12 | $S_{\text{FBP}} \;=\; N_{\theta} \cdot N_{\text{det}} \cdot N_x \cdot N_y$ | FBP **operation count** (order of) | FLOPs |

> [!TIP]
> **Coefficient of Performance (CoP)** and **Performance per Watt** are common in KTU Part-A "define the metric" questions.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of GPU-Accelerated Imaging

| Field | Concrete Utility |
|---|---|
| **Diagnostic Radiology** | Real-time 3D CT volume rendering, low-dose lung screening |
| **Radiotherapy (Linac + kV/MV imaging)** | On-board CBCT reconstruction during treatment |
| **Nuclear Medicine (PET/SPECT)** | Time-of-flight (TOF) PET with sub-200 ps timing |
| **Interventional X-Ray** | 30 fps 2D fluoroscopy + 3D overlay |
| **AI-Aided Diagnosis** | Convolutional / Transformer inference on reconstructed images |
| **Industrial NDT** | Baggage-scanner CT, weld inspection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟧 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (The Speedup Bound)

Amdahl's Law is the **single most important equation** in any KTU GPU-parallelism question. We derive it rigorously.

**Step 1 — Decompose the runtime.**

Let the serial runtime of a task be $T_s$. Split it into a *parallel portion* $P \cdot T_s$ and a *strictly serial portion* $(1-P) \cdot T_s$, with $0 \le P \le 1$.

$$T_s \;=\; T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} \;=\; (1-P)\,T_s + P\,T_s$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the parallel part over $N$ workers.**

The serial part cannot be parallelised; the parallel part runs $N$ times faster (idealised).

$$T_{\text{parallel}}(N) \;=\; \dfrac{P\,T_s}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Add serial + parallel runtimes.**

$$T_{\text{wall}}(N) \;=\; (1-P)\,T_s + \dfrac{P\,T_s}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $T_s$.**

$$T_{\text{wall}}(N) \;=\; T_s \cdot \left[\,(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}\,\right]$$

**Step 5 — Define speedup as $S(N) = T_s \,/\, T_{\text{wall}}(N)$.**

$$\boxed{\,S(N) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit as $N \to \infty$:** the serial part dominates and
> $S_\infty \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1-P}$. This is the *hard ceiling* of parallel speedup — the reason GPU code must keep $P$ as close to 1 as possible.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Amdahl in CT Reconstruction

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A CT reconstruction algorithm takes **8.0 s** on a single CPU core. Of this, the back-projection loop (highly parallel) accounts for **92 %** of the runtime, while filtering and I/O (serial) account for the rest. The GPU provides the equivalent of **512 parallel "lanes"** for the back-projection.
> **(a)** Compute the maximum speedup.
> **(b)** Compute the wall-clock time on the GPU.
> **(c)** Compute the parallel efficiency.

**Given:**

$$T_s = 8.0 \text{ s}, \quad P = 0.92, \quad (1-P) = 0.08, \quad N = 512$$

**Part (a) — Speedup:**

$$S(512) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{512}}$$

Compute the denominator term-by-term:

$$\dfrac{0.92}{512} \;=\; 0.001796875 \;\approx\; 1.797 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$0.08 + 0.001797 \;=\; 0.081797$$

$$S(512) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.081797} \;\approx\; 12.225$$

**Part (b) — Wall-clock time:**

$$T_{\text{wall}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_s}{S(512)} \;=\; \dfrac{8.0}{12.225} \;\approx\; 0.654 \text{ s}$$

**Part (c) — Parallel efficiency:**

$$e \;=\; \dfrac{S(512)}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{12.225}{512} \;\approx\; 0.0239 \;=\; 2.39\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with $N=512$ lanes, the speedup is bounded to $\approx 12.5 \times$ because the **8 % serial part is the bottleneck**. To go faster, we must *reduce the serial fraction* (e.g., parallelise the filtering, overlap I/O with compute, use pinned-memory DMA).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Gustafson's Scaled Speedup

Amdahl's Law assumes the **problem size is fixed**. Gustafson (1988) argued that in practice, when given more processors, we solve a **bigger problem** in the same wall-clock time.

**Step 1 — Let the parallel run on $N$ workers take time $t$.**

$$t \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,t + \dfrac{\alpha\,t}{N} \quad \text{(normalised)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the serial fraction $\alpha$.**

Rearrange:

$$t - (1-\alpha)t \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,t}{N} \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha\,t \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,t}{N} \cdot N \;(\text{re-derive via scaled work})$$

Following the standard Gustafson treatment — define the **scaled work** $W = (1-\alpha)N + \alpha N = N[(1-\alpha) + \alpha] = N$ when normalised. The scaled speedup is:

$$\boxed{\,S_{\text{Gustafson}}(N) \;=\; N - \alpha(N-1)\,}$$

**Numerical check** — same CT example, $\alpha = 0.08$, $N=512$:

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(512) \;=\; 512 - 0.08(511) \;=\; 512 - 40.88 \;=\; 471.1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ~38× higher figure reflects reality for *clinical CT*: with more GPU lanes, we don't shrink a 1-second scan to 8 ms — we keep the scan at 1 s and acquire **38× more projection angles / finer slices / iterative refinement**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Memory-Coalesced Access Pattern

For a 1D thread index $i$ in a warp of 32 threads, an **uncoalesced** load has each thread reading 1 byte from 32 separate cache lines (32 × 32 B = 1024 B total DRAM transactions).

A **coalesced** load has the 32 threads reading 32 consecutive 4-byte words from a single 128 B transaction.

$$\eta_{\text{mem}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Ideal transactions}}{\text{Actual transactions}} \;=\; \dfrac{1 \cdot 128\,\text{B}}{32 \cdot 128\,\text{B}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{32} \;=\; 3.125\% \quad \text{(un-coalesced)}$$

For back-projection, the **pixel-driven** pattern is naturally coalesced because neighbouring threads (neighbouring $x$ indices) read **neighbouring sinogram bins**. The **ray-driven** pattern is the opposite: threads collaborate on one pixel, hence divergent.

---

## 3.5 Performance Model — Roofline for FBP

The **Roofline model** lets us predict whether a kernel is compute-bound or memory-bound:

$$\text{Attainable FLOPS} \;=\; \min\!\left( F_{\text{peak}},\; \beta \cdot I \right)$$

where $\beta$ = memory bandwidth (FLOP/s) and $I$ = arithmetic intensity (FLOPs / byte).

For **pixel-driven FBP** on a 1024-projection, 1024-detector, 512×512 image:

$$I_{\text{FBP}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{2 \, N_{\theta} \, N_{\text{det}}}{4 \cdot N_{\theta} \cdot N_{\text{det}} + 4 \cdot N_x N_y} \;\approx\; 0.5 \text{ FLOP/byte}$$

With $\beta = 1.5$ TB/s on an HBM3 GPU:

$$\text{Attainable} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{12} \times 0.5 \;=\; 750 \text{ GFLOPS}$$

Modern GPUs peak at **~80 TFLOPS**, so **FBP is firmly memory-bound** (we are at <1% of peak compute). The optimisation target is *bandwidth*, not arithmetic.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CPU vs GPU vs FPGA for Reconstruction

| Parameter | Multi-core CPU (e.g., 64-core Xeon) | GPU (e.g., RTX-class / H100) | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak FP32 | ~5 TFLOPS | ~80 TFLOPS | ~1–5 TFLOPS (DSP) |
| Memory BW | ~200 GB/s (DDR5 octa-channel) | **~1.5 TB/s (HBM3)** | ~25 GB/s (DDR4) |
| Latency to first result | Low (~µs) | High (~10 µs kernel launch) | **Lowest (~ns)** |
| Power | ~250 W | ~350–700 W | ~30–100 W |
| Programmability | High (C/C++, OpenMP) | High (CUDA, OpenCL) | Low (HDL/HLS) |
| Best for reconstruction | OS-EM orchestration | **FBP, FDK, OS-EM inner loop** | Inline detector preprocessing |
| Cost per node | Medium | High | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> The GPU's 7.5× memory bandwidth advantage over CPU is **the** reason FBP feels "free" on GPU. Without HBM, GPUs would be irrelevant to imaging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🟪 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GPU-Accelerated Radiation-Imaging Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a GPU-driven CT scanner, from detector photons to displayed DICOM volume. It uses safe node identifiers and label formatting per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[X-Ray Tube & Detector Array] --> B[Raw Projection Frames]
    B --> C[Detector Pre-Processing FPGA]
    C --> D[Sinogram Assembly in Host DRAM]
    D --> E[PCIe DMA Transfer to GPU VRAM]
    E --> F[GPU Kernel 1: 1D Ramp Filtering]
    F --> G[GPU Kernel 2: Pixel-Driven Back-Projection]
    G --> H[GPU Kernel 3: Volume Assembly 3D]
    H --> I[Iterative Refinement OS-EM optional]
    I --> J[GPU Kernel 4: Volume Rendering / MPR]
    J --> K[DICOM Output to PACS]
    K --> L[Radiologist Workstation Display]

    subgraph HOST_CPU_SIDE
        D
        K
    end

    subgraph DEVICE_GPU_SIDE
        E
        F
        G
        H
        I
        J
    end

    M[Scanner Gantry Controller] --> A
    L --> N[Diagnostic Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *vertical spine* (A → L) is the data path. The **subgraph overlays** show what runs on the host vs device. The **iterative refinement** step (I) is the only stage that loops back to the GPU multiple times per volume — a classic OS-EM pattern.

---

## 4.2 SIMT Thread Hierarchy in a Reconstruction Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Grid: full image 512x512] --> B1[Block 16x16 at 0,0]
    G1 --> B2[Block 16x16 at 0,16]
    G1 --> B3[Block 16x16 at 16,0]
    G1 --> B4[Block 16x16 at 16,16]
    B1 --> W1[Warp 0: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W2[Warp 1: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W3[Warp 2: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W4[Warp 3: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W5[Warp 4: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W6[Warp 5: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W7[Warp 6: 32 threads]
    B1 --> W8[Warp 7: 32 threads]
    W1 --> T1[Thread tx,ty]
    W1 --> T2[Thread tx+1,ty]
    W1 --> T3[Thread tx+2,ty]
    W1 --> TN[... 32 SIMT lanes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block contains 256 threads = 8 warps. Each thread processes exactly one image pixel — the canonical pixel-driven FBP mapping.

---

## 4.3 GPU Memory Access Pattern for Back-Projection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Thread i reads sinogram] --> B{Is access coalesced?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Single 128 B DRAM transaction]
    B -- No --> D[32 separate transactions 4 KB penalty]
    C --> E[L2 cache hit next iteration]
    D --> F[Bandwidth wasted 32x]
    E --> G[High kernel efficiency]
    F --> H[Memory-bound kernel stagnates]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison: CPU-FBP vs GPU-FBP Wall-Clock Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Reconstruction Timeline (512x512x512 volume, 1024 projections)
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %S
    section CPU Pipeline
    Filter all projections    :a1, 0, 4000
    Back-project              :a2, 4000, 22000
    section GPU Pipeline
    PCIe H2D copy             :b1, 0, 200
    Filter kernel             :b2, 200, 400
    Back-project kernel       :b3, 400, 900
    PCIe D2H copy             :b4, 900, 1000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The GPU pipeline (~1 s) is dominated by **PCIe transfer of the sinogram** (≈200 ms). This is why high-end imaging systems use **NVLink or GPUDirect RDMA** to bypass PCIe for streaming detector data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🟥 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define GPU Programming Mode. With a neat block diagram, explain the host–device heterogeneous execution model.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80–100 words):**
A *GPU Programming Mode* is an execution model that allows the host CPU to offload data-parallel kernels to the many-core GPU. The CPU (host) prepares data, allocates device memory, transfers the sinogram over PCIe, and launches a kernel. The GPU (device) executes the kernel in **SIMT (Single-Instruction Multiple-Thread)** fashion: every thread runs the same code on a different data element (e.g., one image pixel). Communication between host and device is via DMA over PCIe/NVLink. CUDA, OpenCL, and SYCL are typical implementations of this model.

*Block diagram:* CPU/DRAM ↔ PCIe ↔ GPU/VRAM (with kernel-launch arrows and data-DMA arrows).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List any three applications of GPU computing in radiation-based medical imaging. Why is FBP (Filtered Back-Projection) a natural fit for GPU acceleration?**

**Model Answer (≈ 70–90 words):**
Three applications: (i) **CT reconstruction** (FBP, FDK, iterative), (ii) **PET image reconstruction** (OS-EM, TOF), (iii) **Cone-Beam CT in image-guided radiotherapy**, and (iv) **real-time X-ray fluoroscopy denoising**.
FBP is naturally parallel because each output pixel is computed **independently** by smearing filtered projection data along known ray paths. The per-pixel computation is a simple multiply-accumulate loop across all angles, requiring only local sinogram reads. This **data-parallel, embarrassingly parallel** structure maps 1-to-1 onto the SIMT threads of a GPU, achieving hundreds of GFLOPS of sustained throughput.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⬛ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Architecture & Programming-Mode Focus*

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Part B]`
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **SIMT execution model of a modern GPU**. Explain the relationship between *grid → block → warp → thread* and the role of **latency hiding via thread-level parallelism**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the **memory hierarchy** of a GPU (registers, shared, L1, L2, global VRAM, constant). Discuss why **memory bandwidth, not peak FLOPS, is the limiting factor in FBP back-projection** for CT. Use the **Roofline model** to justify your answer. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
SIMT = **Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread**. A single instruction stream is broadcast to many threads that execute it in lockstep on different data items. This is the GPU's primary programming abstraction.

**Hierarchy diagram (3 marks):**

```
Grid (entire image)
 └── Block (CTA) [256 threads typical]
      ├── Warp 0 [32 threads]  → SM executes 1 instr/cycle
      ├── Warp 1
      ├── …
      └── Warp 7
           ├── Thread 0
           ├── Thread 1
           └── …
```

**Latency hiding (2 marks):**
Each SM holds 1000s of resident threads partitioned into warps. While one warp stalls on a memory access (~400 cycles), the warp scheduler switches to another ready warp in **zero-overhead** cycles. Because there are always many warps in flight, the *throughput* (FLOPS/s) is high even though *per-thread latency* is high. This is **TLP-driven latency tolerance**, the opposite of CPU's *speculative-OoO latency reduction*.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Memory hierarchy (3 marks):**

| Level | Per-thread accessibility | Use in FBP |
|---|---|---|
| Register | Yes | Pixel index `i,j`, accumulators |
| Shared | Within block | Filter coefficients for a warp's slice |
| Constant | All threads, broadcast | Ramp filter kernel (read once, reused) |
| L1/L2 cache | Transparent | Sinogram locality |
| Global VRAM | All threads | Full sinogram + output image |

**Bandwidth-bound argument (3 marks):**
FBP per pixel requires $N_\theta$ reads from the sinogram (one per angle) and one write. With $N_x N_y$ pixels, total memory traffic $\approx 4 N_\theta N_{\text{det}} + 4 N_x N_y$ bytes. The arithmetic per pixel is only $\approx 2 N_\theta$ FLOPs. Hence arithmetic intensity
$I \approx 0.5$ FLOP/byte. With $\beta = 1.5$ TB/s, the **Roofline** gives an attainable rate of $0.75$ TFLOPS, far below GPU peak (~80 TFLOPS). Therefore FBP is **memory-bound**; optimisation must focus on **coalesced access, sinogram tiling in shared memory, and minimising redundant reads**.

**Closing (1 mark):**
This is why radiation-imaging GPU code is judged by *bandwidth utilisation* (achieving >70% of $\beta$) rather than by FLOPS.

---

### ⬛ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Algorithm & Application Focus*

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | Part B]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm** for CT reconstruction. Show mathematically how the reconstructed image $f(x,y)$ is obtained from filtered projections. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A CT reconstruction takes **12 s** on a CPU. **90 %** of the runtime is in the parallelisable back-projection. The GPU offers **256 effective parallel lanes**.
> (i) Compute the Amdahl speedup. (ii) Compute the wall-clock time on the GPU. (iii) Comment on the **limiting factor** and suggest **two engineering strategies** to push the speedup closer to the theoretical $1/(1-P) = 10\times$ ceiling. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Continuous FBP equation (3 marks):**

$$f(x,y) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[\,p_{\theta}(s)\,*\,h(s)\,\right]_{s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta} \, d\theta$$

where:
- $p_\theta(s)$ = projection at angle $\theta$ and detector position $s$
- $h(s)$ = ramp-filter kernel (inverse Fourier of $\vert \omega \vert$)
- $*$ = 1D convolution along the detector axis

**Steps in a digital implementation (3 marks):**
1. For each projection angle $\theta_k$, sample the detector array to get $p_{\theta_k}[s_n]$.
2. Filter each projection in the Fourier domain: $P_{\theta_k}(\omega) \cdot \vert \omega \vert$ (the "ramp filter"), or equivalently convolve in spatial domain with discrete $h[n]$.
3. For every image pixel $(x_i, y_j)$, compute the *back-projection coordinate* $s = x_i \cos\theta_k + y_j \sin\theta_k$, interpolate the filtered projection, and accumulate over all $k$.
4. The accumulated value at $(x_i, y_j)$ is the reconstructed pixel.

**Why it is GPU-friendly (1 mark):**
The inner loop (step 3) is purely data-parallel across pixels; with one thread per pixel and pre-filtered projections in VRAM, the kernel is a textbook SIMT workload.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $T_s = 12$ s, $P = 0.90$, $N = 256$.

**(i) Amdahl speedup (2 marks):**

$$S(256) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{256}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.003516} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.103516}$$

$$S(256) \;\approx\; 9.661$$

**(ii) Wall-clock time (1 mark):**

$$T_{\text{GPU}} \;=\; \dfrac{12}{9.661} \;\approx\; 1.242 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Limiting factor and remedies (4 marks):**

**Limiting factor:** The 10 % serial portion (pre-processing, I/O, filter pipeline) dominates. As $N \to \infty$ the speedup ceiling is $1/(1-P) = 10\times$, and we are already at 9.66× — i.e., adding more GPU lanes is *useless* without attacking the serial fraction.

**Engineering strategies:**
1. **Parallelise the filtering stage** (currently serial) by launching a 1D FFT / convolution kernel on the GPU before back-projection. This raises $P$ to ≥ 0.97.
2. **Overlap I/O with compute** using *double-buffered* PCIe transfers (CUDA streams / OpenCL pipelines), so the next sinogram streams in while the current volume is being back-projected. This effectively hides the DMA time.
3. *(Bonus)* Replace FBP with **OS-EM iterative reconstruction** that uses the GPU for both forward- and back-projection; the inner loops are larger and amortise the serial cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing throughput with latency.** Students write "GPU is fast" — KTU expects "GPU has high *throughput* (TFLOPS) achieved by hiding *latency* via TLP."
> 2. **Forgetting the serial fraction.** A common mistake is to write $S = N$ (i.e., 256×) when the question clearly says $P=0.90$. This loses **3 of 7** marks.
> 3. **Calling CUDA "the" GPU programming mode.** CUDA is one vendor's mode; the *concept* is the heterogeneous host–device SIMT model. Examiners will deduct for "CUDA = GPU programming" statements.
> 4. **Mixing up "kernel" meanings.** A CUDA/OpenCL kernel is *not* an OS kernel. State "a kernel is a function executed on the GPU by many threads" — not "a kernel is the operating system core."
> 5. **No units on throughput.** Writing "GPU does 80" without "TFLOPS" loses the mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin these to your wall one week before the exam.**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **GPU Programming Mode** = abstraction for offloading SIMT kernels to many-core GPU
- ☐ **Host–Device model** = CPU orchestrates, GPU executes
- ☐ **SIMT** = single instruction, many threads (lockstep warps)
- ☐ **Kernel** = function executed in parallel by GPU threads
- ☐ **Grid → Block → Warp → Thread** = GPU execution hierarchy
- ☐ **Latency Hiding** = switching warps while others stall on memory

### Reconstruction Knowledge
- ☐ **FBP** = filter projections with ramp kernel + back-project onto image grid
- ☐ **FDK** = FBP for cone-beam geometry
- ☐ **OS-EM** = ordered-subset EM; iterative, statistical, low-dose
- ☐ **Pixel-driven FBP** is **coalesced**; ray-driven is **divergent** (avoid on GPU)
- ☐ **Memory-bound**: FBP arithmetic intensity ≈ 0.5 FLOP/byte
- ☐ **PCIe is the bottleneck** for streaming sinograms; use NVLink/RDMA

### Key Numbers to Memorise
- ☐ Amdahl: $S = 1 / [(1-P) + P/N]$, limit $1/(1-P)$
- ☐ Gustafson: $S = N - \alpha(N-1)$
- ☐ Efficiency: $e = S/N$
- ☐ Roofline: $\min(F_{\text{peak}}, \beta I)$
- ☐ Modern GPU peak: **~80 TFLOPS (FP32)**, **~1.5 TB/s HBM3**
- ☐ CT volume 512³ float32 ≈ **512 MB**
- ☐ FBP ops ≈ $N_\theta \cdot N_{\text{det}} \cdot N_x \cdot N_y$

### KTU Buzzwords to Drop in Answers (for top-band marks)
- ☐ *Embarrassingly parallel workload*
- ☐ *Memory coalesced access pattern*
- ☐ *Arithmetic intensity / Roofline bound*
- ☐ *Throughput-oriented, latency-tolerant architecture*
- ☐ *Heterogeneous host–device execution*
- ☐ *Domain-decomposed grid (one block per tile)*
- ☐ *Real-time reconstruction budget (~1 s/slice)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of ESP32 Development Board
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Features of ESP32 Development Board

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **ESP32 Development Board** is a low-cost, low-power, dual-core **System on Chip (SoC)** microcontroller module developed by **Espressif Systems (Shanghai, China)**. It integrates a powerful **32-bit Xtensa LX6 (or LX7) microprocessor**, on-chip **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** and **dual-mode Bluetooth (Classic BT v4.2 + BLE)** radio transceivers, alongside a rich set of peripherals including **GPIO**, **ADC**, **DAC**, **SPI**, **I²C**, **UART**, **I²S**, **PWM**, **Capacitive Touch Sensors**, **Hall Effect Sensors**, and an **Ultra-Low-Power (ULP) co-processor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ESP32 belongs to the family of **IoT-enabled microcontrollers** and is a direct successor to the **ESP8266**. It is the de-facto industry standard for **Wireless Sensor Nodes (WSN)** and **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** prototyping under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Advanced Microprocessors syllabus.

## 1.2 Key Variants in the ESP32 Family

| Variant | Core | Notable Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **ESP32-D0WD** | Dual-core LX6 | Original flagship |
| **ESP32-S2** | Single-core LX7 | USB-OTG native |
| **ESP32-S3** | Dual-core LX7 | AI vector instructions |
| **ESP32-C3** | Single-core RISC-V | Open-source ISA |
| **ESP32-H2** | Single-core RISC-V | 802.15.4 (Thread/Zigbee) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Swiss Army Knife"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of ESP32 as a Swiss Army Knife for IoT Engineers.**
>
> Just as a Swiss Army knife integrates a blade, scissors, screwdriver, and corkscrew into one compact tool, the **ESP32 SoC integrates a CPU, RAM, ROM, Wi-Fi radio, Bluetooth radio, ADC, DAC, timers, and security accelerators onto a single 5 mm × 5 mm silicon die.** Where a traditional microcontroller (e.g., 8051) is like a single screwdriver — useful but limited — the ESP32 is a *connected multi-tool* that can sense the physical world (via ADC/touch), process data (via CPU), and transmit it wirelessly (via Wi-Fi/BT) all at once, on a coin-cell battery.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Metric:** As of 2024, over **1 billion** ESP32 chips have been deployed globally, making it the most widely shipped Wi-Fi microcontroller in the embedded industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Specification Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown

The ESP32 is built around a **multi-stage bus matrix** allowing simultaneous data transfer between peripherals. The core architecture can be decomposed as follows:

### 2.1.1 Processing Cores
- **CPU:** Dual-core (or single-core in some variants) 32-bit **Xtensa LX6** architecture
- **Clock Frequency:** **80 MHz** (default) → **160 MHz** (overclocked) → **240 MHz** (max)
- **Performance:** Up to **600 DMIPS** (Dhrystone Million Instructions Per Second)
- **Co-processor:** **ULP (Ultra-Low-Power)** coprocessor for sleep-mode sensing at ~**150 µA** current draw

### 2.1.2 Memory Subsystem

| Memory Type | Capacity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ROM** | 448 KB | Bootloader & Core functions |
| **SRAM** | 520 KB | Data + Instruction cache |
| **RTC SRAM** | 16 KB | Retained during Deep-Sleep |
| **eFuse** | 1 Kbit | MAC address, Flash encryption keys |
| **External Flash** | 4 MB (typical) | Program storage (SPI-connected) |

### 2.1.3 Wireless Subsystem
- **Wi-Fi:** 802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz, up to **150 Mbps** (HT40), WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise support
- **Bluetooth:** v4.2 BR/EDR + **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)**
- **Range:** ~**45 m** (open field) for Wi-Fi, ~**10 m** for BLE
- **Antenna:** On-board PCB trace antenna **AND** IPEX (U.FL) connector for external antenna

### 2.1.4 Power & Sleep Modes

| Mode | Current Draw | Wake-up Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Active (Tx)** | **160–260 mA** | — |
| **Active (Rx)** | **80–100 mA** | — |
| **Modem-Sleep** | **15–20 mA** | Wi-Fi, BT, Timer |
| **Light-Sleep** | **0.8 mA** | GPIO, Touch, ULP |
| **Deep-Sleep** | **10 µA** | RTC timer, Touch, ULP |
| **Hibernation** | **2.5 µA** | RTC timer only |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Peripheral Specification Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all exam-relevant peripheral parameters** in a single glance. These numbers are the most frequently asked in KTU Part-A and Part-B questions.

| Peripheral | Specification | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **GPIO Pins** | **34** programmable GPIOs (multiplexed) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ADC** | **2 × 12-bit SAR** ADCs, 18 channels, **0–3.3 V** range | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **DAC** | **2 × 8-bit** DAC channels | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Touch Sensors** | **10** capacitive GPIOs (T0–T9) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **UART** | **3** hardware UARTs | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SPI** | **4** SPI interfaces (up to **80 MHz**) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **I²C** | **2** I²C interfaces (up to **400 kHz** Fast-mode) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **I²S** | **2** I²S for digital audio | ⭐⭐ |
| **PWM (LEDC)** | **16** channels, 20-bit resolution | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **CAN** | **1 × TWAI (CAN 2.0B)** controller | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **SD Host** | **1** SD/MMC interface | ⭐⭐ |
| **Ethernet** | **10/100 Mbps** MAC (requires external PHY) | ⭐⭐ |
| **Hall Sensor** | Internal (for magnetic field detection) | ⭐ |
| **Temperature Sensor** | Internal (accuracy ±10°C) | ⭐ |

## 2.3 Operational Voltage & Logic Levels

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Unlike Arduino (5 V logic), **ESP32 is a 3.3 V device**. Connecting a 5 V signal directly to any GPIO will **damage** the chip. Always use a **logic level shifter** when interfacing with 5 V peripherals (e.g., HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, certain RFID modules).

$$V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V} \pm 10\% \quad ; \quad V_{IH}(\text{min}) = 0.75 \times V_{DD} = 2.475\text{ V}$$
$$V_{IL}(\text{max}) = 0.25 \times V_{DD} = 0.825\text{ V}$$

## 2.4 Why ESP32? — Engineering Utility

1. **IoT Edge Devices** — Smart bulbs, smart locks, wearables.
2. **Industrial Automation** — Predictive maintenance via BLE/Wi-Fi telemetry.
3. **Smart Agriculture** — Soil-Moisture + Temperature sensing nodes with cloud upload.
4. **Audio Applications** — I²S-based MP3 players, voice assistants.
5. **Robotics** — Real-time motor control + wireless command relay.
6. **RTOS Deployment** — Native FreeRTOS support for deterministic multitasking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Numerical Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ESP32 is a *digital* topic, KTU examiners frequently test **numerical derivations** involving **ADC resolution, clock frequency scaling, power budgeting, and Wi-Fi throughput**. This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step solutions to standard numerical patterns.

## 3.1 ADC Resolution & Quantization Error Derivation

**Problem:** Compute the **resolution** and **maximum quantization error** of the ESP32's 12-bit ADC operating in the **0–3.3 V** range.

### Step 1 — Identify the Number of Discrete Levels
For an $n$-bit ADC, the number of quantization levels is:

$$L = 2^n$$

Substituting $n = 12$ (ESP32 ADC bits):

$$L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Voltage Resolution (LSB)
The voltage corresponding to **1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)** is:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF} - V_{MIN}}{L} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n - 1}$$

Substituting the standard ESP32 ADC full-scale voltage:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3\text{ V}}{4096 - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095}$$

After performing the division:

$$\Delta V \approx 0.00080586\text{ V} \approx 805.86\ \mu V$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Quantization Error (Q-Error)
The maximum quantization error equals **half** of one LSB:

$$Q_{err} = \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \frac{3.3}{2 \times 4095}$$

$$Q_{err} \approx 0.00040293\text{ V} \approx 402.93\ \mu V$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
> **Conclusion:** The ESP32 can distinguish voltages as small as **≈ 806 µV**, and the maximum measurement uncertainty is **≈ 403 µV**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **formula, substitution, and final unit** in the answer. Most students lose 1 mark by omitting the unit conversion (e.g., writing `403` instead of `403 µV`).

---

## 3.2 PWM Frequency & Duty-Cycle Numerical Derivation

**Problem:** The ESP32 LEDC peripheral is configured with a **20-bit timer** and a **base clock of 80 MHz**. The desired PWM frequency is **$f_{PWM} = 5\text{ kHz}$**. Compute the **prescaler** and the **duty-cycle value** for a **45%** duty cycle.

### Step 1 — Prescaler Derivation
The PWM output frequency is given by:

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{2^{N_{bit}} \times D_{div}}$$

where $N_{bit} = 20$ and $f_{CLK} = 80\text{ MHz}$. Rearranging for the clock-divider $D_{div}$:

$$D_{div} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{f_{PWM} \times 2^{N_{bit}}}$$

Substituting values:

$$D_{div} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{3} \times 2^{20}}$$

Evaluating the denominator:

$$5 \times 10^{3} \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 5.24288 \times 10^{9}$$

Therefore:

$$D_{div} = \frac{8 \times 10^{7}}{5.24288 \times 10^{9}} = 0.01526$$

Since $D_{div}$ must be an integer, we round to the nearest valid prescaler:

$$D_{div} = 1 \text{ (minimum value)}$$

This yields an **actual** output frequency:

$$f_{PWM,actual} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 2^{20}} \approx 76.29\text{ Hz}$$

This is too low → so we must reduce $N_{bit}$. The ESP32's hardware limits are:

$$1 \leq N_{bit} \leq 20 \quad ; \quad D_{div} \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}, \quad 1 \leq D_{div} \leq 1023$$

### Step 2 — Recompute Using $N_{bit} = 14$

$$D_{div} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{3} \times 2^{14}} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{81{,}920{,}000} \approx 0.977$$

Round to nearest valid integer:

$$D_{div} = 1 \quad ; \quad f_{PWM,actual} = \frac{80 \times 10^{6}}{2^{14}} = 4882.8\text{ Hz} \approx 4.88\text{ kHz}$$

### Step 3 — Duty-Cycle Computation
The maximum count for $N_{bit} = 14$ is:

$$M = 2^{14} - 1 = 16{,}383$$

For a 45% duty cycle:

$$D = \lfloor M \times 0.45 \rfloor = \lfloor 16{,}383 \times 0.45 \rfloor = \lfloor 7372.35 \rfloor = 7372$$

> **Final Configuration:** $D_{div} = 1$, $N_{bit} = 14$, Duty = **7372 / 16383** ≈ **45.0%**.

---

## 3.3 Power Budgeting for a Battery-Operated Node

**Problem:** An ESP32 IoT node operates in the following duty cycle: **Active 2 s @ 160 mA**, **Deep-Sleep 58 s @ 10 µA**. Compute the **average current** and **battery life** for a **2000 mAh** Li-Po battery.

### Step 1 — Average Current
Using the weighted-average formula:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{(I_{active} \times t_{active}) + (I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep})}{t_{active} + t_{sleep}}$$

Substituting:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{(160 \times 10^{-3} \times 2) + (10 \times 10^{-6} \times 58)}{2 + 58}$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$(0.160 \times 2) + (0.000010 \times 58) = 0.320 + 0.00058 = 0.32058\text{ mA}$$

Dividing by 60 s:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{0.32058}{60} = 0.005343\text{ mA} = 5.343\ \mu A$$

### Step 2 — Battery Life
The theoretical battery life (in hours) is:

$$T_{hours} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}}$$

$$T_{hours} = \frac{2000\text{ mAh}}{0.005343\text{ mA}} \approx 374{,}325\text{ h}$$

Converting to years:

$$T_{years} = \frac{374{,}325}{8760} \approx 42.7\text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** This is the **ideal** value. Real-world battery life is **20–30%** of this due to self-discharge, temperature, and Wi-Fi connection variability.

---

## 3.4 Wi-Fi Throughput vs. Range Trade-off

**Problem:** Given a transmitted power of $P_{tx} = 19.5\text{ dBm}$ at 2.4 GHz, and a path loss of $L = 80\text{ dB}$ over a distance of 30 m in an open field, determine the **received power** $P_{rx}$.

$$P_{rx}(\text{dBm}) = P_{tx}(\text{dBm}) - L(\text{dB})$$

$$P_{rx} = 19.5 - 80 = -60.5\text{ dBm}$$

The ESP32's typical **RX sensitivity** is **−98 dBm** at 1 Mbps (802.11b), so this link has a **link margin** of:

$$\text{Margin} = -60.5 - (-98) = 37.5\text{ dB}$$

This is a **very robust** link suitable for high-throughput operation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ESP32 vs. Competitors

| Parameter | **ESP32** | **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)** | **Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040)** | **STM32F407** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Xtensa LX6 Dual 32-bit | AVR 8-bit | Dual ARM Cortex-M0+ | ARM Cortex-M4 |
| **Clock** | 240 MHz | 16 MHz | 133 MHz | 168 MHz |
| **Flash** | 4 MB (ext) | 32 KB | 2 MB (ext) | 1 MB |
| **RAM** | 520 KB | 2 KB | 264 KB | 192 KB |
| **Wi-Fi/BT** | ✔ Built-in | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **ADC** | 2× 12-bit | 6× 10-bit | 3× 12-bit | 3× 12-bit |
| **Operating Voltage** | 3.3 V | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V |
| **Cost (INR, approx.)** | ₹250 | ₹450 | ₹400 | ₹550 |

---

## 4.1 ESP32 SoC Block Architecture (Functional Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **on-chip functional block architecture** of the ESP32, mapping how the CPU cores, memory, wireless, and peripherals intercommunicate through the **bus matrix**.



## 4.3 ESP32 Boot & Reset Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR["Power ON or RESET"] --> CHK{GPIO 0 State}
    CHK -- LOW --> BOOT["Bootloader Mode UART Download"]
    CHK -- HIGH --> NORM["Normal Boot from SPI Flash"]
    BOOT --> FLASH["Read Application from External Flash"]
    NORM --> FLASH
    FLASH --> CRYPTO{Flash Encryption Enabled}
    CRYPTO -- YES --> DECRYPT["Decrypt via AES Hardware Accelerator"]
    CRYPTO -- NO --> EXEC["Execute Program in SRAM XIP Mode"]
    DECRYPT --> EXEC
    EXEC --> FREE["FreeRTOS Scheduler Starts"]
    FREE --> TASK1["Task 1 on Core 0"]
    FREE --> TASK2["Task 2 on Core 1"]
    FREE --> ULP_RDY["ULP Coprocessor Ready for Sleep Duty"]
```

## 4.4 Power Mode State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> ModemSleep : WiFi Idle
    ModemSleep --> Active : Packet Tx Rx
    Active --> LightSleep : CPU Idle
    LightSleep --> Active : GPIO Touch ULP
    LightSleep --> DeepSleep : timer expires
    ModemSleep --> DeepSleep : long idle
    DeepSleep --> Hibernation : RTC config
    Hibernation --> Active : RTC Wake
    DeepSleep --> Active : GPIO Touch ULP Wake
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**List any six prominent features of the ESP32 Development Board that make it suitable for IoT applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Dual-core 32-bit Xtensa LX6 processor** running up to **240 MHz** for parallel processing.
2. **Built-in Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) and dual-mode Bluetooth (v4.2 + BLE)** eliminating the need for external radio modules.
3. **Ultra-Low-Power (ULP) co-processor** enabling **Deep-Sleep current of ~10 µA**, ideal for battery-powered nodes.
4. **34 programmable GPIOs** with rich peripherals: **2× 12-bit ADC, 2× 8-bit DAC, 10× touch sensors, 3× UART, 2× I²C, 4× SPI**.
5. **520 KB SRAM + 4 MB external flash** for complex firmware and OTA (Over-The-Air) updates.
6. **Native FreeRTOS support** allowing deterministic, real-time multitasking on dual cores.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Compare ESP32's Deep-Sleep and Active mode current consumption. Why is Deep-Sleep critical for IoT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Typical Current Draw |
|---|---|
| **Active (Wi-Fi Tx)** | **160–260 mA** |
| **Active (Wi-Fi Rx)** | **80–100 mA** |
| **Deep-Sleep** | **~10 µA** (≈ **10,000× lower**) |

**Why Deep-Sleep is Critical for IoT:** Most IoT nodes (e.g., temperature sensors) transmit data only a few times per hour. If a node stayed active continuously, a **2000 mAh battery** would last only ~**10–12 hours**. By entering Deep-Sleep between transmissions, the average current drops to **µA-level**, extending battery life to **months or even years** (refer Section 3.3 derivation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 2 × 14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Explain the **internal architecture of the ESP32** with a neat block diagram. List the roles of its **CPU cores, memory subsystem, and ULP coprocessor**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. CPU Architecture (2 marks):**
The ESP32 employs a **dual-core Xtensa LX6 32-bit RISC architecture**, with both cores running at a default clock of **80 MHz**, scalable to **160 MHz** and **240 MHz**. Each core has its own **interrupt controller, watchdog timer, and hardware timer**. The cores are independent but synchronize via **atomic operations** and **spinlocks**. This dual-core design enables **parallel task execution** — Core 0 typically handles the Wi-Fi/Bluetooth stack (Protocol CPU) while Core 1 runs user applications (Application CPU).

**2. Memory Subsystem (3 marks):**

| Memory | Size | Role |
|---|---|---|
| ROM | 448 KB | Storing bootloader, basic system functions, and the Wi-Fi/BT stack |
| SRAM | 520 KB | Divided into DRAM0 (160 KB), DRAM1 (128 KB), and IRAM/IRAM0 (192 KB) for instruction cache and data |
| RTC SRAM | 16 KB | Retained during Deep-Sleep for ULP and RTC data |
| eFuse | 1 Kbit | One-time programmable storage for MAC address, Flash Encryption keys, ADC calibration |

**3. ULP Co-processor (2 marks):**
The **ULP (Ultra-Low-Power) co-processor** is a low-frequency FSM (Finite-State Machine) that operates while the main CPUs are in Deep-Sleep. It can:
- Read **ADC values**, **temperature sensor**, and **touch sensor** data
- Execute pre-compiled instructions stored in RTC Slow Memory
- Wake the main CPU via **interrupts** when a threshold is crossed

This enables battery-operated sensors to monitor the environment at **~150 µA** instead of 160 mA, achieving a **1000× power reduction** for continuous monitoring.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Describe the **wireless capabilities** of ESP32. Differentiate between **Wi-Fi and Bluetooth Classic vs BLE** modes, and list **two real-world applications** of each.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Wi-Fi Subsystem (2 marks):**
ESP32 supports **802.11 b/g/n** in the **2.4 GHz ISM band**, with a maximum throughput of **150 Mbps** (40 MHz channel). Features include:
- **WPA/WPA2/WPA3** enterprise security
- **Station (STA)**, **Access Point (AP)**, and **STA+AP** coexistence modes
- **WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup)** for easy pairing
- Integrated **PCB antenna** plus **U.FL connector** for external antenna

**2. Bluetooth Subsystem (3 marks):**

| Mode | Version | Data Rate | Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR)** | v4.2 | 1–3 Mbps | ~40 mA | Audio streaming (A2DP), SPP |
| **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** | v4.2 | 125 kbps – 2 Mbps | ~15 mA | Sensor beacons, health monitors |

ESP32 supports **dual-mode coexistence**, allowing simultaneous operation of Classic BT and BLE. BLE uses **40 channels** in 2.4 GHz with **frequency hopping** and **AES-128 encryption**, optimized for **burst transmissions** of small data packets.

**3. Applications (2 marks):**
- **Wi-Fi Applications:** (i) Smart home automation cloud nodes (e.g., MQTT-based temperature upload to AWS IoT), (ii) Wireless IP camera streaming.
- **BLE Applications:** (i) Wearable fitness band transmitting heart-rate data to phone, (ii) Indoor proximity-based retail beacons (Apple iBeacon protocol).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Discuss the **power management** of ESP32 in detail. List the **five major power modes** with typical current values, and explain the role of the **ULP coprocessor** in power-critical applications.

**Model Solution:**

ESP32 implements a **hierarchical power management strategy** controlled by the **RTC (Real-Time Clock) domain** and a dedicated **Power Management Unit (PMU)**.

**Five Major Power Modes (4 marks):**

| Mode | Current | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Active** | **80–260 mA** | All systems ON, CPU @ 240 MHz, RF active |
| **Modem-Sleep** | **15–20 mA** | CPU active, Wi-Fi/BT radio OFF (paused) |
| **Light-Sleep** | **0.8 mA** | CPU paused, RTC + ULP active, wake-up in < 1 ms |
| **Deep-Sleep** | **10 µA** | Only RTC + ULP + RTC memory active |
| **Hibernation** | **2.5 µA** | Internal 8 MHz oscillator OFF, RTC timer + GPIO wake |

**ULP Co-processor's Role (3 marks):**
In a **battery-powered soil moisture sensor** scenario, the main CPU would consume **~160 mA** if it stayed active to poll the ADC every minute. Instead:
1. The main CPU goes to **Deep-Sleep (10 µA)**.
2. The **ULP coprocessor** wakes periodically, reads the ADC (~150 µA for the active duration), compares against a threshold, and stores in RTC SRAM.
3. If the threshold is breached (or after N samples), the ULP **wakes the main CPU**, which then transmits data via Wi-Fi and returns to sleep.

This hybrid architecture achieves **multi-year** battery life in real-world deployments.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Explain the **ADC and DAC peripherals** of ESP32. Calculate the **resolution** and **quantization error** for a reference voltage of **3.3 V**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. ADC Peripheral (3 marks):**
- ESP32 has **2 SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADCs** — ADC1 and ADC2.
- Resolution: **12-bit** (4096 discrete levels).
- ADC1: **8 channels** (GPIO 32–39).
- ADC2: **10 channels** (GPIO 0, 2, 4, 12–15, 25–27).
- ⚠️ **ADC2 is shared with Wi-Fi** and cannot be used when Wi-Fi is active.
- Total measurable voltage range: **0 V to 3.3 V** (configurable via attenuation: 0 dB, 2.5 dB, 6 dB, 11 dB).
- Input impedance: Very high (high-Z), supporting low-current sensors.

**2. DAC Peripheral (2 marks):**
- **2 channels** (DAC1 = GPIO 25, DAC2 = GPIO 26).
- **8-bit resolution** → 256 levels.
- Output range: **0 V to 3.3 V**.
- Used for waveform generation, audio output, and analog reference voltages.

**3. Numerical Derivation (2 marks):**
As derived in **Section 3.1**:

$$\Delta V = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 805.86\ \mu V$$

$$Q_{err} = \frac{\Delta V}{2} \approx 402.93\ \mu V$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing ADC1 and ADC2:** ADC2 is *unusable* during Wi-Fi operation. **−1 mark** if student claims "32 ADC channels" without this caveat.
> 2. **Quoting wrong voltage range:** ESP32 GPIOs are **3.3 V only**, not 5 V. **−2 marks** if not specified.
> 3. **Forgetting ULP:** ULP coprocessor is *not* the same as the RTC. ULP is a programmable FSM. **−1 mark** for vague descriptions.
> 4. **Wrong clock frequency:** Many textbooks still cite **160 MHz** as the max, but ESP32 can be overclocked to **240 MHz**. **−1 mark** for using outdated spec.
> 5. **Ignoring strapping pins:** Pins GPIO 0, 2, 5, 12, 15 affect boot behavior. **−2 marks** if a hardware design question ignores them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Before going into the KTU exam, verify you can tick off every item below:**

- [x] ESP32 is a **dual-core 32-bit Xtensa LX6** SoC by **Espressif Systems**.
- [x] Default clock is **80 MHz**, max overclocked to **240 MHz**.
- [x] Integrated **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz)** + **Bluetooth v4.2 (BR/EDR + BLE)**.
- [x] Memory: **448 KB ROM, 520 KB SRAM, 16 KB RTC SRAM, 4 MB ext. Flash**.
- [x] **34 GPIOs**, **2× 12-bit ADC** (18 channels), **2× 8-bit DAC**, **10× touch sensors**.
- [x] **3× UART, 2× I²C, 4× SPI, 2× I²S, 1× CAN (TWAI), 16× PWM channels**.
- [x] **Operating Voltage: 3.3 V** — NOT 5 V tolerant on GPIOs.
- [x] **Deep-Sleep current: ~10 µA**, Hibernation: ~2.5 µA.
- [x] **ULP co-processor** can read ADC/touch/temperature while main CPU sleeps.
- [x] **FreeRTOS** pre-installed; supports OTA updates.
- [x] **Strapping pins:** GPIO 0, 2, 5, 12, 15 — must be carefully managed.
- [x] **ADC2 conflicts with Wi-Fi** — use ADC1 when Wi-Fi is active.
- [x] ADC resolution = $V_{ref} / (2^n - 1) = 3.3 / 4095 \approx 806\ \mu V$.
- [x] Max quantization error = $\Delta V / 2 \approx 403\ \mu V$.
- [x] ESP32 variants: **ESP32, ESP32-S2, ESP32-S3, ESP32-C3, ESP32-H2**.
- [x] Native support for **AES, SHA, RSA, RNG** hardware crypto accelerators.
- [x] **PCB trace antenna** + **U.FL connector** for external antenna.

> **KTU 2024 Closing Note:** ESP32 questions typically test **architectural knowledge** (Part A) and **analytical/derivation** skills (Part B). Always pair your answer with a **neat block diagram** and at least one **numerical calculation** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's **PEEBT522 – Advanced Microprocessors & Microcontrollers (2024 Scheme)**, **Module 4 – Architecture** addresses the internal organization of advanced **32-bit RISC microcontrollers**, with a primary focus on the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture** and its derivatives (ARM7TDMI / Cortex-M family). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> *Architecture of advanced microcontrollers – study of programmer's model, register banks, operating modes, exception handling, instruction pipeline, AMBA bus architecture, and memory organization of ARM-based systems.*

**Architecture** in this context refers to the **programmer-visible model** plus the underlying **hardware organization** that dictates how a microcontroller fetches, decodes, and executes instructions while interacting with memory, peripherals, and the external world.

### 1.1.1 Three Pillars of Architecture
| Pillar | Description | Example in ARM7TDMI |
|---|---|---|
| **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** | Defines instructions, addressing modes, data types | Thumb (16-bit) + ARM (32-bit) |
| **Micro-architecture** | Pipeline depth, caches, bus width | 3-stage pipeline |
| **System Architecture** | Memory map, bus protocol, peripherals | AMBA AHB / ASB / APB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**  
> The *Architecture* module is **NOT** about the instruction set. It is about the **structural & functional blueprint** of the chip — registers, modes, pipeline stages, exceptions, and bus structure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern hospital**:

- **The CPU Core** = the **Doctor** (decision-maker, processes cases/operations)
- **The Register Bank** = the **Doctor's clipboard** (holds immediate patient info — current case, status flags, scratch notes)
- **The Pipeline** = the **Triage assembly line** (one nurse checks temperature, another checks BP, another prescribes — tasks overlap)
- **The Memory Bus (AMBA AHB)** = the **elevator system** (high-speed transport for patients/data between floors)
- **The Exception Handler** = the **Emergency Room** (high-priority interruptions — accidents/heart attacks jump the queue)
- **Operating Modes** = the **different wards** (General Ward, ICU, Surgery — same doctor, different rules)

Just as a hospital's efficiency depends on its **organizational design**, a microcontroller's performance depends on its **architecture** — the arrangement of registers, modes, buses, and pipeline stages that enable parallel and efficient data flow.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Intuition:** *Architecture is the "blueprint" that tells you where every register, bus, and pipeline stage lives inside the chip, and how they cooperate to execute one instruction in the fewest possible clock cycles.*

---

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics (KTU Highlights)

The following **bold** metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU university exams:

- **CISC vs RISC** – ARM is a **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) architecture.
- **Word size** – **32 bits** (data path) in ARM7TDMI.
- **Pipeline depth** – **3 stages** (Fetch → Decode → Execute) for ARM7TDMI.
- **Operating Modes** – **7 modes** in standard ARM7.
- **Register Count** – **37 registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status).
- **Bus Protocol** – **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** with AHB, ASB, APB layers.
- **Thumb Instruction** – **16-bit compressed** alternative to **32-bit ARM** instructions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Architecture* (Module 4) with *Programming/Instruction Set* (Module 5). The architecture module focuses on **structural design** — not on writing assembly code.
