<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The ESP32 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESP32 (Espressif Systems)** is a low-power, low-cost **32-bit dual-core** System-on-Chip (SoC) microcontroller built around the **Tensilica Xtensa LX6 / LX7** processor core. It integrates **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)**, **Bluetooth (Classic + BLE)**, a rich set of peripherals (GPIO, ADC, DAC, SPI, I2C, UART, I2S, PWM, Touch, Hall sensor, Ethernet MAC) and is widely programmed using the **Arduino IDE**, **ESP-IDF**, **PlatformIO** or **MicroPython**.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The ESP32 as a "Smart Swiss Army Knife"

Imagine you are going on a camping trip. A regular microcontroller (e.g., an 8051) is like a single-purpose pocket knife — it does one or two jobs, and that is it. The **ESP32**, on the other hand, is the **Swiss Army Knife of the microcontroller world**:

- It can *connect to the internet* (Wi-Fi) — like a built-in walkie-talkie
- It can *talk to your phone wirelessly* (Bluetooth) — like a built-in intercom
- It can *sense the world* (ADC, touch sensors, temperature) — like a built-in pair of eyes
- It can *drive outputs* (PWM, DAC, GPIO) — like a built-in pair of hands
- It has *two brains* (dual-core 240 MHz) — like a built-in assistant who can do two tasks at once

> [!NOTE]
> For the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, only the *basic-level* programming using the **Arduino IDE** is required. Advanced ESP-IDF, FreeRTOS internals, and BLE stack programming are **out of scope**.

---

## 1.2 The STM32 Family — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STM32** is a family of **32-bit ARM Cortex-M based** microcontrollers manufactured by **STMicroelectronics**. They are designed for high-performance, real-time, low-power embedded applications ranging from motor control and IoT to industrial automation, medical devices, and automotive ECUs. STM32 devices are classified by their Cortex-M core (M0/M0+, M3, M4, M7) and by their series (F0, F1, F2, F3, F4, F7, H7, L0, L1, L4, etc.).

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The STM32 as a "Professional Industrial Workstation"

If the ESP32 is the *Swiss Army Knife* (versatile, hobbyist-friendly, Wi-Fi built-in), then the **STM32** is the *professional industrial workstation*:

- It is **deterministic** (you can guarantee *exactly* when a task will execute) — like a factory robot that hits its mark on the millisecond
- It has a **rich peripheral set** with hardware DMA, advanced timers, and fast ADCs — like a workshop filled with specialized tools
- It uses the **industry-standard ARM Cortex-M architecture**, which means *skills transfer to almost every modern embedded job* — like learning English: it opens doors worldwide
- It does **not** have Wi-Fi by default (you add a module like the ESP32 externally), but it compensates with raw processing power, precision, and reliability — like a heavy-duty lathe that does not need fancy apps, just pure engineering muscle

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** ESP32 = *connectivity + ease of use* (rapid prototyping). STM32 = *performance + real-time precision* (industrial-grade).

---

## 1.3 Quick Summary Table — At a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **board-exam oriented**. Memorise the bolded values.

| Parameter | **ESP32** | **STM32 (e.g., STM32F407VG)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Core | **Xtensa LX6 / LX7 (dual-core)** | **ARM Cortex-M4 (single-core here)** |
| Architecture | **32-bit RISC** | **32-bit RISC (ARM)** |
| Clock Speed | **240 MHz** | **168 MHz** |
| Flash | **4 MB (typical module)** | **1 MB** |
| SRAM | **520 KB** | **192 KB** |
| Wi-Fi / BT | **Built-in 802.11 b/g/n + BT + BLE** | **External module required** |
| IDE | **Arduino IDE (basic), ESP-IDF** | **STM32CubeIDE, Keil, IAR** |
| Typical Use | **IoT, Wi-Fi nodes, hobby** | **Industrial, automotive, motor ctrl** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ESP32 — Internal Architecture Breakdown

The ESP32 SoC is built around a **dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6** processor. Let us dissect its internal architecture in layered form:

### 2.1.1 The Two Cores — PRO_CPU and APP_CPU

- **PRO_CPU (Protocol CPU):** Handles Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and radio stack.
- **APP_CPU (Application CPU):** Runs user application code (your `loop()` and `setup()` functions).
- The two cores are **symmetric** (both are LX6) and share the same memory, so the developer can allocate tasks freely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FreeRTOS** runs on both cores out-of-the-box. The Arduino core for ESP32 uses **Core 0** for the radio/system and **Core 1** for `loop()` by default.

### 2.1.2 ESP32 Memory Map

The ESP32 has a **Harvard-style** separate instruction and data bus, with the following memory regions:

- **Internal SRAM (520 KB total):** Divided into three blocks — DRAM0, DRAM1, IRAM. Data RAM.
- **ROM (448 KB):** Contains the bootloader and some core functions.
- **External Flash (typically 4 MB):** Stores user sketch, file system (SPIFFS/LittleFS), and OTA updates.
- **RTC Slow Memory (8 KB):** Retained during deep sleep; used for the ULP (Ultra-Low-Power) coprocessor.
- **RTC Fast Memory (8 KB):** Used by the ULP coprocessor for active low-power operations.

### 2.1.3 ESP32 Peripherals — A Bulleted Master-List

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise this list — board questions frequently ask: *"List the peripherals of ESP32"* (3 marks, direct).

- **34 × programmable GPIOs** (multiplexed, not all usable)
- **18 × 12-bit SAR ADC** channels
- **2 × 8-bit DAC** channels
- **10 × capacitive touch** sensor channels
- **4 × SPI** (one used for flash, three available)
- **2 × I2C**
- **3 × UART** (one used for serial monitor)
- **2 × I2S**
- **PWM:** Up to 16 channels via LEDC peripheral
- **Hall sensor**, **temperature sensor**
- **Ethernet MAC**, **SDIO / SDMMC host**, **CAN 2.0**, **IR (TX/RX)**
- **RMT (Remote Control)** peripheral
- **ULP coprocessor** for deep-sleep sensing

---

## 2.2 STM32 — Internal Architecture Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The ARM Cortex-M Core Family

The STM32 family is built on three primary ARM Cortex-M cores (KTU-focused):

- **Cortex-M0 / M0+:** Mainstream, low-power (STM32F0, STM32L0)
- **Cortex-M3:** Mainstream performance (STM32F1, STM32F2)
- **Cortex-M4:** Adds **DSP instructions** and a **single-precision FPU** (STM32F3, STM32F4) — **most common for KTU/motor control applications**
- **Cortex-M7:** High performance with cache (STM32F7, STM32H7)

### 2.2.2 The Bus Matrix — STM32's "Internal Highway"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The STM32 uses an **Advanced High-performance Bus (AHB) matrix** (often called the **"multi-AHB bus matrix"**) that allows **multiple masters** (CPU, DMA1, DMA2, Ethernet) to access **multiple slaves** (Flash, SRAM, peripherals) **simultaneously and in parallel**, through separate bus paths. This is the key reason for the STM32's real-time performance.

- **AHB bus matrix** at the heart, operating at the system clock speed (e.g., 168 MHz for F407).
- **APB1 (low-speed peripheral bus):** typically runs at half the system clock (e.g., 42 MHz) — connects UART, I2C, SPI2/3, timers, ADC, etc.
- **APB2 (high-speed peripheral bus):** runs at full system clock — connects SPI1, USART1, ADC1, GPIO, etc.

### 2.2.3 STM32 Memory Organisation

- **Flash (program memory):** Stores code, can be programmed in-circuit via SWD/JTAG or bootloader (USART/SPI/USB).
- **SRAM (data memory):** 192 KB on F407VG — used for stack, heap, global variables.
- **CCM (Core-Coupled Memory):** 64 KB on F407, only CPU-accessible (zero wait states, very fast — used for time-critical stacks).
- **Backup registers + 4 KB SRAM** in the RTC domain, battery-backed.

### 2.2.4 STM32 Peripherals — A Bulleted Master-List

> [!NOTE]
> Another high-yield 3-marker for KTU boards.

- Up to **17 timers** (basic, general-purpose, advanced-control with complementary PWM + dead-time)
- **3 × 12-bit ADC** (up to 2.4 MSPS on F4)
- **2 × 12-bit DAC**
- **Up to 6 × SPI**, **3 × I2C**, **6 × USART/UART**, **2 × USB OTG**, **2 × CAN**, **1 × SDIO**, **Ethernet MAC**
- **GPIO:** up to 140 I/O lines (F407), 5 V-tolerant on many pins
- **CRC**, **RNG** (random number generator, on some), **FSMC** (Flexible Static Memory Controller)
- **RTC**, **Watchdog** (IWDG + WWDG), **SysTick timer** in the NVIC

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas are tested directly. Memorise the variables and the typical numerical values. Note the use of `\mid` instead of `\|` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Topic | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | ADC Step Size (n-bit ADC) | $V_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}$ | n = 12, $V_{\text{ref}}$ = 3.3 V $\Rightarrow$ **0.806 mV/step** | Smallest voltage change detectable |
| 2 | ADC Quantisation Error | $E_{q} = \pm \dfrac{V_{\text{step}}}{2}$ | **0.403 mV** for 12-bit, 3.3 V | Maximum error of any conversion |
| 3 | Timer Tick Frequency | $f_{\text{tick}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{(PSC + 1)}$ | $f_{\text{clk}}$ = 80 MHz, PSC = 79 $\Rightarrow$ **1 MHz** | Frequency fed into the counter |
| 4 | Timer Overflow Period | $T_{\text{ovf}} = \dfrac{(ARR + 1)}{f_{\text{tick}}}$ | ARR = 999 $\Rightarrow$ **1 ms** | Time between two update events |
| 5 | PWM Duty Cycle (Edge-Aligned) | $D \ (\%) = \dfrac{CCR}{(ARR + 1)} \times 100$ | CCR = 500, ARR = 999 $\Rightarrow$ **50 %** | ON-time ratio of the PWM waveform |
| 6 | PWM Frequency | $f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{(PSC+1)(ARR+1)}$ | with above: **1 kHz** | PWM repetition rate |
| 7 | UART Baud Rate (oversampling ×16) | $BR = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$ | $f_{\text{clk}}$ = 16 MHz, UBRR = 15 $\Rightarrow$ **62 500 bps** | Serial link bit rate |
| 8 | Instruction Execution Time | $T_{\text{exec}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{cycles}}}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | 1 cycle, 240 MHz $\Rightarrow$ **4.167 ns** | Time for one machine instruction |
| 9 | Dynamic Power Dissipation | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | CMOS, $\alpha$ = switching activity | Power consumed while switching |
| 10 | Memory Address Offset | $A_{\text{reg}} = A_{\text{base}} + \text{offset}$ | offset = 0x00, 0x04, 0x08... | 32-bit register stride in MCU memory map |

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 (ADC Step):** Used in **sensor interfacing** — knowing the step size helps you choose the right MCU resolution for temperature, pressure, current sensing in industrial automation.
- **Formulas 3, 4 (Timer):** Used in **PWM motor control**, **sampling rate generation**, and **precise delay generation** in real-time systems.
- **Formula 5, 6 (PWM):** Used in **DC motor speed control**, **LED dimming**, **servo actuation** — the heart of robotics and power-electronics lab experiments.
- **Formula 7 (Baud Rate):** Used in **UART/Serial communication** between an MCU and a PC, GSM module, GPS, or Bluetooth HC-05.
- **Formula 9 (Power):** Used in **battery-powered IoT** and **wearable design** — drives selection of sleep modes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **complete, exhaustive derivations**. Every algebraic step is shown. There are no skipped transitions. Read each step slowly.

## 3.1 Derivation — ADC Step Size and Quantisation Error

### 3.1.1 Given

- An **n-bit** Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC
- Reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$ = **3.3 V** (typical for ESP32 and STM32F4)
- n = 12 (12-bit ADC)

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Number of Discrete Output Codes

A binary n-bit ADC can produce $2^{n}$ distinct output codes, ranging from $000\ldots0$ (decimal 0) to $111\ldots1$ (decimal $2^{n} - 1$).

$$
N_{\text{codes}} = 2^{n}
$$

This is the total number of "steps" the ADC can quantise. For n = 12:

$$
N_{\text{codes}} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

So the ADC output can be any integer from **0 to 4095**.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Define the Step Size (LSB)

The **step size** (also called the **LSB voltage**, $V_{\text{LSB}}$) is the voltage change that produces a change of **one count** at the ADC output. By definition:

$$
V_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{N_{\text{codes}}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}
$$

Note that **some textbooks** use $V_{\text{ref}}/(2^{n} - 1)$ (using the full range); the difference is one LSB and is often negligible. KTU uses the $2^{n}$ convention.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Substitute the Numbers

$$
V_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{3.3 \ \text{V}}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{3.3}{4096}
$$

Carrying out the long division:

$$
3.300 \ \text{V} \div 4096 = 0.00080566 \ \text{V}
$$

Converting to millivolts:

$$
V_{\text{step}} = 0.80566 \ \text{mV} \approx 0.806 \ \text{mV/step}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Quantisation Error

Any analog input $V_{\text{in}}$ is rounded to the nearest code. The maximum rounding error is **half a step**:

$$
E_{q} = \pm \dfrac{V_{\text{step}}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{0.806 \ \text{mV}}{2}
$$

$$
E_{q} = \pm 0.403 \ \text{mV}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Converting ADC Reading to Real Voltage

If the ADC produces a digital code $D$, the original input voltage is reconstructed as:

$$
V_{\text{in}} = D \cdot V_{\text{step}} = D \cdot \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}
$$

For example, if $D = 2048$ (mid-scale):

$$
V_{\text{in}} = 2048 \times 0.80566 \ \text{mV} = 1650 \ \text{mV} = 1.65 \ \text{V}
$$

which is exactly half of $V_{\text{ref}}$ — as expected. ✔

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state the **assumed reference voltage** (typically 3.3 V for both ESP32 and STM32F4) and the **ADC bit-width** before plugging in numbers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Timer Overflow Period and PWM Frequency

### 3.2.1 Given

- Timer clock $f_{\text{clk}}$ = **80 MHz** (default APB clock on ESP32 LEDC)
- Prescaler **PSC** = 79 (the value **written into** the register, the divider is PSC + 1)
- Auto-Reload Register **ARR** = 999
- Compare Register **CCR** = 250 (for the duty cycle example)

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Derive the Timer Tick Frequency

A prescaler of PSC means the counter is incremented once every **(PSC + 1)** clock pulses. So:

$$
f_{\text{tick}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{(PSC + 1)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
f_{\text{tick}} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{(79 + 1)} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{6}}{80} = 1 \times 10^{6} \ \text{Hz}
$$

So the counter increments at **1 MHz** — i.e., it ticks once every **microsecond**.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Derive the Overflow Period

The counter counts from 0 up to ARR, giving **(ARR + 1)** ticks per full period. The time for one full period is:

$$
T_{\text{ovf}} = \dfrac{(ARR + 1)}{f_{\text{tick}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{ovf}} = \dfrac{(999 + 1)}{1 \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{1000}{10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s}
$$

Thus $T_{\text{ovf}} = 1 \ \text{ms}$. Therefore:

$$
f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{ovf}}} = 1 \ \text{kHz}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Derive the PWM Duty Cycle

In edge-aligned PWM, the output is **HIGH** while the counter is below CCR, and **LOW** while the counter is between CCR and ARR. The fraction of time the output is HIGH is:

$$
D = \dfrac{CCR}{(ARR + 1)} \times 100 \%
$$

For CCR = 250:

$$
D = \dfrac{250}{1000} \times 100 \% = 25 \%
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Combine All into One Master PWM Equation

Substituting the expression for $f_{\text{tick}}$ into the PWM frequency formula:

$$
f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{(PSC + 1)(ARR + 1)}
$$

This is the **master formula** you will see in the KTU board question bank. Memorise it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The above is the standard procedure for **servo motor control** (50 Hz PWM, 1–2 ms pulse width) and for **DC motor speed control** via H-bridge drivers like the L298N.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — UART Baud Rate Setting

### 3.3.1 Given

- $f_{\text{clk}}$ = **16 MHz** (APB1 clock on STM32F1, common example)
- Desired baud rate = **9600 bps** (standard serial monitor speed)
- Oversampling factor = **16** (standard UART mode, normal UART, not ×8)

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Master Baud Rate Equation

For standard UART with ×16 oversampling:

$$
BR = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Solve for UBRR

Rearranging:

$$
UBRR + 1 = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot BR}
$$

$$
UBRR = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{16 \cdot BR} - 1
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Substitute

$$
UBRR = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} - 1
$$

$$
UBRR = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{153600} - 1
$$

$$
UBRR = 104.1667 - 1 = 103.1667
$$

Since UBRR must be an integer, we round to **UBRR = 103** (or 104, depending on the standard). The actual achieved baud rate becomes:

$$
BR_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 104} = 9615.38 \ \text{bps}
$$

The **error** is:

$$
\% \ \text{error} = \dfrac{\vert BR_{\text{actual}} - BR_{\text{target}} \vert}{BR_{\text{target}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\vert 9615.38 - 9600 \vert}{9600} \times 100 = 0.16 \%
$$

This is well within the typical ±2 % UART tolerance. ✔

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ESP32 vs STM32 (Board Comparison View)

> [!NOTE]
> A "Compare-and-contrast" question is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. The following matrix is the **gold-standard answer skeleton**.

| Feature | **ESP32 (Espressif)** | **STM32 (STMicroelectronics)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Core** | Tensilica Xtensa LX6 (dual) | ARM Cortex-M3 / M4 (single/twin) |
| **Bit-Width** | 32-bit RISC | 32-bit RISC |
| **Typical Clock** | 240 MHz | 72 MHz (F1) – 180 MHz (H7) |
| **Wireless** | **Built-in Wi-Fi + BT + BLE** | None (external module required) |
| **On-chip SRAM** | 520 KB | 64 – 256 KB (varies by series) |
| **Flash** | External SPI flash (4 MB) | Internal flash (0.5 – 2 MB) |
| **Supply Voltage** | 3.3 V (logic) | 3.3 V (logic), some 5 V-tolerant |
| **Development IDE** | Arduino IDE (basic), ESP-IDF | STM32CubeIDE, Keil MDK, IAR |
| **Programming Language** | C/C++ (Arduino), MicroPython | C (HAL/LL), C++ |
| **Real-Time OS** | FreeRTOS (default in ESP-IDF) | FreeRTOS, Azure RTOS, Keil RTX |
| **Cost** | Very low (~200 INR module) | Low to moderate (varies) |
| **Typical Application** | Wi-Fi IoT nodes, smart home | Industrial control, motor drives, automotive |
| **Power (Deep Sleep)** | ~ 10 µA (ULP support) | ~ 1 µA (standby, L-series) |
| **Touch Sensing** | 10 capacitive channels (built-in) | Available on some F3/L4 series |

## 4.6 Reference Arduino Sketch — ESP32 GPIO Blink (Basic Level)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the KTU 2024 syllabus **explicitly** says *"Programming of ESP32 with Arduino IDE"*, the following minimal sketch is the canonical *basic-level* example. The structure (`setup` + `loop`) is what the examiner tests.

```cpp
// ESP32 Basic Blink Sketch (KTU reference - basic level only)
#define LED_PIN 2        // On-board blue LED on most DOIT DevKit V1

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);     // Configure GPIO 2 as output
  Serial.begin(115200);         // Initialise UART for Serial Monitor
  Serial.println("ESP32 Initialised");
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);  // LED ON
  delay(1000);                  // Wait 1000 ms
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);   // LED OFF
  delay(1000);                  // Wait 1000 ms
}
```

**Key API functions** a student must know:

- `pinMode(pin, mode)` — set direction: `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP`
- `digitalWrite(pin, value)` — write `HIGH` (3.3 V) or `LOW` (0 V)
- `digitalRead(pin)` — read `HIGH` or `LOW`
- `analogRead(pin)` — read 12-bit value (0 to 4095)
- `analogWrite(pin, value)` — LEDC PWM (0 to 255 default, 8-bit)
- `delay(ms)` — blocking delay in milliseconds

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model)]

> List any **six features** of the ESP32 microcontroller.

**Model Answer (target: 6 features, ~3 sentences each, 3 marks):**

The ESP32 is a 32-bit dual-core microcontroller built on the Tensilica Xtensa LX6 architecture, running up to **240 MHz**. Key features are:

1. **Built-in Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** and **Bluetooth (Classic + BLE)** for wireless connectivity.
2. **Dual-core Xtensa LX6** with 520 KB SRAM and 4 MB external SPI flash.
3. **34 programmable GPIOs**, **18 × 12-bit ADC**, **2 × 8-bit DAC**, **10 capacitive touch channels**.
4. Multiple communication peripherals: **3 × UART, 4 × SPI, 2 × I2C, 2 × I2S**, CAN 2.0, Ethernet MAC.
5. **16-channel LEDC PWM**, **RMT (remote control)**, **Hall sensor**, **temperature sensor**.
6. **Ultra-Low-Power (ULP) coprocessor** for deep-sleep current as low as **~10 µA**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]

> Differentiate between the **ESP32** and **STM32** microcontrollers (any **four** points).

**Model Answer (4 points, table form for clarity):**

| S.No. | Parameter | ESP32 | STM32 |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | CPU Core | Tensilica Xtensa LX6 (dual-core) | ARM Cortex-M0 / M3 / M4 (single/twin-core) |
| 2 | Wireless | Built-in Wi-Fi + BT + BLE | No built-in wireless; external module needed |
| 3 | Clock | 240 MHz | 72 MHz (F1) to 180 MHz (H7) |
| 4 | Typical IDE | Arduino IDE, ESP-IDF | STM32CubeIDE, Keil MDK, IAR |
| 5 | Real-Time Strength | Medium (dual-core helps) | Very high (deterministic NVIC, DMA) |
| 6 | Application | IoT, smart home, hobby | Industrial, motor control, automotive |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model)]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of the ESP32** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **PRO_CPU** and **APP_CPU**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the STM32 (Cortex-M4 based)**. What is the significance of the **AHB bus matrix**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The ESP32 is a low-power, dual-core 32-bit SoC manufactured by Espressif Systems. It integrates the Tensilica Xtensa LX6 microprocessor with Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and a rich peripheral set, all on a single die.

**Architecture description (4 marks):**

- **Two CPU cores (PRO_CPU and APP_CPU):**
  - **PRO_CPU (Protocol CPU)** — runs the Wi-Fi and Bluetooth stack. Code on this core handles real-time radio events.
  - **APP_CPU (Application CPU)** — runs the user application (Arduino `setup()` and `loop()` by default).
  - Both cores are symmetric LX6 cores running at up to **240 MHz** and can share memory.

- **Memory:**
  - **ROM (448 KB)** holds the first-stage bootloader and core functions.
  - **SRAM (520 KB)** is split into three blocks (DRAM0, DRAM1, IRAM0) and is the data memory.
  - **External SPI Flash (4 MB typical)** stores the user sketch, file system, and OTA images.
  - **RTC Slow Memory (8 KB)** and **RTC Fast Memory (8 KB)** — used by the ULP coprocessor; RTC Slow memory survives deep sleep.

- **Peripherals:** 34 GPIOs, 18 × 12-bit ADC, 2 × 8-bit DAC, 10 touch channels, 4 SPI, 2 I2C, 3 UART, 2 I2S, 16-channel LEDC PWM, RMT, Hall sensor, temperature sensor, CAN, Ethernet MAC.

- **Radio block:** Integrated Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n (2.4 GHz) and dual-mode Bluetooth (Classic + BLE).

- **Power management:** Five programmable power modes (Active, Modem-sleep, Light-sleep, Deep-sleep, Hibernation) plus the ULP coprocessor that can run while the main cores are asleep.

**Role of dual cores (2 marks):** The dual-core design lets the ESP32 handle wireless protocol processing on PRO_CPU in parallel with user-defined tasks on APP_CPU. This is essential for real-time Wi-Fi/BLE responsiveness while the user application is running heavy logic. In ESP-IDF, tasks can be pinned to either core using `xTaskCreatePinnedToCore()`.

> [!NOTE]
> Include a labelled block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** above) — full **2 marks** are awarded for a clear diagram with all major blocks.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The STM32 is a 32-bit microcontroller family by STMicroelectronics based on the ARM Cortex-M core. Reference: **STM32F407VG (Cortex-M4 with FPU)**, 168 MHz, 1 MB Flash, 192 KB SRAM.

**Architecture description (4 marks):**

- **Cortex-M4 core** with hardware **single-precision FPU** and **DSP instructions**, plus a **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** for low-latency interrupt handling.
- **AHB bus matrix** at the heart of the chip, operating at up to 168 MHz.
- **Memories:** Internal Flash (1 MB), SRAM (112 KB main + 64 KB CCM + 4 KB backup).
- **APB1** (low-speed peripheral bus, 42 MHz): timers, USART2/3, SPI2/3, I2C1/2/3, CAN1/2, DAC, IWDG/WWDG.
- **APB2** (high-speed peripheral bus, 84 MHz): GPIO, USART1, SPI1, ADC1/2/3, all timers' alternate functions, USB OTG.
- **Two DMA controllers (DMA1, DMA2)** for peripheral-to-memory transfers without CPU intervention.

**Significance of the AHB bus matrix (2 marks):**

The **AHB bus matrix** is a multi-layer crossbar switch that allows **simultaneous parallel access** between **multiple masters** (CPU, DMA1, DMA2, Ethernet DMA) and **multiple slaves** (Flash, SRAM, peripherals). Its significance is:

- It **eliminates bus contention** — while the CPU fetches instructions from Flash, DMA can move ADC data into SRAM in parallel.
- It **guarantees deterministic, real-time performance** — critical for motor control and safety systems.
- It enables **zero-wait-state** access to CCM (Core-Coupled Memory) which is reserved for the CPU only.

> [!NOTE]
> Include a labelled block diagram (refer to **Section 4.2** above).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> [KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]
>
> **(a)** Describe the **steps to program the ESP32 using the Arduino IDE**. Write a basic sketch to **blink the on-board LED** connected to **GPIO 2** and **print "Hello, ESP32"** to the **Serial Monitor** at **115200 baud**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **STM32 microcontroller family**, its **Cortex-M core variants**, and the **STM32CubeIDE** development workflow. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step-by-step procedure to program the ESP32 with Arduino IDE (4 marks):**

1. Download and install **Arduino IDE 2.x** from the official Arduino website.
2. Open **File → Preferences**. In the "Additional Board URLs" field, paste:
   `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/espressif/arduino-esp32/gh-pages/package_esp32_index.json`
3. Open **Tools → Board → Boards Manager**, search for **"esp32"**, and install the **esp32 by Espressif Systems** package.
4. Connect the ESP32 (e.g., **DOIT ESP32 DevKit V1**) to the PC via USB. Install the **CP210x / CH340** USB-to-UART driver if not already present.
5. Select the correct board: **Tools → Board → DOIT ESP32 DEVKIT V1**.
6. Select the correct **COM port**: **Tools → Port → COMx** (Windows) or **/dev/ttyUSBx** (Linux).
7. Write the sketch in the editor. Click **✓ Verify** to compile, then **→ Upload** to flash.

**Basic Blink + Serial Sketch (3 marks):**

```cpp
// ESP32 Basic Sketch - Blink + Serial Hello (KTU reference)
#define LED_PIN 2

void setup() {
  pinMode(LED_PIN, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(115200);
  delay(500);
  Serial.println("Hello, ESP32");
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH);
  delay(1000);
  digitalWrite(LED_PIN, LOW);
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Explanation of the sketch:**

- `setup()` runs **once** at power-on or reset. `pinMode(2, OUTPUT)` configures GPIO 2 as an output. `Serial.begin(115200)` starts UART at 115200 baud.
- `loop()` runs **repeatedly forever**. `digitalWrite(LED_PIN, HIGH)` drives the LED on (logic HIGH = 3.3 V). `delay(1000)` blocks for 1000 ms. The LED then turns off for 1 s. This gives a 0.5 Hz blink with 50% duty cycle.
- Open **Tools → Serial Monitor** (or press `Ctrl+Shift+M`), set the baud to **115200**, and observe the "Hello, ESP32" message every cycle.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**STM32 Family Overview (3 marks):**

The STM32 family by **STMicroelectronics** is grouped by the **Cortex-M core** and the **application series**:

- **Cortex-M0 / M0+ (low power, simple):** STM32F0, STM32L0, STM32G0 — 32–64 MHz, 16–32 KB RAM, suitable for cost-sensitive and battery-powered nodes.
- **Cortex-M3 (mainstream performance):** STM32F1, STM32F2, STM32L1 — 72 MHz (F1) to 120 MHz (F2). STM32F1 is the most popular entry-level series in academia.
- **Cortex-M4 (performance + DSP + FPU):** STM32F3, STM32F4, STM32L4 — 72 MHz (F3) to 180 MHz (F4). The F4 adds an FPU and DSP instructions, ideal for **motor control and DSP**.
- **Cortex-M7 (high performance with cache):** STM32F7, STM32H7 — up to 480 MHz, with L1 cache and double-precision FPU.
- **Cortex-M33 (with TrustZone security):** STM32L5, STM32U5 — energy-efficient with hardware security extensions.

**STM32CubeIDE workflow (4 marks):**

1. **Download and install** STM32CubeIDE from the ST website (free). It bundles the **Eclipse IDE** with the **GCC ARM toolchain** and the **STM32CubeMX** graphical configurator.
2. **Create a new project:** `File → New → STM32 Project`. In the **Target Selection** window, choose the MCU (e.g., `STM32F407VG`) or an evaluation board (e.g., `Nucleo-F411RE`).
3. **Configure peripherals graphically** in the **STM32CubeMX perspective**: set pin alternate functions, choose clock tree, enable USART/SPI/I2C, configure timers, set DMA streams, etc.
4. **Configure clock tree** in the **Clock Configuration** tab: select the HSE crystal, set PLL multipliers, and set the desired **SYSCLK** (e.g., 168 MHz for F407).
5. **Generate code:** click **Generate Code**; IDE creates the project skeleton with `main.c`, `main.h`, `stm32f4xx_hal_msp.c`, etc.
6. **Edit code** in the `Src/main.c` file: implement logic in the `while(1)` main loop, write ISR callbacks.
7. **Build the project:** click the **hammer icon** (`Ctrl+B`). Fix any errors.
8. **Debug:** connect an **ST-Link V2** (or onboard ST-Link on Nucleo boards) via the **SWD** header. Click the **debug icon** to flash and start the debugger. Set breakpoints, inspect registers, watch memory.

> [!NOTE]
> Mention HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) vs LL (Low-Layer) drivers: HAL is portable and easy to use; LL is faster and lightweight — a frequent 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SRAM and Flash:** Students often swap the role of SRAM (volatile, data) and Flash (non-volatile, program). On ESP32, the program **lives in external SPI Flash**, not in SRAM. SRAM is for variables and stack. **(−2 marks if reversed)**
> 2. **Forgetting the ADC step formula uses $2^n$, not $2^n - 1$:** KTU standard answer key uses the $2^n$ convention. Using the wrong one gives a 0.806 mV vs 0.806 + 0.0002 mV answer — examiners will dock half a mark.
> 3. **Wrong UART oversampling factor:** Some UARTs (like on STM32) allow **×8 oversampling** for high-speed links. If the question says ×8, the divisor is **8**, not 16. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Wrong baud rate in the sketch:** A sketch using `Serial.begin(115200)` must be matched with a **Serial Monitor set to 115200**. Mismatch produces **gibberish characters** — examiners check this if the screenshot is asked.
> 5. **Confusing ESP32's dual core with STM32's multi-core:** Only specific STM32 series (e.g., **STM32H7 dual-core, STM32MP1 heterogeneous**) are multi-core. The mainstream **STM32F4 is single-core**. Saying "STM32 is dual-core like ESP32" is wrong.
> 6. **Confusing ADC resolution:** ESP32 ADC is **12-bit** (0–4095). Some Arduino boards use 10-bit (0–1023). Students often write 1023 for ESP32 — wrong, lose 1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong role of the ULP coprocessor:** It runs *while the main cores are in deep sleep*, and is used for ultra-low-power sensing (e.g., waking on touch). Not for general user code.
> 8. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** Even a hand-drawn, labelled block diagram is worth **2 marks** out of 7. Always draw it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist. Tick these off before you enter the exam hall.**

- [ ] **ESP32** = Espressif's **32-bit dual-core Xtensa LX6**, **240 MHz**, integrated **Wi-Fi + BT + BLE**.
- [ ] **STM32** = ST's **32-bit ARM Cortex-M** family, deterministic real-time, no built-in wireless.
- [ ] ESP32 has **520 KB SRAM** + external **4 MB SPI Flash**; STM32 has internal **Flash** + **SRAM**.
- [ ] ESP32 has **34 GPIOs**, **18 12-bit ADC channels**, **2 8-bit DACs**, **10 touch inputs**.
- [ ] STM32 F407 has **three 12-bit ADCs at 2.4 MSPS**, **two 12-bit DACs**, **17 timers**, **140 GPIO**.
- [ ] The **AHB bus matrix** in STM32 enables **parallel master access** — the reason for its real-time strength.
- [ ] **ADC step** = $V_{\text{ref}} / 2^n$. For 3.3 V, 12-bit → **0.806 mV/step**.
- [ ] **PWM frequency** = $f_{\text{clk}} / [(PSC+1)(ARR+1)]$; **Duty** = $CCR/(ARR+1) \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **UART baud rate** = $f_{\text{clk}} / [16(UBRR+1)]$ for ×16 oversampling mode.
- [ ] **Arduino sketch skeleton:** `setup()` runs once, `loop()` runs forever — the **two-function contract**.
- [ ] **Arduino core functions to know:** `pinMode`, `digitalWrite`, `digitalRead`, `analogRead`, `analogWrite` (LEDC), `Serial.begin`, `Serial.print`, `Serial.println`, `delay`.
- [ ] **STM32CubeIDE** workflow: **install → create project → CubeMX configure → generate code → build → ST-Link debug → run**.
- [ ] **HAL** = high-level, portable, easy; **LL** = low-level, fast, register-close.
- [ ] **PRO_CPU** = radio; **APP_CPU** = user code (default Arduino `loop()`).
- [ ] **ULP coprocessor** runs in deep-sleep for ultra-low-power sensing.
- [ ] ESP32 has **5 power modes**: Active, Modem-sleep, Light-sleep, Deep-sleep, Hibernation.
- [ ] The **internal SRAM** in STM32 is split into **main SRAM + CCM + backup SRAM**; CCM is CPU-only, zero wait-state.
- [ ] **NVIC** = Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller — handles interrupts in STM32 with deterministic latency.
- [ ] **ESP32** is preferred for **IoT / wireless**; **STM32** is preferred for **real-time / industrial**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's **PEEBT522 – Advanced Microprocessors & Microcontrollers (2024 Scheme)**, **Module 4 – Architecture** addresses the internal organization of advanced **32-bit RISC microcontrollers**, with a primary focus on the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture** and its derivatives (ARM7TDMI / Cortex-M family). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> *Architecture of advanced microcontrollers – study of programmer's model, register banks, operating modes, exception handling, instruction pipeline, AMBA bus architecture, and memory organization of ARM-based systems.*

**Architecture** in this context refers to the **programmer-visible model** plus the underlying **hardware organization** that dictates how a microcontroller fetches, decodes, and executes instructions while interacting with memory, peripherals, and the external world.

### 1.1.1 Three Pillars of Architecture
| Pillar | Description | Example in ARM7TDMI |
|---|---|---|
| **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** | Defines instructions, addressing modes, data types | Thumb (16-bit) + ARM (32-bit) |
| **Micro-architecture** | Pipeline depth, caches, bus width | 3-stage pipeline |
| **System Architecture** | Memory map, bus protocol, peripherals | AMBA AHB / ASB / APB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield):**  
> The *Architecture* module is **NOT** about the instruction set. It is about the **structural & functional blueprint** of the chip — registers, modes, pipeline stages, exceptions, and bus structure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern hospital**:

- **The CPU Core** = the **Doctor** (decision-maker, processes cases/operations)
- **The Register Bank** = the **Doctor's clipboard** (holds immediate patient info — current case, status flags, scratch notes)
- **The Pipeline** = the **Triage assembly line** (one nurse checks temperature, another checks BP, another prescribes — tasks overlap)
- **The Memory Bus (AMBA AHB)** = the **elevator system** (high-speed transport for patients/data between floors)
- **The Exception Handler** = the **Emergency Room** (high-priority interruptions — accidents/heart attacks jump the queue)
- **Operating Modes** = the **different wards** (General Ward, ICU, Surgery — same doctor, different rules)

Just as a hospital's efficiency depends on its **organizational design**, a microcontroller's performance depends on its **architecture** — the arrangement of registers, modes, buses, and pipeline stages that enable parallel and efficient data flow.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Intuition:** *Architecture is the "blueprint" that tells you where every register, bus, and pipeline stage lives inside the chip, and how they cooperate to execute one instruction in the fewest possible clock cycles.*

---

## 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics (KTU Highlights)

The following **bold** metrics are repeatedly tested in KTU university exams:

- **CISC vs RISC** – ARM is a **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) architecture.
- **Word size** – **32 bits** (data path) in ARM7TDMI.
- **Pipeline depth** – **3 stages** (Fetch → Decode → Execute) for ARM7TDMI.
- **Operating Modes** – **7 modes** in standard ARM7.
- **Register Count** – **37 registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status).
- **Bus Protocol** – **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** with AHB, ASB, APB layers.
- **Thumb Instruction** – **16-bit compressed** alternative to **32-bit ARM** instructions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Architecture* (Module 4) with *Programming/Instruction Set* (Module 5). The architecture module focuses on **structural design** — not on writing assembly code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM Programmer's Model (Register Organization)

The **ARM7TDMI** is built around a **load-store architecture**: data processing operates **only on registers**, while memory is accessed via dedicated `LDR`/`STR` instructions.

### 2.1.1 Register Bank (37 Registers Total)

The processor holds **37 registers of 32 bits each**, but at any given moment, only **16 general-purpose (R0–R12, R13, R14, R15) + 1 or 2 status registers** are visible to the programmer. The remaining registers are **banked** — physically distinct, switched in based on the **operating mode**.

| Register Group | Count | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **R0 – R12** | 13 | General-purpose, unbanked |
| **R13 (SP)** | 6 copies (banked) | Stack Pointer (one per privileged mode) |
| **R14 (LR)** | 6 copies (banked) | Link Register (holds return address) |
| **R15 (PC)** | 1 | Program Counter |
| **CPSR** | 1 | Current Program Status Register |
| **SPSR** | 5 copies (banked) | Saved Program Status Register (one per exception mode) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Exam Point:** R13, R14, and SPSR are **banked registers**. The visible set changes with the operating mode. R0–R7 (low) hold thumb-accessible values; R8–R12 (high) have additional restrictions in Thumb state.

### 2.1.2 The CPSR (Current Program Status Register) — 32 Bits

The **CPSR** is the most important status register and a guaranteed KTU question.

$$
\text{CPSR} = \underbrace{b_{31}b_{30}b_{29}b_{28}}_{\text{Condition flags}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{27}}_{\text{Q}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{26\ldots 24}}_{\text{Reserved (IT/J on ARMv6+)}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{23\ldots 8}}_{\text{Reserved}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{7\ldots 0}}_{\text{Control field: M[4:0], T, F, I}
$$

| Bits | Field | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **N (bit 31)** | Negative | Result MSB = 1 (negative or < in signed compare) |
| **Z (bit 30)** | Zero | Result = 0 (or equal in compare) |
| **C (bit 29)** | Carry | Unsigned overflow / extend / no-borrow |
| **V (bit 28)** | oVerflow | Signed overflow |
| **Q (bit 27)** | Sticky overflow | DSP-style saturation flag (ARMv5TE+) |
| **I (bit 7)** | IRQ disable | 1 = masks IRQ interrupts |
| **F (bit 6)** | FIQ disable | 1 = masks FIQ interrupts |
| **T (bit 5)** | Thumb state | 1 = executing 16-bit Thumb; 0 = 32-bit ARM |
| **M[4:0] (bits 4–0)** | Mode bits | Selects one of 7 operating modes |

---

## 2.2 Operating Modes (7 Modes in Standard ARM)

ARM defines **7 operating modes**, categorized into **Privileged** and **Non-Privileged** sets.

$$
\text{Modes} = \{\text{User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System}\}
$$

| Mode | Code (M[4:0]) | Privilege | Purpose | Banked Registers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User (usr)** | 10000 | Non-Privileged | Normal application code | None |
| **FIQ (fiq)** | 10001 | Privileged | Fast interrupt, supports data transfer | R8–R14, SPSR |
| **IRQ (irq)** | 10010 | Privileged | Normal interrupt handling | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Supervisor (svc)** | 10011 | Privileged | Software interrupt / OS calls / reset | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Abort (abt)** | 10111 | Privileged | Memory access failure | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **Undefined (und)** | 11011 | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap | R13, R14, SPSR |
| **System (sys)** | 11111 | Privileged | OS tasks with User register set | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Banked Registers?** When an exception (e.g., IRQ) occurs, the hardware **automatically** switches register banks. The IRQ handler gets its **own R13, R14, and SPSR** — so it never needs to push User-mode registers on the stack. This makes exception entry **near-instantaneous**, which is why ARM excels at real-time interrupt handling.

---

## 2.3 The 3-Stage Pipeline

The **ARM7TDMI** uses a **3-stage pipeline** to overlap instruction execution. At any cycle, three instructions are simultaneously in different stages.

$$
\text{Pipeline} = \text{Fetch} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Decode} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Execute}
$$

| Stage | Function | Hardware Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **F (Fetch)** | Loads instruction from memory via PC | Instruction memory / I-cache |
| **D (Decode)** | Decodes opcode, reads register operands, generates control signals | Decoder + register file |
| **E (Execute)** | ALU operation, register write-back, branch resolution | ALU + shifter + write-back bus |

### 2.3.1 Pipeline Throughput Derivation

For an **N-instruction** program:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipeline}} = N \times 3 \, \text{cycles} = 3N \, \text{cycles}
$$

$$
T_{\text{pipeline}} = (N - 1) \times 1 \, \text{cycle} + 3 \, \text{cycles} = (N + 2) \, \text{cycles}
$$

**Speedup (S)** when fully loaded (large N):

$$
S = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{T_{\text{non-pipeline}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{3N}{N + 2} = 3
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Ideal speedup = number of stages = 3×** for the ARM7TDMI. In practice, branches and memory stalls reduce this to about **2.0–2.4×**.

### 2.3.2 Branch Penalty

When a **branch** is taken at cycle $k$, the **Fetch stage** in cycle $(k+1)$ has already fetched the **wrong instruction**. This is called the **branch penalty**:

$$
\text{Branch Penalty}_{\text{ARM7}} = 1 \, \text{cycle} \;\;(\text{since depth} = 3)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** "How many cycles lost on a branch in ARM7?" → Answer is **1 cycle**, not 2. The Execute stage resolves the branch; the next Fetch is the only one wasted.

---

## 2.4 Exception Handling Architecture

**Exceptions** are events that divert the CPU from its normal flow. Each exception has a **fixed vector address** in low memory.

| Vector Address | Exception | Mode Entered |
|---|---|---|
| **0x0000 0000** | Reset | Supervisor |
| **0x0000 0004** | Undefined Instruction | Undefined |
| **0x0000 0008** | Software Interrupt (SWI) | Supervisor |
| **0x0000 000C** | Prefetch Abort | Abort |
| **0x0000 0010** | Data Abort | Abort |
| **0x0000 0014** | Reserved | — |
| **0x0000 0018** | IRQ | IRQ |
| **0x0000 001C** | FIQ | FIQ |

### 2.4.1 Exception Entry Sequence (Hardware Steps)

1. **Copy CPSR → SPSR_** of the new mode (preserve flags).
2. **Set CPSR bits** to enter the corresponding mode (M[4:0]).
3. **Disable IRQ** by setting I-bit (and **F-bit for FIQ**).
4. **Clear T-bit** → force **ARM state** (32-bit execution) for handler.
5. **R14_** = return address (`PC + 4` for most; `PC + 8` for data abort).
6. **R15 (PC) = vector address** → fetch handler from low memory.

### 2.4.2 Exception Return Sequence

1. **SPSR_ → CPSR** (restore flags and mode).
2. **R14_ → PC** (return to the caller, using `SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `#8`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Return Link Offset Table (Critical for KTU):**
> | Exception | Offset from `PC` stored in `LR` | Restore with |
> |---|---|---|
> | Reset / SWI / Undef | $+4$ | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |
> | IRQ / FIQ / Prefetch Abort | $+4$ | `SUBS PC, LR, #4` |
> | Data Abort | $+8$ | `SUBS PC, LR, #8` |

---

## 2.5 AMBA Bus Architecture (System Interconnect)

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is ARM's on-chip bus standard. It defines **three layers**:

| Bus | Full Name | Purpose | Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AHB** | Advanced High-performance Bus | CPU, memory, DMA — high-speed backbone | 32–128 bit |
| **ASB** | Advanced System Bus | Older alternative to AHB | 32 bit |
| **APB** | Advanced Peripheral Bus | Low-speed peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO) | 32 bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bridges** connect the buses: **AHB-to-APB bridge** allows the CPU (on AHB) to talk to peripherals (on APB) without burdening the high-speed bus.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline Stages** | $n$ (Fetch, Decode, Execute) | $n = 3$ for ARM7TDMI |
| **Ideal Speedup** | $S_{\text{ideal}} = n$ | $3$ |
| **Pipeline Throughput** | $T_p = \dfrac{N + n - 1}{N \cdot t_{\text{stage}}}$ | instructions/cycle |
| **Branch Penalty** | $P_b = n - 2$ | $1$ cycle (ARM7) |
| **CPI (ideal)** | $1$ (one instruction per cycle when no stalls) | cycles/instruction |
| **Banked Registers** | $N_{\text{banked}} = 6 \times 3 + 5 = 23$ | 23 distinct register copies |
| **Total Registers** | $N_{\text{phys}} = 13 + 6 + 6 + 1 + 1 + 5 + 5 = 37$ | 32-bit wide |
| **Vector Table Size** | $V = 8 \times 4$ | **32 bytes** |
| **Exception Return** | $PC = LR_{\text{mode}} - 4$ (or $-8$ for Data Abort) | — |
| **AMBA Layers** | $\{AHB, ASB, APB\}$ | 3 layers |
| **CPSR Width** | $32$ bits | bits |
| **Modes** | $M[4:0] = 5$ bits encode $7$ valid modes | $2^5 = 32$ possible |
| **Thumb vs ARM** | $T = 1 \Rightarrow$ Thumb (16-bit), $T = 0 \Rightarrow$ ARM (32-bit) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"FIQ is Fast and has many banked regs (R8–R14, 7 registers banked) to avoid saving them on stack — saving time."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pipeline Speedup vs Number of Instructions

**Goal:** Show that the pipeline speedup approaches the number of stages as program length grows.

### Step 1 — Define the baseline (non-pipelined execution)

Without a pipeline, each instruction requires **3 clock cycles** (one for each stage: F, D, E). For $N$ instructions, the total time is:

$$
T_{\text{non}} = 3N \;\; \text{cycles}
$$

**Logic:** In a non-pipelined CPU, when the ALU is computing an instruction in cycle 3, the instruction memory and decoder are **idle**. This is sequential by definition.

### Step 2 — Define the pipelined execution

In a 3-stage pipeline, after the **first 3 cycles** (fill-up), the pipeline produces **1 instruction per cycle**. The total time becomes:

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = 3 \;\; (\text{fill}) \;+\; (N - 1) \;\; (\text{steady state}) \;=\; (N + 2) \;\; \text{cycles}
$$

**Logic:** Each stage does its work in 1 cycle. After the first instruction exits the Execute stage (cycle 3), the next instruction finishes every subsequent cycle.

### Step 3 — Compute the speedup ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{T_{\text{non}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{3N}{N + 2} \\
     &= \frac{3N}{N + 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the limit for large programs

For typical embedded programs ($N \gg 1$, say $N = 1000$ instructions):

$$
S(1000) = \frac{3 \times 1000}{1000 + 2} = \frac{3000}{1002} \approx 2.994 \approx 3
$$

In the limit:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{3N}{N + 2} = 3
$$

**Conclusion:** The ideal speedup equals the **number of pipeline stages**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective CPI with Branch Stalls

**Goal:** Calculate the **average cycles per instruction (CPI)** accounting for branch penalty.

### Step 1 — Define CPI components

Let:
- $p_b$ = probability of a taken branch
- $P$ = branch penalty in cycles (1 for ARM7)
- $S_b$ = no penalty (the branch itself still costs 1 cycle)

### Step 2 — Write the average CPI

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + p_b \times P
$$

For ARM7TDMI, $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} = 1$ (ideal), $P = 1$:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = 1 + p_b \times 1 = 1 + p_b
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example

Assume $p_b = 0.20$ (20% of instructions are taken branches, typical for loops):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} &= 1 + 0.20 \times 1 \\
                        &= 1.20 \;\; \text{cycles/instruction}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute throughput

For a 50 MHz CPU clock:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MIPS} &= \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6} \\
            &= \frac{50 \times 10^6}{1.20 \times 10^6} \\
            &\approx 41.67 \;\; \text{MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Without branches**, MIPS = 50. With 20% branch loss, MIPS drops to 41.67 — a **16.6% loss** in throughput. This is why ARM Cortex-M3+ added **branch speculation** and **predicated execution**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Address and Memory Bandwidth

**Goal:** Show how AMBA AHB bandwidth is computed.

### Step 1 — Define the bus parameters

For an **AHB bus** with width $W = 32$ bits and clock frequency $f_{bus} = 50$ MHz:

$$
\text{Bus Clock Period} = T_{bus} = \frac{1}{f_{bus}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20 \; \text{ns}
$$

### Step 2 — Bytes transferred per cycle

For a 32-bit bus, each cycle transfers:

$$
D = 32 \; \text{bits} = 4 \; \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Bandwidth formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{bus}} &= D \times f_{bus} \\
               &= 4 \; \text{bytes} \times 50 \times 10^6 \; \text{Hz} \\
               &= 200 \times 10^6 \; \text{bytes/s} \\
               &= 200 \; \text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AHB bandwidth = **200 MB/s** at 50 MHz. Doubling the bus width to 64 bits would double this to 400 MB/s. This is why high-end ARM cores (Cortex-A) use 64- or 128-bit buses.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode vs. Banked Registers

| Mode | M[4:0] | Privilege? | R13_banked? | R14_banked? | SPSR_banked? | Extra banked |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | 10000 | No | No | No | No | None |
| FIQ | 10001 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | R8–R12 (5 extra) |
| IRQ | 10010 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | None |
| Supervisor | 10011 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | None |
| Abort | 10111 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | None |
| Undefined | 11011 | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | None |
| System | 11111 | Yes | No | No | No | None |

**Banked register count check:**

$$
N_{\text{banked}} = \underbrace{5}_{\text{R8–R12 in FIQ}} + \underbrace{6 \times 3}_{\text{R13, R14, SPSR in 6 priv modes (excl. System)}} = 5 + 18 = 23
$$

This matches the **23 banked registers** the ARM7TDMI has beyond the 14 fixed (R0–R12, R15) plus 1 CPSR:

$$
\underbrace{13}_{R0–R12} + \underbrace{1}_{R15} + \underbrace{1}_{CPSR} + \underbrace{23}_{banked} = 38
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Note:** 38 vs 37 — the official ARM7TDMI count of **37** uses a slightly different grouping (R8–R12 in FIQ = 5, plus R13 + R14 + SPSR in 5 modes = 15, plus 13 + 1 + 1 = 33, total = 33 + 5 - 1 = 37). The standard is **37 distinct 32-bit registers**. The "extra" is double-counting of R13/R14 across user/system. KTU exam answer: **37 registers**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Exception Latency

**Goal:** Compute the time from an IRQ signal to the first instruction of the handler.

### Step 1 — Identify the steps on entry

1. **Current instruction completes** in Execute stage: 1 cycle
2. **CPSR → SPSR_irq**: 1 cycle (done in parallel with stage 1)
3. **PC + 4 → LR_irq** & **PC = 0x18**: 1 cycle
4. **Mode bits updated**, I-bit set: 1 cycle (parallel)
5. **First instruction of handler fetched**: 1 cycle

### Step 2 — Total latency

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{latency}} &= 1 \;(\text{finish current}) + 1 \;(\text{vector fetch}) + 1 \;(\text{handler fetch}) \\
                   &= 3 \;\; \text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"What is IRQ latency in ARM7TDMI?"* → Answer: **3 cycles** (the minimum possible latency — assuming no wait states, no higher-priority FIQ active, and the IRQ is sampled on a non-aliased boundary).

---

## 3.6 Verification — Mapping Exception to Mode Bits

| Exception | Enters Mode | M[4:0] (binary) |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | Supervisor | `10011` |
| SWI | Supervisor | `10011` |
| IRQ | IRQ | `10010` |
| FIQ | FIQ | `10001` |
| Prefetch Abort | Abort | `10111` |
| Data Abort | Abort | `10111` |
| Undef Instruction | Undefined | `11011` |

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **mandatory** memorization item for KTU exams — usually a 7-mark question asks for "explain the exception vector table with modes entered."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ARM7TDMI Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Memory / AMBA Bus Interface] --> B[Instruction Fetch Unit]
    A --> C[Data Memory Interface]
    B --> D[Instruction Decode and Register File]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Barrel Shifter]
    E --> F[ALU 32 bit]
    F --> G[Write Back Bus]
    G --> D
    F --> H[Multiplier]
    H --> G
    F --> I[Condition Code Flags N Z C V]
    I --> D
    D --> J[CPSR Current Program Status Register]
    J --> K[SPSR Banked Copies 5]
    D --> L[Banked Registers R13 R14 per mode]
    L --> M[Operating Mode Decoder M4 to M0]
    M --> N[7 Operating Modes]
    N --> L
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram traces the data flow. Instructions flow from memory through Fetch → Decode → ALU (with optional multiplier & barrel shifter) → write-back. The **mode decoder** governs which banked register set is visible, and **CPSR/SPSR** hold the state.

---

## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (3-Stage)

```mermaid
gantt
    title ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline Timing
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instruction 1
    Fetch    :a1, 0, 1
    Decode   :a2, 1, 1
    Execute  :a3, 2, 1
    section Instruction 2
    Fetch    :b1, 1, 1
    Decode   :b2, 2, 1
    Execute  :b3, 3, 1
    section Instruction 3
    Fetch    :c1, 2, 1
    Decode   :c2, 3, 1
    Execute  :c3, 4, 1
    section Instruction 4
    Fetch    :d1, 3, 1
    Decode   :d2, 4, 1
    Execute  :d3, 5, 1
```

**Reading the diagram:** At cycle 2, Instruction 1 is in Execute, Instruction 2 is in Decode, and Instruction 3 is in Fetch — all **simultaneously**. The pipeline was "filled" in cycle 1 and produces one finished instruction per cycle thereafter.

---

## 4.3 AMBA Bus Architecture (System-Level View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[ARM7 CPU Core] -->|Instruction and Data| AHB[AHB Advanced High performance Bus]
    DMA[DMA Controller] -->|High Speed Transfer| AHB
    AHB -->|Bridge| APB[APB Advanced Peripheral Bus]
    APB --> UART1[UART 1]
    APB --> UART2[UART 2]
    APB --> TIMER[Timers 0 1 2 3]
    APB --> GPIO[GPIO Ports]
    APB --> SPI[SPI Controller]
    APB --> I2C[I2C Controller]
    AHB --> SRAM[On Chip SRAM]
    AHB --> EXT[External Memory Controller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **AHB-to-APB bridge** is essential — it decouples high-speed CPU traffic (AHB) from low-speed peripheral traffic (APB), so a slow UART cannot stall the CPU's instruction fetch.

---

## 4.4 Exception Entry Flow (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Normal Execution User Mode] --> S1{Exception Pending}
    S1 -->|IRQ raised| S2[Save CPSR to SPSR_irq]
    S2 --> S3[Set CPSR to IRQ Mode M equals 10010]
    S3 --> S4[Set I bit in CPSR Disable further IRQ]
    S4 --> S5[Clear T bit Force ARM state]
    S5 --> S6[LR_irq equals PC plus 4]
    S6 --> S7[PC equals 0x18 Vector Address]
    S7 --> S8[Begin Handler in ARM State]
    S8 --> S9[Handler restores SPSR to CPSR]
    S9 --> S10[SUBS PC LR 4 Return from IRQ]
    S10 --> S0
```

---

## 4.5 Register Banking Across Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    User[User Mode R0 to R12 shared] --> SVC[Supervisor Mode]
    User --> IRQ_M[IRQ Mode]
    User --> FIQ_M[FIQ Mode]
    User --> ABT[Abort Mode]
    User --> UND[Undefined Mode]
    User --> SYS[System Mode]
    FIQ_M -.banked.-> FR8[Banked R8]
    FIQ_M -.banked.-> FR9[Banked R9]
    FIQ_M -.banked.-> FR10[Banked R10]
    FIQ_M -.banked.-> FR11[Banked R11]
    FIQ_M -.banked.-> FR12[Banked R12]
    SVC -.banked.-> SVC_SP[SVC SP R13]
    SVC -.banked.-> SVC_LR[SVC LR R14]
    SVC -.banked.-> SVC_SPSR[SVC SPSR]
    IRQ_M -.banked.-> IRQ_SP[IRQ SP R13]
    IRQ_M -.banked.-> IRQ_LR[IRQ LR R14]
    IRQ_M -.banked.-> IRQ_SPSR[IRQ SPSR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** All modes share R0–R12. R13, R14, and SPSR are private to each privileged mode (except System, which shares with User). **FIQ has 7 extra banked registers (R8–R14)** — the most of any mode.

---

## 4.6 CPSR Bit Map Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    N[Bit 31 N Negative] --> Z[Bit 30 Z Zero]
    Z --> C[Bit 29 C Carry]
    C --> V[Bit 28 V Overflow]
    V --> Q[Bit 27 Q Sticky Saturation]
    Q --> R1[Bits 26 to 8 Reserved]
    R1 --> R2[Bit 7 I IRQ Disable]
    R2 --> R3[Bit 6 F FIQ Disable]
    R3 --> T[Bit 5 T Thumb State]
    T --> M[Bits 4 to 0 M Mode Select]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the ARM programmer's model. What is the total number of registers in ARM7TDMI?  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]**

**Model Answer (Points-wise):**

- The ARM programmer's model defines the **visible register set** for the active mode.  
- It contains **37 registers** of 32 bits each.  
- Of these, the programmer directly sees: **R0–R12** (general purpose), **R13 (SP)**, **R14 (LR)**, **R15 (PC)**, and **CPSR**.  
- The remaining registers (banked copies of R13, R14, and SPSR) are switched in based on the active exception mode.  
- R0–R12 are **unbanked** and shared across all modes.  
- R13, R14, and SPSR are **banked** — multiple physical copies exist (one per privileged mode).

---

### Q2. Differentiate between privileged and non-privileged operating modes in ARM.  
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 (Model)]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Non-Privileged Mode:** Only **User (usr)** mode. Program runs with full memory access **restricted** by the MMU/MPU. Cannot change CPSR directly; cannot disable interrupts; cannot enter privileged modes without an exception.
- **Privileged Modes:** All other 6 modes — **System, Supervisor, IRQ, FIQ, Abort, Undefined**. Full read/write to CPSR; can disable interrupts; can switch to other modes via software.
- The **System** mode is special — it uses User's register set (no banked copies) but retains privileged access.  
- Mode bits `M[4:0]` in CPSR determine the active mode. Code can read these bits to detect current privilege.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the 3-stage pipeline of ARM7TDMI with a timing diagram. Discuss the branch penalty. [7 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Pipeline Stages (3 marks):**  
The ARM7TDMI pipeline has three stages:
- **F (Fetch):** Reads the next instruction from memory using the address in PC.
- **D (Decode):** Decodes the instruction, reads source registers, generates control signals.
- **E (Execute):** Performs ALU operation, branch resolution, and register write-back.

**2. Timing Diagram (2 marks):**

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I1 | F | D | E | — | — | — | — | — |
| I2 | — | F | D | E | — | — | — | — |
| I3 | — | — | F | D | E | — | — | — |
| I4 | — | — | — | F | D | E | — | — |
| I5 | — | — | — | — | F | D | E | — |

At cycle 3, instruction I1 finishes; from cycle 4 onward, **one instruction completes per cycle** (throughput = 1).

**3. Branch Penalty (2 marks):**  
When a branch is taken in cycle $k$, the Execute stage resolves the new PC. The next instruction to enter Fetch is the **branch target**. However, the instruction that was fetched in cycle $(k+1)$ (the one after the branch) is the **wrong instruction** — it must be **flushed**. Hence, **1 cycle wasted** per branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch Penalty** for ARM7 = $\boxed{1 \text{ cycle}}$

---

#### (b) Explain the ARM exception handling mechanism. List the seven exception vectors with their modes. [7 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. What is an Exception? (1 mark):**  
An exception is a forced change in program flow triggered by an external or internal event (reset, interrupt, illegal instruction, memory fault). The CPU saves state and jumps to a **handler** at a **fixed vector address**.

**2. Exception Entry Steps (2 marks):**
1. CPSR → SPSR_new_mode (preserves flags and old mode).
2. CPSR M[4:0] ← new mode code.
3. CPSR I-bit = 1 (disable IRQ). For FIQ, also F-bit = 1.
4. CPSR T-bit = 0 (force ARM state, 32-bit instructions).
5. R14_new ← return address (PC + 4, or + 8 for data abort).
6. PC ← vector address.
7. Begin execution at vector.

**3. Exception Vector Table (3 marks):**

| Vector Address | Exception | Mode Entered | M[4:0] |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00000000 | Reset | Supervisor | 10011 |
| 0x00000004 | Undefined Instruction | Undefined | 11011 |
| 0x00000008 | Software Interrupt (SWI) | Supervisor | 10011 |
| 0x0000000C | Prefetch Abort | Abort | 10111 |
| 0x00000010 | Data Abort | Abort | 10111 |
| 0x00000018 | IRQ | IRQ | 10010 |
| 0x0000001C | FIQ | FIQ | 10001 |

**4. Exception Return (1 mark):**  
- SPSR_new → CPSR (restore flags and mode).
- PC ← LR_new (offset by 4 or 8 depending on exception).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the AMBA bus architecture. Why is a bridge used between AHB and APB? [7 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. AMBA Definition (1 mark):**  
AMBA (**Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture**) is ARM's on-chip bus standard that connects the CPU, memory, and peripherals in a System-on-Chip (SoC).

**2. Three Bus Layers (3 marks):**

- **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus):** 32–128 bit wide, used by CPU, on-chip SRAM, external memory controller, DMA. Pipelined, supports burst transfers.
- **ASB (Advanced System Bus):** Older, non-pipelined alternative to AHB. Most modern designs use AHB instead.
- **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus):** Simple, low-power, non-pipelined bus for slow peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO, I²C, SPI).

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** [As in Section 4.3]  
CPU and DMA on AHB → Bridge → APB → Peripherals. AHB connects to SRAM and external memory.

**4. Why the AHB-to-APB Bridge? (1 mark):**  
- The bridge **decouples timing**: AHB runs at the CPU clock; APB runs at a slower peripheral clock.
- Prevents **slow peripherals from stalling** the high-speed AHB.
- Acts as a **protocol converter** between pipelined AHB and non-pipelined APB.
- Reduces **power consumption** by clock-gating the APB segment when peripherals are idle.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam answer:** *"The bridge isolates high-speed CPU traffic from slow peripherals, ensuring the CPU is never stalled by a slow peripheral access."*

---

#### (b) Explain the register organization of ARM7TDMI. What are banked registers? Why does FIQ have more banked registers than IRQ? [7 Marks]  
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted Model)]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Register Organization (3 marks):**  
The ARM7TDMI has **37 registers**, all 32 bits wide:
- **R0–R12:** 13 unbanked general-purpose registers. Shared across all modes.
- **R13 (SP):** 6 banked copies — one per privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS — note: User shares SYS's R13).
- **R14 (LR):** 6 banked copies for link/return address.
- **R15 (PC):** Single program counter.
- **CPSR:** Single current program status register.
- **SPSR:** 5 banked copies — one per exception mode (excl. User and System).

**2. Banked Registers Definition (2 marks):**  
Banked registers are **physically distinct 32-bit registers** that occupy the **same logical name** (R13, R14, or SPSR). When the CPU switches mode, the active register set automatically changes — the previously visible register is "saved" by being banked away. The hardware does this **without explicit PUSH/POP** instructions, making exception entry near-instantaneous.

**3. Why FIQ has more banked registers (2 marks):**  
- FIQ is designed for **high-speed data transfer** (e.g., DSP routines, fast peripherals).
- To avoid the overhead of saving R8–R12 on the stack on every FIQ entry, ARM **bakes in banked copies of R8–R12** for the FIQ mode (5 extra registers).
- This means a typical FIQ handler can use R8–R12 directly as **local scratch** without corrupting User-mode values — saving the time and code of stack save/restore.
- The FIQ banked register count = 5 (R8–R12) + 3 (R13, R14, SPSR) = **8 banked registers** — the most of any mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIQ total banked: 8; IRQ/SVC/ABT/UND total banked: 3 each; User/System: 0.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks (KTU Board pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the banked register count** — students write "37" for the total but then "8 banked" or "3 banked" inconsistently. Use the standard: *23 banked register copies* beyond the fixed set.
> 2. **Mixing up LR offsets** — Data Abort uses `PC + 8`; everything else uses `PC + 4`. This is a **sure-shot 1-mark loss** if you don't know it.
> 3. **Confusing CPSR fields** — The I, F, T bits are in the **low byte** (bits 7–0), not in the high nibble. Many students draw the bit map wrong.
> 4. **Branch penalty confusion** — In a 3-stage pipeline, branch penalty is **1**, not 2 or 3. The classic trap: "deeper pipeline = bigger penalty."
> 5. **Drawing wrong exception vector addresses** — Must use `0x0000_0000`, `0x0000_0004`, ..., `0x0000_001C`. Off-by-4 errors are common.
> 6. **AMBA bus purpose** — Don't just list AHB/APB/ASB; **explain why a bridge is needed** (timing isolation, protocol conversion, power gating).
> 7. **Forgetting to clear T-bit on exception** — When an exception occurs in Thumb state, the hardware **forces ARM state** to ensure the vector handler is 32-bit. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ ARM7TDMI has **37 registers**, all 32 bits, in a **load-store RISC** architecture.
- ✅ Operating modes = **7** total — User (non-priv) + 6 privileged (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS).
- ✅ **R0–R12** are unbanked; **R13 (SP), R14 (LR), SPSR** are banked. FIQ has **extra banked R8–R12** (5 more).
- ✅ **CPSR** flags: **N, Z, C, V** in high nibble (bits 31–28); control bits **I, F, T, M[4:0]** in low byte (bits 7–0).
- ✅ Mode encoding (M[4:0]): User=`10000`, FIQ=`10001`, IRQ=`10010`, SVC=`10011`, ABT=`10111`, UND=`11011`, SYS=`11111`.
- ✅ **3-stage pipeline**: Fetch → Decode → Execute. Ideal speedup = **3×**.
- ✅ **Branch penalty in ARM7 = 1 cycle** (formula: penalty = n − 2, where n = stages).
- ✅ Exception vector table: **8 entries × 4 bytes = 32 bytes**, at fixed addresses from `0x0000_0000` to `0x0000_001C`.
- ✅ Exception entry does **CPSR → SPSR**, sets mode bits, sets **I-bit (and F-bit for FIQ)**, **clears T-bit**, sets **LR = PC + 4** (or + 8 for data abort), and **PC = vector address**.
- ✅ Exception return uses **SPSR → CPSR** and **PC = LR − 4** (or − 8).
- ✅ **AMBA** layers: **AHB** (high-speed CPU/DMA/memory), **ASB** (older alternative), **APB** (low-speed peripherals). A **bridge** connects AHB to APB.
- ✅ **Thumb state** = T-bit = 1 → 16-bit instructions; **ARM state** = T-bit = 0 → 32-bit instructions. Exceptions **force ARM state**.
- ✅ IRQ latency in ARM7 = **3 cycles** (finish current + vector fetch + first handler fetch).
- ✅ Pipeline throughput formula: $T_p = (N + n - 1)$ cycles for $N$ instructions in $n$-stage pipeline.
- ✅ CPI with branch stalls: $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = 1 + p_b \times P$ (where $p_b$ = branch probability, $P$ = penalty).
- ✅ FIQ = "Fast" because of its **8 banked registers** and its **highest priority** (cannot be masked by IRQ).
- ✅ Architecture ≠ Programming — focus on **structure** (register map, modes, pipeline, buses), not assembly syntax.

> **Good luck with the KTU 2024 ESE!** Focus on the **CPSR bit map**, the **exception vector table**, the **pipeline timing**, and the **AMBA bus hierarchy** — these four are the most-tested topics in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STM Boot Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT522 - ADVANCED MICROPROCESSORS & MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** STM Boot Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STM Boot Modes** are hardware-configurable startup configurations of the STMicroelectronics STM32 ARM Cortex-M microcontroller family. The boot mode is selected by sampling the logic level of the **BOOT0** pin (and on most families, the **BOOT1** pin) on the **4th rising edge of SYSCLK** after a reset (or at the deassertion of NRST). Based on these sampled values, the on-chip bootloader remaps one of three physical memories — **Main Flash Memory**, **System Memory**, or **Embedded SRAM** — to the **alias address 0x0000 0000**, from where the Cortex-M CPU fetches its initial stack pointer and reset vector.

The three canonical boot modes standardized across the STM32 family (F0, F1, F2, F3, F4, F7, H7, L0, L1, L4, G0, G4, H5, U5, WBA) are:

| Mode | BOOT0 | BOOT1 | Boot Source |
|------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Main Flash | 0 | x | 0x0800 0000 |
| System Memory | 1 | 0 | 0x1FFF 0000 (or family-specific) |
| Embedded SRAM | 1 | 1 | 0x2000 0000 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hotel Reception Desk**
> Imagine you walk into a large hotel. There are **three entrances**: the **Main Lobby** (your permanent room — *Main Flash*), the **Emergency Service Door** (only hotel staff use it for maintenance — *System Memory with ST Bootloader*), and the **Staff-Only Backdoor** (used by engineers during renovation — *SRAM for debug loading*).
> A security guard at the reception desk checks a small **switch** (the BOOT0 pin) the moment the hotel opens (the 4th clock edge after reset). Depending on the switch position, the guard redirects all guests (the CPU) to enter through one of the three doors. Once you enter, that entrance becomes your "address 0" — the very first instruction location for the CPU.
> The Main Lobby is where your compiled C program lives permanently. The Emergency Door contains a **pre-installed tiny program** from ST that knows how to receive new firmware over UART, USB, CAN, SPI, or I2C. The Staff Door is for engineers loading temporary code straight into RAM for ultra-fast debugging without flashing.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Latching Event:** BOOT pins are sampled on the **4th rising edge of SYSCLK** after reset, OR on the rising edge of **NRST** (whichever occurs first).
> - **Alias Region:** Selected memory is mapped to **0x0000 0000** (alias), original physical address preserved.
> - **Vector Table Offset:** Initial MSP (Main Stack Pointer) fetched from **0x0000 0000**; Reset Vector from **0x0000 0004**.
> - **Internal Pull-Down** is present on the **BOOT0** pin in most STM32 packages, defaulting the chip to **Main Flash boot** when the pin is left floating.
> - **BOOT1** is **not an external pin** on some devices (F2, F4, F7, H7, L4, G4) — it is replaced by an option byte **nBOOT1** in FLASH_OPTR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Boot Process

The boot sequence of any STM32 device follows a deterministic hardware state machine:

- **Step 1 — Power-On Reset / NRST Assertion**
  The on-chip reset circuitry releases the CPU. A counter starts monitoring SYSCLK.

- **Step 2 — BOOT Pin Latching**
  On the 4th rising edge of SYSCLK, the values of BOOT0 (physical pin) and BOOT1 (physical pin OR option byte nBOOT1) are latched into a hidden internal register. After this event, the BOOT0 pin can be repurposed as a normal GPIO (after re-configuration in software).

- **Step 3 — Physical-to-Alias Memory Remapping**
  The bus matrix is reconfigured. The memory selected by the BOOT pins is aliased to **0x0000 0000**. The original physical addresses remain valid and accessible by absolute address (e.g., code can still read `0x0800 0000` directly).

- **Step 4 — Vector Table Read**
  - Word at `0x0000 0000` → loaded into **MSP** (Main Stack Pointer).
  - Word at `0x0000 0004` → loaded into **PC** (Program Counter), i.e., the **Reset_Handler** address.

- **Step 5 — Execution Handoff**
  The Cortex-M core begins fetching instructions from the address held in PC. The on-chip **System Memory bootloader** (ST's IP) is now executing and will poll USART1, USART2, USART3, USB-OTG-FS, CAN, SPI, or I2C for a valid host handshake (0x7F for USART).

## 2.2 The Three Boot Modes — Deep Dive

### Mode 1: Boot from Main Flash Memory (BOOT0 = 0)

- **Use case:** Normal production application execution.
- **Memory:** User's `.text` and `.rodata` sections.
- **Starting physical address:** **0x0800 0000** (STM32F4/F7/H7 — 1 MB range).
- **Sector layout:** Flash is divided into sectors of varying sizes (e.g., STM32F407: 4×16 KB + 1×64 KB + 7×128 KB = 1 MB).
- **Persistence:** Non-volatile; survives power cycle.
- **Vector table:** At 0x0800 0000 (first 0x188 bytes typically contain the Cortex-M vector table).

### Mode 2: Boot from System Memory (BOOT0 = 1, BOOT1 = 0)

- **Use case:** In-System Programming (ISP) using the **ST Built-in Bootloader** (a.k.a. ROM Bootloader).
- **Memory:** Factory-programmed, read-only ROM containing ST's peripheral bootloaders.
- **Starting physical address:**
  - STM32F1/F0/F3/L0/L1: **0x1FFF 0000**
  - STM32F2/F4: **0x1FFF 0000** (size 30 KB)
  - STM32F7: **0x1FF0 0000** (size 64 KB)
  - STM32H7: **0x1FF0 0000**
  - STM32L4/G4: **0x1FFF 0000**
- **Supported protocols:** **USART1/2/3**, **USB DFU**, **CAN2**, **SPI1/2/3**, **I2C1/2/3** (varies by device).
- **Activation pattern:** On USART, host sends byte **0x7F** at a configured baud rate; the bootloader auto-detects baud by measuring the bit timing of the falling edge.
- **Real-world utility:** Field firmware updates without an SWD programmer; recovery from a "bricked" application.

### Mode 3: Boot from Embedded SRAM (BOOT0 = 1, BOOT1 = 1)

- **Use case:** In-RAM debugging with IDEs (Keil, IAR, STM32CubeIDE) using the `RAM.ini` script; custom bootloader stage-1 loaders.
- **Memory:** Volatile **CCM/SRAM** starting at **0x2000 0000** (or 0x1000 0000 for CCM on some devices).
- **Size:** 4 KB to 1 MB+ depending on family (e.g., STM32F407 has 192 KB SRAM + 64 KB CCM).
- **Persistence:** None — content is lost on power cycle.
- **Advantage:** No flash wear during iterative debug cycles; instant code load via SWD.

## 2.3 Boot Mode Selection Pin & Option Byte Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> On modern STM32 devices (F7, H7, L4, G0, G4, H5, U5, WBA), the boot configuration is augmented by **option bytes** in Flash:
> - **nBOOT0** (FLASH_OPTR bit): when 0, **forces BOOT0=0** regardless of pin (security feature).
> - **nSWBOOT0** (FLASH_OPTR bit): when 0, BOOT0 is taken from the option byte; when 1, BOOT0 is taken from the physical pin.
> - **nBOOT1** (FLASH_OPTR bit): selects BOOT1 value when the physical pin is not bonded out.
> This enables **secure boot**, **write-protection of option bytes via RDP (Readout Protection) levels 0/1/2**.

## 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Summary Table

| Parameter | Main Flash | System Memory | Embedded SRAM |
|-----------|------------|---------------|---------------|
| **BOOT0 pin** | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **BOOT1 pin / nBOOT1** | x (don't care) | 0 | 1 |
| **Alias start addr** | 0x0000 0000 | 0x0000 0000 | 0x0000 0000 |
| **Physical start (F4)** | 0x0800 0000 | 0x1FFF 0000 | 0x2000 0000 |
| **Memory type** | NOR Flash (volatile cache backed) | Mask ROM | SRAM |
| **Volatility** | Non-volatile | Non-volatile (factory) | Volatile |
| **Typical size (F407)** | 1 MB | 30 KB | 192 KB + 64 KB CCM |
| **Used for** | Application execution | ISP programming | RAM debug |
| **Vector table base** | 0x0800 0000 | 0x1FFF 0000 | 0x2000 0000 |
| **Access via SCB->VTOR** | Yes | Yes (rare) | Yes (debug) |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

STM boot modes are foundational to several real-world engineering workflows:
- **Mass production programming** — Using the System Memory bootloader, manufacturers can program thousands of boards over a single UART/USB line with the **STM32CubeProgrammer** tool.
- **OTA (Over-The-Air) updates** — A custom application-level bootloader (e.g., residing in sector 0) chains into an application at sector 1, with rollback capability.
- **Secure firmware install** — RDP Level 2 disables all boot modes except Main Flash, preventing external readout.
- **Live debugging** — Engineers use SRAM boot + SWD for sub-second iteration cycles during development.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Vector Table Relocation on Boot

The Cortex-M3/M4/M7 core uses a fixed hardware mechanism to locate the initial stack pointer and program counter after reset. We can derive the addressing logic mathematically.

**Given:**
- After reset, the CPU's vector table offset register **SCB->VTOR** is reset to **0x0000 0000**.
- The bus matrix remaps the BOOT-selected memory to **0x0000 0000**.
- The Cortex-M vector table follows a strict layout convention.

**Step 1 — MSP Loading**

The hardware reads the 32-bit word at:

$$
\text{MSP}_{init} = \text{Mem32}[0x0000\,0000]
$$

**Step 2 — Reset Vector Loading**

The hardware reads the 32-bit word at:

$$
\text{PC}_{init} = \text{Mem32}[0x0000\,0004]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitution Based on BOOT Mode**

For **Main Flash** mode (BOOT0=0), the alias resolves to physical address **0x0800 0000**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSP}_{init} &= \text{Mem32}[0x0800\,0000] \\
\text{PC}_{init} &= \text{Mem32}[0x0800\,0004]
\end{aligned}
$$

For **System Memory** mode (BOOT0=1, BOOT1=0), the alias resolves to **0x1FFF 0000** (STM32F4 family):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSP}_{init} &= \text{Mem32}[0x1FFF\,0000] \\
